If collected before flowering, the root color is vivid and bright; if collected after the flowers have passed, the root color becomes dark and poor. At the time of collection, press flat with a stone and sun-dry. During storage, keep away from human urine, donkey and horse dung, and smoke, as all of these will cause the herb to turn a dull yellow color.

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Bitter, cold, non-toxic. [Zhang] Quan states: Sweet, balanced [ping]. [Liu] Wansu states: Bitter, warm.

Li Shizhen states: Sweet and salty, cold. Enters the hand and foot jueyin meridians.

**[Principal Indications]**
Evil qi [xie qi] in the heart and abdomen; the five types of jaundice [wu dan]; supplements the center and boosts qi; benefits the nine orifices; unblocks the water passages (Shennong Bencao Jing). Treats abdominal swelling, fullness, and pain. Combined into a paste [gao], treats sores in children and facial blemishes [面䵴] (Bielu). Treats malignant sores and tinea [ringworm] (Zhen Quan). Treats macular rashes, pox-toxin [dou du], quickens the blood, cools the blood, and promotes the movement of the large intestine (Li Shizhen).

**[Elaboration / Elucidation]**
Su Song states: Zicao was rarely used in ancient prescriptions. Today physicians commonly use it to treat cases of cold-damage [shanghan] seasonal illness where sores and rashes fail to erupt, using it as a medicinal to bring the eruption forth. The Weizhou Duxing Fang [Wei Zhou's Solitary Formulary] treats pea-pox [smallpox / cowpox-like eruption] by decocting zicao into a soup to drink; later practitioners have continued this practice, and its effect is particularly swift.

Li Shizhen states: Zicao is sweet and salty in flavor and cold in qi; it enters the pericardiac network [xinbao luo] and the blood aspect [xue fen] of the Liver meridian. Its strength lies in cooling the blood and quickening the blood, and in promoting the movement of the large and small intestines. Therefore, for pox and rashes that are about to but have not yet erupted — when there is blood-heat with abundant toxin and constipation — it is appropriate to use it. It may also be used when [the rash] has already erupted but is purple-black in color and accompanied by constipation. However, if [the rash] has already erupted and is a healthy red color, or if there is a white-sunken [eruption] with loose stools, it is especially [contra-]indicated [lit. 'particularly beneficial to avoid it'].

Furthermore, Zeng Shirong's Huoyou Xinshu [A Heart's Guide to Reviving the Young] states: Zicao is cold in nature; it may still be used for children with a replete Spleen qi, but in those with deficient Spleen qi it will instead cause diarrhea. Ancient prescriptions used only the fuzz [rong] of the root, taking advantage of its first receiving yang qi — using like to activate like — which is the reason it was used to bring forth pox-sores. People today do not understand this principle and use it indiscriminately, which is incorrect.

segments.title_inferred: Baitouweng (Pulsatilla / Pasque Flower) — Names, Descriptions, Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】野丈人（《本經》）、胡王使者（《本經》）、奈何草（《別錄》）。

弘景曰：處處有之。近根處有白茸，狀似白頭老翁，故以為名。

時珍曰：丈人、胡使、奈何，皆狀老翁之意。

【集解】《別錄》曰：白頭翁，生高山山谷及田野。四月采。

恭曰：其葉似芍藥而大，抽一莖。莖頭一花，紫色，似木槿花。實大者如雞子，白毛寸餘，皆披下，似纛頭，正似白頭老翁，故名焉。陶言近根有白茸，似不識也。太常所貯蔓生者，乃是女葳。其白頭翁根，似續斷而扁。

保升曰：所在有之。有細毛，不滑澤，花蕊黃。二月采花，四月采實，八月采根，皆晒乾。

頌曰：處處有之。正月生苗，作叢生，狀似白薇而柔細稍長。葉生莖頭，如杏葉，上有細白毛而不滑澤。近根有白茸。根紫色，深如蔓菁。其苗有風則靜，無風而搖，與赤箭、獨活同也。陶注未述莖葉，蘇注言葉似芍藥，實如雞子，白毛寸餘者，皆誤矣。

宗奭曰：白頭翁生河南洛陽界，其新安山野中屢嘗見之，正如蘇恭所說。至今本處山中人賣白頭翁丸，言服之壽考，又失古人命名之義。陶氏所說，失於不審，宜其排叱也。

機曰：寇宗奭以蘇恭為是，蘇頌以陶說為是。大抵此物用根，命名取象，當準蘇頌《圖經》，而恭說恐別是一物也。

【氣味】苦，溫，無毒。《別錄》曰：有毒。吳綬曰：苦、辛，寒。權曰：甘、苦，有小毒。豚實為之使。大明曰：得酒良。花、子、莖、葉同。

【主治】溫瘧狂易，寒熱，症瘕積聚癭氣，逐血，止痛，療金瘡（《本經》）。鼻衄（《別錄》）。止毒痢（弘景）。赤痢腹痛，齒痛，百骨節痛，項下瘤癭。（甄權）。一切風氣，暖腰膝，明目消贅（大明）。瘧疾寒熱，白禿頭瘡（時珍）。

【發明】頌曰：俗醫合補下藥，甚驗，亦沖人。

杲曰：氣濃味薄，可升可降，陰中陽也。張仲景治熱痢下重，用白頭翁湯主之。蓋腎欲堅，急食苦以堅之。痢則下焦虛，故以純苦之劑堅之。男子陰疝偏墜，小兒頭禿膻腥，鼻衄無此不效，毒痢有此獲功。

吳綬曰：熱毒下痢紫血鮮血者，宜之。
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Alternative names: Yě Zhàngrén [Wild Old Man] (Shennong Bencao Jing), Húwáng Shǐzhě [Envoy of the Barbarian King] (Shennong Bencao Jing), Nàihé Cǎo [Alas Herb] (Mingyi Bielu).

Táo Hóngjǐng states: It is found everywhere. Near the root there is white downy fluff, resembling the white hair of an elderly man, hence the name.

Lǐ Shízhēn states: 'Old Man,' 'Barbarian Envoy,' and 'Alas' all evoke the image of an aged man.

**[Collected Commentaries]** The Míngyi Biélù states: Baitouweng grows in mountain valleys at high elevations and in open fields. It is harvested in the fourth month.

Sū Gōng states: Its leaves resemble those of peony [Paeonia] but are larger, and it sends up a single stalk. At the tip of the stalk is one flower, purple in color, resembling the flower of the rose of Sharon [Hibiscus syriacus]. The fruit, when large, is the size of a hen's egg; its white hairs are more than an inch long, all hanging downward like a war-banner tassel, exactly resembling the white head of an old man — hence the name. Táo's claim that white downy fluff is found near the root suggests he did not recognize the plant. The climbing plant stored by the Tàicháng [Imperial Medical Office] is in fact Nǚwēi [Clematis or similar vine]. The root of Baitouweng resembles that of Xùduàn [Dipsacus] but is flatter.

Hán Bǎoshēng states: It is found everywhere. It has fine hairs, lacks a smooth, glossy surface, and has yellow stamens. Flowers are collected in the second month, fruit in the fourth month, and roots in the eighth month; all are sun-dried.

Sū Sòng states: It is found everywhere. Sprouts emerge in the first month, growing in clumps, resembling Báiwēi [Cynanchum atratum] but more slender, pliable, and slightly longer. Leaves grow at the tip of the stalk, shaped like apricot leaves, covered with fine white hairs and lacking a glossy surface. Near the root there is white downy fluff. The root is purple in color, deeply resembling that of the turnip [Brassica rapa]. Curiously, the plant is still when the wind blows but sways when there is no wind — a trait shared with Chìjiàn [Gastrodia elata] and Dúhuó [Angelica pubescens]. Táo's annotation fails to describe the stem and leaves; Sū Gōng's note claiming the leaves resemble peony, the fruit is the size of a hen's egg, and the white hairs are over an inch long is erroneous.

Kòu Zōngshì states: Baitouweng grows in the Luòyáng region of Hénán; I have repeatedly seen it in the mountain fields of Xīn'ān, exactly as Sū Gōng described. To this day, local mountain people sell Baitouweng pills, claiming that taking them prolongs life — which entirely misses the original meaning behind the plant's name. Táo's account reflects careless observation, and it is fitting that it has been criticized and dismissed.

Wáng Jī states: Kòu Zōngshì takes Sū Gōng to be correct, while Sū Sòng takes Táo's account to be correct. Since this substance is used for its root, and the name derives from the plant's appearance, one should follow Sū Sòng's Tújīng [Illustrated Classic] as the standard; Sū Gōng's description likely refers to an altogether different plant.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter; warm; non-toxic. The Míngyi Biélù states: toxic. Wú Shù states: bitter, acrid, cold. Zhèn Quán states: sweet, bitter; slightly toxic. Tún shí [hog's-fennel fruit, Peucedanum] acts as its guiding herb [shǐ]. Rìhuá zǐ states: Efficacy is enhanced with wine. The flower, seed, stem, and leaves share the same properties.

**[Indications and Treatments]** Warm malaria with deranged behavior [kuáng yì], alternating chills and fever, abdominal masses [zhèng jiǎ], accumulations and gatherings [jī jù], goiter [yǐng qì], expelling stagnant blood, relieving pain, and treating wounds from metal implements [jīn chuāng] (Shennong Bencao Jing). Nosebleed [bí nǜ] (Míngyi Biélù). Stopping toxic dysentery [dú lì] (Táo Hóngjǐng). Red dysentery with abdominal pain, toothache, pain in the hundred joints, and goiter swelling below the neck (Zhèn Quán). All wind-related conditions [fēng qì], warming the lumbar region and knees, improving vision, and dispersing warts and excrescences (Rìhuá zǐ). Malarial chills and fever, and white-scab scalp sores [bái tū tóu chuāng] (Lǐ Shízhēn).

**[Elucidation]** Sū Sòng states: Common physicians compound it into tonifying lower-body [bǔ xià] prescriptions with very good results, though it can also be overpowering.

Lǐ Gǎo states: Its qi is thick and its flavor thin; it can both ascend and descend — it is yáng within yīn. Zhāng Zhòngjǐng treated hot dysentery with tenesmus [xià zhòng] using the Baitouweng Decoction [Baitouweng Tang] as the principal formula. The kidney desires consolidation [jiān], and one should urgently consume bitter [flavor] to consolidate it. Dysentery indicates vacuity in the lower burner, so a purely bitter preparation is used to consolidate it. For male inguinal hernia with prolapse [yīn shàn piān zhuì], scalp baldness with foul-smelling odor in children, and nosebleed, there is no efficacy without this herb; for toxic dysentery, its use achieves good results.

Wú Shù states: It is appropriate for heat-toxin dysentery with discharge of dark purple blood or fresh red blood.

segments.title_inferred: Sanqi (Three-Seven Root): Names, Origins, Properties, and Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】山漆（《綱目》）、金不換。

時珍曰︰彼人言其葉左三右四，故名三七，蓋恐不然。或云本名山漆，謂其能合金瘡，如漆粘物也，此說近之。金不換，貴重之稱也。

【集解】時珍曰︰生廣西、南丹諸州番峒深山中，采根曝乾，黃黑色。團結者，狀略似白芨；長者，如老乾地黃，有節。味微甘而苦，頗似人參之味。或云︰試法，以末糝豬血中，血化為水者乃真。近傳一種草，春生苗，夏高三、四尺。葉似菊艾而勁濃，有歧尖。莖有赤棱。夏秋開黃花，蕊如金絲，盤紐可愛，而氣不香。花乾則吐絮如苦絮。根葉味甘。治金瘡折傷出血，及上下血病，甚效。云是三七，而根大如牛蒡根，與南中來者不類，恐是劉寄奴之屬，甚易繁衍。

【氣味】甘、微苦，溫，無毒。

【主治】止血散血定痛，金刃箭傷、跌撲杖瘡、血出不止者，嚼爛涂，或為末摻之，其血即止。亦主吐血衄血，下血血痢，崩中經水不止，產後惡血不下，血運血痛，赤目癰腫，虎咬蛇傷諸病（時珍）。
折傷跌撲出血，敷之即止；青腫，經夜即散，餘功同根（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰此藥近時始出，南人軍中用為金瘡要藥，云有奇功。又云︰凡杖撲傷損，瘀血淋漓者，隨即嚼爛，罨之即止；青腫者，即消散。若受杖時，先服一、二錢，則血不沖心；杖後，尤宜服之。產後服，亦良。大抵此藥氣溫、味甘微苦，乃陽明、厥陰血分之藥，故能治一切血病，與麒麟竭、紫礦相同。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Also known as Shanqi [Mountain Lacquer] (Bencao Gangmu) and Jinbuhuan ["Gold Cannot Replace It," i.e., Beyond Price].

Li Shizhen states: The local people say the leaves grow three on the left and four on the right, hence the name Sanqi [Three-Seven]; however, this explanation is likely incorrect. Another account holds that the original name was Shanqi [Mountain Lacquer], referring to its ability to close [traumatic] wounds [lit. "metal sores"] just as lacquer binds objects together — this explanation seems closer to the truth. "Jinbuhuan" is a term expressing the herb's great value.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Li Shizhen states: It grows in the deep mountains of the tribal territories [lit. "番峒"] of Guangxi, Nandan, and neighboring prefectures. The root is collected and sun-dried; it is yellow-black in color. The compact, rounded specimens somewhat resemble Baiji [Bletilla striata]; the elongated specimens resemble aged, dried Dihuang [Rehmannia], and have nodes. The flavor is slightly sweet and bitter, quite similar to the taste of Renshen [ginseng]. One method of authentication is said to be: scatter the powdered root into pig's blood — if the blood dissolves into water, the specimen is genuine.

Recently, a plant has been described that sprouts in spring and grows three to four feet tall by summer. Its leaves resemble those of chrysanthemum and mugwort but are firm and thick, with forked, pointed tips. The stems have reddish ridges. In summer and autumn it bears yellow flowers with stamens like golden threads, wound together charmingly, though without fragrance. When the flowers dry, they release down like that of the sowthistle [Ixeris]. Both root and leaves are sweet in flavor. It is said to be highly effective for treating traumatic wounds, fractures with bleeding, and hemorrhagic conditions both above and below [the diaphragm]. It is claimed to be Sanqi, but the root is as large as that of burdock [Arctium lappa] and bears no resemblance to specimens from the south. It is more likely a member of the Liujinu [Artemisia anomala] group, and it spreads and propagates very easily.

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Sweet, slightly bitter; warm; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Therapeutic Uses]**
Stops bleeding, disperses [stagnant] blood, and relieves pain. For wounds from blades or arrows, injuries from falls and blows, and sores from flogging where bleeding does not stop: chew the root to a pulp and apply it, or grind it to powder and apply to the wound — the bleeding will cease immediately. Also indicated for: vomiting of blood, nosebleed, rectal bleeding, bloody dysentery, uterine flooding and incessant menstruation, failure of lochia to descend after childbirth, blood dizziness [blood-induced fainting], blood pain, red and swollen eyes, abscesses and swellings, bites from tigers and snakes, and various other conditions (Li Shizhen).
For fractures, falls, and traumatic bleeding: apply [the leaves] to stop the bleeding immediately; for bruising and swelling, one night's application will disperse it. The remaining properties are the same as those of the root (Li Shizhen).

**[Elucidation]**
Li Shizhen states: This drug has only recently come into use. Military personnel in the south employ it as an essential drug for traumatic wounds, and it is said to produce remarkable results. It is also said that for any injury from flogging or beating with pooling and dripping of blood stasis, one should immediately chew it to a pulp and apply it as a poultice — the bleeding will stop at once; bruising and swelling will disperse immediately. If one takes one to two qian [a unit of weight, approx. 3–6 g] prior to receiving a flogging, the blood will not rush upward to the heart; after the flogging, it is especially appropriate to take it. It is also beneficial when taken postpartum. In general, this drug is warm in qi and sweet with a slight bitterness in flavor; it is a drug that acts on the blood aspect [xuefen] of the Yangming and Jueyin [meridians], and thus is able to treat all blood disorders — in this regard it is comparable to Qilinjie [Dragon's blood resin, Daemonorops draco] and Zikuang [lac resin, Laccifer lacca].

segments.title_inferred: Hu Huanglian (Picrorhiza / False Gentian) – Herb Entry
segments.original_text:
【釋名】割孤露澤。

時珍曰：其性味功用似黃連，故名。割孤露澤，胡語也！

【集解】恭曰：胡黃連出波斯國，生海畔陸地。苗若夏枯草，根頭似鳥嘴，折之內似鵒眼者，良。八月上旬采之。頌曰：今南海及秦隴間亦有之。初生似蘆，乾則似楊柳枯枝，心黑外黃，不拘時月收采。承曰：折之塵出如煙者，乃爲真也！

根

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

恭曰：大寒。惡菊花、玄參、白蘚皮，解巴豆毒。忌豬肉，令人漏精。

【主治】補肝膽，明目，治骨蒸勞熱，三消，五心煩熱，婦人胎蒸虛驚，冷熱泄痢，五痔，濃腸胃，益顏色。浸人乳汁，點目甚良（蘇恭）。治久痢成疳，小兒驚癇、寒熱、不下食，霍亂下痢，傷寒咳嗽溫瘧，理腰腎，去陰汗（《開寶》）。去果子積（震亨）。
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also called 'Gēgūlùzé' [a transcription of a foreign word].

Li Shizhen says: Its nature, flavor, and medicinal functions resemble those of Huanglian [Coptis], hence the name [Hu Huanglian, meaning 'foreign Huanglian']. 'Gēgūlùzé' is a term from a foreign [Hu] language.

**[Collected Explanations]** Su Gong says: Hu Huanglian comes from the kingdom of Persia [Bosi], growing on land near the sea. Its shoots resemble those of Xiakucao [self-heal, Prunella vulgaris]; the tip of the root resembles a bird's beak; those whose interior, when snapped, resembles the eye of a mynah bird [starling] are of superior quality. It is harvested in the first ten days of the eighth month. Su Song says: It is now also found in the Nanhai region and in the Qin-Long [Shaanxi-Gansu] area. When young the plant resembles a reed; once dried it resembles the withered branches of a willow, black at the core and yellow on the outside. It may be collected at any time of year. Chen Cheng says: A genuine specimen, when snapped, releases dust like smoke — this is the true article.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

Su Gong says: [In nature it is] greatly cold. It is incompatible with [è: antagonized by] chrysanthemum flowers [júhuā], scrophularia root [xuánshēn], and dictamnus root-bark [báixiānpí]. It counteracts the toxicity of croton seed [bādòu]. Pork is to be avoided when using it, as it causes seminal leakage.

**[Principal Treatments]**
- Supplements [tones] the liver and gallbladder; brightens the eyes.
- Treats bone-steaming taxation fever [gǔzhēng láorè], the three wasting conditions [sānxiāo / diabetes-like syndromes], and vexing heat in the five centers [wǔxīn fánrè: palms, soles, and chest].
- Treats women's fetal steaming with vacuity fright [tāizhēng xūjīng], cold-heat diarrhea and dysentery, the five hemorrhoids [wǔzhì].
- Thickens [strengthens] the intestines and stomach; improves complexion.
- Soaked in human breast milk and applied as eye drops, it is highly effective. (Su Gong)
- Treats chronic dysentery that has developed into gan [malnutrition/infantile gan-accumulation]; childhood fright epilepsy [jīngxián], alternating cold and heat, inability to eat; cholera with diarrhea; cold damage [shanghán] with cough and warm malaria [wēnnüè]; regulates the lumbar region and kidneys; eliminates yin sweating [sweating of the genitals/groin]. (Kaibao [Bencao])
- Disperses accumulations from fruit [fruit stagnation]. (Zhu Zhenheng [Danxi])

segments.title_inferred: Qinjiao (Gentiana macrophylla) — Root Monograph
segments.original_text:
【釋名】秦糺（《唐本》）、秦爪（蕭炳）。

恭曰：秦艽，俗作秦膠，本名秦糺，與糾同。

時珍曰：秦艽出秦中，以根作羅紋交糾者佳，故名秦艽、秦糺。

【集解】《別錄》曰：秦艽，生飛鳥山谷，二月、八月採根，曝乾。

弘景曰：今出甘松、龍洞蠶咬，以根作羅紋，相交長大，黃白色者爲佳，中多衝土，用宜破去。

恭曰：今出涇州、鄜州、岐州者，良。

頌曰：今河陝州郡多有之。其根土黃色而相交糾，長一尺以來，粗細不等。枝幹高五、六寸。葉婆娑，連莖梗俱青色，如萵苣葉。六月中開花紫色，似葛花，當月結子。每於春秋采根，陰乾。

根

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。《別錄》曰：辛，微溫。大明曰：苦，冷。

元素曰：氣微溫，味苦、辛，陰中微陽，可升可降，入手陽明經。

之才曰：菖蒲爲之使，畏牛乳。

【主治】寒熱邪氣，寒濕風痺，肢節痛，下水利小便（《本經》）。療風無問久新，通身攣急（《別錄》）。傳尸骨蒸，治疳及時氣（大明）。牛乳點服，利大小便，療酒黃、黃膽，解酒毒，去頭風（甄權）。除陽明風濕，及手足不遂，口噤牙痛口瘡，腸風瀉血，養血榮筋（元素）。泄熱，益膽氣（好古）。治胃熱虛勞發熱（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：秦艽，手足陽明經藥也，兼入肝膽，故手足不遂、黃膽煩渴之病須之，取其去陽明之濕熱也。陽明有濕，則身體酸疼煩熱；有熱，則日晡潮熱骨蒸。所以《聖惠方》治急勞煩熱，身體酸疼。用秦艽、柴胡各一兩，甘草五錢。爲末，每服三錢，白湯調下。治小兒骨蒸潮熱，減食瘦弱。用秦艽、炙甘草各一兩。每用一、二錢，水煎服之。錢乙加薄荷葉五錢。
segments.translation:
**Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Qinjiu [秦糺] (Tang Materia Medica); Qinzhao [秦爪] (Xiao Bing).

Su Gong states: Qinjiao is commonly written as Qinjiao [秦膠]; its original name is Qinjiu [秦糺], which is equivalent in meaning to 'twisted/intertwined' [糾].

Li Shizhen states: Qinjiao originates from the Qin region [present-day Shaanxi/Gansu]. The finest specimens have roots with a spiral, rope-like [luowen] intertwisted pattern; hence the names Qinjiao and Qinjiu.

**Collected Explanations**
*Mingyi Bielu* states: Qinjiao grows in the mountain valleys of Feiniao [Flying Bird Mountain]. The roots are collected in the second and eighth months and sun-dried.

Tao Hongjing states: It is currently produced in Gansong and Longdong [ILLEGIBLE: 'Canjiao/蠶咬' — likely a place name or descriptor, possibly a scribal error]. Specimens whose roots form large, intertwisted rope-like patterns and are yellow-white in colour are the finest. The roots often contain much compacted earth, which should be broken off and removed before use.

Su Gong states: Specimens currently from Jingzhou, Fuzhou, and Qizhou are of superior quality.

Su Song states: It is found widely in the prefectures of He [Henan] and Shan [Shaanxi]. The root is earth-yellow in colour and intertwisted, reaching roughly one Chinese foot [chi] in length, varying in thickness. The stems grow five to six inches tall. The leaves are full and spreading, with both leaves and stems wholly green in colour, resembling lettuce [woju] leaves. In mid-sixth month it produces purple flowers resembling kudzu [ge] flowers, and sets seed the same month. Roots are gathered in spring and autumn and shade-dried.

**Root**

**Qi and Flavour**
Bitter, balanced [ping], non-toxic. *Bielu* states: acrid, slightly warm. Rihua Zhu states: bitter, cold.

Zhang Yuansu states: Qi slightly warm; flavour bitter and acrid. Yin with a slight Yang quality; capable of ascending and descending; enters the Hand Yangming [Large Intestine] Channel.

Xu Zhicai states: Calamus [changpu, Acorus calamus] acts as its guide herb; it is antagonised by cow's milk.

**Principal Indications [Zhizhi]**
- Cold-heat [alternating fever-chills] and pathogenic qi; cold-damp wind-impediment [fengbi]; joint and limb pain; promotes urination and drains water (*Shennong Bencao Jing*).
- Treats wind [disorders], whether of long standing or recent onset; general bodily spasm and contracture (*Mingyi Bielu*).
- Consumption with bone-steaming fever [chuanshi guzhen]; treats gan [nutritional disorders in children] and seasonal epidemic qi [Rihua Zhu / Da Ming].
- Taken dissolved in cow's milk, promotes both urination and defecation; treats alcohol-induced jaundice [jiuhuang] and jaundice [huangdan]; resolves alcohol toxicity; eliminates head-wind headache (Zhen Quan).
- Eliminates Yangming wind-damp; treats paralysis of the hands and feet, lockjaw, toothache, oral sores, intestinal wind with bloody diarrhoea; nourishes blood and invigorates the sinews (Zhang Yuansu).
- Drains heat; benefits gallbladder qi (Wang Haogu).
- Treats stomach heat and deficiency-taxation fever (Li Shizhen).

**Elaboration [Faming]**
Li Shizhen states: Qinjiao is a medicinal for the Hand and Foot Yangming Channels, and additionally enters the Liver and Gallbladder Channels. It is therefore indispensable in conditions of paralysis of the hands and feet, and jaundice with vexation-thirst, since it removes damp-heat from the Yangming. When the Yangming suffers from dampness, the body aches and there is vexing heat; when it suffers from heat, there is afternoon tidal fever and bone-steaming [hectic fever].

For this reason, the *Sheng Hui Fang* [Formulary of Sagely Benevolence] uses Qinjiao to treat acute consumption [ji lao] with vexing heat and aching of the body: take one liang each of Qinjiao and Bupleurum [chaihu], and five qian of Licorice [gancao]; grind to powder; take three qian per dose dissolved in plain hot water.

To treat bone-steaming tidal fever in children with reduced appetite and emaciation: take one liang each of Qinjiao and honey-roasted Licorice [zhi gancao]; use one to two qian per dose, decocted in water. Qian Yi [the paediatrician] added five qian of peppermint leaves [bohe ye] to this formula.

segments.title_inferred: Tu Danggui (Earth Angelica / Wild Angelica) — Root Entry
segments.original_text:
【集解】生密縣山野，莖圓而有線楞，葉似芹菜葉而硬，邊有細鋸齒刺。又似蒼朮葉而大，每三葉攢生一處，開黃花，根似前胡，又似野胡蘿蔔根。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】除風和血，煎酒服之。閃拗手足，同荊芥、蔥白煎湯淋洗之（時珍。出《衛生易簡方》）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】 Grows in the mountain wilds of Mi County [present-day Mixian, Henan]. The stem is round with fine ridges. The leaves resemble those of celery [Apium graveolens] but are stiffer, with fine serrated teeth along the margins. They also resemble the leaves of Cangzhu [Atractylodes lancea] but are larger; every three leaves grow clustered together at one point. It bears yellow flowers. The root resembles that of Qianhu [Peucedanum praeruptorum] and also that of wild carrot [Daucus carota].

Root

【Qi and Flavor】 Acrid [xin], warm, non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatment】 Dispels wind and harmonizes the blood; decoct in wine and take orally. For sprains and twists of the hands and feet, decoct together with Jingjie [Schizonepeta tenuifolia] and scallion whites [Allium fistulosum, the white part] and use the liquid to wash and rinse the affected area. (Li Shizhen; sourced from the Weisheng Yijian Fang [Easy and Simple Formulas for Health Preservation].)

segments.title_inferred: Duguancao (都管草) — Root, Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：都管草生宜州田野，根似羌活頭，歲長一節。苗高一尺許，葉似土當歸，有重台。二月、八月採根，陰乾。施州生者作蔓，又名香球，蔓長丈餘，赤色，秋結紅實，四時皆有，采其根枝，淋洗風毒瘡腫。時珍曰：按：范成大《桂海志》云：廣西出之，一莖六葉。

根

【氣味】苦、辛，寒，無毒。

【主治】風腫癰毒赤疣，以醋摩塗之。亦治咽喉腫痛，切片含之，立愈（蘇頌）。解蜈蚣、蛇毒（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Su Song states: Duguancao grows in the fields and open country of Yizhou [modern northern Guangxi]. Its root resembles the head of Qianghuo [Notopterygium root], growing one joint per year. The plant stands about one chi [approximately 30 cm] in height; its leaves resemble those of Tu Danggui [wild Angelica], and it bears tiered whorls. The root is collected in the second and eighth months and dried in the shade. The variety growing in Shizhou is a climbing vine, also called Xiangqiu ['fragrant ball']; the vine extends more than one zhang [over 3 m] in length, is red in color, and bears red fruits in autumn. It is available in all four seasons. Its roots and branches are gathered and used in washing decoctions for wind-toxin [feng du] sores and swellings. Li Shizhen states: According to Fan Chengda's Guihai Zhi [Record of the Gui Sea Region], it is produced in Guangxi and has one stem with six leaves.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter, acrid; cold; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]** For wind-swelling [feng zhong], carbuncle-toxin [yong du], and red warts: grind with vinegar and apply topically. Also treats throat swelling and pain: slice and hold in the mouth; relief is immediate. (Su Song.) Counteracts the venom of centipedes and snakes. (Li Shizhen.)

segments.title_inferred: Báixiān (Dictamnus Root Bark) — Medicinal Uses and Description
segments.original_text:
【釋名】白膻（弘景）、白羊鮮（弘景）、地羊鮮（《圖經》）、金雀兒椒（《日華》）。

弘景曰：俗呼爲白羊鮮。氣息正似羊膻，故又名白膻。

時珍曰：鮮者，羊之氣也。此草根白色，作羊膻氣，其子累累如椒，故有諸名。

【集解】《別錄》曰：白鮮皮，生上谷川谷及冤句。四月、五月採根，陰乾。

弘景曰：近道處處有，以蜀中者爲良。

恭曰：其葉似茱萸，苗高尺餘，根皮白而心實，花紫白色。根宜二月採，若四月、五月採，便虛惡矣。

頌曰：今河中、江寧府、滁州、潤州皆有之。苗高尺餘，莖青，葉稍白，如槐亦似茱萸。四月開花淡紫色，似小蜀葵花。根似小蔓菁，皮黃白而心實。山人采嫩苗爲菜茹。

根皮

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。《別錄》曰：咸。之才曰：惡螵蛸、桔梗、茯苓、萆薢。

【主治】頭風黃疸，咳逆淋瀝，女子陰中腫痛，濕痺死肌，不可屈伸起止行步（《本經》）。

療四肢不安，時行腹中大熱飲水，欲走大呼，小兒驚癇，婦人產後余痛（《別錄》）。

治一切熱毒風、惡風，風瘡疥癬赤爛，眉髮脫脆，皮肌急，壯熱惡寒，解熱黃、酒黃、急黃、谷黃、勞黃（甄權）。通關節，利九竅及血脈，通小腸水氣，天行時疾，頭痛、眼疼。其花同功（大明）。

治肺嗽（蘇頌）。

【發明】時珍曰：白鮮皮氣寒善行，味苦性燥，足太陰、陽明經去濕熱藥也，兼入手太陰、陽明，爲諸黃風痺要藥。世醫只施之瘡科，淺矣。
segments.translation:
**Explanatory Names**
Báishān [White Goat-stench] (Hongjing), Báiyángxiān [White Sheep Xiān] (Hongjing), Dìyángxiān [Ground Sheep Xiān] (Tujing), Jīnquèrjiāo [Goldfinch Pepper] (Rihua).

Hongjing states: It is commonly called Báiyángxiān. Its odor is exactly like that of a goat [mutton-stench], hence the additional name Báishān [White Goat-stench].

Shizhen states: Xiān [鮮] here refers to the odor of sheep. The root of this plant is white in color and carries a goat-like smell; its seeds are clustered one upon another like pepper berries — hence all these various names.

**Collected Explanations**
*Bielu* states: The root bark of Báixiān grows in the river valleys of Shanggu and in Yuanju. The root is collected in the fourth and fifth months and dried in the shade.

Hongjing states: It is found everywhere in the vicinity of the main roads; the variety from Shu [Sichuan] is considered the finest.

Gong [Su Gong] states: Its leaves resemble those of Zhuyu [Evodia/Tetradium]. The plant stands over a foot tall; the root bark is white while the core is solid, and the flowers are purplish-white in color. The root is best collected in the second month; if collected in the fourth or fifth month, it will be hollow and inferior.

Song [Su Song] states: It is now found in Hezhong, Jianning Prefecture, Chuzhou, and Runzhou. The plant stands over a foot tall, with green stems and slightly whitish leaves resembling those of the scholar tree [Sophora] and also of Zhuyu. In the fourth month it opens pale purple flowers resembling those of the small Shu mallow [Malva]. The root resembles a small turnip; the bark is yellowish-white and the core is solid. Mountain-dwellers harvest the tender shoots to eat as a vegetable.

**Root Bark**

**Qi and Flavor**
Bitter; cold; non-toxic. *Bielu* adds: salty. Zhicai states: it is incompatible with [i.e., antagonized by] Piāoxiāo [mantis egg-case, *Tenodera* sp.], Jiégěng [Platycodon], Fúlíng [Poria], and Bìxiè [Dioscorea tokoro or similar].

**Indications and Treatments**
- Head wind [migraine-type headache], jaundice [huánɡdǎn], cough with counterflow [qi rebellion], dribbling urinary difficulty [lín lì], swelling and pain inside a woman's vagina, damp impediment [shī bì] with dead/numb flesh [sǐjī], and inability to flex or extend, to rise, stop, or walk. (*Benjing*)
- Treats unease and restlessness of the four limbs; seasonal epidemic disorder with intense heat in the abdomen and excessive drinking of water, with urges to run about and shout loudly; childhood fright epilepsy [jīng xián]; and residual pain in women after childbirth. (*Bielu*)
- Treats all heat-toxin wind disorders and malign wind; wind sores, scabies, tinea, and weeping red erosions; falling and brittleness of eyebrows and hair; tightness of skin and flesh; high fever with aversion to cold; and resolves heat jaundice [rè huáng], wine jaundice [jiǔ huáng], acute jaundice [jí huáng], grain jaundice [gǔ huáng], and taxation jaundice [láo huáng]. (Zhen Quan)
- Opens and frees the joints; benefits the nine orifices and the blood vessels; opens the water qi of the small intestine; treats seasonal epidemic diseases [tiānxíng shíjí], headache, and eye pain. The flowers have the same functions. (Daming)
- Treats lung cough. (Su Song)

**Elucidation**
Shizhen states: The root bark of Báixiān has a cold qi and moves readily; its flavor is bitter and its nature drying. It is a drug for eliminating damp-heat from the foot tài-yīn [spleen] and yáng-míng [stomach] channels, and also enters the hand tài-yīn [lung] and yáng-míng [large intestine] channels. It is an essential medicine for the various jaundice conditions and for wind impediment. Physicians of the present age apply it only in the treatment of sores and lesions — a superficial use indeed.

segments.title_inferred: Shanci Gu (Mountain Corm / Pleione / Cremastra) — Herb Entry
segments.original_text:
【釋名】金燈（《拾遺》）、鬼燈檠（《綱目》）、朱姑（《綱目》）、鹿蹄草（《綱目》）、無義草。時珍曰：根狀如水慈菇，花狀如燈籠而朱色，故有諸名。段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：金燈之花與葉不相見，人惡種之，謂之無義草。又有試劍草，亦名鹿蹄草，與此同名，見後草之五。

【集解】藏器曰：山慈菇生山中濕地，葉似車前，根如慈菇。大明曰：零陵間有一種團慈菇，根如小蒜，所主略同。時珍曰：山慈菇處處有之。冬月生葉，如水仙花之葉而狹。二月中抽一莖，如箭杆，高尺許。莖端開花白色，亦有紅色、黃色者，上有黑點，其花乃衆花簇成一朵，如絲紐成可愛。三月結子，有三棱。四月初苗枯，即掘取其根，狀如慈菇及小蒜，遲則苗腐難尋矣。根苗與老鴉蒜極相類，但老鴉根無毛，慈菇有毛殼包裹爲異爾。用之，去毛殼。

根

【氣味】甘、微辛，有小毒。

【主治】癰腫瘡瘍、瘰癧結核等，醋磨敷之。亦剝人面皮，除斑（藏器）。主疔腫，攻毒破皮，解諸毒蠱毒，蛇蟲狂犬傷（時珍）。瘡腫，入蜜搗塗瘡口，候清血出，效（慎微）。塗乳癰、便毒，尤妙（時珍）。小便血淋澀痛，同地柏花陰乾，每用三錢，水煎服（《聖惠》）。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Jīndēng [Golden Lamp] (from *Shiyi* [Supplement to Materia Medica]), Guǐdēngqíng [Ghost Lamp Stand] (from *Gangmu*), Zhūgū [Red Corm] (from *Gangmu*), Lùtícǎo [Deer's Hoof Herb] (from *Gangmu*), and Wúyìcǎo [Faithless Herb].

Li Shizhen [the author] remarks: The root resembles that of the water caltrop [*Sagittaria*, i.e., water-arrowhead corm], and the flower resembles a lantern and is vermillion in color — hence all these names. Duan Chengshi, in his *Youyang Zazu* [Miscellany of Youyang], writes: "The flower of Jīndēng and its leaves never appear at the same time; people dislike growing it and call it Wúyìcǎo [Faithless Herb]." There is also a plant called Shìjiàncǎo [Sword-Testing Herb], likewise named Lùtícǎo, which shares this alternate name — see later in Chapter Five of the Herb section.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Chen Cangqi [Tang-dynasty compiler of *Bencao Shiyi*] states: Mountain corm [Shāncígū] grows in damp mountain terrain; its leaves resemble those of plantain [*Plantago*], and its root resembles that of the arrowhead corm.

Da Ming [another cited authority] states: In the Lingling region there is a variety called tuán-cígū [round corm], whose root resembles a small garlic bulb; its medicinal uses are broadly similar.

Li Shizhen states: Shāncígū is found everywhere. It puts forth leaves in winter, resembling the leaves of the narcissus [*Narcissus tazetta*] but narrower. In the second month [approximately March], a single stalk is sent up, resembling an arrow shaft, reaching about one *chi* [roughly 30 cm] in height. At the tip of the stalk it bears flowers that are white — though some are red or yellow — marked with black spots. The flower is made up of many smaller florets clustered into a single head, prettily twisted together like braided threads. Seeds are set in the third month [April] and have three ridges. In the early fourth month [May] the seedlings wither; one should dig up the root at that time, as it resembles an arrowhead corm or a small garlic bulb. If one delays, the seedlings rot and become difficult to locate. The root and seedling closely resemble those of lǎoyāsuàn [crow garlic / *Lycoris radiata*], but the roots of crow garlic have no fibrous covering, while the mountain corm root is enclosed in a hairy husk — this is the distinguishing feature. When preparing it for use, remove the hairy husk.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Sweet, slightly acrid [pungent]; mildly toxic.

**[Principal Indications and Treatments]**
- For carbuncles, swellings, sores [*yōng zhǒng chuāng yáng*], and scrofulous lumps and nodules [*luǒlì jiéhé*]: grind with vinegar and apply topically. It also exfoliates facial skin and removes blemishes [*bān*]. (Chen Cangqi)
- For boils [*dīng zhǒng*]: attacks toxins and breaks through the skin; resolves all manner of poisons including *gu* toxin [parasitic/magical poisoning]; [effective for] snakebite, insect bite, and mad-dog [rabid animal] wounds. (Li Shizhen)
- For sores and swellings: pound with honey and apply to the mouth of the wound; wait until clear blood emerges — effective. (Tang Shenwei [*Zhenghe Bencao* compiler, attributed as Shenwei/慎微])
- Applied to breast abscesses [*rǔyōng*] and venereal sores [*biàndú*]: especially effective. (Li Shizhen)
- For bloody painful dribbling urination [*xiǎobiàn xuè lín sè tòng*]: combine with shade-dried *Selaginella* flowers [*dìbǎihuā*]; use three *qian* per dose, decocted in water and taken orally. (From *Shenghui Fang* [Prescriptions for Sacred Relief])

segments.title_inferred: Shi Suan (Stone Garlic / Red Spider Lily) — Root Properties and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】烏蒜（《綱目》）、老鴉蒜（《救荒》）、蒜頭草（《綱目》）、婆婆酸（《綱目》）、一枝箭

【集解】頌曰：水麻生鼎州、黔州，其根名石蒜，九月採之。或云金燈花根，亦名石蒜，即此類也。時珍曰：石蒜，處處下濕地有之，古謂之烏蒜，俗謂之老鴉蒜、一枝箭是也。春初生葉，如蒜秧及山慈菇葉，背有劍脊，四散布地。七月苗枯，乃於平地抽出一莖如箭杆，長尺許。莖端開花四、五朵，六出紅色，如山丹花狀而瓣長，黃蕊長須。其根狀如蒜，皮色紫赤，肉白色。此有小毒，而《救荒本草》言其可炸熟水浸過食，蓋爲救荒爾。一種葉如大韭，四、五月抽莖，開花如小萱花黃白色者，謂之鐵色箭，功與此同。二物並抽莖開花，後乃生葉，葉、花不相見，與金燈同。

根

【氣味】辛、甘，溫，有小毒。

【主治】敷貼腫毒（蘇頌）。疔瘡惡核，可水煎服取汗，及搗敷之。又中溪毒者，酒煎半升服，取吐良（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Names:** Wu Suan [Black Garlic] (Bencao Gangmu); Lao Ya Suan [Crow's Garlic] (Jiuhuang Bencao); Suan Tou Cao [Garlic-head Herb] (Bencao Gangmu); Popo Suan [Old Woman's Sour] (Bencao Gangmu); Yi Zhi Jian [Single-shaft Arrow] (implied by text).

**Collected Explanations:** Su Song states: Shui Ma [Water Hemp] grows in Dingzhou and Qianzhou; its root is called Shi Suan [Stone Garlic] and is harvested in the ninth month. Some say it is the root of Jin Deng Hua [Golden Lantern Flower], also known as Shi Suan — these are of the same kind. Li Shizhen states: Shi Suan is found everywhere in low-lying damp ground. In ancient times it was called Wu Suan [Black Garlic]; in common usage it is called Lao Ya Suan [Crow's Garlic] or Yi Zhi Jian [Single-shaft Arrow]. In early spring it sends up leaves resembling garlic shoots or the leaves of Shan Ci Gu [Pleione / mountain tuber orchid], with a sword-like ridge along the back, spreading out flat across the ground in all directions. By the seventh month the foliage has withered, whereupon a single stem, resembling an arrow shaft and about a foot in length, rises straight from level ground. At the tip of the stem it bears four or five flowers of six petals, red in colour, resembling the flowers of Shan Dan [Mountain Lily] but with longer petals and long yellow stamens. The root is shaped like garlic, with a purplish-red outer skin and white flesh. It has mild toxicity; however, the Jiuhuang Bencao [Famine Relief Materia Medica] states it may be eaten after deep-frying and soaking in water — this being a measure adopted only in times of famine. There is a related variety whose leaves resemble large chives; in the fourth and fifth months it puts up a stem and bears small yellow-white flowers resembling those of Xuan Hua [daylily], called Tie Se Jian [Iron-coloured Arrow]. Its properties and functions are the same as those of Shi Suan. Both plants produce stems and flowers before the leaves emerge; leaf and flower are never seen together — as is also the case with Jin Deng [Golden Lantern].

**Root**

**Qi and Flavour:** Acrid and sweet; warm; mildly toxic.

**Principal Indications and Treatments:**
- Applied externally as a poultice for swellings and toxic sores (Su Song).
- For deep-rooted sores [ding chuang] and malignant hard nodules [e he]: decoct in water, take orally to induce sweating; also pound fresh root and apply as a poultice externally.
- For poisoning from mountain stream water [xi du]: decoct in half a sheng [approx. 0.5 litre] of wine, take orally to induce vomiting — this is an effective treatment (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Water Narcissus (Shuixian) — Root and Flower
segments.original_text:
【釋名】金盞銀台。時珍曰：此物宜卑濕處，不可缺水，故名水仙。金盞銀台，花之狀也。

【集解】機曰：水仙花葉似蒜，其花香甚清。九月初栽於肥壤，則花茂盛，瘦地則無花。五月初收根，以童尿浸一宿，晒乾，懸火暖處。若不移宿根更旺。時珍曰：水仙叢生下濕處。其根似蒜及薤而長，外有赤皮裹之。冬月生葉，似薤及蒜。春初抽莖，如蔥頭。莖頭開花數朵，大如簪頭，狀如酒杯，五尖上承，黃心，宛然盞樣，其花瑩韻，其香清幽。一種千葉者，花皺，下輕黃而上淡白，不作杯狀，人重之，指爲真水仙，蓋不然，乃一物二種爾。亦有紅花者。按：段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：捺祗出拂林國，根大如雞卵，苗長三四尺，葉似蒜葉，中心抽條，莖端開花，六出紅白色，花心黃赤，不結子，冬生夏死。取花壓油，塗身去風氣。據此形狀，與水仙仿佛，豈外國名謂不同耶？

根

【氣味】苦、微辛，滑，寒，無毒。土宿真君曰：取汁伏汞，煮雄黃，拒火。

【主治】花
作香澤，塗身理發，去風氣。又療婦人五心發熱，同乾荷葉、赤芍藥等分，爲末，白湯每服二錢，熱自退也（時珍，出《衛生易簡方》）。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Appellations]** Also known as 'Golden Cup, Silver Terrace' [金盞銀台]. Li Shizhen states: This plant thrives in low-lying, moist places and cannot do without water; hence its name 'Water Immortal' [水仙, Shuixian]. 'Golden Cup, Silver Terrace' describes the appearance of the flower.

**[Collected Explanations]** [Wang] Ji states: The leaves of the Water Narcissus resemble those of garlic, and its flowers have an exceptionally clear fragrance. If planted in rich soil at the beginning of the ninth month, the flowers will bloom abundantly; in poor soil, it will not flower at all. At the beginning of the fifth month, collect the roots, soak them overnight in boys' urine [童尿], dry them in the sun, and hang them in a warm place near a fire. If the roots are not transplanted, the established roots will grow even more vigorously.

Li Shizhen states: The Water Narcissus grows in clusters in low, damp places. Its roots resemble those of garlic and Chinese scallion [薤, Allium chinense] but are longer, with a reddish skin encasing them. In winter it sends out leaves resembling those of Chinese scallion and garlic. In early spring it puts forth a stem like that of a scallion head. At the top of the stem several flowers open; they are as large as the head of a hairpin, shaped like a wine cup, borne on five pointed petals, with a yellow centre that truly resembles a small cup [盞]. The flowers are lustrous and graceful, their fragrance clear and subtle. There is a double-flowered [千葉, 'thousand-petalled'] variety whose flowers are ruffled, pale yellow below and light white above, and do not form a cup shape. People prize this variety and call it the 'true' Water Narcissus, but this is incorrect — the two are merely two forms of the same plant. There is also a red-flowered variety.

Note: Duan Chengshi's *Youyang Zazu* [酉陽雜俎] records: 'Naqiha [捺祗] comes from the kingdom of Fulin [拂林, i.e., Byzantium/Rome]. Its root is as large as a hen's egg; the sprout grows three to four feet tall; the leaves resemble garlic leaves; from the centre a stalk rises, and flowers open at the tip of the stem — six-petalled, in red and white, with a yellow-red flower centre; it bears no seeds; it grows in winter and dies in summer. The flowers are pressed to extract oil, which is applied to the body to dispel wind-qi [風氣].' Judging by this description, it closely resembles the Water Narcissus — could it simply be that the foreign name differs?

**Root**

**[Flavour and Nature]** Bitter, slightly acrid, slippery [滑], cold, non-toxic. The Venerable Tuxu [土宿真君] states: The juice can be used to subdue mercury [伏汞], to treat [i.e., react with] realgar [雄黃] when boiled, and to resist fire. [The indications for the root are] lacking [ILLEGIBLE: the main-treatment entry for the root appears to have been omitted in the source].

**[Indications] — Flower**
Prepared as a fragrant emollient [香澤], applied to the body and used to dress the hair, it dispels wind-qi. It also treats heat effusion in a woman's five centres [五心發熱, i.e., heat in the palms, soles, and chest]. Combined in equal parts with dried lotus leaf [乾荷葉] and red peony root [赤芍藥], the mixture is ground into a powder; take two qian [錢] per dose in plain hot water — the heat will subside of itself. (Li Shizhen, citing the *Weisheng Yijian Fang* [衛生易簡方].)

segments.title_inferred: Dijin (Earth Sinew Root) — Cogon/Sedge-type Grass Root
segments.original_text:
【釋名】菅根（《別錄》）、土筋（同）。

【集解】《別錄》曰：地筋生澤中，根有毛。三月生，四月實白，三月三日采根。弘景曰：疑此即是白茅而小異也。藏器曰：地筋如地黃，根葉並相似，而細多毛，生平澤，功用亦同地黃，李邕方中用之。時珍曰：此乃黃菅茅之根也，功與白茅根相同，詳見白茅下。陳藏器所說，別是一物。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】益氣止渴，除熱在腹臍，利筋（《別錄》）。根、苗、花，功與白茅同（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** Jiangen [sedge root] (from the *Bielu*); Tujin [earth sinew] (same source).

**Collected Commentaries:** The *Bielu* states: Dijin grows in marshy ground; its root is hairy. It sprouts in the third month, bears white fruit in the fourth month, and the root is collected on the third day of the third month. [Tao] Hongjing remarks: I suspect this is simply a slightly different form of baimao [white cogon grass, *Imperata cylindrica*]. [Chen] Cangqi states: Dijin resembles dihuang [*Rehmannia*] in appearance; its roots and leaves are similar to it but are finer and more hairy. It grows in level, marshy ground, and its medicinal functions are likewise similar to dihuang. It was used by Li Yong in his formularies. [Li] Shizhen states: This is in fact the root of huangjianmao [yellow sedge-grass], whose efficacy is the same as that of baimao root — see the entry on baimao for details. What Chen Cangqi described is a separate substance altogether.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet; balanced [neutral]; non-toxic.

**Principal Indications:** Supplements qi and alleviates thirst; disperses heat located in the abdomen and around the navel; benefits the sinews (from the *Bielu*). The root, sprouts, and flowers share the same efficacy as baimao [white cogon grass] (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Du Heng (Wild Ginger / Horse-hoof Incense): Names, Description, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】杜葵（《綱目》）、馬蹄香（《唐本》）、土鹵（《爾雅》）、土細辛（《綱目》）。

恭曰：杜衡，葉似葵，形似馬蹄，故俗名馬蹄香。頌曰：《爾雅》杜又名土鹵，然杜若亦名杜衡，或疑是杜若。而郭璞注云：似葵，當是杜衡也。

【集解】《別錄》曰：杜衡生山谷，三月三日采根，熟洗曝乾。弘景曰：根葉都似細辛，惟氣小異爾。處處有之。方藥少用，惟道家服之。令人身衣香。恭曰：生山之陰，水澤下濕地。葉似葵，形如馬蹄。根似細辛、白前等。今俗以及己代之，謬矣。及己獨莖，莖端四葉，葉間白花，殊無芳氣。有毒，服之令人吐，惟療瘡疥，不可亂杜衡也。頌曰：今江淮間皆有之。春初於宿根上生苗，葉似馬蹄下狀，高二、三寸，莖如麥稿粗細，每窠上有五七葉，或八九葉，別無枝蔓。又於莖葉間罅內蘆頭上貼地生紫花，其花似見不見，暗結實如豆大，窠內有碎子，似天仙子。苗葉俱青，經霜即枯，其根成空，有似飯帚密鬧，細長四五寸，粗於細辛，微黃白色，味辛，江淮俗呼爲馬蹄香。謹按《山海經》云：天帝之山有草焉。其狀如葵，其臭如蘼蕪，名曰杜衡。可以走馬，食之已癭。郭璞注云：帶之可以走馬。或曰：馬得之而健走也。宗奭曰：杜衡用根似細辛，但根色白，葉如馬蹄之下。市人往往以亂細辛，將二物相對，便見真偽。況細辛惟出華州者良。杜衡色黃，拳局而脆，乾則作團。詳細辛下。

時珍曰：按《土宿本草》云：杜細辛，葉圓如馬蹄，紫背者良，江南、荊、湖、川、陝、閩、廣俱有之。取自然汁，可伏硫、砒，製汞。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】風寒咳逆。作浴湯，香人衣體（《別錄》）。止氣奔喘促，消痰飲，破留血、項間癭瘤之疾（甄權）。下氣殺蟲（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：古方吐藥往往用杜衡者，非杜衡也，乃及己也。及己似細辛而有毒，吐人。昔人多以及己當杜衡，杜衡當細辛，故爾錯誤也。杜衡則無毒，不吐人，功雖不及細辛，而亦能散風寒，下氣消痰，行水破血也。
segments.translation:
**[Names]** Du Kui [lit. 'du mallow'] (Bencao Gangmu); Ma Ti Xiang ['horse-hoof fragrance'] (Tang Bencao); Tu Lu (Erya); Tu Xi Xin ['earth asarum'] (Bencao Gangmu).

Su Gong states: Du Heng has leaves resembling mallow [葵] and a shape like a horse's hoof, hence the common name 'horse-hoof fragrance.' Su Song states: The Erya records that du [杜] is also called tu lu [土鹵]. However, Du Ruo [Pollia japonica] is likewise referred to as Du Heng, so some suspect the two may be the same. Yet Guo Pu's commentary notes that it resembles mallow, which points to Du Heng being the correct identification.

**[Collected Explanations]** The Mingyi Bielu states: Du Heng grows in mountain valleys. The roots are collected on the third day of the third month [lunar calendar], washed thoroughly, and dried in the sun. Tao Hongjing states: The roots and leaves closely resemble those of Xi Xin [Asarum], differing only slightly in fragrance. It is found everywhere. It is seldom used in medical prescriptions; Daoist practitioners consume it, and it imparts a fragrant scent to the body and clothing. Su Gong states: It grows on the shaded sides of mountains, in low-lying wet areas near water and marshes. The leaves resemble mallow, shaped like a horse's hoof. The roots resemble those of Xi Xin and Bai Qian [Cynanchum]. Nowadays people commonly substitute Ji Ji [Chloranthus serratus] for it, which is a serious error. Ji Ji has a single stem with four leaves at its tip, white flowers among the leaves, and entirely lacks aromatic fragrance. It is toxic; taking it causes vomiting, and it is useful only for treating sores and scabies — it must not be confused with Du Heng. Su Song states: It is found throughout the Jianghuai region today. In early spring, new shoots arise from the old rootstock; the leaves are shaped like the underside of a horse's hoof, growing two to three cun tall. The stems are as thick as wheat straw. Each clump bears five to seven leaves, sometimes eight or nine, with no separate branches or tendrils. Purple flowers grow pressed to the ground from the nodes in the gaps between stems and leaves, at the base of the rhizome-heads; the flowers are barely visible and inconspicuous. Seeds form in concealed fashion, roughly the size of a bean, with fine seeds inside the pod resembling those of Hyoscyamus [天仙子, henbane]. Both shoots and leaves are green, withering after frost. The roots are hollow inside, densely packed like a rice-straw brush, slender and four to five cun long, coarser than Xi Xin, slightly yellowish-white in color, and pungent in flavor. In the Jianghuai region it is commonly called 'Ma Ti Xiang.' I note that the Shanhaijing records: On the Mountain of the Heavenly Emperor there is an herb shaped like mallow, with a fragrance like Mi Wu [Ligusticum striatum], named Du Heng. It can make horses run swiftly; eating it cures goiter [癭]. Guo Pu's commentary states: Carrying it on one's person can make horses run swiftly; or, horses that eat it become vigorous runners. Kou Zongshi states: The root of Du Heng, used medicinally, resembles Xi Xin, but is white in color, and the leaves resemble the underside of a horse's hoof. Merchants often use it to adulterate Xi Xin; placing the two side by side will reveal which is genuine. Moreover, only Xi Xin produced in Huazhou is of superior quality. Du Heng is yellowish, coiled and brittle, and forms a tight clump when dried. See also the entry on Xi Xin for further details.

Li Shizhen states: According to the Tushu Bencao ['Earthly Lodging Materia Medica']: Du Xi Xin has round leaves like a horse's hoof; specimens with purple undersides are superior. It is found throughout Jiangnan, Jing, Hu, Chuan, Shaanxi, Min, and Guang. The raw juice can be used to immobilize [伏, render inert/fix] sulfur and arsenic, and to process mercury [in alchemical preparations].

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Pungent; warm; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Wind-cold cough with rebellious qi [wind-cold咳逆]. Prepared as a bath decoction, it imparts fragrance to the body and clothing. (Mingyi Bielu)
- Stops rushing qi [奔喘] and labored breathing; disperses phlegm-fluid accumulation [痰飲]; breaks up static blood; treats goiter and tumor growths at the neck [項間癭瘤]. (Zhen Quan)
- Descends qi; kills parasites/worms. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: In ancient prescriptions, emetic formulas that list Du Heng as an ingredient do not actually refer to Du Heng — they mean Ji Ji [Chloranthus serratus]. Ji Ji resembles Xi Xin but is toxic and induces vomiting. In earlier times, people often mistook Ji Ji for Du Heng, and Du Heng for Xi Xin, hence the accumulated errors. Du Heng itself is non-toxic and does not cause vomiting. Though its efficacy does not match that of Xi Xin, it is nonetheless capable of dispersing wind-cold, descending qi, dispersing phlegm, promoting water movement, and breaking up static blood.

segments.title_inferred: Jiji (及己) — '獐耳細辛' (Deer-ear Asarum), a toxic herb used for skin ailments and sores
segments.original_text:
【釋名】獐耳細辛。時珍曰：及己名義未詳。二月生苗，先開白花，後方生葉三片，狀如獐耳，根如細辛，故名獐耳細辛。

【集解】恭曰：及己生山谷陰虛軟地。其草一莖，莖頭四葉，隙著白花。根似細辛而黑，有

根

【氣味】苦，平，有毒。恭曰：入口使人吐血。

【主治】諸惡瘡疥痂蝕，及牛馬諸瘡（《唐本》）。頭瘡白禿風瘙，皮膚蟲癢，可煎汁浸並敷之（大明）。殺蟲（時珍）。

【發明】弘景曰：今人以合瘡疥膏，甚驗。時珍曰：今人不知及己，往往以當杜衡，卻以杜衡當細辛，故杜衡諸方多是及己也。辯見細辛、杜衡二條。
segments.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** [Also called] 'Deer-ear Asarum' [獐耳細辛]. Li Shizhen states: The meaning of the name 及己 (Jiji) is not entirely clear. In the second month [of the lunar calendar] the seedlings emerge; white flowers bloom first, and only afterward three leaves appear, shaped like the ears of a river deer [獐, Hydropotes inermis]. The roots resemble those of Asarum [細辛], hence the name 'Deer-ear Asarum.'

**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Jiji grows in the shaded, moist, soft soils of mountain valleys. The plant has a single stem with four leaves at the top, and white flowers appear in the interstices [between the leaves]. The roots resemble those of Asarum but are black [the remainder of this passage appears incomplete or truncated in the source].

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [平]; toxic. Su Gong states: If taken by mouth, it causes a person to vomit blood.

**Indications and Uses:** [Treats] all manner of malignant sores, scabies [疥], crusted and erosive lesions, as well as various sores on cattle and horses (cited from the *Tang Materia Medica* [《唐本草》]). [For] head sores, bald white ringworm [白禿], wind-induced itching, and skin parasites causing itchiness — decoct the juice and use it to soak [the affected area] or apply it topically (cited from Daming [《大明本草》]). Kills parasites/insects (Li Shizhen).

**Elucidation:** Tao Hongjing states: People today use it compounded into an ointment [膏] for sores and scabies, with very good results. Li Shizhen states: People today do not recognise Jiji and frequently mistake it for Duheng [杜衡, *Asarum forbesii*], while also mistaking Duheng for Asarum [細辛]; consequently, prescriptions calling for Duheng are in many cases actually Jiji. See the entries on Asarum and Duheng for further discussion.

segments.title_inferred: Gui Du You (Ghost Postal Supervisor Herb) — Root Entry
segments.original_text:
【釋名】獨搖草（《唐本》）。時珍曰：此草獨莖而葉攢其端，無風自動，故曰鬼獨搖草，後人訛爲鬼督郵爾。因其專主鬼病，猶司鬼之督郵也。古者傳舍有督郵之官主之。徐長卿、赤箭皆治鬼病，故並有鬼督郵之名，名同而物異。

【集解】恭曰：鬼督郵所在有之。有必叢生，苗惟一莖，莖端生葉若傘狀，根如牛膝而細黑。今人以徐長卿代之，非也。保升曰：莖似細箭杆，高二尺以下。葉生莖端，狀如傘。花生葉心，黃白色。根橫生而無須，二月、八月採根。徐長卿、赤箭並有鬼督郵之名，而主治不同，宜審用之。時珍曰：鬼督郵與及己同類，根苗皆相似。但以根如細辛而色黑者，爲及己；根如細辛而色黃白者，爲鬼督郵。

根

【氣味】辛、苦，平，無毒。時珍曰：有小毒。

【主治】鬼疰卒忤中惡，心腹邪氣，百精毒，溫瘧疫疾，強腰腳，益膂力（《唐本》）。

【發明】時珍曰：按東晉《深師方》，治上氣嗽、飲嗽、邪嗽、燥嗽、冷嗽，四滿丸，用鬼督郵同蜈蚣、芫花、躑躅諸毒藥爲丸，則其有毒可知矣。非毒藥不能治鬼疰邪惡之病，《唐本》云無毒，蓋不然。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate name: Dú Yáo Cǎo ["Solitary-Swaying Herb"] (from the *Newly Revised Materia Medica* [Tang Bencao]). Li Shizhen states: This plant has a single stem with leaves clustered at its tip, and it sways of its own accord even without wind. Hence it was called Guǐ Dú Yáo Cǎo ["Ghost's Solitary-Swaying Herb"]; later people corrupted this into Guǐ Dū Yóu ["Ghost Postal Supervisor"]. Because it specializes in treating ghost-affliction diseases [guǐ bìng], it is likened to a postal supervisor [dū yóu] overseeing ghosts. In ancient times, the postal relay stations [chuán shè] were administered by officials called dū yóu. Both Xú Cháng Qīng [Cynanchum paniculatum] and Chì Jiàn [Gastrodia elata, "Red Arrow"] treat ghost-affliction diseases and thus share the name Guǐ Dū Yóu—same name, different plants.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Sū Gōng states: Guǐ Dū Yóu grows in many places. Where it grows, it invariably forms clumps; the plant has only a single stem, with leaves clustered at the tip in an umbrella-like form, and roots resembling those of Niú Xī [Achyranthes bidentata] but finer and black in color. Nowadays people substitute Xú Cháng Qīng for it, which is incorrect. Hán Bǎoshēng states: The stem resembles a slender arrow shaft and grows no taller than two Chinese feet [approximately 60 cm]. The leaves grow at the tip of the stem in an umbrella shape. The flowers emerge from the center of the leaves and are yellow-white in color. The roots grow horizontally and have no rootlets; the roots are collected in the second and eighth months [of the lunar calendar]. Both Xú Cháng Qīng and Chì Jiàn share the name Guǐ Dū Yóu, yet their therapeutic applications differ, so one must use them with careful discernment. Li Shizhen states: Guǐ Dū Yóu belongs to the same category as Jǐ Jǐ [Chloranthus serratus], and their roots and shoots closely resemble one another. The distinction is: roots resembling Xì Xīn [Asarum] in form but black in color are Jǐ Jǐ; roots resembling Xì Xīn but yellow-white in color are Guǐ Dū Yóu.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Acrid and bitter; balanced [píng]; non-toxic. Li Shizhen states: [It is in fact] mildly toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Ghost-affliction and sudden-onset malign possession [guǐ zhù cù wǔ zhōng è]; evil qi [邪氣] in the chest and abdomen; the toxins of the hundred spirits [bǎi jīng dú]; warm malaria and epidemic diseases; strengthening the lumbar region and legs; and building muscular strength in the back. (From the *Tang Bencao*.)

**[Discussion and Elaboration]**
Li Shizhen states: According to the Eastern Jin dynasty *Fang of Master Shen* [Shēn Shī Fāng], the "Four Fullnesses Pill" [Sì Mǎn Wán], used to treat ascending-qi cough, phlegm-fluid cough, evil-qi cough, dry cough, and cold cough, combines Guǐ Dū Yóu with centipede [wú gōng], genkwa [yuán huā], azalea [zhí zhú], and other toxic drugs to form pills—from which it is clear that this herb is indeed toxic. Only toxic drugs are capable of treating ghost-affliction and malign evil diseases. The *Tang Bencao*'s claim that it is non-toxic is simply mistaken.

segments.title_inferred: Xu Changqing (Cynanchum paniculatum) — Root Monograph
segments.original_text:
【釋名】鬼督郵（《本經》）、別仙蹤（蘇頌）。

時珍曰：徐長卿，人名也，常以此藥治邪病，人遂以名之。《名醫別錄》於有名未用復出石下長卿條，云一名徐長卿。陶弘景注云：此是誤爾。方家無用，亦不復識。今考二條功療相似。按《吳普本草》云：徐長卿一名石下長卿。其爲一物甚明，但石間生者爲良。前人欠氣亦相似。今狗脊散用鬼督郵者，取其強悍宜腰腳，故知是徐長卿，而非鬼箭、赤箭。

【集解】《別錄》曰：徐長卿，生泰山山谷及隴西，三月採。又曰：石下長卿生隴西山谷池澤，三月採。恭曰：所在川澤有之。葉似柳，兩葉相當，有光澤。根如細辛，微粗長，黃色而有臊氣。今俗以代鬼督郵，非也。鬼督郵自有本條。保升曰：生下濕川澤之間。苗似小桑，兩葉相對。三月苗青，七月、八月著子，似蘿摩子而小。九月苗黃，十月凋。八月採根，晒乾。頌曰：今淄、齊、淮、泗間皆有之，三月、四月採，謂之別仙蹤。時珍曰：鬼督郵、及己之亂杜衡，其功不同，苗亦不同也。徐長卿之亂鬼督郵，其苗不同，其功同也。杜衡之亂細辛，則根苗功用皆仿佛，乃彌近而大亂也。不可不審。

根

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。《別錄》曰：石下長卿：鹹，平，有毒。普曰：徐長卿一名石下長卿。神農、雷公：辛。時珍曰：治鬼之藥多有毒，當從《別錄》。

【主治】鬼物百精蠱毒，疫疾邪惡氣，溫瘧。久服強悍輕身（《本經》）。益氣延年。又曰：石下長卿：主鬼疰精物邪惡氣，殺百精蠱毒，老魅注易，亡走啼哭，悲傷恍惚（《別錄》）。

【發明】時珍曰：《抱朴子》言：上古辟瘟疫有徐長卿散，良效。今人不知用此。
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also known as Guidupou [Ghost Superintendent's Mail] (Shennong Bencao Jing), and Bié Xiān Zōng [Traces of a Parting Immortal] (Su Song).

Li Shizhen states: Xu Changqing was a person's name. He regularly used this herb to treat illnesses caused by evil [spirits], and people thereafter named the plant after him. The Mingyi Bielu [Supplementary Records of Famous Physicians], in its section of named but unused drugs, lists a separate entry for Shixia Changqing [Stone-dwelling Changqing], stating that one of its alternate names is Xu Changqing. Tao Hongjing's annotation remarks: this is simply an error, as no physician uses it and it is no longer recognisable. On examining both entries today, their therapeutic properties are alike. Wu Pu's Bencao [Materia Medica] states: Xu Changqing is also called Shixia Changqing. That they are one and the same plant is quite clear; it is merely that the variety growing among rocks is considered superior. Earlier authorities' [assessments of] qi are also similar. The Gouji San [Cibotium Powder] that employs Guidupou does so for its vigorous, bracing effect on the lower back and legs — this confirms that it is Xu Changqing, and not Guijian [Euonymus alatus] or Chijian [Gastrodia elata].

**[Collected Explanations]** The Bielu states: Xu Changqing grows in mountain valleys of Taishan and Longxi; it is gathered in the third month. It further states: Shixia Changqing grows in mountain valleys, pools, and marshes of Longxi; it is gathered in the third month. [Su] Gong states: it is found wherever there are rivers and marshes. The leaves resemble those of the willow, growing in opposite pairs, and are glossy. The root resembles that of Asarum [Xixin], but is slightly thicker and longer, yellow in colour, and has a rank [goat-like] odour. It is now popularly used as a substitute for Guidupou, which is incorrect — Guidupou has its own proper entry. [Han] Baosheng states: it grows in low-lying, damp riverside marshes. The shoots resemble those of a small mulberry, with two leaves growing opposite each other. In the third month the shoots are green; in the seventh and eighth months it bears seeds resembling those of Metaplexis japonica [Luomo] but smaller. In the ninth month the shoots yellow; by the tenth month the plant withers. The root is gathered in the eighth month and dried in the sun. [Su] Song states: it is now found throughout the regions of Zi, Qi, Huai, and Si; it is collected in the third and fourth months and is called Bié Xiān Zōng [Traces of a Parting Immortal]. Li Shizhen states: The confusion of Guidupou and Jiji [Chloranthus serratus] with Duheng [Asarum forbesii] involves plants that differ in both appearance and therapeutic function. The confusion of Xu Changqing with Guidupou involves plants whose shoots differ, yet whose therapeutic functions are the same. The confusion of Duheng with Xixin [Asarum], however, involves plants whose roots, shoots, and functions are all alike — this is a far closer and therefore far more serious confusion, and one must examine it with great care.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavour]** Acrid [xin], warm, non-toxic. The Bielu states: Shixia Changqing — salty, neutral [ping], toxic. [Wu] Pu states: Xu Changqing, also named Shixia Changqing — Shennong and Leigong both classify it as acrid. Li Shizhen states: drugs used to treat ghost[-related) ailments are for the most part toxic; one should follow the Bielu's classification.

**[Principal Indications]** Demonic entities, the hundred essences [bai jing], and gu-poison [parasitic toxin]; epidemic pestilence and evil malignant qi; warm malaria [wen nüe]. Long-term use fortifies and invigorates the body and makes it light (Shennong Bencao Jing). It also augments qi and prolongs life. Regarding Shixia Changqing: it governs ghost-possession [gui zhu], the qi of malignant spirit-essences, and kills the hundred essences and gu-poison; [it treats] afflictions transmitted by old demons, wandering about and weeping, grief, and mental confusion (Mingyi Bielu).

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: The Baopuzi records that in high antiquity there was a powder called Xu Changqing San used to ward off epidemic pestilence, with excellent efficacy. People today are unaware of this use.

segments.title_inferred: Baiwei (Cynanchum atratum) — Root monograph from Bencao Gangmu, Chapter Cao zhi Er
segments.original_text:
【釋名】薇草（《別錄》）、白幕（《別錄》）、春草（《別錄》）、䕵（音尾）、骨美。時珍曰：薇，細也。其根細而白也。按：《爾雅》：䕵，春草也。薇、䕵音相近，則白薇又䕵音之轉也。《別錄》以䕵爲莽草之名，誤矣。

【集解】《別錄》曰：白薇生平原川谷。三月三日采根陰乾。弘景曰：近道處處有之。

頌曰：今陝西諸郡及舒、滁、潤、遼州亦有之。莖葉俱青，頗類柳葉。六、七月開紅花，八月結實。其根黃白色，類牛膝而短小，今人八月採之。

根

【氣味】苦、鹹，平，無毒。《別錄》曰：大寒。之才曰：惡黃芪、大黃、大戟、乾薑、大棗、干漆、山茱萸。

【主治】暴中風身熱肢滿，忽忽不知人，狂惑邪氣，寒熱酸疼，溫瘧洗洗，發作有時（《本經》）。療傷中淋露，下水氣，利陰氣，益精。久服利人（《別錄》）。治驚邪風狂癇病，百邪鬼魅（弘景）。風溫灼熱多眠，及熱淋遺尿，金瘡出血（時珍）。

【發明】好古曰：古方多用治婦人，以本草有療傷中淋露之故也。

時珍曰：白薇古人多用，後世罕能知之。按：張仲景治婦人產中虛煩嘔逆，安中益氣，竹皮丸方中，用白薇同桂枝各一分，竹皮、石膏各三分，甘草七分。棗肉爲大丸，每以飲化一丸服。云有熱者，倍白薇，則白薇性寒，乃陽明經藥也。徐之才《藥對》言白薇惡大棗，而此方又以棗肉爲丸，蓋恐諸藥寒涼傷脾胃爾。朱肱《活人書》治風溫發汗後，身猶灼熱，自汗身重多眠，鼻息必鼾，語言難出者，葳蕤湯中亦用之。孫真人《千金方》，有詔書發汗白薇散焉。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Appellations]**
Alternate names: Weicao [Slender Herb] (Bielu), Baimu [White Veil] (Bielu), Chuncao [Spring Herb] (Bielu), 䕵 (pronounced 'wei'), and Gumei. Li Shizhen states: 'Wei [薇] means slender/fine. Its roots are fine and white.' According to the Erya: '䕵 is the spring herb.' The pronunciations of wei [薇] and 䕵 are close, so baiwei [白薇] is a phonetic transformation of 䕵. The Bielu's identification of 䕵 as a name for mangcao [Illicium lanceolatum, a toxic plant] is erroneous.

**[Collected Explanations]**
The Bielu states: Baiwei grows in flatland valleys and ravines. The root is collected on the third day of the third month and dried in the shade. Tao Hongjing states: It is found everywhere along the roadsides near inhabited areas.

Su Song states: Today it is found in various commanderies of Shaanxi, and also in Shu, Chu, Run, and Liaozhou. The stems and leaves are both green, resembling willow leaves. It opens red flowers in the sixth and seventh months, and bears fruit in the eighth month. Its roots are yellow-white in color, resembling those of niuxi [Achyranthes bidentata] but shorter and smaller; people today collect it in the eighth month.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Bitter and salty; balanced [ping]; non-toxic. The Bielu states: greatly cold [da han]. Xu Zhicai states: It is incompatible with [e] huangqi [Astragalus], dahuang [rhubarb], daji [Euphorbia], dried ginger, Chinese dates [dazao], dried lacquer [ganqi], and Asiatic cornelian cherry [shanzhuyu].

**[Principal Indications]**
Sudden wind-stroke [apoplexy] with bodily fever and fullness of the limbs, sudden loss of consciousness of others, manic confusion due to pathogenic qi, alternating cold and heat with aching pain, warm malaria [wenüe] with shivering, occurring at fixed intervals (Shennong Bencao Jing). Treats internal damage with dribbling discharge [linlu], disperses water qi, benefits yin qi, and augments essence. Prolonged use is beneficial to the person (Bielu). Treats fright-induced pathogenic wind-mania and epileptic disorders [jingxie feng kuang xian bing], and all manner of demonic and spectral afflictions (Hongjing). Wind-warmth [fengwen] with scorching fever and excessive somnolence, as well as hot strangury [re lin], urinary incontinence, and bleeding from metal-inflicted wounds (Shizhen).

**[Elucidations]**
Wang Haogu states: Ancient formulas frequently employed it to treat women, because the materia medica records its efficacy in treating internal damage with dribbling discharge.

Li Shizhen states: Baiwei was widely used by the ancients but is rarely understood by later generations. According to Zhang Zhongjing's formula for treating postpartum deficiency-vexation and counterflow vomiting in women — the Zhupi Wan [Bamboo Bark Pill] for calming the center and augmenting qi — baiwei and guizhi [Cinnamomum cassia twig] are each used at one fen, bamboo bark [zhupi] and gypsum [shigao] each at three fen, and licorice [gancao] at seven fen. Large pills are made with jujube flesh, and one pill is dissolved in liquid and taken per dose. It is said that in cases with heat, the dose of baiwei is doubled — indicating that baiwei is cold in nature and is a medicinal of the Yangming channel. Xu Zhicai's Yaodui states that baiwei is incompatible with Chinese dates, yet this formula uses jujube flesh to form the pills — this is evidently out of concern that the cold and cooling properties of the other drugs might damage the spleen and stomach. Zhu Hong's Huoren Shu [Book for Reviving the Living], in treating the condition after sweating induced for wind-warmth where the body remains scorching hot, with spontaneous sweating, heaviness of the body, excessive somnolence, nasal snoring, and difficulty producing speech, also uses baiwei in the Weirui Tang [Solomon's Seal Decoction]. The Immortal Master Sun's Qianjin Fang [Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold] contains an imperially proclaimed sweating formula called Baiwei San [Baiwei Powder].

segments.title_inferred: Baiqian (White前) — Root, Flavour, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】石藍（《唐本》）、嗽藥（同上）。時珍曰：名義未詳。

【集解】弘景曰：白前出近道，根似細辛而大，色白不柔易折，氣嗽方多用之。恭曰：苗高尺許，其葉似柳，或似芫花，根長於細辛，白色。生州渚沙磧之上，不生近道。俗名石藍，又名嗽藥。今用蔓生者味苦，非真也。志曰：根似白薇、牛膝輩，二月、八月採，陰乾用。

嘉謨曰：似牛膝，粗長堅直易斷者，白前也。似牛膝，短小柔軟能彎者，白薇也。近道俱有，形色頗同，以此別之，不致差誤。

根

【氣味】甘，微溫，無毒。權曰：辛。恭曰：微寒。

【主治】胸脅逆氣，咳嗽上氣，呼吸欲絕（《別錄》）。主一切氣，肺氣煩悶，奔豚腎氣（大明）。

【發明】宗奭曰：白前能保定肺氣，治嗽多用，以溫藥相佐使尤佳。時珍曰：白前色白而味微辛甘，手太陰藥也。長於降氣，肺氣壅實而有痰者宜之。若虛而長哽氣者，不可用也。
segments.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]** Shilan [Stone Indigo] (Tang Bencao); Sou Yao [Cough Medicine] (same source). Li Shizhen notes: The meaning behind the name is unclear.

**[Collected Commentaries]** Tao Hongjing states: Baiqian [White前] is found on nearby roads. Its root resembles that of Asarum [细辛, xixin] but is larger, white in colour, not pliable, and breaks easily. Prescriptions for qi-cough [respiratory cough conditions] frequently employ it. Su Gong states: The plant grows to about one chi [approximately 30 cm] in height; its leaves resemble those of willow, or alternatively those of Daphne genkwa [芫花, yuanhua]. The root is longer than that of Asarum and is white in colour. It grows on sandy shoals and river islets, not along nearby roadsides. Its common names are Shilan and Sou Yao. What is currently used — a vine-growing variety with a bitter flavour — is not the genuine article. Chen Zangqi states: The root resembles those of Cynanchum atratum [白薇, baiwei] and Achyranthes [牛膝, niuxi]. It is collected in the second and eighth months and dried in the shade before use.

Jia Mo states: That which resembles Achyranthes but is thick, long, firm, straight, and breaks easily is Baiqian. That which resembles Achyranthes but is short, small, soft, and can be bent is Baiwei [Cynanchum atratum]. Both are found on nearby roads, and their appearance and colour are quite similar; the distinction above should prevent errors in identification.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavour]** Sweet, slightly warm, non-toxic. [Lei] Xiao states: Acrid. Su Gong states: Slightly cold.

**[Principal Treatments]** Counterflow qi in the chest and flanks; cough with qi rushing upward; breathing on the verge of cessation (Mingyi Bielu). Governs all qi disorders; oppressive congestion of lung qi; running-piglet kidney qi [奔豚腎氣, a condition of surging qi from the lower abdomen] (Rihuazi Bencao).

**[Elucidation]** Kou Zongshi states: Baiqian is able to stabilise and settle lung qi; it is widely used in treating cough, and is especially effective when combined with warming adjuvant medicinals. Li Shizhen states: Baiqian is white in colour and has a slightly acrid-sweet flavour; it is a medicinal of the hand taiyin [lung] channel. It excels at descending qi and is appropriate for conditions of obstructed, replete lung qi with phlegm. If the patient is deficient and suffers from persistent hiccupping or qi obstruction, it must not be used.

segments.title_inferred: Grass Rhinoceros Horn (Cao Xi) — a detoxifying herb
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰：其解毒之功如犀角，故曰草犀。

【集解】藏器曰：草犀生衢、婺、洪、饒間。苗高二、三尺，獨莖，根如細辛。生水中者名水犀。

曰：《廣州記》云：生嶺南及海中，獨莖對葉而生，如燈台草，根若細辛。

根

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。

【主治】解一切毒气，虎野狼蟲虺所傷，溪毒野蠱惡刺等毒，並宜燒研服之，臨死者亦得活（李珣）。天行瘧瘴寒熱，咳嗽痰壅，飛尸喉痺瘡腫，小兒寒熱丹毒，中惡注忤，痢血等病，煮汁服之。嶺南及睦、婺間中毒者，以此及千金藤並解之（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of the Name:** Li Shizhen states: Its power to resolve toxins is comparable to rhinoceros horn [xi jiao], hence it is called Cao Xi ['Grass Rhinoceros Horn'].

**Collected Commentaries:** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Cao Xi grows in the regions of Qu, Wu, Hong, and Rao [prefectures in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi]. The plant stands two to three chi tall, has a single stem, and a root resembling that of Asarum [xi xin]. The variety growing in water is called Shui Xi ['Water Rhinoceros Horn'].

[Another source] states: The Guangzhou Ji records that it grows in Lingnan and along the coast. It has a single stem with leaves growing in opposite pairs, resembling the lamp-stand herb [deng tai cao], and a root like that of Asarum.

**Root**

**Qi and Flavor:** Acrid [xin]; neutral [ping]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Resolves all manner of toxic qi; for wounds inflicted by tigers, wolves, insects, or vipers [hui], as well as stream-toxins [xi du], wild gu-poison [ye gu], and malicious-thorn toxins [e ci]: burn the root, grind it to powder, and take orally — even those on the verge of death can be revived. (Li Xun)
- For epidemic malaria and miasmic fever-and-chills [tian xing nüe zhang han re], coughing with phlegm obstruction, flying-corpse [fei shi] illness, throat blockage [hou bi], sores and swellings, childhood fever-and-chills with erysipelas [dan du], sudden foul-qi affliction [zhong e], repletion-intrusion [zhu wu], and dysenteric bleeding: decoct and take the juice orally.
- For persons poisoned in Lingnan and the Mu and Wu regions, this herb together with Qian Jin Teng [Stephania or related vine] may both be used as antidotes. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Chāi Zǐ Gǔ (Gold Hairpin Herb) — Root Monograph
segments.original_text:
【釋名】金釵股。時珍曰：石斛名金釵花，此草狀似之，故名。

【集解】藏器曰：金釵股生嶺南及南海山谷，根如細辛，每莖三四十根。曰：忠州、萬州者亦佳，草莖功力相似。緣嶺南多毒，家家貯之。時珍曰：按《嶺表錄》云：廣中多蠱毒，彼人以草藥金釵股治之，十救八、九，其狀如石斛也。又忍冬藤解毒，亦號金釵股，與此同名云。

根

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

【主治】解毒癰疽神驗，以水煎服（李）。解諸藥毒，煮汁服。亦生研，更烈，必大吐下。如無毒，亦吐去熱痰。瘧瘴天行，蠱毒喉痺（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Also called Jīn Chāi Gǔ [Gold Hairpin Stock]. Li Shizhen states: Shí hú [Dendrobium] is known as 'Gold Hairpin Flower' [Jīn Chāi Huā]; this herb resembles it in appearance, hence the name.

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: Jīn Chāi Gǔ grows in the valleys of Lingnan [the region south of the Nanling Mountains] and Nanhai [South Sea region]. Its roots resemble those of xì xīn [Asarum], and each stem bears thirty to forty roots. [Another source] states: Specimens from Zhongzhou and Wanzhou are also excellent; the medicinal potency of the stems and roots is comparable. Because Lingnan has many [kinds of] poison, every household keeps a store of it. Li Shizhen states: According to the Lǐng Biǎo Lù [Record of the Lingnan Region], the Guangdong area has much gǔ poison [gu toxin, a form of parasitic/magical poisoning]; the local people treat it with the herb Jīn Chāi Gǔ, saving eight or nine out of ten patients. Its appearance resembles shí hú [Dendrobium]. Additionally, the vine of rěn dōng [Lonicera japonica, honeysuckle], which also counteracts poison, is likewise called Jīn Chāi Gǔ — sharing the same name as this herb.

**Root**

**Qi and Flavor**
Bitter; balanced [neither warm nor cold]; non-toxic.

**Principal Treatments**
Remarkably effective at resolving toxins [causing] carbuncles and sores [yōng jū]; decoct in water and take orally (Li). Counteracts all manner of medicinal poisoning; boil and drink the juice. It may also be ground fresh [and taken raw], which is more potent and will invariably cause strong vomiting and purging. Even in the absence of poison, it will expel hot phlegm by inducing vomiting. [Also treats] malarial miasma [nüè zhàng], epidemic fevers [tiān xíng], gǔ poisoning, and throat obstruction [hóu bì] (Chen Cangqi).

segments.title_inferred: Jili Herb (Jili Cao) — an antidote plant from southern China
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：按嵇含《南方草木狀》云：此草生交廣，莖如金釵股，形類石斛，根類芍藥。吳黃武中，江夏李俁徙合浦遇毒，其奴吉利偶得此草與服，遂解，而吉利即遁去。俁以此濟人，不知其數也。又高涼郡產良耀草，枝葉如麻黃，花白似牛李，秋結子如小粟，煨食解毒，功亞於吉利草。始因梁耀得之，因以爲名，轉梁爲良耳。

根

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

【主治】解蠱毒，極驗（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: According to Ji Han's *Nanfang Caomu Zhuang* ["A Description of Plants and Trees of the South"]: This herb grows in Jiao and Guang [i.e., present-day Vietnam and Guangdong/Guangxi]. Its stem resembles the shank of a golden hairpin [a description also applied to Dendrobium], its form is similar to shihu [Dendrobium orchid], and its root resembles shaoyao [peony root]. During the Huangwu reign period of Wu [222–229 CE], Li Yu of Jiangxia, having been relocated to Hepu, was poisoned. His servant Jili happened to obtain this herb and administered it to him, whereupon the poison was resolved — and Jili immediately fled [vanishing after the cure]. Yu thereafter used this herb to save countless people. Furthermore, Gaoliangprefecture produces an herb called Liangyao Cao ["Liang Yao herb"]: its branches and leaves resemble those of mahuang [Ephedra], its flowers are white and similar to those of niuli [Rhamnus/buckthorn], and in autumn it bears seeds like small millet. When roasted in embers and eaten, it counteracts poison, though its efficacy is somewhat inferior to that of Jili Cao. It was originally obtained by [a man named] Liang Yao, and was thus named after him; the character 梁 (Liang) was later changed to 良 (Liáng).

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [neutral]; non-toxic.

**Primary Indications:** Resolves gu toxin [蠱毒, a category of poison or parasitic illness caused by malevolent sorcery or ingested toxins]; extremely efficacious. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Zhushagen (Cinnabar Root) — plant description and medicinal use
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：朱砂根生深山中，今惟太和山人采之。苗高尺許，葉似冬青葉，背甚赤，夏月長茂。根大如箸，赤色，此與百兩金仿佛。

根

【氣味】苦，涼，無毒。

【主治】咽喉腫痺，磨水或醋咽之，甚良（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Zhushagen [Cinnabar Root] grows deep in the mountains; at present only the people of Taihe Mountain gather it. The plant stands about a foot tall, with leaves resembling those of wintergreen [冬青, *Ilex* sp.], the undersides being a deep red. It flourishes and grows thickly in the summer months. The root is as thick as a chopstick, red in color, and closely resembles Bǎiliǎngjīn [百兩金, *Ardisia crispa*].

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter; cooling; non-toxic.

**[Principal Treatments]** For swelling and obstruction of the throat [咽喉腫痺]: grind the root with water or vinegar and swallow — this is highly effective. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Bìhuǐléi (Snake-warding Thunder Root) — Herb Entry
segments.original_text:
【釋名】辟蛇雷（《唐本》）。時珍曰：此物辟蛇虺有威，故以雷名之。

【集解】恭曰：辟虺雷狀如粗塊蒼朮，節中有眼。時珍曰：今川中峨嵋、鶴鳴諸山皆有之。根狀如蒼朮，大者若拳。彼人以充方物，苗狀當俟訪問。

根

【氣味】苦，大寒，無毒。

【主治】解百毒，消痰，祛大熱，療頭痛，辟瘟疫（《唐本》）。治咽喉痛，痺解蛇虺毒（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Also called Bìshéléi [Snake-warding Thunder] (Tang Bencao [Tang Materia Medica]). Li Shizhen states: This substance has a powerful effect in warding off snakes and vipers [虺, huǐ], and it is therefore named after thunder [雷, léi] to convey its might.

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: Bìhuǐléi resembles coarse, lumpy Atractylodes [cāngzhú], with nodes that bear eye-like markings. Li Shizhen states: Today it is found in the mountains of Sichuan, including Éméi and Hèmíng and other ranges. The root resembles Atractylodes in form; larger specimens are about the size of a fist. The local people offer it as a regional product. The appearance of its shoots and seedlings must await further inquiry.

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature**
Bitter; greatly cold [大寒]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**
Resolves the hundred poisons [i.e., all manner of toxins]; disperses phlegm; expels intense heat; treats headache; wards off epidemic pestilence [瘟疫] (Tang Bencao). Treats throat pain and numbness [痺]; counteracts the venom of snakes and vipers [蛇虺] (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Jindiluo — Root Properties and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：錦地羅出廣西慶遠山岩間，鎮安、歸順、柳州皆有之。根似萆薢及栝蔞根狀。彼人頗重之，以充方物。

根

【氣味】微苦，平，無毒。

【主治】山嵐瘴毒瘡毒，並中諸毒。以根研生酒服一錢匕，即解（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Jindiluo [錦地羅] grows among the mountain crags of Qingyuan in Guangxi; it is also found in Zhen'an, Guishun, and Liuzhou. Its root resembles that of bixie [Dioscorea tokoro or related yam species] and the root of gualou [Trichosanthes kirilowii]. The local people hold it in high regard and present it as a regional tribute product.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Slightly bitter; balanced [平, neutral in temperature]; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]** For miasmatic mountain poisons [山嵐瘴毒], sores caused by toxic agents [瘡毒], and poisoning by various toxic substances: grind the root and take one coin-spoonful [錢匕, approximately 1–2 g] mixed with unheated [raw] wine; the poison is promptly resolved. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Zijin Niu (Ardisia japonica) – Herb Entry
segments.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：生福州。葉如茶葉，上綠下紫。結實圓，紅色如丹朱。根微紫色，八月採根，去心曝乾，頗似巴戟。

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。

【主治】時疾膈氣，去風痰（蘇頌）。解毒破血（時珍）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Su Song states: It grows in Fuzhou. Its leaves resemble tea leaves, green on the upper surface and purple on the underside. It bears round fruits, red in color like vermilion [dan zhu]. The root is slightly purple in color. The root is collected in the eighth month, the core removed, and dried in the sun; it closely resembles bajì [Morinda officinalis].

【Qi and Flavor】Acrid [xin], neutral [ping], non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Seasonal illnesses [shi ji] and diaphragm qi [ge qi]; disperses wind-phlegm [feng tan] (Su Song). Resolves toxins and breaks up blood stasis [po xue] (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Quanshen (Bistort Root) — Medicinal Uses and Description
segments.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：生淄州田野，葉如羊蹄，根似海蝦，黑色，土人五月採之。

【氣味】缺。

【主治】為末，淋渫腫氣（蘇頌）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Su Song states: It grows in the fields and wilds of Zizhou. The leaves resemble those of the dock plant [Rumex], and the root looks like a sea shrimp, black in color. Local people harvest it in the fifth month.

【Qi and Flavor】Not recorded.

【Indications and Treatments】Grind into a powder and use as a wash [lin xie, i.e., a heated decoction applied externally] for swelling and qi stagnation. (Su Song)

segments.title_inferred: Iron-Wire Grass (Tiexiancao) — Medicinal Root Entry
segments.original_text:
【集解】頌曰：生饒州，三月採根陰乾。時珍曰：今俗呼蓄爲鐵線草，蓋同名耳。

【氣味】微苦，平，無毒。

【主治】療風消腫毒，有效（蘇頌）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Su Song states: It grows in Raozhou [a prefecture in present-day Jiangxi province]; the root is gathered in the third month and dried in the shade. Li Shizhen states: In common parlance today, the plant known as biǎn xù [Polygonum aviculare, knotgrass] is also called 'iron-wire grass' [tiexiancao] — this is merely a case of shared naming [i.e., two different plants bearing the same popular name].

【Qi and Flavor】Slightly bitter; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Effective for treating wind[-related ailments] and dispersing swelling and toxic inflammation (Su Song).

segments.title_inferred: Golden Silk Grass (Jin Si Cao) — Entry from Bencao Gangmu, Chapter Cao zhi Er
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰：金絲草出慶陽山谷，苗狀當俟訪問。

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。

【主治】吐血咳血，衄血下血，血崩瘴氣，解諸藥毒，療癰疽疔腫惡瘡，涼血散熱（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: Golden Silk Grass [Jin Si Cao] grows in the mountain valleys of Qingyang. The appearance of its seedlings and form must await further inquiry and investigation.

**Qi and Flavor:** Bitter; cold; non-toxic.

**Principal Indications:** [It treats] vomiting of blood and coughing of blood, nosebleed [epistaxis] and bleeding from below [rectal or uterine bleeding], flooding of blood [metrorrhagia/uterine hemorrhage], miasmic [malarial] qi [zhang qi], and counteracts the toxicity of various medicinal substances. It also treats carbuncles [yong], ju-abscesses, boils and swellings [ding zhong], and malignant sores; it cools the blood and disperses heat. (Li Shizhen.)

segments.title_inferred: Cnidium Seeds (Snake-Bed / 蛇牀) — Materia Medica Entry
segments.original_text:
【釋名】蛇粟（《本經》）、蛇米（《本經》）、虺牀（《爾雅》）、馬牀（《廣雅》）、蘠蘼（《別錄》, 又名思益、繩毒、棗棘）。

時珍曰︰蛇虺喜臥於下食其子，故有蛇牀、蛇粟諸名。其葉似蘼蕪，故曰牆蘼。《爾雅》云︰盱，虺牀也。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰蛇牀，生臨淄川谷及田野。五月採實陰乾。

弘景曰︰田野墟落甚多，花葉正似蘼蕪。

保升曰︰葉似小葉芎藭，花白，子如黍粒，黃白色。生下濕地，所在皆有，以揚州、襄州者爲良。

頌曰︰三月生苗，高三二尺，葉青碎，作叢似蒿枝。每枝上有花頭百餘，結同一窠，似馬芹類。四五月乃開白色，又似傘狀。子黃褐色，如黍米，至輕虛。

時珍曰︰其花如碎米攢簇。其子兩片合成，似蒔羅子而細，亦有細棱。凡花實似蛇牀者，當歸、芎藭、水芹、藁本、胡蘿蔔是也。

子

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。《別錄》曰︰辛、甘，無毒。權曰︰有小毒。之才曰︰惡牡丹、貝母、巴豆。伏硫黃。
