**Indications:** Strengthens the spleen and astringes the intestines; stops severe nosebleed and vomiting of blood; [treats] intestinal wind [bloody diarrhea]; [treats] flooding [uterine hemorrhage] and vaginal discharge (Da Ming). Treats hot [heat-type] cough (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Mabo (Puffball Fungus) — Names, Properties, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】馬𤶧（音屁）、馬𤬪（音庀）、灰菰（《綱目》）、牛屎菰。

【集解】《別錄》曰：馬勃，生園中久腐處。

弘景曰：俗呼馬勃是也。紫色虛軟，狀如狗肺，彈之粉出。宗奭曰：生濕地及腐木上，夏秋采之。有大如斗者，小亦如升杓。韓退之所謂牛溲、馬勃。

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。

【主治】惡瘡馬疥（《別錄》）。敷諸瘡，甚良（弘景）。去膜，以蜜拌揉，少以水調呷，治喉痺咽疼（宗奭）。清肺，散血，解熱毒（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：馬勃輕虛，上焦肺經藥也。故能清肺熱、咳嗽、喉痺、衄血、失音諸病。李東垣治大頭病，咽喉不利，普濟消毒飲亦用之。
segments.translation:
**Names:** Mabo [馬𤶧, pronounced 'pì']; Ma-pǐ [馬𤬪, pronounced 'pǐ']; Huīgū [Ash Mushroom] (Bencao Gangmu); Niúshǐgū [Cow-dung Mushroom].

**Collected Explanations:** The Bielu states: Mabo grows in gardens in places that have been rotting for a long time.

Hongjing [Tao Hongjing] says: It is what the common people call 'mabo.' It is purple in color, hollow and soft, resembling a dog's lung in shape; when struck, powder flies out. Zongshi [Kou Zongshi] says: It grows on damp ground and on rotting wood, and is gathered in summer and autumn. Some specimens are as large as a dou [a dry measure], while smaller ones are the size of a sheng ladle. It is the 'niúsōu mǎbó' [cow's urine and horse-puff; i.e., things of humble origin] referred to by Han Tuizhi [Han Yu, the Tang dynasty writer].

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xīn]; balanced [píng]; non-toxic.

**Primary Treatments:** Malignant sores and horse-mange [equine scabies] (Bielu). Applied to all manner of sores, it is very effective (Hongjing). After removing the outer membrane, knead with honey, then mix a small amount with water and sip; treats throat obstruction [laryngeal bi] and throat pain (Zongshi). Clears the lungs, disperses blood [stasis], and resolves heat-toxin (Shizhen).

**Elaboration:** Shizhen [Li Shizhen] says: Mabo is light and hollow — it is a medicinal that acts on the upper burner and the lung channel. Therefore it can clear lung heat and treat cough, throat obstruction [laryngeal bi], nosebleed [epistaxis], and loss of voice, among other conditions. Li Dongyuan used it in treating 'big-head disease' [epidemic parotitis/diphtheria-type illness with severe head and throat swelling], with poor pharyngeal function; it is also an ingredient in the Puji Xiaodu Decoction [Universal Benefit Toxin-Dispersing Drink].

segments.title_inferred: Hundred Herbs (Mixed Grasses) — medicinal uses of mixed-herb ash
segments.original_text:
（《拾遺》）

藏器曰︰五月五日采一百種草，陰乾燒灰，和鍛石為團，研，敷金瘡止血，亦敷犬咬。

又主腋臭，燒灰和井華水作團，白，以釅醋和作餅，腋下夾之，乾即易，當抽一身盡痛悶，瘡出即止，以小便洗之，不過三度愈。

時珍曰︰按︰《千金方》治洞注下痢，以五月五日百草灰吹入下部。又治瘰癧已破，五月五日采一切雜草，煮汁洗之。
segments.translation:
([From] the Shiyi [i.e., Bencao Shiyi, by Chen Cangqi])

Cangqi states: On the fifth day of the fifth month, collect one hundred kinds of herbs, dry them in the shade, burn them to ash, and mix with calcined lime [鍛石, slaked/burnt lime] to form a cake. Grind and apply to metal-weapon wounds [金瘡] to stop bleeding; it may also be applied to dog bites.

It also treats axillary odor [腋臭, bromhidrosis]: burn the herbs to ash, mix with well-surface water [井華水, the first water drawn from a well at dawn] to form a cake, then blend with strong vinegar to make a patty. Place this under the armpit and hold it there; replace when dry. The patient will feel aching and oppression throughout the body — once sores [瘡] emerge, stop the treatment. Wash the area with urine; recovery is achieved in no more than three applications.

Li Shizhen states: According to the Qianjin Fang [Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold], for the treatment of severe watery diarrhea [洞注下痢], blow ash of the hundred herbs collected on the fifth day of the fifth month into the lower orifice [rectum]. For scrofula [瘰癧] that has already ulcerated, collect all manner of mixed herbs on the fifth day of the fifth month, boil to make a decoction, and wash the affected area with it.

segments.title_inferred: Hundred Grasses Flowers and Grass from the Well's Edge
segments.original_text:
（《拾遺》）

藏器曰︰主治百病，長生神仙，亦煮汁釀酒服。按《異類》云︰鳳剛者，漁陽人。常采百花水漬，泥封埋百日，煎為丸。卒死者，納口中即活也。剛服

井口邊草（《拾遺》）

藏器曰︰小兒夜啼，私著席下，勿令母知。

思邈曰︰五月五
segments.translation:
(**Hundred Grasses Flowers** [百草花], from *Shiyi* [Bencao Shiyi])

Zangqi [Chen Zangqi] states: [This] treats the hundred diseases, and prolongs life to the level of an immortal. It may also be decocted into a juice and used to brew wine for consumption. According to the *Yilei* [a text of anomalies/wonders]: Feng Gang was a man from Yuyang. He would regularly collect the flowers of a hundred plants, steep them in water, seal them in mud, and bury them for a hundred days, then decoct and form them into pills. For those who have died suddenly, placing [a pill] into the mouth will revive them. Gang himself took [these pills]…

(**Grass from the Well's Edge** [井口邊草], from *Shiyi*)

Zangqi states: For children who cry at night, secretly place [the grass] beneath the sleeping mat, and do not let the mother know.

Simiao [Sun Simiao] states: On the fifth day of the fifth month… [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-sentence]

segments.title_inferred: Grass from Tree Hollows (Miscellaneous Herbs)
segments.original_text:
（《綱目》）

時珍曰︰主小兒腹痛夜啼，暗著戶上即止。（出《聖惠方》）
segments.translation:
([Source:] Bencao Gangmu)

Li Shizhen states: [This herb] is indicated for abdominal pain and nighttime crying in young children. Silently [i.e., without the child's knowledge] affix it above the doorway, and [the crying] will cease at once. (Cited from the Shenghui Fang [Prescriptions of Sagely Beneficence].)

segments.title_inferred: Grass from the Grave of a Woman Who Died in Childbirth (Miscellaneous Herbs)
segments.original_text:
（《拾遺》）

藏器曰：小兒醋瘡。取之勿回顧，作湯浴之，不過三度瘥。
segments.translation:
([Source:] Shiyi [i.e., Chen Cangqi's 'Bencao Shiyi'])

Chen Cangqi states: For children's sour sores [醋瘡, likely a type of eczema or acidic skin eruption]. Gather the grass without looking back, prepare it as a decoction, and bathe [the affected area] with it. The condition will be cured in no more than three treatments.

segments.title_inferred: Swallow Nest Grass (Yan Ru Cao) — Miscellaneous Grasses
segments.original_text:
（宋《嘉祐》）

藏器曰︰即燕窠中草也。無毒。主眠中遺尿。燒黑研末，水進方寸匕。亦止噦逆。

時珍曰︰《千金方》︰治丈夫婦人無故尿血。用胡燕窠中草，燒末，酒服半錢匕。《聖惠方》︰消渴飲水。燕窠中草（燒灰）一兩，牡蠣（煅）二兩，白羊肺一具。切晒研末。每於食後，新汲水調下三錢。又一切瘡痕不滅。用燕蓐草（燒灰）、鷹屎白等分。人乳和塗，日三、五次。

又浸淫瘡出黃水，燒灰敷之。
segments.translation:
Yan Ru Cao [Swallow Nest Grass] (Miscellaneous Grasses)

[From the Song dynasty Jiayou Bencao]

Zangqi states: This is simply the grass found inside a swallow's nest. It is non-toxic. It principally treats bedwetting during sleep [nocturnal enuresis]. Burn it to a black char, grind into powder, and take one square-cun spoonful [fang cun bi] with water. It also stops hiccupping and retching [nausea/belching].

Li Shizhen states: The Qianjin Fang [Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold] says: To treat unexplained hematuria [blood in the urine] in men and women alike — take the grass from a barn swallow's nest, burn it to powder, and take half a qian-spoonful with wine.

The Shenghui Fang [Prescriptions of the Holy Beneficence] says: For wasting-thirst [xiaoke, i.e., a diabetes-like condition] with excessive drinking of water — use one liang of swallow nest grass (burned to ash), two liang of oyster shell (calcined), and one whole white sheep's lung. Slice and sun-dry, then grind into powder. After each meal, take three qian dissolved in freshly drawn water.

Furthermore, for all types of scar tissue that will not fade — use equal parts of swallow nest grass (burned to ash) and eagle's white droppings. Mix with human breast milk and apply to the affected area three to five times daily.

Additionally, for spreading sores [impetiginous or eczematous lesions] that weep yellow fluid — burn to ash and apply topically.

segments.title_inferred: Chicken Nest Grass (Miscellaneous Grasses)
segments.original_text:
（宋《嘉祐》）

大明曰︰小兒夜啼。安席下，勿令母知。

藏器曰︰小兒白禿瘡。和白頭翁花燒灰，臘月豬脂和敷之。瘡先以酸泔洗淨。

時珍曰︰《千金方》︰治產後遺尿。燒末，酒服一錢。又《不自秘方》︰治天絲入目。燒灰淋汁，洗之。
segments.translation:
([Sourced from the Song dynasty] 'Jiayou Bencao')

Da Ming states: [For] night crying in infants — place [the herb] under the sleeping mat without letting the mother know.

Zang Qi states: [For] white-scab sores [tinea capitis / favus] in infants — mix with the flowers of Pulsatilla [Baitouweng], burn to ash, blend with lard from the twelfth lunar month [preserved winter lard], and apply topically. First wash the sores clean with soured rice-water.

Li Shizhen states: The 'Qianjin Fang' [Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold]: to treat post-partum urinary incontinence — burn to powder and take one qian [approx. 3–4 g] with wine. Also, the 'Bu Zi Mi Fang' [Prescriptions Not Kept Secret]: to treat airborne silk or fine threads entering the eye — burn to ash, leach with water to obtain the filtered liquid, and wash the eye with it.

segments.title_inferred: Pig's Nest Grass (Miscellaneous Herbs) and Ox Grass cross-reference, with Shennong Bencao Jing header
segments.original_text:
大明曰︰小兒夜啼。密安席下，勿令母知。

牛草

見獸部牛下。

《神農本經》（已下有名未用）
segments.translation:
Da Ming [i.e., Rihua Zhu] states: [For] night crying in infants — secretly place [the herb] beneath the sleeping mat; do not let the mother know.

Ox Grass [牛草]

See the entry under 'Ox' in the Beasts section [獸部].

'Shennong's Classic of Materia Medica' (The following entries are those listed by name but not yet assigned a use [有名未用].)

segments.title_inferred: Qucao (Bent Herb) — Miscellaneous Grasses
segments.original_text:
《本經》曰︰味苦，微寒，無毒。主胸脅下痛，邪氣，腸間寒熱，陰痺。久服輕身益氣耐老。

《別錄》曰︰生漢中川澤。五月採。
segments.translation:
The 'Classic of Materia Medica' [Shennong Bencao Jing] states: Flavor bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. It governs pain below the chest and flanks, pathogenic qi [xie qi], cold and heat in the intestines, and yin bi [painful obstruction of the lower body / genitourinary region]. Long-term use lightens the body, replenishes qi, and promotes longevity.

The 'Supplementary Records' [Mingyi Bielu] states: It grows in the river marshes of Hanzhong. It is harvested in the fifth month.

segments.title_inferred: Bieji (Miscellaneous Herb)
segments.original_text:
《本經》曰：味苦，微溫，無毒。主風寒濕痺身重，四肢疼酸，寒邪歷節痛。

《別錄》曰：一名別枝。生藍田川谷。二月、八月採。弘景曰：方家時有用處，今亦絕矣。
segments.translation:
The Classic of Materia Medica [Shennong Bencao Jing] states: Flavor is bitter, slightly warm, non-toxic. It governs [treats] wind-cold-damp impediment [bi syndrome] with heaviness of the body, aching and soreness of the four limbs, and cold-evil migratory joint pain [li-jie pain].

The Supplementary Records [Ming Yi Bie Lu] states: An alternate name is Biezhi ["Separate Branch"]. It grows in the river valleys of Lantian. Collected in the second and eighth months. Tao Hongjing [弘景] remarks: Formulists made occasional use of it in their time, but today it has fallen entirely out of use.

segments.title_inferred: Tianzhu Cinnamon (天竺桂) – Bark: Properties and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【集解】宗奭曰︰皮與牡桂相同，但薄耳。
時珍曰︰此即今閩、粵、浙中山桂也，而台州天竺最多，故名。大樹繁花，結實如蓮子狀。天竺僧人稱為月桂是矣。詳月桂下。

皮
【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。
【主治】腹內諸冷，血氣脹痛（藏器）。破產後惡血，治血痢腸風，補心同，方家少用。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations**

Kou Zongshi states: The bark is the same as that of mùguì [male/official cinnamon, *Cinnamomum cassia*], but thinner.

Li Shizhen states: This is the mountain cinnamon [shān guì] found today in Fujian, Guangdong, and Zhejiang. It grows most abundantly at Tianzhu in Taizhou, hence the name. It is a large tree with profuse flowers, bearing fruit shaped like lotus seeds [*Nelumbo* seeds]. Buddhist monks from Tianzhu [India] call it 'moon laurel' [yuè guì], which is indeed what it is. See the entry on Moon Laurel [月桂] for further detail.

**Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xīn], warm, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** All cold conditions within the abdomen; distension and pain due to blood and qi [藏器, Chen Cangqi]. Breaks up lochia [malign blood] after childbirth; treats bloody dysentery [xuè lì] and intestinal wind [cháng fēng]; tonifies the heart [bǔ xīn] similarly [to other cinnamons]. Seldom used by formulary physicians [fāng jiā].

segments.title_inferred: Moon Laurel (Yuegui) — Botanical Lore, Celestial Origins, and Medicinal Use
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰今江東諸處，每至四、五月後晦，多於衢路間得月桂子，大於狸豆，破之辛香，古者相傳是月中下也。余杭靈隱寺僧種得一株，近代詩人多所論述。《洞冥記》云︰有遠飛雞，朝往夕還，常銜桂實歸於南土。南土月路也，故北方無之。山桂猶堪為藥，況

時珍曰︰吳剛伐月桂之說，起於隋唐小說。月桂落子之說，起於武后之時。相傳有梵僧自天竺鷲嶺飛來，故八月常有桂子落於天竺。《唐書》亦云︰垂拱四年三月，有月桂子降於台州，十餘日乃止。宋仁宗天聖丁卯八月十五日夜，月明天淨，杭州靈隱寺月桂子降，其繁如雨，其大如豆，其圓如珠，其色有白者、黃者、黑者，殼如芡實，味辛。拾以進呈，寺僧種之，得二十五株，慈雲式公有序記之。張君房宿錢塘月輪寺，亦見桂子紛如煙霧，回旋成穗，墜如牽牛子，黃白相間，咀之無味。據此，則月中真若有樹矣。竊謂月乃陰魄，其中婆娑者，山河之影爾。月既無桂，則空中所墜者何物耶？泛觀群史，有雨塵沙土石，雨金鉛錢汞，雨絮帛谷粟，雨草木花藥，雨毛血魚肉之類甚眾。則桂子之雨，亦妖怪所致，非月中有桂也。桂生南方，故惟南方有之。《宋史》云︰元豐三年六月，饒州雨木子數畝，狀類山芋子，味辛而香。

子

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】小兒耳後月蝕瘡，研碎敷之（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: In various places in Jiangdong, after the last day of the fourth or fifth month, seeds of the moon laurel [yuegui] are frequently found along roadsides. They are larger than fox beans [lǐdòu]; when broken open, they are pungently fragrant. It has been transmitted from antiquity that they fall from the moon. A monk at Lingyin Temple in Yuhang [near Hangzhou] cultivated one specimen, and many poets of recent times have written about it. The *Dongming Ji* [Record of Obscure Mysteries] states: There are birds called the Far-Flying Fowl [yuǎnfēi jī] that go out in the morning and return in the evening, and they regularly carry laurel fruits back to the southern lands. The southern lands are the domain of the moon's path, which is why [the moon laurel] is not found in the north. Even mountain laurel [shān guì] can serve as medicine, how much more so [the moon laurel] — [text appears to be truncated here].

Li Shizhen states: The legend of Wu Gang chopping the moon laurel originated in fiction [xiaoshuo] of the Sui and Tang dynasties. The story of moon laurel seeds falling from the sky originated in the time of Empress Wu [Wu Zetian]. It is said that a Brahmin monk flew over from Vulture Peak [Jiling] in India [Tianzhu], and therefore in the eighth month laurel seeds regularly fall at Tianzhu [a temple in Hangzhou, also sharing the name with India]. The *Tang Shu* [Book of Tang] also records: In the third month of the fourth year of the Chui Gong reign period [688 CE], moon laurel seeds descended upon Taizhou for more than ten days before stopping. On the night of the fifteenth day of the eighth month in the Tiansheng Dingmao year [1027 CE] of the reign of Emperor Renzong of Song, when the moon was bright and the sky clear, moon laurel seeds descended upon Lingyin Temple in Hangzhou — as plentiful as rain, as large as beans, as round as pearls, in colors of white, yellow, and black, with shells like those of Gordon euryale seeds [qiàn shí], and with a pungent flavor. They were gathered and presented [to the authorities], and the monks of the temple planted them, obtaining twenty-five saplings; the Venerable Ciyun Shi recorded the event in a preface. Zhang Junfang, spending the night at Yuelun Temple in Qiantang, also witnessed the laurel seeds falling like smoke and mist, swirling into clusters and dropping like seeds of morning glory [qiānniú zǐ], alternating yellow and white, but tasteless when chewed. Based on these accounts, it truly seems as though there is a tree on the moon. However, I venture to suggest that the moon is a body of yin essence [yīn pò], and what undulates within it is merely the reflection of mountains and rivers. Since the moon in fact contains no laurel, what then are the objects that fall from the sky? A broad survey of historical records shows numerous accounts of showers of dust, sand, earth, and stones; showers of gold, lead, coins, and mercury; showers of floss, silk, grain, and millet; showers of grasses, trees, flowers, and medicines; and showers of hair, blood, fish, and flesh — a great many such events. The rain of laurel seeds is therefore likewise caused by some uncanny [yāoguài] phenomenon, not by an actual laurel growing on the moon. The laurel [guì] grows in the south, which is why it is found only in the south. The *Song Shi* [History of the Song] records: In the sixth month of the third year of the Yuanfeng reign period [1080 CE], in Raozhou, tree seeds rained down over an area of several mu; they resembled the seeds of wild taro [shānyù zǐ] in appearance, and were pungently fragrant in flavor.

**Seeds**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Pungent; warm; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatment]** For moon-bite sores [yuè shí chuāng, a form of skin ulceration] behind the ears in children: grind [the seeds] to a powder and apply topically. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Mìxiāng (Honey Fragrance Wood) — Names, Collected Explanations, Properties, and Indications
segments.original_text:
【釋名】木蜜（《內典》）、沒香（《綱目》）
【集解】
藏器曰：蜜香生交州。大樹，節如沉香。《法華經》注云：木蜜，香蜜也。樹形似槐而香，伐之五、六年，乃取其香。《異物志》云：其葉如椿。樹生千歲，斫仆之，四、五歲乃往看，已腐敗，惟中節堅貞者是香。曰：生南海諸山中。種之五、六年便有香。《交州記》云：樹似沉香無異也。時珍曰：按《魏王花木志》云：木蜜號千歲樹，根本甚大，伐之四、五歲，取不腐者為香。觀此，則陳藏器所謂生千歲乃斫者，蓋誤訛也。段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：沒樹出波斯國、拂林國，人呼為阿[KT]。樹長丈餘，皮青白色，葉似槐而長，花似橘花而大。子黑色，大如山茱萸，酸甜可食。《廣州志》云：肇慶新興縣出多香木，俗名蜜香。辟惡氣，殺鬼精。《晉書》云：太康五年，大秦國獻蜜香樹皮紙，微褐色，有紋如魚子，極香而堅韌。觀此數說，則蜜香亦沉香之類，故形狀功用兩相仿佛。《南越志》謂：交人稱沉香為蜜香。《交州志》謂：蜜香似沉香。《嶺表錄異》言：棧香皮紙似魚子。尤可互証。楊慎《丹鉛錄》言蜜樹是蜜蒙花樹者，謬也。又枳木亦名木蜜，不知亦同類否？詳見果部。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】去臭，除鬼氣（藏器）。辟惡，去邪鬼尸注心氣。
segments.translation:
**Names [Shìmíng]:** Mùmì [Wood-Honey] (cited in the *Nèidiǎn* [Buddhist canonical texts]); Mòxiāng [Lost/Absent Fragrance] (cited in the *Gāngmù*).

**Collected Explanations [Jíjiě]:**

Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Mìxiāng grows in Jiaozhou [modern northern Vietnam]. It is a large tree whose heartwood nodes resemble those of chen-xiang [agarwood/aloeswood]. The commentary on the *Lotus Sūtra* (*Fāhuājīng*) states: 'Mùmì is fragrant honey-wood. The tree's form resembles the Chinese scholar tree [huái, *Sophora japonica*] and is fragrant; one fells it and then, after five or six years, harvests the fragrance.' The *Yìwùzhì* [Record of Strange Things] states: 'Its leaves resemble those of the chūn tree [*Toona sinensis*]. The tree lives a thousand years; after it is felled, one returns four or five years later to find it rotted, and only the solid, unyielding heartwood nodes constitute the fragrance.'

[Source unnamed, possibly a cited authority:] It grows in the mountains of the South Seas. Once planted, it yields fragrance within five or six years. The *Jiāozhōu Jì* [Record of Jiaozhou] states: 'The tree is indistinguishable from chen-xiang.'

Li Shizhen [Shízhēn] states: I note that the *Wèi Wáng Huāmùzhì* [Prince of Wei's Flora] states: 'Mùmì is called the Thousand-Year Tree; its root and trunk are very large. After felling, one waits four or five years and then takes the non-rotted portions as fragrance.' In light of this, the claim by Chen Cangqi that the tree must live a thousand years before being felled is evidently an error. Duan Chengshi's *Yǒuyáng Záz'ǔ* [Miscellany of Youyang] states: 'The mò tree comes from Persia [Bōsī guó] and the kingdom of Fúlín [Byzantine Syria/Rome]; its people call it A-[KT] [ILLEGIBLE: transcription of a foreign-language name]. The tree grows to over a zhàng [≈3 m] in height; its bark is greenish-white; its leaves resemble those of the scholar tree but are longer; its flowers resemble orange blossoms but are larger; its fruits are black, about the size of cornelian cherry [shān zhūyú, *Cornus officinalis*], and are sour-sweet and edible.'

The *Guǎngzhōu Zhì* [Guangzhou Gazetteer] states: 'Zhàoqìng's Xīnxīng County produces much fragrant wood commonly called mìxiāng. It wards off noxious qi and destroys malevolent spirits.'

The *Jìnshū* [History of the Jin Dynasty] states: 'In the fifth year of the Tàikāng reign [284 CE], the kingdom of Dàqín [Rome/the Byzantine Empire] presented paper made from mìxiāng tree bark — slightly brownish in color, with a texture patterned like fish roe, extremely fragrant, and tough and pliable.'

Considering these various accounts, mìxiāng belongs to the same category as chen-xiang [agarwood], and accordingly their appearance and uses closely resemble one another. The *Nányuèzhì* [Record of Southern Yue] states that the people of Jiao call chen-xiang 'mìxiāng.' The *Jiāozhōu Zhì* states that mìxiāng resembles chen-xiang. The *Lǐngbiǎo Lùyì* [Record of the Wonders of the Southern Frontier] notes that the bark paper of zhàn-xiāng [a grade of agarwood] resembles fish roe. These sources mutually corroborate one another.

Yang Shen's [Yáng Shèn's] *Dānqiān Lù* [Red-Lead Record] claims that the mì tree is the mìméng flower tree [*Buddleja officinalis*] — this is mistaken. Furthermore, the zhǐ wood [*Hovenia dulcis*?] is also called mùmì; it is unknown whether this is the same category. See the Fruits section [Guǒ bù] for details.

**Qi and Flavor [Qìwèi]:** Acrid [xīn]; warm [wēn]; non-toxic.

**Indications [Zhǔzhì]:** Removes foul odors; dispels ghost qi [malevolent spiritual influences] (Chen Cangqi). Wards off evil; expels the qi of malevolent ghosts, corpse-reanimation afflictions [shī zhù], and heart qi [i.e., heart-related afflictions caused by such entities].

segments.title_inferred: Jiangzhen Xiang (降真香) — Rosewoood/Rosewood Incense: Names, Origins, Properties, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】紫藤香（《綱目》）、雞骨香。

曰︰《仙傳》︰拌和諸香，燒煙直上，感引鶴降。醮星辰，燒此香為第一，度功力極驗。降真之名以此。

時珍曰︰俗呼舶上來者為番降，亦名雞骨，與沉香同名。

【集解】慎微曰︰降真香出黔南。

曰︰生南海山中及大秦國。其香似蘇方木，燒之初不甚香，得諸香和之則特美。入藥以番降紫而潤者為良。

時珍曰︰今廣東、廣西、雲南、漢中、施州、永順、保靖，及占城、安南、暹羅、渤泥、琉球諸地皆有之。朱輔《溪蠻叢笑》云︰雞骨香即降香，本出海南。今溪峒僻處所出者，似是而非，勁瘦不甚香。周達觀《真臘記》云︰降香生叢林中，番人頗費砍斫之功，乃樹心也。其外白皮，濃八、九寸，或五、六寸。焚之氣勁而遠。又嵇含《草木狀》云︰紫藤香，長莖細葉，根極堅實，重重有皮，花白子黑。其莖截置煙炱中，經久成紫香，可降神。按︰嵇氏所說，與前說稍異，豈即朱氏所謂似是而非者乎？抑中國者與番降不同乎？

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】燒之，辟天行時氣，宅舍怪異。小兒帶之，辟邪惡氣（李珣）。療折傷金瘡，止血定痛，消腫生肌（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰降香，唐、宋本草失收。唐慎微始增入之，而不著其功用。今折傷金瘡家多用其節，云可代沒藥、血竭。按︰《名醫錄》云︰周某被海寇刃傷，血出不止，筋如斷，骨如折，用花蕊石散不效。軍士李高用紫金散掩之，血止痛定。明日結痂如鐵，遂愈，且無瘢痕。叩其方，則用紫藤香瓷瓦刮下研末爾。云即降香之最佳者，曾救萬人。羅天益《衛生寶鑑》
segments.translation:
**[Nomenclature]** Also known as Zǐténg xiāng [Purple Wisteria Incense] (Bencao Gangmu) and Jīgǔ xiāng [Chicken-bone Incense].

[A source] states: According to the Xiān Chuán [Immortals' Traditions], when this substance is blended with various incenses and burned, the smoke rises straight upward and is said to attract cranes to descend. For offering rites to the stars and constellations, burning this incense is of the first rank, and its efficacy is remarkably proven. The name Jiàngzhēn ["Bringing Down the Immortals/True Ones"] derives from this.

Li Shizhen states: Commonly, that which arrives by ship is called Fān jiàng ["Foreign Jiangzhen"]; it is also called Jīgǔ [Chicken-bone], a name it shares with chen xiang [eaglewood/agarwood].

**[Collected Explanations]** Tang Shenwei states: Jiangzhen xiang originates from Qiannan [southern Guizhou].

[Another source] states: It grows in the mountains of the South Sea region and in the land of Daqin [a name used for the Roman/Byzantine East or Persia]. Its fragrance resembles that of sumu [sappanwood]; when first burned it is not particularly fragrant, but when blended with other incenses it becomes especially fine. For medicinal use, the foreign variety (fān jiàng) that is purple and moist is considered the best.

Li Shizhen states: Today it is found in Guangdong, Guangxi, Yunnan, Hanzhong, Shizhou, Yongshun, Baojing, and also in the foreign lands of Champa [Zhancheng], Annam [present-day Vietnam], Siam [Thailand], Brunei [Boni], and Ryukyu [Liu Qiu], among other places. Zhu Fu, in his Xī Mán Cóng Xiào, writes: "Chicken-bone incense is jiàng xiāng; it originally comes from Hainan. What is now produced in remote mountain-stream settlements appears to be the same but is not — it is lean and tough and not very fragrant." Zhou Daguan, in his Zhēnlà Jì [Record of Cambodia], writes: "Jiàng xiāng grows in dense forests; the foreign people expend considerable labor in chopping it out, for it is the heartwood of the tree. The outer white bark is eight or nine inches thick, or five or six inches. When burned, its fragrance is strong and far-reaching." Furthermore, Ji Han in his Cǎomù Zhuàng [Account of Plants and Trees] writes: "Zǐténg xiāng has long stems and fine leaves; its roots are extremely firm and solid, with layer upon layer of bark; its flowers are white and its seeds black. When sections of its stem are placed in soot and smoke over a long period, they eventually become purple incense, which can summon spirits [jiàng shén]." Note: What Ji Han describes differs somewhat from the accounts above — might this be the variety Zhu Fu called 'appearing similar but actually different'? Or could it be that the Chinese variety is distinct from the foreign import?

**[Flavor and Nature]** Acrid [xīn], warm, non-toxic.

**[Principal Treatments]** When burned, it wards off epidemic seasonal qi [tiān xíng shí qì] and repels uncanny disturbances in the home. When worn by children, it repels malign and evil qi (Li Xun). It treats fractures and metal-weapon wounds [jīn chuāng], stops bleeding, settles pain, disperses swelling, and promotes the regeneration of flesh (Li Shizhen).

**[Elaboration]** Li Shizhen states: Jiàng xiāng was omitted from the materia medica of the Tang and Song dynasties. Tang Shenwei was the first to add it, yet he did not record its medicinal functions. Today, practitioners treating fractures and metal-weapon wounds frequently use its nodal sections, saying it can substitute for myrrh [mòyào] and dragon's blood resin [xuèjié]. According to the Míng Yī Lù [Records of Renowned Physicians]: a certain Zhou [surname] was wounded by a blade wielded by sea pirates; the bleeding would not stop, his sinews seemed severed and his bones seemed broken. The application of Huā Ruǐ Shí Sǎn [Ophicalcite Powder] was ineffective. The soldier Li Gao applied Zǐjīn Sǎn [Purple Gold Powder] to cover the wound; the bleeding stopped and the pain subsided. By the next day a crust had formed as hard as iron, and he fully recovered without even a scar. When pressed for the formula, it turned out to consist solely of zǐténg xiāng scraped from a ceramic tile and ground into powder. It is said to be the finest form of jiàng xiāng and has reportedly saved ten thousand people. Luo Tianyi's Wèishēng Bǎo Jiàn [Precious Mirror of Health] … [text ends here; passage is cut off]

segments.title_inferred: Camphor Tree (Zhang) — Wood Section One
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰：其木理多紋章，故謂之樟。

【集解】藏器曰：江東造船多用樟木。縣名豫章，因木得名。

時珍曰：西南處處山谷有之。木高丈餘。小葉似楠而尖長，背有黃赤茸毛，四時不凋。夏開細花，結小子。木大者數抱，肌理細而錯縱有紋，宜於雕刻，氣甚芬烈。豫、章乃二木名，一類二種也。豫即釣樟，見下條。

樟材

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】惡氣中惡，心腹痛鬼疰，霍亂腹脹，宿食不消，常吐酸臭水，酒煮服，無藥處用之。煎湯，浴腳氣、疥癬風癢。作履，除腳氣。

【發明】時珍曰：霍亂及乾霍亂須吐者，以樟木屑煎濃汁吐之，甚良。又中惡死者，以樟木燒煙熏之，待蘇乃用藥。此物辛烈香竄，能去濕氣、辟邪惡故也。

風疰鬼邪（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen states: The grain of this wood displays many intricate patterns [wen zhang 紋章], and thus it is called zhang [camphor].

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: In Jiangdong [eastern China], ships are commonly built using camphor wood. The prefecture name Yuzhang [modern Jiangxi region] was derived from this tree.

Li Shizhen states: This tree is found throughout the mountain valleys of the southwest. The trunk grows to over a zhang in height [approx. 3+ metres]. Its small leaves resemble those of the nan tree [Phoebe/Machilus sp.] but are more pointed and elongated, with yellow-reddish downy hairs on the underside, and they do not fall in any of the four seasons. In summer it produces delicate flowers and sets small fruit. Large specimens require several arm-spans to embrace. The grain is fine yet interlocking and patterned, making it well-suited for carving; the fragrance is exceedingly powerful and piercing. Yu and Zhang are in fact names for two trees — two varieties of one category. Yu refers to the 'fishing camphor' [Diao Zhang, Lindera aggregate or related species], described in the following entry.

**Camphor Timber**

**Flavor and Nature**
Acrid [xin], warm, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**
- Malign qi and being struck by malign influences [zhong e]; pain in the chest and abdomen; ghost-infusion illness [gui zhu, a condition of chronic wandering pain attributed to supernatural causes]
- Cholera [huo luan] with abdominal distension
- Undigested retained food, with habitual vomiting of sour, foul-smelling fluid — decoct in wine and take orally; to be used where no other medicine is available
- Decoct into a wash-bath to treat leg-qi [jiao qi, beriberi/lower limb swelling], scabies, ringworm, and wind-itch
- Made into footwear to relieve leg-qi
- Wind-infusion and ghost-evil [feng zhu gui xie] (according to Li Shizhen)

**Elaboration**
Li Shizhen states: For cholera and dry cholera where vomiting must be induced, decocting camphor wood shavings into a strong concentrated liquid and using it to provoke vomiting is highly effective. Furthermore, for those who have collapsed from being struck by malign influences, burn camphor wood to produce smoke and fumigate the patient; once they revive, administer medicines. This substance is acrid, intensely aromatic, and penetrating in nature, and is therefore capable of dispelling dampness and warding off evil and malign influences.

segments.title_inferred: Diao Zhang (Lesser Camphor / Lindera aggregate relative) — Root Bark and Stems
segments.original_text:
【釋名】烏樟（弘景）、[KT]

時珍曰︰樟有大、小二種，紫、淡二色。此即樟之小者。按︰鄭樵《通志》云︰釣樟亦樟之類，即《爾雅》所謂「[KT]，無疵」是也。又相如賦云︰[KT]、楠、豫、章。顏師古注云︰豫即枕木，章即樟木。二木生至七年，乃可分別。觀此，則豫即《別錄》所謂釣樟者也。根似烏藥香，故又名烏樟。

【集解】弘景曰︰釣樟出桂陽、邵陵諸處，亦呼作烏樟。恭曰︰生郴州山谷。樹高丈餘。葉似楠葉而尖長，背有赤毛，若枇杷葉上毛。八月、九月採根皮，晒乾。

炳曰︰根似烏藥香。

藏器曰︰枕生南海山谷。作[KT]船，次於樟木。

根皮

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】金瘡止血，刮屑敷之，甚驗（《別錄》）。磨服，治霍亂（蕭炳）。治奔豚腳氣水腫，煎湯服。亦可浴瘡痍疥癬風瘙，並研末敷之（大明）。

莖葉
置門上，辟天行時氣（蕭炳）。
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Also called Wu Zhang ["black camphor"] (Tao Hongjing); [additional name illegible: likely a character from the Erya].

Li Shizhen states: There are two varieties of zhang [camphor tree] — large and small — and two colours — purple and pale. This plant is the smaller variety. According to Zheng Qiao's *Tongzhi*: Diao Zhang belongs to the camphor family and is the plant referred to in the *Erya* as "[ILLEGIBLE character], without blemish." Furthermore, in Sima Xiangru's rhapsody, the trees [ILLEGIBLE character], nan [Phoebe nanmu], yu, and zhang are listed together. Yan Shigu's commentary states: yu is the zhen [pillow] wood, and zhang is the camphor wood. The two trees cannot be distinguished until they have grown for seven years. From this it is clear that yu as described in the *Bielu* [Miscellaneous Records] is the same as Diao Zhang. Its root has an aroma resembling that of wu yao [Lindera aggregate], hence it is also called Wu Zhang ["black camphor"].

**Collected Explanations**
Tao Hongjing states: Diao Zhang grows in Guiyang, Shaoling, and surrounding areas; it is also called Wu Zhang.
Su Gong states: It grows in the mountain valleys of Chenzhou. The tree stands over one zhang [~3 m] tall. Its leaves resemble those of nan [Phoebe] but are more pointed and elongated, with reddish hairs on the undersides, like the hairs on the upper surface of loquat leaves. The root bark is collected in the eighth and ninth months and dried in the sun.

Xiao Bing states: The root has the fragrance of wu yao.

Chen Cangqi states: The zhen [pillow] tree grows in the mountain valleys of the South Seas. It is used to make [ILLEGIBLE: a type of] boats, being second in quality to camphor wood.

---
**Root Bark**

*Qi and Flavour:* Acrid [xin], warm [wen], non-toxic.

*Indications and Treatments:*
- For metal-weapon wounds [jin chuang] with bleeding: scrape off shavings and apply topically — extremely effective. (*Bielu*)
- Ground and taken orally, treats sudden turmoil illness [huo luan / acute cholera-like disorder]. (Xiao Bing)
- Treats running piglet disorder [ben tun], leg qi [jiao qi / beriberi], and water swelling [shui zhong / edema]: decoct and take orally. May also be used as a bath for sores, scabies, tinea, and wind-itch [feng sao]; alternatively grind into powder and apply topically. (Da Ming)

---
**Stems and Leaves**
Placed above the door, they ward off epidemic seasonal qi [tian xing shi qi / airborne pestilential influences]. (Xiao Bing)

segments.title_inferred: Huai Xiang / Douloupoxiang – An Aromatic Wood and Its Root
segments.original_text:
【釋名】兜婁婆香。

【集解】時珍曰︰香，江淮、湖嶺山中有之。木大者近葉青而長，有鋸齒，狀如小薊葉而香，對節生。其根狀如枸杞根而大，煨之甚香。《楞嚴經》云︰壇前

根

【氣味】苦，澀，平，無毒。

【主治】頭癤腫毒。碾末，麻脂調塗，七日腐落（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** Also called Douloupoxiang [兜婁婆香; a transliteration of a Sanskrit term, likely *dhūpa* or a related aromatic].

**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: This fragrant plant is found in the mountains of Jianghuai [the Yangtze–Huai River region] and Huling [Hunan/Lingnan area]. The larger trees have leaves that are greenish and elongated near the tips, with serrated edges, resembling the leaves of small thistle [小薊, *Cephalanoplos segetum*] and are fragrant; the leaves grow in opposite pairs at the nodes. The root resembles the root of wolfberry [枸杞, *Lycium chinense*] but is larger; when roasted over embers it is very fragrant. The *Śūraṃgama Sūtra* [《楞嚴經》] states: 'Before the altar—' [ILLEGIBLE: text appears truncated here; likely a quotation about burning this incense before a ritual altar].

**Root**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, astringent, neutral [平], non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment:** Furuncles and toxic swellings of the head. Grind to a powder, mix with hemp oil [麻脂] and apply topically; within seven days the necrotic tissue will slough off. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Bilì Xiāng (必栗香) — A Fragrant Mountain Wood
segments.original_text:
【釋名】花木香、詹香。

【集解】藏器曰︰必栗香生高山中。葉如老椿，搗置上流，魚悉暴腮而死。木白魚不損書也。

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】鬼疰心氣，斷一切惡氣，煮汁服之。燒為香，殺蟲、魚（藏器）。
segments.translation:
【Names】 Also known as Huā Mù Xiāng [Flower Wood Fragrance] and Zhān Xiāng [Zhān Fragrance].

【Collected Explanations】 [Chen] Cangqi states: Bìlì Xiāng grows in high mountains. Its leaves resemble those of the aged Chinese toon [Toona sinensis]. When pounded and placed in a stream above [a pool of fish], all the fish expose their gills and die. The wood [or: the white of the wood] does not harm fish used as bookmarks [i.e., it repels bookworms without damaging stored texts].

【Qi and Flavor】 Acrid [pungent], warm, non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】 [It treats] ghost-infixation [guì zhù, a condition of chronic wasting illness attributed to malevolent spirits] and heart-qi [disturbances]; it cuts off all malevolent qi — decoct and drink the juice. When burned as incense, it kills insects and fish. (Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Sweetgum Resin (Fēng Xiāng Zhī) — Entry from Chapter Wood I
segments.original_text:
【釋名】白膠香。

時珍曰︰楓樹枝弱善搖，故字從風。俗呼香楓。《金光明經》謂其香為須薩折羅婆香。

頌曰︰《爾雅》謂楓為攤，言風至則攤而鳴也。《梵書》謂之薩折羅婆香。

【集解】恭曰︰楓香脂，所在大山中皆有之。

頌曰︰今南方及關陝甚多。樹甚高碩，似白楊。葉圓而作歧，有三角而香。二月有花，白色。乃連著實，大如鴨卵。八月、九月熟時，曝乾可燒。《南方草木狀》云︰楓實惟九真有楓木，南中有楓子鬼。木之老者為人形，亦呼為靈楓，蓋瘤癭也。至今越巫可致靈異。

為楓木之林。《爾雅》注云︰其脂入地，千年為琥珀。

其木甚堅，有赤有白，白者細膩。其實成球，有否？孫炎《爾雅正義》云︰楓子鬼乃荀伯子《臨海記》所記。《宋齊丘化書》云︰老楓化為羽人。

【氣味】辛、苦，平，無毒。
辛，平，有小毒（蘇恭）。
有毒，食之令人笑不止，地漿解之。

【主治】癮疹風癢浮腫，煮水浴之。又主齒痛（《唐本》）。一切癰疽瘡疥，金瘡吐衄咯血，活血生肌，止痛解毒。燒過揩牙，永無牙疾（時珍）。水腫，下水氣，煮汁用之（蘇恭）。煎飲，止水痢為最（藏器）。止霍亂，刺風冷風，煎湯浴之。癰疽已成，擂酒飲，以滓貼之（時珍）。

【發明】震亨曰︰楓香屬金，有水與火。其性疏通故木易有蟲穴，為外科要藥。近世不知，誤以松脂之清瑩者為之，甚謬。

宗奭曰︰楓香、松脂，皆可亂乳香。但楓香微白黃色，燒之可見真偽。

時珍曰︰楓香、松脂皆可亂乳香，其功雖次於乳香，而亦仿佛不遠。
segments.translation:
**[Nomenclature]** Also known as Báijiāo Xiāng [White Gum Resin].

Li Shizhen states: The branches of the fēng [sweetgum] tree are slender and readily sway, hence the character is written with the element for 'wind' [風]. It is commonly called Xiāng Fēng [Fragrant Maple/Sweetgum]. The *Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra* [Golden Light Sutra] refers to its fragrance as 'Susajjara-bha incense.'

Su Song states: The *Erya* calls the fēng tree [a character indicating swaying], signifying that when the wind comes it sways and makes a sound. The Sanskrit texts call it 'Sajjara-bha incense.'

**[Collected Explanations]** Su Gong states: Sweetgum resin [Fēng xiāng zhī] is found throughout the great mountains wherever the tree grows.

Su Song states: Today it is very abundant in the southern regions and in the Guān and Shǎn areas. The tree is very tall and large, resembling the white poplar [Populus alba]. Its leaves are rounded and lobed, with three-pointed angles, and are fragrant. In the second month it produces white flowers. The fruits are borne in clusters, as large as a duck's egg. When they ripen in the eighth or ninth month, they may be sun-dried and burned as incense. The *Nanfang Caomu Zhuang* [Accounts of Plants of the Southern Regions] states: As for the fruit of the sweetgum, only in Jiūzhēn [northern Vietnam] does the sweetgum grow; in the southern regions there are 'fēng-child spirits' [fēngzǐ guǐ]. Very old trees take on human forms and are also called 'spirit sweetgums' [líng fēng]—these are in fact tumorous galls [liú yǐng]. To this day, the shamans of Yuè can invoke strange powers from them.

[A passage describes] a forest of sweetgum trees. The commentary to the *Erya* states: When the resin enters the ground, after a thousand years it becomes amber [hǔpò].

The wood is very hard; there are red and white varieties, with the white being finer-grained. The fruit forms a sphere. Sun Yan's *Erya Zhengyi* [Correct Meaning of the Erya] states that the 'fēng-child spirit' is the same entity recorded by Xún Bózi in the *Linhai Ji* [Records of Linhai]. The *Huashu* [Book of Transformation] attributed to Song Qi-qiu states: An aged sweetgum transforms into a feathered immortal [yǔrén].

**[Flavor and Nature]** Acrid and bitter; balanced [píng]; non-toxic.
Acrid; balanced; slightly toxic (Su Gong).
Toxic: ingesting it causes uncontrollable laughter; this is antidoted by earth-wash [dì jiāng, a preparation of water stirred with earth].

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Urticaria [yǐnzhěn], wind-itching [fēng yǎng], and edema [fú zhǒng]: boil in water and use as a bath. Also indicated for toothache. (*Tangben* [Tang Materia Medica])
- All manner of abscesses [yōng jū], sores and scabies [chuāng jiè], metal-wound bleeding [jīn chuāng], vomiting of blood, nosebleed, and coughing of blood: invigorates the blood, promotes tissue regeneration [shēng jī], relieves pain, and resolves toxins. Burning the resin and rubbing the teeth with the ash will permanently prevent dental disease. (Li Shizhen)
- Edema [shuǐ zhǒng], to discharge water-qi [xià shuǐ qì]: cook the juice and use it internally. (Su Gong)
- Decoct and drink: most effective for stopping watery dysentery [shuǐ lì]. (Zang Qi [Chen Cangqi])
- Stops cholera [huòluàn]; for piercing wind [cì fēng] and cold wind [lěng fēng]: decoct and use as a bath.
- For established abscess [yōng jū yǐ chéng]: grind into liquor and drink; apply the dregs topically as a poultice. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elaboration]** Zhu Zhenheng states: Sweetgum resin belongs to the Metal phase and partakes of both Water and Fire. Its nature is dispersing and penetrating, which is why the wood is easily riddled with insect holes; it is an important medicine for external [surgical] medicine [wàikē]. In recent times this has not been understood, and people mistakenly substitute the clear, translucent variety of pine resin for it — a serious error.

Kou Zongshi states: Sweetgum resin and pine resin can both be used to adulterate frankincense [rǔ xiāng]. However, sweetgum resin is slightly whitish-yellow in color; burning it will reveal whether it is genuine or not.

Li Shizhen states: Both sweetgum resin and pine resin can be used to adulterate frankincense. Although their efficacy is somewhat inferior to frankincense, it is not far removed.

segments.title_inferred: Myrrh (Mòyào) — Properties, Origins, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】末藥。

時珍曰︰沒、末皆梵言。

【集解】志曰︰沒藥生波斯國。其塊大小不定，黑色，似安息香。

頌曰︰今海南諸國及廣州或有之。木之根株皆如橄欖，葉青而密。歲久者，則有脂液流滴在地下，凝結成塊，或大或小，亦類安息香。采無時。

曰︰按︰徐表《南州記》云︰是波斯松脂也。狀如神香。

時珍曰︰按︰《一統志》云︰沒藥樹高碩如松，皮濃一、二寸。采時掘樹下為坎，用斧伐其皮之誤爾。所謂神香者，不知何物也？

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

【主治】破血止痛，療金瘡杖瘡，諸惡瘡痔漏，猝下血，目中翳暈痛膚赤（《開寶》）。破症瘕宿血，損傷瘀血，消腫痛（大明）。心膽虛，肝血不足（好古）。墮胎，及產後心腹血氣痛，並入丸散服（李珣）。

【發明】權曰︰凡金刃所傷，打損跌墜馬調服。推陳致新，能生好血。

急，經絡滿急故痛且腫。凡打撲跌，皆傷經絡，氣血不行，瘀壅作腫痛也。

活血，沒藥散血，皆能止痛消腫生肌。故二藥每每相兼而用。
segments.translation:
【Explanation of Names】
Also called Mòyào [末藥]. Li Shizhen states: both 沒 (mò) and 末 (mò) are Sanskrit-derived terms.

【Collected Commentaries】
Zhi [Su Jing] states: Mòyào originates from Persia [Bōsī]. Its lumps vary in size, are black in color, and resemble benzoin [ānxī xiāng].

Su Song states: Today it can be found in the various countries of the South Seas [Hǎinán zhū guó] and sometimes in Guangzhou. The roots and trunks of the tree resemble those of the olive [gǎnlǎn]; the leaves are green and dense. In older trees, a resinous liquid drips down and congeals on the ground into lumps, some large and some small, also similar in appearance to benzoin. There is no fixed season for harvesting.

[Another commentator] states: According to Xu Biao's Nánzhōu Jì [Records of the Southern Regions]: it is the pine resin of Persia, and its appearance resembles shén xiāng ["divine incense"].

Li Shizhen states: According to the Yītǒng Zhì [Comprehensive Gazetteer], the myrrh tree is tall and stout like a pine, with bark one to two inches thick. When harvesting, a pit is dug beneath the tree and an axe is used to cut the bark — [any other account] would be in error. As for the so-called shén xiāng, it is unknown what substance that refers to.

【Qi and Flavor】
Bitter; balanced [píng]; non-toxic.

【Principal Indications】
Breaks up blood stasis and relieves pain; treats wounds from blades and wounds from beating [jīn chuāng, zhàng chuāng]; all manner of malignant sores, hemorrhoids, and fistulas; sudden rectal bleeding; corneal opacity with dizziness, pain, and redness of the eye surface (Kāibǎo Běncǎo). Breaks up abdominal masses [zhèng jiǎ] and old stagnant blood, disperses blood stasis from trauma, reduces swelling and pain (Dà Míng). Deficiency of the heart and gallbladder, insufficiency of liver blood (Hǎogǔ). Induces abortion; also treats postpartum heart and abdominal pain due to blood and qi — all to be taken in pill or powder form (Li Xun).

【Elaboration on Properties】
Quán [Zhen Quan] states: For all wounds from blades or injuries from falls from horses, [myrrh should be] mixed and taken [orally]. It expels the old and promotes the new, and is capable of generating healthy blood.

[The text is fragmentary here:] When [the channels are] constricted and the channels and collaterals are full and taut, there is pain and swelling. All instances of striking, stumbling, and falling injure the channels and collaterals; when qi and blood cannot flow, stasis accumulates and produces swelling and pain.

[Rǔxiāng] quickens the blood; mòyào disperses the blood — both are capable of relieving pain, reducing swelling, and promoting tissue regeneration. For this reason, these two medicinals are very frequently used together.

segments.title_inferred: Qijie / Blood Dragon's Blood Resin (騏竭)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】血竭。

時珍曰：騏竭亦馬名也。此物如乾血，故謂之血竭。曰騏竭者，隱之也。舊與紫鉚同條，紫鉚乃此樹

【集解】恭曰：騏竭樹名渴留，紫鉚樹名渴廩，二物大同小異。

志曰：二物同條，功效全別。紫鉚色赤而黑，其葉大如盤，鉚從葉上出。騏竭色黃而赤，從木中出，如松脂。

曰：按：《南越志》云：騏竭，是紫鉚樹之脂也。欲驗真偽，但嚼之不爛如蠟者為上。

木中流出，滴下如膠飴狀，久而堅凝，乃成竭，赤作血色。采無時。舊說與紫鉚大都相類，而別是一物，功力亦殊。曰：凡使勿用海母血，真相似，只是味鹹並腥氣。騏竭味微鹹、甘，似梔子氣也。時珍曰：騏竭是樹脂，紫鉚是蟲造。按：《一統志》云：血竭樹略如沒藥樹，其肌赤色。采法亦於樹下掘坎，斧伐其樹，脂流於坎，旬日取之。多出大食諸國。今人試之，以透指甲者為真。

獨孤滔《丹房鏡源》云：此物出於西胡，稟熒惑之氣而結。以火燒之，有赤汁涌出，久而灰不變本色者，為真也。

【氣味】甘、鹹，平，無毒。

【主治】心腹猝痛，金瘡血出，破積血，止痛生肌，去五臟邪氣。一切疼痛，血氣攪刺，內傷血聚，補虛，並宜酒服。益陽精，消陰滯氣（大明）。散滯血諸痛，婦人血氣。

【發明】時珍曰：騏竭，木之脂液，如人之膏血，其味甘鹹而走血，蓋手、足厥陰藥也。肝與心包皆主血故爾。河間劉氏云：血竭除血痛，為和血之聖藥，是矣。乳香、沒藥雖主血病，而兼入氣分，此則專於血分者也。
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also known as Xuejie [Blood Exhaustion / Blood Resin].

Li Shizhen states: Qí (騏) is also the name of a horse. This substance resembles dried blood, and is therefore called Xuejie [Blood Resin]. The name Qijie is a veiled or allusive designation. It was formerly listed in the same entry as Zìkuǎng [lac/kermes resin]; Zikuang comes from the same tree [ILLEGIBLE: the sentence appears cut off in the source].

**[Collected Explanations]** Su Gong states: The tree of Qijie is called 'Keliu' [渴留], and the tree of Zikuang is called 'Kelin' [渴廩]. The two substances are broadly similar but differ in detail.

Zhang Ji states: Although the two substances are listed in the same entry, their therapeutic effects are entirely different. Zikuang is red and blackish in color, with leaves as large as a platter, and the kuang [lac] exudes from the leaves. Qijie is yellow-red in color, issues from within the wood, and resembles pine resin.

[Another authority] states: According to the Nanyue Zhi [Record of the Southern Yue], Qijie is the resinous sap of the Zikuang tree. To test for authenticity, chew it: if it does not break down but remains waxy, it is of superior quality.

It flows out from within the wood, dripping down in a glutinous, syrup-like state; over time it hardens and congeals into a solid mass, turning a blood-red color. It may be harvested at any time of year. According to older accounts, it is broadly similar to Zikuang, yet is a distinct substance with different therapeutic properties. [Lei Xiao] states: When using this drug, do not substitute sea-creature blood [haomuxue], which closely resembles it but has a salty and fishy taste. Qijie has a slightly salty, sweet flavor, with an aroma reminiscent of Zhizi [gardenia fruit].

Li Shizhen states: Qijie is a tree resin, whereas Zikuang is produced by insects. According to the Yitong Zhi [Comprehensive Gazetteer], the blood-resin tree resembles the myrrh tree somewhat; its heartwood is red in color. The method of harvesting is also similar: a pit is dug at the base of the tree, the tree is cut with an axe, and the resin flows into the pit; after about ten days it is collected. It is produced chiefly in the countries of the Dashi [Arabia and greater Islamic world]. People today test its authenticity by pressing it against a fingernail: if it is transparent [i.e., stains through], it is genuine.

Dugu Tao, in his Danfang Jingyuan [Mirror Source of Alchemical Formulas], writes: This substance originates in the Western Hu [Central/Western Asia], and is formed by the condensed qi of Mars [Yinghuo, the 'Fitful Glare']. When burned, a red fluid gushes forth; if after long burning the ash still retains its original color, it is genuine.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Sweet, salty; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

**[Primary Therapeutic Uses]** Sudden pain in the heart and abdomen; bleeding from metal-weapon wounds [traumatic lacerations]; breaking up and dispersing congealed blood; relieving pain and promoting the regeneration of flesh [muscle tissue]; expelling evil qi from the five viscera. For all manner of pain, disturbance and stabbing sensations from blood and qi, internal injury with blood accumulation, and vacuity deficiency — all are suitable for administration in wine. Augments yang essence and disperses obstructed yin qi (Da Ming). Disperses stagnant blood and various pain conditions; [treats] women's blood and qi [disorders].

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: Qijie is the resinous fluid of a tree, analogous to the fat and blood of the human body. Its flavor is sweet and salty, and it moves into the blood [aspect]. It is a medicinal that enters the Hand and Foot Jueyin [channels, i.e., the Pericardium and Liver]. Since the Liver and the Pericardium both govern blood, this is why it acts there. Liu Wansu of Hejian states: Blood resin [Xuejie] eliminates blood pain and is the paramount medicine for harmonizing the blood — and indeed this is so. Although frankincense [ruxiang] and myrrh [moyao] also address blood disorders, they additionally enter the qi aspect; this substance, by contrast, is exclusively specialized for the blood aspect.

segments.title_inferred: Zhihan (質汗) — A Compound Western Drug
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰汗音寒，番語也

【集解】藏器曰︰質汗出西番，煎檉乳、松淚、甘草、地黃並熱血成之。番人試藥，以小兒斷一足，以藥納口中，將足踏之，當時能走者良。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】金瘡傷折，瘀血內損，補筋肉，消惡血，下血氣，婦人產後諸血結，腹痛內冷不下食。並以酒消服之，亦敷病處（藏器）。
segments.translation:
【Explanation of Names】Li Shizhen states: The character 汗 is pronounced 'han' [like 寒, hán], and is a word from the Western Barbarian [fan] languages.

【Collected Explanations】[Chen] Zangqi states: Zhihan comes from the Western Regions [Xifan]. It is made by decocting the resin of the tamarisk [cheng ru], pine resin [song lei], licorice root [gancao], and rehmannia root [dihuang], together with warm blood. The people of the Western Regions test the drug as follows: they sever a foot from a child, place the drug in the child's mouth, and have the child walk on the severed foot — if the child can walk immediately, the drug is considered good [effective].

【Qi and Flavor】Sweet, warm, non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatment】Sword and knife wounds [jinchuang], fractures and injuries, internal damage from blood stasis [yu xue], supplementing sinews and flesh, dispersing pathological blood [e xue], moving blood and qi downward, all manner of blood accumulations after childbirth in women, abdominal pain with internal cold and inability to eat. In all cases it is dissolved in wine and taken orally; it may also be applied topically to the affected area. (Zangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Benzoin Resin (Anxixiang) — Properties, Origins, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰此香辟惡，安息諸邪，故名。或云︰安息，國名也。梵書謂之拙貝羅香。

【集解】恭曰︰安息香出西戎。狀如松脂，黃黑色，為塊。新者亦柔軟。

曰︰生南海波斯國，樹中脂也。狀羊桃。秋月採之。

禹錫曰︰按︰段成式《酉陽雜俎》云︰安息香樹出波斯國，呼為辟邪樹。長二、三丈，皮色黃黑。葉有四角，經寒不凋。二月開花黃色，花心微碧。不結實。刻其樹皮，其膠如飴，名安息香，六、七月堅凝乃取之。燒之，通神明，辟眾惡。

時珍曰︰今安南、三佛齊諸地皆有之。《一統志》云︰樹如苦楝，大而且直。葉似羊桃而長。木心有脂作香。葉廷珪《香錄》云︰此乃樹脂，形色類胡桃瓤。不宜於燒，而能發眾香，故人取以和香。今人和香有如餳者，謂之安息油。機曰︰或言燒之能集鼠者為真。

【氣味】辛、苦，平，無毒。

【主治】心腹惡氣，鬼疰（《唐本》）。邪氣魍魎，鬼胎血邪，辟蠱毒，霍亂風痛，男子遺精，暖腎氣，婦人血噤，並產後血暈（大明）。婦人夜夢鬼交，同臭黃合為丸，燒熏丹穴，永斷（李珣）。燒之，去鬼來神。
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Li Shizhen states: This fragrance [香] wards off malevolent influences and pacifies [安息] all manner of evil spirits, hence the name. Some say 'Anxi' is a country name. In Sanskrit texts it is called 'Zhuóbèiluó fragrance' [拙貝羅香].

**[Collected Explanations]** Su Gong states: Anxixiang comes from the Western Rong [i.e., western foreign lands]. It resembles pine resin in appearance, is yellow-black in color, and forms lumps. Freshly gathered specimens are also soft and pliable.

[Another source] states: It grows in Persia [Bōsī guó] in the Southern Seas; it is the resin of a tree. Its appearance resembles yangтао [carambola/goat peach]. It is harvested in autumn.

Yu Xi states: According to Duan Chengshi's 'Youyang Zazu': The Anxixiang tree grows in Persia and is called the 'Bìxié tree' [evil-averting tree]. It grows to a height of two to three zhang [roughly 6–10 metres]. Its bark is yellow-black. The leaves are four-cornered and do not fall in cold weather. It blooms yellow flowers in the second month, with flower centres of a faint blue-green. It bears no fruit. When the bark is incised, the resin that flows out resembles maltose syrup; this is called Anxixiang. In the sixth or seventh month, when it has hardened and congealed, it is collected. When burned, it communicates with the divine spirits and repels all evil.

Li Shizhen states: Today it is found in Annam [northern Vietnam], Srivijaya [Sanfoqi], and other regions. The 'Yitong Zhi' [Comprehensive Gazetteer] records: The tree resembles the Chinaberry [kǔliàn], being large and straight. Its leaves resemble yangтао but are longer. The heartwood contains a resin that serves as the fragrance. Ye Tinggui's 'Xianglu' [Record of Fragrances] states: This is a tree resin whose form and color resemble walnut kernel flesh. It is not well suited to being burned alone, but it has the ability to bring out the qualities of other fragrances; therefore people take it to blend incenses. Those who blend incense today sometimes encounter a variety resembling thick syrup, which is called Anxi oil [Anxiyou]. [Wang] Ji states: Some say that if, when burned, it attracts mice, it is genuine.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Acrid [辛] and bitter [苦]; balanced [平]; non-toxic.

**[Principal Treatments]**
- Malevolent [evil] qi in the chest and abdomen; ghost-possession illness [guǐzhù] (Tang Bencao).
- Evil qi, wàngliǎng spirits [demons], ghost-fetus blood-evil, warding off gǔ poisoning [蠱毒], cholera [霍亂], wind pain, seminal emission in men, warming kidney qi, blood-clenching [blood stasis] in women, and postpartum blood dizziness [blood collapse after delivery] (Da Ming).
- For women who dream nightly of intercourse with ghosts: combine with foul sulfur [臭黃, orpiment/sulfur] to make pills, burn and fumigate the cinnabar cavity [dān xué, a bodily cavity]; this permanently stops the condition (Li Xun).
- When burned, it expels ghosts and invites divine spirits.

segments.title_inferred: Storax / Styrax (Suhexiang) — Origins, Properties, and Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰按︰郭義恭《廣志》云︰此香出蘇合國，因以名之。

【集解】《別錄》曰︰蘇合香出中台川谷。

恭曰︰今從西域及昆侖來，紫赤色，與紫真檀相似，堅實極芳香，惟重如石，燒之灰白者好。

頌曰︰今廣州雖有蘇合香，但類蘇木，無香氣。藥中只用如膏油者，極芬烈。陶隱居以為獅子矢者，亦是指此膏油者言之爾。《梁書》云︰中天竺國出蘇合香，是諸香汁煎成，非自然一物也。又云︰大秦國人采得蘇合香，先煎其汁以為香膏，乃賣其滓與諸國賈人。是以展轉來達中國者，不大香也。然則廣南貨者，其經煎煮之餘乎？今用如膏油者，乃合治成者爾。

無滓者為上。葉廷珪《香譜》云︰蘇合香油出大食國。氣味皆類篤耨香。沈括《筆談》云︰今之蘇合香赤色如堅木，又有蘇合油如膠，人多用之。而劉夢得《傳信方》言︰蘇合香多薄葉，子如金色，按之即少，放之即起，亦是油也，不必致疑。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】辟惡，殺鬼精物，溫瘧、蠱毒、癇痙，去三蟲，除邪，令人無夢魘。久服，通神明，輕身長年（《別錄》）。

【發明】時珍曰︰蘇合香氣竄，能通諸竅臟腑，故其功能辟一切不正之氣。按︰沈括《筆談》云︰太尉王文正公氣羸多病。宋真宗面賜藥酒一瓶，令空腹飲之，可以和氣血，辟外邪。公飲之，大覺安健。次日稱謝。上曰︰此蘇合香酒也。每酒一斗，入蘇合香丸一兩同煮。極能調和五臟，卻腹中諸疾。每冒寒夙興，則宜飲一杯。自此臣庶之家皆仿為之，此方盛行於時。其方本出唐玄宗《開元廣濟方》，謂之白朮丸。後人亦編入《千金》、《外台》，治疾有殊效。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen states: According to Guo Yigong's *Guangzhi* [Extensive Records]: this fragrance comes from the land of Suhé [Sogdia or a western region], and was named after it.

**Collected Commentaries**
The *Bielu* [Supplementary Records] states: Suhexiang [storax/liquidambar resin] comes from the river valleys of Zhongtai.

Kong [Su Gong] states: It now arrives from the Western Regions and Kunlun. It is purple-red in color, resembling purple sandalwood [zǐ zhēntán], solid, and extremely fragrant; it is as heavy as stone. That which, when burned, leaves white-gray ash is considered the finest quality.

Su Song states: Although Guangzhou now produces suhexiang, it merely resembles suwood [sumu] and has no fragrance. In medicine, only the type resembling an oily paste is used, which is intensely aromatic. When Tao Hongjing [Tao Yinju] identified it as lion's dung, he too was referring to this oily paste form. The *Liangshu* [Book of Liang] states: The kingdom of Central India [Zhong Tianzhu] produces suhexiang, which is made by boiling the juices of various fragrant substances together — it is not a single natural substance. It also states: The people of Daqin [the Roman/Byzantine East] collect suhexiang, first boil down its juice to make a fragrant paste, and then sell the residue to merchants of other nations. Thus, what eventually reaches China by roundabout routes is not very fragrant. Is it not likely, then, that what is sold in Guangnan is the remnant after boiling? What is currently used in the form of an oily paste is something compounded and processed.

That which is free of dregs is the finest. Ye Tinggui's *Xiangpu* [Treatise on Fragrances] states: Suhexiang oil comes from the land of Dashi [Arabia]. Its aroma and flavor are similar to those of dunouixiang [elemi resin]. Shen Kuo's *Mengxi Bitan* [Dream Pool Essays] states: The suhexiang of today is red, like hard wood; there is also suhexiang oil, resembling glue, which is widely used. Meanwhile, Liu Mengde's [Liu Yuxi's] *Chuanxin Fang* [Tried-and-True Prescriptions] says: Suhexiang has many thin leaves and seeds of golden color; when pressed they diminish, and when released they spring back — this too refers to the oil form, so there is no need for doubt.

**Qi and Flavor**
Sweet; warm; non-toxic.

**Principal Indications**
Wards off malevolent [influences]; kills demonic spirits and noxious entities; [treats] warm malaria [wēn nüè], gu-poison [gǔdú], epileptic convulsions [xián jìng]; expels the three worms [sān chóng]; removes evil [influences]; prevents nightmares and oppressive dreams. Long-term use opens communication with the divine, lightens the body, and extends one's years (*Bielu*).

**Elaboration**
Li Shizhen states: The qi [vapors] of suhexiang are penetrating and able to open all the orifices and viscera; therefore its function is to repel all deviant and improper qi. According to Shen Kuo's *Mengxi Bitan*: Grand Preceptor Wang Wenzheng was weak in qi and suffered from many ailments. Emperor Zhenzong of the Song personally bestowed on him a bottle of medicinal wine, instructing him to drink it on an empty stomach, saying it could harmonize qi and blood and repel external evil. Upon drinking it, Wang felt greatly refreshed and healthy. The next day he expressed his gratitude. The Emperor said: 'This is suhexiang wine. For every dou [approx. 10 liters] of wine, add one liang of Suhexiang Pills [蘇合香丸] and boil them together. It is highly effective at harmonizing the five viscera and curing all abdominal ailments. Whenever one rises early in cold weather, one should drink a cup.' From that time on, households of officials and commoners alike imitated the practice, and this prescription became widely popular. The original formula came from Emperor Xuanzong of Tang's *Kaiyuan Guangji Fang* [Broadly Beneficial Prescriptions of the Kaiyuan Era], where it was called the Baizhu Pill [White Atractylodes Pill]. Later compilers also included it in the *Qianjin* [Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold] and *Waitai* [Arcane Essentials from the Imperial Library], where it proved remarkably effective in treating illness.

segments.title_inferred: Zhantang Incense (Zhan Sugar Fragrance) — aromatic resin entry
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰詹言其粘，糖言其狀也。

【集解】弘景曰︰出晉安、岑州。上真淳者難得，多以其皮及蠹蟲屎雜之，惟軟者為佳。

皆合香家要用，不正入藥。

恭曰︰詹糖樹似橘。煎枝葉為香，似沙糖而黑。出交廣以南，生晉安。近方多用之。

時珍曰︰其花亦香，如茉莉花香氣。

【氣味】苦，微溫，無毒。

【主治】風水毒腫，去惡氣伏尸（《別錄》）。治惡核惡瘡和胡桃、青皮搗，塗發令黑如漆（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** Li Shizhen states: 'Zhan [詹] refers to its stickiness; tang [糖, sugar] refers to its form [appearance resembling sugar].'

**Collected Explanations:** Tao Hongjing states: It comes from Jin'an and Cenzhou. The finest, pure grade is difficult to obtain; it is frequently adulterated with the bark of the tree and the excrement of wood-boring insects [蠹蟲]. Only the soft variety is considered superior. It is chiefly employed by incense blenders [合香家] and does not properly enter into medicinal use.

Su Gong states: The zhantang tree resembles the tangerine [orange] tree. The branches and leaves are decocted to produce the fragrance, which resembles brown sugar [沙糖] but is black in color. It originates from the regions south of Jiao and Guang, and grows in Jin'an. It is frequently used in recent prescriptions.

Li Shizhen states: Its flowers are also fragrant, with an aroma similar to that of jasmine [茉莉] flowers.

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, slightly warm, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Wind-water toxic swelling [風水毒腫]; eliminates malign qi [惡氣] and 'hidden corpse' [伏尸] conditions. (Source: *Mingyi Bielu* [《別錄》])
- Treats malign nodules [惡核] and malign sores [惡瘡]: pound together with walnut [胡桃] and green tangerine peel [青皮] and apply to the hair to blacken it like lacquer. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Dùnòu Fragrant Resin (Dammara/Daunao Resin)
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰︰篤耨香出真臘國，樹之脂也。樹如松形，其香老則溢出，色白而透明者名白篤耨，盛夏不融，香氣清遠。土人取後，夏月以火炙樹，令脂液再溢，至冬乃凝，復收之。其香夏融冬結，以瓠瓢盛，置陰涼處，乃得不融。雜以樹皮者則色黑，名黑篤耨，為下品。

【氣味】缺

【主治】面黧。同白附子、冬瓜子、白芨、石榴皮等分，為末，酒浸三日，洗面後敷之。久則面瑩如玉（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: Dùnòu [篤耨] fragrance comes from the kingdom of Zhēnlà [真臘, i.e., Cambodia/Khmer]. It is the resin of a tree. The tree resembles a pine in form. When the resin is mature it overflows naturally; that which is white in color and transparent is called White Dùnòu [白篤耨]. It does not melt even in the height of summer, and its fragrance is clear and far-reaching. After the local people have harvested it, during the summer months they scorch the tree with fire to make the resinous liquid flow again; by winter it congeals and they collect it once more. This resin melts in summer and solidifies in winter. It should be stored in a gourd ladle and kept in a cool, shaded place so that it does not melt. That which is mixed with tree bark turns black in color and is called Black Dùnòu [黑篤耨]; this is the inferior grade.

**Flavor and Nature:** [Entry absent / missing]

**Principal Indications:** Dark, sallow complexion [面黧]. Combined in equal parts with White Typhonium Rhizome [白附子, Typhonium giganteum], Winter Melon Seed [冬瓜子, Benincasa hispida], Bletilla Rhizome [白芨, Bletilla striata], and Pomegranate Rind [石榴皮, Punica granatum], grind into a powder, soak in wine for three days, then apply to the face after washing. With prolonged use the face will become lustrous as jade. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Camphor (Zhangnao) — Properties, Preparation, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】韶腦。

【集解】時珍曰︰樟腦出韶州、漳州。狀似龍腦，白色如雪，樟樹脂膏也。胡演升《煉方》云︰煎樟腦法︰用樟木新者切片，以井水浸三日三夜，入鍋煎之，柳木頻攪。待汁減半，柳上有白霜，即濾去滓，傾汁入瓦盆內。經宿，自然結成塊也。他處雖有樟木，不解取腦。

又煉樟腦法︰用銅盆，以陳壁土為粉糝之，卻糝樟腦一重，又糝壁土，如此四、五重。以薄荷安土上，再用一盆覆之，黃泥封固，於火上款款炙之。須以意度之，不可太過、不及。勿令走氣。候冷取出，則腦皆升於上盆，如此升兩、三次，可充片腦也。

【氣味】辛，熱，無毒。

【主治】通關竅，利滯氣，治中惡邪氣，霍亂心腹痛，寒濕腳氣，疥癬風瘙，齲齒，殺蟲辟蠹。著鞋中，去腳氣（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰樟腦純陽，與焰硝同性，水中生火，其焰益熾。

今丹爐及煙火家多用之。辛熱香竄，稟龍火之氣，去濕殺蟲，此其所長。故燒煙熏衣筐席簟，能辟壁虱、蟲蛀。

間，用帛繃定，月餘甚妙。王璽《醫林集要》方︰治腳氣腫痛。用樟腦二兩，烏頭三兩，為末，醋糊丸彈子大。每置一丸於足心踏之，下以微火烘之，衣被圍覆，汗出如涎為效。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Also called Shaonao [Shao camphor].

**Collected Commentaries**
Li Shizhen says: Camphor [zhangnao] is produced in Shaozhou and Zhangzhou. In appearance it resembles borneol [longnao], white as snow; it is the resinous essence of the camphor tree [zhang tree]. Hu Yansheng's *Lianfang* [Refinement Formulary] states the method for decocting camphor: Take fresh-cut slices of camphor wood, soak them in well water for three days and three nights, then place them in a pot and decoct. Stir frequently with a willow-wood implement. When the liquid has reduced by half and a white frost appears on the willow stirrer, filter out the dregs and pour the liquid into an earthenware basin. After one night, the camphor will naturally crystallize into a solid mass. Although camphor trees grow in other localities, the people there do not know how to extract the camphor.

A further method for refining camphor: Use a copper basin and dust it with powdered aged wall-earth [chen bi tu]; then spread a layer of camphor over it, then another layer of wall-earth, repeating this for four or five layers. Place mint [bohe] on top of the earth layer, cover with a second basin, seal firmly with yellow clay, and heat gently over a fire. One must judge the heat carefully — neither too much nor too little — and take care not to let the vapors escape. When cool, remove the contents; the camphor will have all sublimed up into the upper basin. Repeating this sublimation two or three times yields a product that can pass for borneol flakes [piannao].

**Flavor and Nature**
Acrid, hot, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment**
Opens the orifices and passes obstructed qi; treats invasion of malign and evil qi, cholera [huoluan] with heart and abdominal pain, cold-damp leg qi [jiaoqi / beriberi], scabies [jie], ringworm [xuan], wind-itch [feng sao], dental caries [quchi], and kills parasites and repels book-boring insects [du]. Placed inside shoes, it dispels leg qi [jiaoqi]. (Li Shizhen)

**Elaboration**
Li Shizhen says: Camphor is of pure yang nature and shares properties with saltpeter [yan xiao / potassium nitrate] — it ignites fire even in water, and its flame burns all the more intensely. It is therefore much used by alchemical furnace workers and fireworks makers today. Acrid, hot, and aromatically penetrating, it partakes of the qi of dragon-fire [long huo], and its strengths lie in dispelling dampness and killing parasites. For this reason, burning camphor smoke to fumigate clothing, baskets, mats, and bamboo-woven bedding [xi dan] effectively repels bedbugs [bi shi] and wood-boring insects.

[Fragmentary passage:] ...secured with silk cloth binding; after more than a month, the results are excellent.

Wang Xi's *Yilin Jiyao* [Essential Compendium of the Medical Grove] provides a formula for treating swollen and painful leg qi [jiaoqi]: Use two liang of camphor and three liang of aconite [wutou]; grind to a powder and form into balls the size of a bullet [dan zi] using a vinegar-and-paste binder. Each time, place one ball at the center of the sole of the foot and stand on it; heat gently from below with a mild fire, and wrap the body in clothing and bedding. When sweating occurs like drool [xian], the treatment has taken effect.

segments.title_inferred: Aloe (Lúhuì) — Identity, Properties, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】奴會（《開寶》）、訥會（《拾遺》）、時珍曰︰名義未詳。

藏器曰︰俗呼為象膽，以其味苦如膽也。

【集解】曰︰蘆薈生頌曰︰今惟廣州有來者。其木生山野中，滴脂淚而成。采之不拘時月。

時珍曰︰蘆薈原在草部。《藥譜》及《圖經》所狀，皆言是木脂。而《一統志》云︰爪哇、三佛齊諸國所出者，乃草屬，狀如鱟尾，采之以玉器搗成膏。與前說不同，何哉？豈亦木質草形乎？

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。

【主治】熱風煩悶，胸膈間熱氣，明目鎮心，小兒癲癇驚風，療五疳，殺三蟲及痔病瘡，解巴豆毒（《開寶》）。主小兒諸疳熱（李權）。研末，敷齒。

【發明】時珍曰︰蘆薈，乃厥陰經藥也，其功專於殺蟲清熱。以上諸病，皆熱與蟲所生故也。

浸炙
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Also known as Nú Huì (《Kāibǎo》) and Nà Huì (《Shíyí》). Li Shizhen states: the meaning of the name is not clearly known.

Chen Cangqi states: It is commonly called 'elephant gall' [xiàng dǎn], because its flavor is as bitter as gall.

**[Collected Explanations]**
[Su Song] states: Lúhuì comes today only from Guangzhou. The tree grows in mountain wilds, and [the drug] is formed from drops of resinous sap [lit. dripping fatty tears]. It may be gathered at any time of year without restriction.

Li Shizhen states: Lúhuì was originally classified under the Herb [grass] section. The descriptions in the Yào Pǔ [Pharmacopoeia] and the Tú Jīng [Illustrated Canon] both describe it as a tree resin. However, the Yītǒng Zhì [Comprehensive Gazetteer] records that what comes from Java [Zhǎowā] and Srivijaya [Sānfóqí] and other such countries is of the herbaceous kind, shaped like the tail of a horseshoe crab [hòu], and is gathered and pounded into a paste using jade implements. This differs from the earlier account — how can this be? Could it perhaps have woody substance but herbaceous form?

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Bitter; cold; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Heat-wind [rè fēng] agitation and oppression; heat qi [heat sensation] in the chest and diaphragm; brightening the eyes and calming the heart; childhood epilepsy and fright-wind [jīng fēng]; treating the five types of gan [nutritional deficiency disorders]; killing the three worms [intestinal parasites] and treating hemorrhoidal sores; counteracting the toxicity of croton [bā dòu] (《Kāibǎo》). Indicated for all manner of gan-heat disorders in children (Li Quan). Grind to powder and apply to the teeth [and gums].

**[Elucidation]**
Li Shizhen states: Lúhuì is a medicinal of the Jué Yīn channel [Liver and Pericardium meridians]. Its efficacy is focused specifically on killing worms and clearing heat. All of the ailments listed above arise from heat and worms, hence its application.

[Fragment: 'soaked and roasted' — preparation note, context incomplete]

segments.title_inferred: Hu Tong Lei (Poplar resin / alkaline exudate of the Central Asian poplar)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】胡桐鹼（《綱目》）、胡桐律。

曰︰胡桐淚，是胡桐樹脂也，故名淚。作律字者非也，律、淚聲訛爾。

時珍曰︰《西域傳》云︰車師國多胡桐。顏師古注云︰胡桐似桐，不似桑，故名胡桐。

蟲食其樹而汁出下流者，俗名胡桐淚，言似眼淚也。其入土石成塊如鹵鹼者，為胡桐鹼（音減）。或云︰律當作瀝，非訛也，猶松脂名瀝青之義。亦通。

【集解】恭曰︰胡桐淚，出肅州以西平澤及山谷中。形似黃礬而堅實。有夾爛木者，云是胡桐樹脂淪入土石鹼鹵地者。其樹高碩，皮葉似白楊、青桐、桑輩，故名胡桐木，堪器用。

保升曰︰涼州以西有之。初生似柳，大則似桑、桐。其津下入地，與土石相染，狀如薑極鹹苦，得水便消，若礬石、硝石之類。冬月採之。大明曰︰此有二般︰木律不中入藥；惟用石律，石上采之，形如小石片子，黃土色者為上。頌曰︰今西番亦有商人貨之。時珍曰︰木淚乃樹脂流出者，其狀如膏油。石淚乃脂入土石間者，其狀成塊，以其得鹵藥為勝。

【氣味】鹹、苦，大寒，無毒。

恭曰︰伏砒石。可為金銀焊藥。

【主治】大毒熱，心腹煩滿，水和服之，取吐。牛馬急黃黑汗，水研三、二兩灌之，立瘥（《唐本》）。主風蟲牙齒痛，殺火毒、面毒（大明）。風疳齒，骨槽風勞。能軟一切物。

多服令人吐。

【發明】頌曰︰古方稀用。今治口齒家多用，為最要之物。

時珍曰︰石淚入地受鹵氣，故其性寒能除熱，其味鹹能入骨軟堅。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** Also known as Hu Tong Jian [胡桐鹼] (Bencao Gangmu) and Hu Tong Lü [胡桐律].

[An earlier authority] states: Hu Tong Lei is the resin of the hu tong tree; hence it is called 'lei' [tears]. The writing of the character as 'lü' [律] is incorrect — 'lü' and 'lei' [淚] are simply phonetic corruptions of one another.

Li Shizhen states: The 'Account of the Western Regions' [西域傳] records that the kingdom of Jushi abounds in hu tong trees. The commentator Yan Shigu notes: the hu tong resembles the tong [Paulownia] tree, not the mulberry, hence the name hu tong [foreign tong]. When insects bore into the tree and the sap flows downward, it is popularly called hu tong lei, meaning it resembles tears from the eyes. That which seeps into soil and rock and solidifies into lumps like saline alkali is called hu tong jian [胡桐鹼] (pronounced 'jiǎn'). Some say that 'lü' [律] should be written as 'lì' [瀝] (drip/distillate), that this is not a corruption, and that the usage parallels the term 'liqing' [瀝青, pitch/bitumen] for pine resin. This interpretation is also acceptable.

**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Hu Tong Lei is produced in the flat marshlands and mountain valleys west of Suzhou [Jiuquan]. In form it resembles yellow alum [yellow vitriol] and is hard and dense. Specimens containing embedded rotted wood are said to be tree resin from the hu tong that has seeped into saline-alkaline soil and rock. The tree is tall and large; its bark and leaves resemble those of the white poplar [Populus alba], the green tong [Firmiana simplex], and the mulberry, hence the wood is called hu tong mu and is suitable for making utensils.

Han Baosheng states: It is found west of Liangzhou. When young the tree resembles a willow; when mature it resembles the mulberry and tong. Its sap seeps down into the ground and mingles with soil and rock, taking on a ginger-like form; it is extremely salty and bitter, and dissolves readily in water, resembling alum [fan shi] or saltpeter [xiao shi]. It is collected in the winter months. Da Ming states: There are two varieties — the 'wood type' [mu lü] is not suitable for medicinal use; only the 'stone type' [shi lü] is used. It is collected from rock surfaces; pieces shaped like small stone chips and of a yellow-earth color are considered the finest. Su Song states: Merchants from the western regions [Xi Fan] also trade in it today. Li Shizhen states: The 'wood tears' [mu lei] are resin that has flowed out from the tree; their consistency resembles that of fat or oil. The 'stone tears' [shi lei] are resin that has entered the soil and rock; these solidify into lumps. Because they have absorbed alkaline [lǔ] properties from the ground, the stone variety is medicinally superior.

**Flavor and Nature:** Salty and bitter; greatly cold; non-toxic.

Su Gong states: It subdues [detoxifies] arsenolite [arsenic mineral, pi shi]. It can be used as a soldering flux for gold and silver.

**Indications and Treatment:** For severe toxic heat with oppressive fullness in the chest and abdomen: mix with water and administer orally to induce vomiting. For acute jaundice [ji huang] and black-sweat [hei han] in cattle and horses: grind two to three liang in water and drench the animal; recovery is immediate (Tang Bencao). Treats toothache due to wind-worm [dental caries from wind-pathogen or parasitic etiology]; eliminates fire-toxin and facial toxin (Da Ming). [Treats] wind-gan [erosive gum disease] with dental decay, and bone-socket wind-consumption [骨槽風勞, deep jaw/periodontal infection with systemic depletion]. Softens all hard accumulations.

Taken in large doses it causes vomiting.

**Elaboration:** Su Song states: It is rarely used in ancient formulas, but today it is widely employed by practitioners treating oral and dental conditions and is regarded as a most essential substance.

Li Shizhen states: Stone-tears [shi lei] seep into the ground and absorb the alkaline-saline qi [lǔ qi]; therefore their nature is cold and capable of clearing heat, and their salty flavor enables them to enter the bones and soften hardness.

segments.title_inferred: Return-Soul Incense (Fanhun Xiang): A Legendary Reviving Fragrance
segments.original_text: 【集解】曰︰按︰《內傳》云︰西海聚窟州有返魂樹，狀如楓、柏，花、葉香聞百里。采其根於釜中水煮取汁，煉之如漆，乃香成也。其名有六︰曰返魂、驚精、回生、振靈、馬精、卻死。凡有疫死者，燒豆許熏之再活，故曰返魂。時珍曰︰張華《博物志》云︰武帝時，西域月氏國，度弱水貢此香三枚，大如燕卵，黑如桑椹。值長安大疫，西使請燒一枚辟之，宮中病者聞之即起，香聞百里，數日不歇。疫死未三日者，熏之皆活，乃返生神藥也。此說雖涉詭怪，然理外之事，容或有之，未可便指為謬也。
segments.translation:
[Collected Explanations] states: According to the 'Inner Tradition' [Neizhuan]: In Juku Prefecture [Jujuzhou] of the Western Sea there is a tree called the Return-Soul Tree [Fanhun Shu], resembling the maple and cypress in form, whose flowers and leaves emit a fragrance detectable for a hundred li. The roots are gathered and boiled in water in a cauldron to extract the juice, which is then refined until it thickens like lacquer — thus the incense is made. It goes by six names: Return-Soul [Fanhun], Alarm-Essence [Jingjing], Restore-Life [Huisheng], Rouse-the-Numinous [Zhenling], Horse-Essence [Majing], and Repel-Death [Quesi]. Whenever someone has died of pestilence, burning a quantity the size of a bean and fumigating the body will revive them — hence the name 'Return-Soul.'

Li Shizhen states: Zhang Hua's 'Record of the Investigation of Things' [Bowu Zhi] relates that during the reign of Emperor Wu [of the Han dynasty], the Yuezhi kingdom of the Western Regions crossed the Weak Water [Ruoshui] to present three pieces of this incense as tribute. Each was the size of a swallow's egg and as black as a mulberry berry. At the time, a great pestilence had broken out in Chang'an. The western envoy requested that one piece be burned to ward it off. Those who were ill in the palace arose upon smelling it; the fragrance traveled a hundred li and did not dissipate for several days. Those who had died of the plague within three days were fumigated and all came back to life — it was truly a divine medicine for restoring life. Although this account borders on the strange and fantastical, matters that lie beyond ordinary reason may nonetheless sometimes exist; one cannot simply dismiss it as false.

segments.title_inferred: Tanhuan (Root of the Centennial Cypress / Tanhuan Fungus)
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰檀桓乃百歲柏之根，如天門冬，長三四尺，別在一旁，以小根綴之。一名檀桓芝。出《靈寶方》。時珍曰︰《本經》但言黃柏根名檀桓。陳氏所說乃柏旁所生檀桓芝也，與陶弘景所說同。

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。

【主治】心腹百病，安魂魄，不飢渴。久服，輕身延年通神（《別錄》）。長生神仙，去萬病。為散，飲服方寸匕，盡一枚有驗（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Tanhuan is the root of a hundred-year-old cypress [Cupressus/Platycladus sp.]. It resembles Tianmendong [Asparagus cochinchinensis], grows three to four feet in length, branches off to one side, and is connected by small rootlets. It is also called Tanhuan Zhi [Tanhuan Fungus/Anomalous Root]. This comes from the *Lingbao Fang* [Formulary of the Numinous Treasure].

Li Shizhen states: The *Bencao Jing* [Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica] merely says that the root of Huangbo [Phellodendron amurense] is called Tanhuan. What Chen [Cangqi] describes is the Tanhuan Zhi that grows beside the cypress, which accords with what Tao Hongjing described.

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; cold; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** [Treats] the hundred diseases of the heart and abdomen; settles the hun and po [ethereal and corporeal souls]; eliminates hunger and thirst. Long-term consumption lightens the body, prolongs life, and confers spiritual clarity (*Bielu* [Supplementary Records]).

[Enables] long life and transcendence; eliminates the ten thousand diseases. Ground into powder and taken orally in doses of one fang-cun-bi [approximately one square-inch spoonful], consuming an entire specimen has proven efficacy (Chen Cangqi).

segments.title_inferred: Small Cypress-Barberry (Xiao Bo) — Entry from Chapter Wood II
segments.original_text:
【釋名】子柏（弘景）、山石榴。

時珍曰：此與金櫻子、杜鵑花並名山石榴，非一物也。

【集解】弘景曰：子柏樹小，狀如石榴，其皮黃而苦。又一種多刺，皮亦黃。並主口瘡。

恭曰：小柏生山石間，所在皆有，襄陽峴山東者為良。一名山石榴，其樹枝葉與石榴無異，但花異，子細黑圓如牛李子及女貞子爾。其樹皮白，陶云皮黃，恐謬矣。今太常所貯，乃小樹多刺而葉細者，名刺柏，非小柏也。

藏器曰：凡是柏木皆皮黃。今既不黃，非柏也。小柏如石榴，皮黃，子赤如枸杞子，兩頭尖。人銼枝以染黃。若云子黑而圓，恐是別物，非小柏也。

時珍曰：小柏山間時有之，小樹也。其皮外白裡黃，狀如柏皮而薄小。

【氣味】苦，大寒，無毒。

【主治】口瘡疳蝕，殺諸蟲，去心腹中熱氣（《唐本》）。治血崩，阿茄陀丸方中用之。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Synonyms]**
Alternate names: Zi Bo [Child-Cypress] (Hongjing); Shan Shiliu [Mountain Pomegranate].

Li Shizhen states: This plant shares the name 'Mountain Pomegranate' with Jin Ying Zi [Cherokee rose, Rosa laevigata] and the Azalea [Rhododendron], but they are not the same thing.

**[Collected Commentaries]**
Hongjing [Tao Hongjing] states: The Zi Bo is a small tree, resembling the pomegranate in form, with bark that is yellow and bitter in taste. There is also another variety that bears many thorns, and its bark is likewise yellow. Both are primarily used to treat oral ulcers.

Kong [Su Gong] states: Xiao Bo grows among mountain rocks and is found everywhere. The variety from east of Xianshan in Xiangyang is considered the finest. It is also called Mountain Pomegranate. Its branches and leaves are not distinguishable from those of the pomegranate, but its flowers differ; its berries are small, round, and black, resembling those of the Buckthorn [Rhamnus] and the Glossy Privet [Ligustrum lucidum]. The bark of the tree is white — Tao's claim that the bark is yellow is likely mistaken. What the Imperial Medical Office [Taichang] currently stores under this name is in fact a small thorny tree with fine leaves, called Ci Bo [Thorny Cypress], which is not the true Xiao Bo.

Chen Cangqi states: All true bo [Berberis-type] trees have yellow bark. Since this one's bark is not yellow, it is not a bo. The true Xiao Bo resembles the pomegranate, has yellow bark, and bears red berries — pointed at both ends — resembling wolfberries [Lycium]. People chop its branches to use as a yellow dye. If the berries are described as black and round, that is likely a different plant and not the true Xiao Bo.

Li Shizhen states: Xiao Bo is occasionally found in mountain valleys; it is a small tree. Its bark is white on the outside and yellow within, resembling cypress bark [bo pi] but thinner and smaller.

**[Flavor and Nature]**
Bitter; greatly cold; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Treats oral ulcers and gan erosions [gangrenous or parasitic oral/mucosal lesions]; kills all manner of parasitic worms; dispels heat qi from the chest and abdomen (according to the *Tangben* [Tang Materia Medica]). Also used to treat metrorrhagia [blood collapse/崩漏], being an ingredient in the Ajiatuo Pill formula.

segments.title_inferred: Huanglu (Smoke Tree / Cotinus coggygria) — Properties, Treatments, and Formulae
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰黃櫨生商洛山谷，四川界甚有之。葉圓木黃，可染。

【氣味】苦，寒，無毒。

【主治】除煩熱，解酒疸目黃，水煮服之（藏器）。洗赤眼及湯火傷。

【附方】新一。

大風癩疾︰黃櫨木五兩（銼，用新汲水一斗浸二七日，焙研），蘇方木五兩，烏麻子一斗（九蒸九曝），天麻二兩，丁香、乳香各一兩，為末。以赤黍米一升淘淨，用浸黃櫨水煮米粥搗和，丸梧桐子大。每服二、三十丸，食後漿水下，日二、夜一。（《聖濟總錄》）
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Chen Cangqi states: Huanglu [Yellow Smoke Tree] grows in the mountain valleys of Shangluo and is found in great abundance throughout the Sichuan region. Its leaves are round and its wood is yellow; it can be used as a dye.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Bitter; cold; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]** Eliminates vexing heat; resolves alcoholic jaundice [jiǔ dǎn] and yellow eyes — decoct in water and take orally (Chen Cangqi). Used to wash red, inflamed eyes and to treat scalds and burns from hot water or fire.

**[Appended Formulae]** One new formula.
