In the materia medica, different plants sharing the same name is very common; how much more so when only a single character — 'bone' — is shared? To reason on this basis is especially forced and contrived.

Li Shizhen [時珍] states: Wang Ji's conclusion seems reasonable, yet he himself was unable to definitively identify what plant this actually is.

**[Flavor and Nature]**
Acrid [辛], cold [寒], non-toxic. The *Bielu* states: bitter [苦], slightly cold [微寒].

Zheng Quan [之才] states: Loulu [漏盧, *Rhaponticum uniflorum*] acts as its guiding herb [使].

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Treats heat in the skin, eliminates pathogenic [邪] qi, stops urinary incontinence [遺溺], and promotes urination [利水道] (according to the *Bencao Jing* [*Classic of Materia Medica*]).

Eliminates heat in the stomach, descends qi [下氣]. Can be prepared as a medicated bath [浴湯] (according to the *Bielu*). Li Shizhen notes: According to Sun Simiao's [孫真人] *Qianjin Fang* [*Essential Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold*], for treating the thirty-six diseases of the lower burner [下焦] in women, [text continues, passage breaks off here]…

segments.title_inferred: Yanglu (楊櫨) — Leaves and Wood Ear Cross-reference
segments.original_text:
【集解】恭曰︰楊櫨，一名空疏，所在皆有，生籬垣間。其子為莢。

葉

【氣味】苦，寒，有毒。

【主治】疽惡瘡，水煮汁洗之，立瘥（《唐本》）。

木耳 見菜部。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】 Su Gong states: Yanglu [楊櫨], also known as Kongshu [空疏], grows everywhere, commonly found growing along fences and walls. Its fruits are pod-like [莢].

Leaves

【Qi and Flavor】 Bitter; cold; toxic.

【Indications and Treatment】 For carbuncles [疽] and malignant sores [惡瘡]: boil the leaves in water and wash the affected area with the decoction; recovery is swift. (From the Tang Bencao [《唐本》].)

Wood Ear [木耳]: See the Vegetables section [菜部].

segments.title_inferred: Shi Nan (Stone Southern Shrub) — Wood Category III
segments.original_text:
【釋名】風藥。

時珍曰：生於石間向陽之處，故名石南。桂陽呼為風藥，充茗及浸酒飲能愈頭風，故名。

按：《范石湖集》云：修江出欒茶，治頭風。今南人無所謂欒茶者，豈即此乎？

【集解】《別錄》曰：石南生華陰山谷。三月、四月採葉，八月採實。

弘景曰：今東間皆有之，葉如枇杷葉。方用亦稀。

恭曰：葉似莽草，凌冬不凋。關中者葉細為好。江山以南者，葉長大如枇杷葉，無氣味，殊不任用。保升曰：終南斜谷有石處甚饒。今市人以石葦為之，誤矣。頌曰：今南北皆有之。生於石上，株極有高碩者。江湖間出者，葉如枇杷，上有小刺，凌冬不凋。春生白花成簇，秋結細紅實。關隴間出者，葉似莽草，青黃色，背有紫點，雨多則並生，長及二、三寸。根橫，細紫色。無花實，葉至茂密。南北人多移植亭院間，陰翳可愛，不透日氣。入藥以關中葉細者為良。魏王《花木志》云：南方石南樹野生。二月開花，連著實。實如燕覆子，八月熟。民采取核，和魚羹尤美。今無用者。宗奭曰：石南葉似枇杷葉之小者，而背無毛，光而不皺。正、二月間開花。冬有二葉為花苞，苞既開，中有十五余花，大小如椿花，甚細碎。每一苞約彈許大，成一球。一花六葉，一朵有七、八球，淡白綠色，葉末微淡赤色。花既開，蕊滿花，但見蕊不見花。花才罷，去年綠葉盡脫落，漸生新葉。京洛、河北、河東、山東頗少，人故少用。湖南北、江東西、二浙甚多，故人多用。

葉

【氣味】辛、苦，平，有毒。

之才曰：五加皮為使。惡小薊。

【主治】養腎氣，內傷陰衰，利筋骨皮毛（《本經》）。可久服，令思男（《別錄》）。能添腎氣，治軟腳煩悶疼，殺蟲，逐諸風（甄權）。浸酒飲，治頭風（時珍）。殺蠱毒，破積聚，逐風痺。

【發明】恭曰：石南葉為療風邪丸散之要，今醫家不復用其實矣。

權曰：雖能養腎，亦令人陰痿。

時珍曰：古方為治風痺腎弱要藥。今人絕不知用，識者亦少，蓋由甄氏《藥性論》有令陰痿之說也。殊不知服此藥者，能令腎強，嗜欲之人藉此放恣，以致痿弱，歸咎於藥，良可慨也。毛文錫《茶譜》云：湘人四月採楊桐草，搗汁浸水蒸，作為飯食；必采石南芽為茶飲，乃去風也。暑月尤宜。楊桐即南燭也。
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Wind Medicine [feng yao].

Li Shizhen states: It grows among rocks in sun-facing places, hence the name Shi Nan [Stone South/Southern]. In Guiyang it is called Feng Yao [Wind Medicine]; used as a tea or steeped in wine it can cure head-wind [migraine/headache disorders], hence that name.

Note: The *Collected Works of Fan Shihu* states: "The Xiu River produces Luan Cha [Luan Tea], which treats head-wind." Southerners today know of no such thing as Luan Cha — could it perhaps be this very plant?

**[Collected Commentaries]**
*Mingyi Bielu* states: Shi Nan grows in the mountain valleys of Huayin. Leaves are gathered in the third and fourth months; fruit is gathered in the eighth month.

Tao Hongjing states: It is found throughout the eastern regions; its leaves resemble those of the loquat [pipa, *Eriobotrya japonica*]. It is rarely used in prescriptions.

Su Gong states: The leaves resemble those of Mang Cao [*Illicium lanceolatum*] and do not wither in winter. The variety from the Guan[zhong] region, with its finer leaves, is the best. The variety from south of the Jiangshan range has large leaves like loquat leaves, has no fragrance or taste, and is quite unsuitable for use. [Han] Baosheng states: It grows abundantly in rocky areas of the Zhongnan and Xie valleys. Market vendors today use Shi Wei [*Pyrrosia* sp.] as a substitute — this is an error. Su Song states: It is now found in both the north and the south. It grows on rocks and some specimens grow quite tall and large. The variety from the Jianghu region has leaves like loquat with small spines, and does not wither in winter. In spring it produces clusters of white flowers; in autumn it bears small red fruit. The variety from the Guanlong region has leaves resembling Mang Cao, of a blue-yellow color, with purple spots on the underside; when rain is plentiful, leaves grow side by side reaching two to three cun in length. The roots grow horizontally and are fine and purple in color. This variety produces no flowers or fruit, but its foliage is exceedingly dense. People throughout north and south commonly transplant it into garden pavilions and courtyards, where its shade is lovely and it blocks out the sunlight. For medicinal use, the Guanzhong variety with its fine leaves is considered superior. The Wei King's *Record of Flowers and Trees* states: "In the south, the Shi Nan tree grows wild. It blooms in the second month and simultaneously sets fruit. The fruit resembles that of the Yan Fu Zi [unidentified climbing plant]; it ripens in the eighth month. The people gather its seeds and add them to fish stew, which is especially delicious." Today this use is no longer practiced. Kou Zongshi states: The leaves of Shi Nan resemble smaller loquat leaves, but are hairless on the underside, smooth and not wrinkled. It flowers in the first and second months. In winter two leaves form a flower bud; when the bud opens it contains more than fifteen flowers, of a size similar to ailanthus [chun] flowers — very small and delicate. Each bud is roughly the size of a pellet [dan], forming a single ball. Each flower has six petals; one cluster [duo] has seven or eight balls, of a pale whitish-green color with the leaf tips faintly reddish. Once in full bloom, the stamens fill the flower so that only the stamens are visible, not the petals. As soon as flowering ends, all the previous year's green leaves fall off and new leaves gradually emerge. The plant is quite scarce in Jinglo, Hebei, Hedong, and Shandong, and thus rarely used there. It is very abundant in Hunan, Hubei, Jiangdong, Jiangxi, and the two Zhe [Zhejiang] regions, and so is used more widely there.

---
**Leaves**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Acrid [xin], bitter [ku]; balanced [ping]; toxic.

[Xu] Zhicai states: Wu Jia Pi [Acanthopanax bark] acts as its conductor [shi]. It is antagonistic to Xiao Ji [small thistle, *Cirsium setosum*].

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Nourishes kidney qi; [treats] internal injury and decline of yin; benefits the sinews, bones, skin, and hair. (*Shennong Bencao Jing*)
- May be taken over a long period; causes [women to] long for men [i.e., increases female libido]. (*Mingyi Bielu*)
- Can replenish kidney qi; treats weakness of the legs [ruan jiao], vexation, oppression, and pain; kills worms; dispels all manner of wind. (Zhen Quan)
- Steeped in wine and drunk, treats head-wind. (Li Shizhen)
- Kills gu toxin [parasite-based poison]; breaks up accumulations and gatherings [ji ju]; dispels wind-impediment [feng bi].

**[Elucidation]**
Su Gong states: Shi Nan leaf is an essential ingredient in pills and powders for treating wind evil. Physicians today no longer use the fruit.

Zhen Quan states: Although it can nourish the kidneys, it also causes erectile dysfunction [yin wei] in men.

Li Shizhen states: In ancient formulas this was a key medicinal for treating wind-impediment and kidney weakness. People today do not use it at all, and few recognize it — this is largely because Zhen Shi's *Yaoxing Lun* [Treatise on the Properties of Medicinals] contains the statement that it causes erectile dysfunction. Yet what those who use this medicine do not understand is that it strengthens the kidneys; those with excessive desires use the drug as a license for indulgence, thereby bringing about weakness themselves, and then blame the medicine — this is truly lamentable. Mao Wenxi's *Cha Pu* [Tea Manual] states: "The people of Xiang [Hunan] gather Yang Tong Cao [*Eurya japonica*] in the fourth month, pound it into juice, soak water, and steam it into a food; they invariably also gather Shi Nan buds to brew as a tea — this dispels wind. It is especially suitable in summer." Yang Tong is the same as Nan Zhu [*Vaccinium bracteatum*].

segments.title_inferred: Manjing (Vitex trifolia / shrub chaste tree) — Fruit, Properties, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】恭曰︰蔓荊苗蔓生，故名。

【集解】恭曰︰蔓荊生水濱。苗莖蔓延長丈餘。春因舊枝而生小葉，五月葉成，似杏葉。六月有花，紅白色，黃蕊。九月有實，黑斑，大如梧子而虛輕。冬則葉凋。今人誤以小荊為蔓荊，遂將蔓荊為牡荊也。

大明曰︰海鹽亦有之。大如豌豆，蒂有輕軟小蓋子，六、七、八月採之。

頌曰︰近汴京及秦、隴、明、越州多有之。苗莖高四、五尺，對節生枝。葉類小楝，至夏盛茂。有花作穗淡紅色，蕊黃白色，花下有青萼，至秋結子。舊說蔓生，而今所有並非蔓也。

宗奭曰︰諸家所解，蔓荊、牡荊，紛紜不一。《經》既言蔓荊明是蔓生，即非高木也；既言牡荊，則自木上生，又何疑焉？

時珍曰︰其枝小弱如蔓，故曰蔓生。

實

【氣味】苦，微寒，無毒。《別錄》曰︰辛，平、溫。

元素曰︰味辛，溫，氣清，陽中之陰，入太陽經。胃虛人不可服，恐生痰疾。

之才曰︰惡烏頭、石膏。

【主治】筋骨間寒熱，濕痹拘攣，明目堅齒，利九竅，去白蟲。久服，輕身耐老。小荊實亦等（《本經》）。風頭痛，腦鳴，目淚出，益氣。令人光澤脂致（《別錄》）。治賊風，長髭發（甄權）。利關節，治癇疾、赤眼（大明）。太陽頭痛，頭沉昏悶，除目暗，散風邪，涼諸經血，止目睛內痛（元素）。搜肝風（好古）。

【發明】恭曰︰小荊實即牡荊子，其功與蔓荊同，故曰亦等也。

時珍曰︰蔓荊氣清味辛，體輕而浮，上行而散。故所主者，皆頭面風虛之症。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Su Gong [Tang-dynasty commentator] states: The seedlings and stems of Manjing [蔓荊] grow in a trailing, vine-like manner, hence the name [蔓, 'trailing/creeping'].

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: Manjing grows along the banks of waterways. Its stems and shoots trail and extend to more than a zhang [c. 3 metres] in length. In spring, small leaves sprout from the old branches; by the fifth month the leaves are fully formed, resembling apricot leaves. In the sixth month flowers appear, red-white in colour with yellow stamens. In the ninth month it bears fruit — dark-spotted, about the size of a firmiana [Chinese parasol tree, Firmiana simplex] seed, but hollow and light. In winter the leaves fall. People today mistakenly take Xiaojing [小荊, small vitex] for Manjing, and consequently identify Manjing as Mujing [牡荊].

Da Ming [Ri Huazi] states: It is also found in Haiyan [coastal salt regions]. The fruit is about the size of a pea; the stem-end bears a small, light, soft calyx-cap. It is collected in the sixth, seventh, and eighth months.

Su Song [Song-dynasty commentator] states: It is found in abundance near Bianjing [Kaifeng] and in the regions of Qin, Long, Ming, and Yuezhou. The stems grow four to five chi [c. 1.2–1.5 m] tall, with branches growing opposite at each node. The leaves resemble those of the small chinaberry [小楝]; in summer the plant grows lush and dense. The flowers form spikes of pale red, with yellowish-white stamens; below the flowers are green calyces. Fruit forms in autumn. Earlier accounts described it as a creeper, but the plants found today are not creeping at all.

Kou Zongshi [Song-dynasty commentator] states: The various authorities' explanations of Manjing and Mujing are confused and contradictory. The Classics, having clearly stated 'Manjing,' evidently mean a creeping plant — not a tall tree. Having stated 'Mujing,' they mean something that grows as a woody shrub — what doubt can there be?

Li Shizhen states: Its branches are slender and weak like vine tendrils, hence it is called 'creeping' [蔓生].

**Fruit**

**Qi and Flavour**
Bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. The *Mingyi Bielu* states: acrid [pungent], balanced/neutral, warm.

Zhang Yuansu [Jin-dynasty physician] states: Flavour acrid, warm; its qi is clear. It belongs to the category of yin within yang, and enters the Taiyang channel [Greater Yang meridian]. Persons with stomach deficiency should not take it, for fear of engendering phlegm disorders.

Xu Zhicai states: It is incompatible with [i.e., antagonises] Wutou [aconite root, Aconitum spp.] and Shigao [gypsum, CaSO₄·2H₂O].

**Indications and Treatment**
- Cold and heat [alternating fever and chills] in the sinews and bones; damp impediment [湿痹] with cramping and contracture; brightening the eyes; strengthening the teeth; benefiting the nine orifices; expelling white worms [intestinal parasites]. Long-term use lightens the body and retards aging. The fruit of Xiaojing [小荊] is equivalent in efficacy (*Shennong Bencao Jing*).
- Wind headache; ringing in the brain [tinnitus]; tearing of the eyes; augmenting qi; giving the skin a lustrous, smooth, and well-nourished appearance (*Mingyi Bielu*).
- Treats thieving wind [贼风, sudden pathogenic wind invasion]; promotes growth of the beard and hair (Zhen Quan).
- Benefits the joints; treats epileptic conditions and red/inflamed eyes (Da Ming).
- Taiyang [Greater Yang] headache; heaviness and dull oppression of the head; dispelling dimness of vision; scattering wind pathogen; cooling the blood in all channels; stopping pain within the eyeball (Zhang Yuansu).
- Searches out and expels liver wind [肝风] (Wang Haogu).

**Elucidation**
Su Gong states: The fruit of Xiaojing is precisely the seed of Mujing [牡荊子]; its efficacy is the same as that of Manjing — hence the statement 'equivalent in efficacy.'

Li Shizhen states: Manjing's qi is clear and its flavour acrid; its substance is light and floating. It moves upward and disperses. Therefore all the conditions it governs are wind-deficiency disorders of the head and face.

segments.title_inferred: Luanjing (Wan Jing / Vitex-like shrub), Wood Section Three
segments.original_text:
【釋名】頑荊（《圖經》）。

【集解】恭曰︰欒荊莖、葉都似石南，幹亦反卷，經冬不死，葉上有細黑點者，真也，今雍州所用者是。而洛州乃用石荊當之，非也。俗方大用，而本草不載，亦無別名。但有欒華，功用又別，非此物花也。

頌曰︰欒荊今生東海及淄州、汾州。所生者皆枝莖白，葉小圓而青色，頗似榆葉而長，冬夏不凋。六月開花，花有紫、白二種。子似大麻。四月採苗葉，八月採子。

宗奭曰︰欒荊即牡荊也，子青色如茱萸，不合更立此條。蘇恭又稱石荊當之，轉見穿鑿。

時珍曰︰按︰許慎《說文》云︰欒，似木蘭。木蘭葉似桂，與蘇恭所說葉似石南者相近。蘇頌所圖者即今牡荊，與《唐本草》者不合。欒荊是蘇恭收入本草，不應自誤。蓋後人不識。

子

【氣味】辛、苦，溫，有小毒。

權曰︰甘、辛，微熱，無毒。決明為之使。惡石膏。

【主治】大風，頭面手足諸風，癲癇狂痙，濕痺寒冷疼痛（《唐本》）。四肢不遂，通血脈，明目，益精光（甄權）。

合油同熬，塗人畜瘡疥。
segments.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** Wanjing [Stubborn/Intractable Jing] (from the *Illustrated Classic* [Tujing]).

**Collected Commentaries:**

[Su] Gong states: The stems and leaves of Luanjing closely resemble those of Shinan [Photinia / Chinese Photinia]. The trunk also curves backward; it does not die in winter. Those specimens whose leaves bear fine black dots are the genuine article — these are the ones currently used in Yongzhou. In Luozhou, people substitute Shijing [Stone Jing] for it, which is incorrect. It is widely used in common prescriptions, yet is not recorded in the materia medica canon, nor does it have an alternate name. There is a separate entry for Luanhua [Luan flower], whose properties and uses are different — it is not the flower of this plant.

[Su] Song states: Luanjing today grows in the Eastern Sea region and in Zizhou and Fenzhou. The plants there all have white branches and stems; the leaves are small, round, and green, rather resembling elm leaves but more elongated. They do not wither in summer or winter. Flowers appear in the sixth month, coming in two varieties: purple and white. The seeds resemble those of hemp [da ma]. Seedling-leaves are gathered in the fourth month; seeds in the eighth month.

[Kou] Zongshi states: Luanjing is in fact Mujing [Vitex negundo]. Its seeds are green, like those of Zhuyu [Evodia]. It is therefore unnecessary to establish a separate entry for it. Su Gong's further claim that Shijing can be substituted for it only makes things more convoluted and forced.

Li Shizhen states: According to Xu Shen's *Shuowen* [Explaining Writing]: "Luan resembles Mulan [Magnolia]." The leaves of Mulan resemble those of Gui [cinnamon/laurel], which is consistent with what Su Gong says about the leaves resembling Shinan. What Su Song illustrated is the Mujing of today, which does not accord with the *Tang Materia Medica*. Since Luanjing was included in the materia medica by Su Gong himself, he would not have made an error in identifying it. It is likely that later scholars simply could not identify it [and confused the entries].

**Seeds**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [xin], bitter [ku], warm, slightly toxic.

[Zhen] Quan states: Sweet [gan], acrid [xin], mildly hot, non-toxic. Juemingzi [Semen Cassiae] acts as its guide [shi, facilitating herb]. It is antagonistic to Shigao [Gypsum fibrosum].

**Primary Therapeutic Uses:** Great wind [da feng, severe wind-type disorders], various wind conditions affecting the head, face, hands, and feet, epilepsy [dian xian], mania, spasms [kuang jing], damp-impediment [shi bi] with cold pain (*Tang Materia Medica*). Paralysis of the four limbs, promoting circulation in the blood vessels, brightening the eyes, and enhancing the essence of vision (Zhen Quan).

Combined with oil and simmered together, the preparation is applied topically to treat sores and scabies [chuang jie] in both humans and livestock.

segments.title_inferred: Stone Jing (Shi Jing) — a Water-Side Shrub for Hair Growth
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰石荊似荊而小，生水旁，《廣濟方》一名水荊是也。蘇頌言洛人以當欒荊者，非也。

【主治】燒灰淋汁浴頭，生發令長（藏器）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】 Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: Shijing [Stone Jing] resembles jing [Vitex] but is smaller, and grows beside water. It is the same plant called 'Shuijing' [Water Jing] in the Guangji Fang [Guangji Formulary]. Su Song's claim that people in Luoyang use it as a substitute for Luanjing is incorrect.

【Indications and Treatments】 Burn it to ash, leach the ash with water to produce a lye, and use this liquid to wash the head; it promotes hair growth and lengthens the hair. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Zǐjīng (Redbud / Chinese Judas Tree) — Wood Section Three
segments.original_text:
【釋名】紫珠（《拾遺》），皮名肉紅（《綱目》）、內消。時珍曰：其木似黃荊而色紫，故名。其皮色紅而消腫，故瘍科呼為肉紅，又曰內消，與何首烏同名。

【集解】頌曰：紫荊處處有之，人多種於庭院間。木似黃荊，葉小無丫，花深紫可愛。

藏器曰：即田氏之荊也。至秋子熟，正紫，圓如小珠，名紫珠。江東林澤間尤多。

宗奭曰：春開紫花甚細碎，共作朵生，出無常處，或生於木身之上，或附根上枝下，直出花。花罷葉出，光緊微圓。園圃多植之。

時珍曰：高樹柔條，其花甚繁，歲二、三次。其皮入藥，以川中濃而紫色、味苦如膽者為勝。

木並皮

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。藏器曰：苦，寒。

大明曰：皮、梗及花，氣味功用並同。

【主治】破宿血，下五淋，濃煮汁服（《開寶》）。通小腸（大明）。解諸毒物，癰疽喉痺，飛尸蠱毒，腫下，蛇、虺、蟲、蠶、狂犬毒，並煮汁服。亦以汁洗瘡腫，除血長膚（藏器）。活血行氣，消腫解毒，治婦人血氣疼痛，經水凝澀。

【發明】時珍曰：紫荊氣寒味苦，色紫性降，入手、足厥陰血分。寒勝熱，苦走骨，紫入營。故能活血消腫，利小便而解毒。楊清叟《仙傳方》有沖和膏，以紫荊為君，蓋亦得此意也。其方治一切癰疽發背流注諸腫毒，冷熱不明者。紫荊皮（炒）三兩，獨活（去節，炒）三兩，赤芍藥（炒）二兩，生白芷一兩，木蠟（炒）一兩，為末。用蔥湯調，熱敷。血得熱則行，蔥能散氣也。瘡不甚熱者，酒調之。痛甚者，加乳香。筋不伸者，亦加乳香。大抵癰疽流注，皆是氣血凝滯所成，遇溫則散，遇涼則凝。此方溫平，紫荊皮乃木之精，破血消腫。獨活乃土之精，止風動血，引拔骨中毒，去痺濕氣。芍藥乃火之精，生血止痛。木蠟乃水之精，消腫散血，同獨活能破石腫堅硬。白芷乃金之精，去風生肌止痛。蓋血生則不死，血動則流通，肌生則不爛，痛止則不潰，風去則血自散，氣破則硬可消，毒自除。五者交治，病安有不愈者乎？
segments.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]**
Also called Zǐzhū ['Purple Pearl'] (from *Shiyi* [*Materia Medica Supplement*]); the bark is called Ròuhóng ['Flesh-Red'] (from *Bencao Gangmu*) or Nèixiāo ['Internal Disperser']. Li Shizhen states: The tree resembles Huángjīng [Vitex] but is purple in color, hence the name [Zǐjīng, 'Purple Jīng']. Its bark is red in color and disperses swelling, so surgeons [瘍科] call it Ròuhóng, and also Nèixiāo — a name it shares with Héshǒuwū [Polygonum multiflorum].

**[Collected Commentaries]**
Sòng [Su Song] states: Zǐjīng is found everywhere; people commonly plant it in courtyards. The tree resembles Huángjīng, with small leaves that are not forked, and the deep purple flowers are lovely.

Zàngqì [Chen Zangqi] states: This is the jīng tree of the Tián family. In autumn the seeds ripen, perfectly purple and round like small pearls, hence the name Zǐzhū ['Purple Pearl']. It is especially abundant in the forests and wetlands of Jiangdong.

Zōngshì [Kou Zongshi] states: In spring it opens very fine, clustered purple flowers, which emerge in bunches at irregular places — sometimes on the trunk itself, sometimes close to the roots or below the branches, blooming directly from the wood. After the flowers finish, the leaves emerge, which are glossy, firm, and slightly rounded. It is widely cultivated in gardens.

Li Shizhen states: It is a tall tree with pliant branches; the flowers are extremely abundant, blooming two or three times per year. Its bark is used medicinally; that from Sichuan, which is thick, deep purple, and bitter in taste like bile, is considered superior.

**Wood and Bark**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Bitter, balanced [平], non-toxic. Zàngqì states: Bitter, cold. Dàmíng [Ri Huazi] states: The bark, stems, and flowers are identical in qi, flavor, and medicinal function.

**[Principal Treatments]**
Breaks up stagnant blood [宿血]; relieves the five types of urinary obstruction [五淋] — take a thick decoction of the juice (*Kaibao* [*Kaibao Bencao*]). Promotes flow through the small intestine (Dàmíng). Resolves all manner of toxins: carbuncles and deep abscesses [癰疽], throat obstruction [喉痺], flying-corpse [飛尸] and gu-poison [蠱毒], swelling and redness, bites or stings of snakes, vipers, insects, silkworms, and mad dogs — in all cases decoct and take the juice. The juice may also be used to wash sores and swellings; it removes stagnant blood and promotes growth of new flesh (Zàngqì). Invigorates blood and moves qi, disperses swelling and resolves toxins; treats women's pain due to blood and qi [disorders], and menstrual blockage and congealing.

**[Elucidation]**
Li Shizhen states: Zǐjīng is cold in qi and bitter in flavor; its purple color and descending nature direct it into the blood aspect [血分] of the hand and foot jueyin [pericardium and liver channels]. Cold overcomes heat; bitterness penetrates the bones; purple enters the ying [nutritive level]. Therefore it can invigorate blood, reduce swelling, promote urination, and resolve toxins. Yang Qingsou's *Xianchuanfang* [*Immortal-Transmitted Formulary*] contains the formula Chōnghé Gāo ['Harmonizing Paste'], which uses Zǐjīng as the chief ingredient — a choice that reflects exactly this rationale. That formula treats all carbuncles, back-abscesses [發背], migratory deep abscesses [流注], and various swollen toxins where the distinction between cold and hot is unclear.

*Formula:* Zǐjīng bark (stir-fried) 3 liǎng; Dúhuó [Angelica pubescens] (nodes removed, stir-fried) 3 liǎng; Red peony root [赤芍藥] (stir-fried) 2 liǎng; Fresh Báizhǐ [Angelica dahurica] 1 liǎng; Mùlà [Ligustrum lucidum bark / 'tree wax'] (stir-fried) 1 liǎng. Grind to powder. Mix with scallion [蔥] broth and apply hot as a poultice. Blood moves when warmed, and scallion disperses qi. For sores that are not greatly hot, mix with wine instead. For severe pain, add frankincense [乳香]. For sinews that cannot stretch, also add frankincense.

In general, carbuncles, abscesses, and migratory deep abscesses are all caused by congealing and stagnation of qi and blood — they disperse with warmth and congeal with cold. This formula is warm and balanced. Zǐjīng bark is the essence of Wood [木之精]: it breaks blood and reduces swelling. Dúhuó is the essence of Earth [土之精]: it arrests wind-movement of blood, draws out toxins lodged in the bones, and removes painful obstruction [痺] and damp qi. Peony is the essence of Fire [火之精]: it engenders blood and stops pain. Mùlà is the essence of Water [水之精]: it reduces swelling and disperses blood, and together with Dúhuó can break up hard, stone-like swellings. Báizhǐ is the essence of Metal [金之精]: it expels wind, generates flesh, and stops pain. For when blood is engendered the tissue does not die; when blood moves it flows freely; when flesh is engendered it does not putrefy; when pain is stopped there is no ulceration; when wind is expelled the blood disperses of itself; when qi is broken up, hardness can be dissolved, and toxins are eliminated of themselves. With all five [ingredients] treating [the condition] in concert, how could any illness fail to be cured?

segments.title_inferred: Mujin (Rose of Sharon / Hibiscus syriacus) — Bark, Flowers, and Roots
segments.original_text:
【釋名】椴（音徒亂切）、櫬（音襯）、蕣（音舜）、日及（《綱目》）、朝開暮落花（《綱目》）、藩籬草（《綱目》）、花奴、王蒸。時珍曰︰此花朝開暮落，故名日及。曰槿曰蕣，猶僅榮一瞬之義也。《爾雅》云︰椴，木槿。櫬，木槿。郭璞注云︰別二名也。或云︰白曰椴，赤曰櫬。齊魯謂之王蒸，言其美而多也。《詩》云「顏如舜華」即此。

【集解】宗奭曰︰木槿花如小葵，淡紅色，五葉成一花，朝開暮斂。湖南北人家多種植為籬障。花與枝兩用。

花小而艷，或白或紅是也。結實輕虛，大葉可茹，作飲代茶。今瘍醫用皮治瘡癬，多取川中來者，濃而色紅。

皮並根

【氣味】甘，平，滑，無毒。大明曰︰涼。
同皮。
同皮。

【主治】止腸風瀉血，痢後熱渴，作飲服之，令人得睡，並炒用（藏器）。治赤白帶下，腫痛疥癬，洗目令明，潤燥活血（時珍）。
腸風瀉血，赤白痢，並焙入藥。作湯代茶，治風（大明）。消瘡腫，利小便，除濕熱（時珍）。
偏正頭風，燒煙熏患處。又治黃水膿瘡，燒存性，豬骨髓調塗之（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰木槿皮及花，並滑如葵花，故能潤燥。色如紫荊，故能活血。川中來者，氣濃力優，故尤有效。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Duàn [椴, pronounced tú-luàn-qiē], Chèn [櫬, pronounced chèn], Shùn [蕣, pronounced shùn], Rìjí [日及] (Bencao Gangmu), Zhāo kāi mù luò huā [朝開暮落花, 'Flower That Opens at Dawn and Falls at Dusk'] (Bencao Gangmu), Fánlí cǎo [藩籬草, 'Hedge Grass'] (Bencao Gangmu), Huānú [花奴, 'Flower Servant'], and Wángzhēng [王蒸].

Li Shizhen states: This flower opens in the morning and falls in the evening, hence the name Rìjí ['reaching only one day']. The names Jǐn [槿] and Shùn [蕣] carry the meaning of flourishing for only a single instant. The Erya states: 'Duàn is mùjǐn; Chèn is mùjǐn.' Guo Pu's commentary notes these are simply two different names for the same plant. Some say the white-flowered variety is called Duàn and the red-flowered variety is called Chèn. The people of Qi and Lu call it Wángzhēng, indicating its beauty and abundance. The line in the Book of Odes, 'A complexion like the Shùn flower,' refers to this plant.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Kou Zongshi states: The flower of mùjǐn resembles a small mallow [葵], is pale red in color, has five petals forming a single flower, opens at dawn, and closes at dusk. Families in Hunan and Hubei often plant it as a hedge or fence. Both the flowers and the branches are used medicinally.

The flowers are small but vivid, either white or red. The fruit is light and hollow. The large leaves are edible and can be steeped as a tea substitute. Physicians who treat sores [瘍醫] currently use the bark to treat sores and ringworm [瘡癬]; most prefer specimens from Sichuan, which are thick and reddish.

**Bark and Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Sweet, balanced [平], smooth, non-toxic. Da Ming states: cooling in nature.
[Flowers and leaves share the same properties as the bark.]

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Stops intestinal wind [腸風] with bloody diarrhea; for thirst and fever following dysentery, prepare as a decoction and drink it — it also promotes sleep. Used stir-fried [炒用]. (Chen Cangqi)
- Treats red and white vaginal discharge [赤白帶下], swelling and pain, scabies and ringworm [疥癬]; used as an eye wash to brighten vision; moistens dryness and invigorates the blood. (Li Shizhen)
- For intestinal wind with bloody stools and red or white dysentery, dry-roast [焙] and incorporate into medicine. Prepared as a tea-substitute decoction, it treats wind disorders. (Da Ming)
- Disperses sores and swelling, promotes urination, eliminates damp-heat. (Li Shizhen)
- For unilateral or bilateral headache [偏正頭風], burn and fumigate the affected area with the smoke. Also treats yellow-water festering sores [黃水膿瘡]: burn to preserve the medicinal properties [燒存性], mix with pig bone marrow, and apply topically. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elucidation]**
Li Shizhen states: The bark and flowers of mùjǐn are both smooth and slippery like mallow flowers, and thus have the ability to moisten dryness. Their color resembles that of redbud [紫荊], and thus they can invigorate the blood. Specimens from Sichuan are dense and potent in qi, and therefore especially effective.

segments.title_inferred: Fusang (Hibiscus rosa-sinensis) — Leaves and Flowers
segments.original_text:
【釋名】佛桑（《霏雪錄》）、朱槿（《草木狀》）

時珍曰：東海日出處有扶桑樹。此花光艷照日，其葉似桑，因以比之。後人訛為佛桑，乃木槿別種，故日及諸名亦與之同。

【集解】時珍曰：扶桑產南方，乃木槿別種。其枝柯柔弱，葉深綠，微澀如桑。其花有紅、黃、白三色，紅者尤貴，呼為朱槿。嵇含《草木狀》云：朱槿一名赤槿，一名日及，出高涼郡。花、莖、葉皆如桑。其葉光而濃。木高四、五尺，而枝葉婆娑。其花深紅色，五出，大如蜀葵，重敷柔澤。有蕊一條，長於花葉，上綴金屑，日光所爍，疑若焰生。一叢之上，日開數百朵，朝開暮落。自二月始，至中冬乃歇。插枝即活。

葉及花

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】癰疽腮腫，取葉或花，同白芙蓉葉、牛蒡葉、白蜜研膏敷之，即散（時珍）
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Fosang [Buddha-mulberry] (from *Feixue Lu*); Zhujin [Crimson Hibiscus] (from *Caomuzhuang*).

Li Shizhen states: In the eastern sea, where the sun rises, there is said to grow a great tree called Fusang. This flower is brilliantly radiant in sunlight, and its leaves resemble those of the mulberry, hence the comparison. Later people corrupted the name to 'Fosang' [Buddha-mulberry]. It is a distinct variety of Mujin [Hibiscus syriacus], and accordingly shares several of the same alternate names, including 'Riji' [daily renewal].

**Collected Explanations**
Li Shizhen states: Fusang is produced in the southern regions and is a distinct variety of Mujin. Its branches are slender and pliable, its leaves are deep green and slightly rough, resembling mulberry leaves. Its flowers come in three colors — red, yellow, and white — of which red is most prized, and is called Zhujin [Crimson Hibiscus]. Ji Han's *Caomuzhuang* [Records of Plants and Trees] states: Zhujin is also known as Chijin [Red Hibiscus] and Riji [Daily-opening]; it originates from Gaoliang Commandery. The flowers, stems, and leaves all resemble those of the mulberry. The leaves are glossy and thick. The tree grows four to five feet tall, with branches and foliage spreading gracefully. Its flowers are deep red, with five petals, as large as those of the Shu mallow [Alcea rosea], richly layered and softly lustrous. There is a single stamen column, longer than the petals, adorned at the tip with golden specks that glitter in sunlight as though flames were dancing. A single clump may open several hundred blossoms in a day; they open in the morning and fall by evening. Flowering begins in the second month [approximately March] and does not cease until mid-winter. It propagates readily from cuttings.

**Leaves and Flowers**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet; balanced [neutral]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment:** For carbuncles, abscesses, and swelling of the cheeks [parotid region]: take the leaves or flowers together with white Furong [Confederate rose, *Hibiscus mutabilis*] leaves, burdock [*Arctium lappa*] leaves, and white honey; grind into a paste and apply externally — the swelling will disperse. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Mù Fúróng (Confederate Rose / Cotton Rose): Names, Description, and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】地芙蓉（《圖經》）、木蓮（《綱目》）

時珍曰︰此花艷如荷花，故有芙蓉、木蓮之名。八、九月始開，故名拒霜。俗呼為芙皮樹。《相如賦》謂之華木。注云︰皮可為索也。蘇東坡詩云︰喚作拒霜猶未稱，看來卻是最宜霜。蘇頌《圖經本草》有地芙蓉，云出鼎州，九月採葉，治瘡。

【集解】時珍曰；木芙蓉處處有之，插條即生，小木也。其幹叢生如荊，高者丈許。其葉大如桐，有五尖及七尖者，冬凋夏茂。秋半始著花，花類牡丹、芍藥，有紅者、白者、黃者、千葉者，最耐寒而不落。不結實。山人取其皮為索。川、廣有添色拒霜花，初開白色，次日稍紅，又明日則深紅，先後相間如數色。霜時采花，霜後採葉，陰乾入藥。

葉並花

【氣味】微辛，平，無毒。

【主治】清肺涼血，散熱解毒，治一切大小癰疽腫毒惡瘡，消腫排膿止痛（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰芙蓉花並葉，氣平而不寒不熱，味微辛而性滑涎粘，其治癰腫之功，殊有神效。近時瘍醫秘其名為清涼膏、清露散、鐵箍散，皆此物也。其方治一切癰疽發背，乳癰惡瘡，不拘已成未成，已穿未穿。並用芙蓉葉，或根皮，或花，或生研，或乾研末，以蜜調塗於腫處四圍，中間留頭，乾則頻換。初起者，即覺清涼，痛止腫消。已成者，即膿聚毒出。已穿者，即膿出易斂。妙不可言。或加生赤小豆末，尤妙。
segments.translation:
**[Names]** Also called Dì Fúróng [Ground Lotus] (according to the *Illustrated Classic* [Tújīng]); Mù Lián [Woody Lotus] (according to the *Compendium* [Gāngmù]).

Li Shizhen states: The flower is as brilliantly beautiful as the lotus blossom [hé huā], hence the names Fúróng [Lotus] and Mù Lián [Woody Lotus]. It begins to bloom in the eighth and ninth months, and is therefore called Jù Shuāng [Frost-Defying]. Commonly it is called the Fúpí Tree [Bast Tree]. In the Rhapsody of Sima Xiangru it is referred to as Huá Mù [Splendid Wood]; the commentary notes that its bark can be used to make rope. Su Dongpo's poem reads: 'To call it Frost-Defying is still not quite right — looking at it, it clearly suits the frost best.' Su Song's *Illustrated Classic of Materia Medica* [Tújīng Běncǎo] includes an entry for Dì Fúróng, stating it comes from Dingzhou, that its leaves are gathered in the ninth month, and that it is used to treat sores.

**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Mù Fúróng grows everywhere; it readily propagates from cuttings, and is a small tree. Its trunk grows in dense clumps like the vitex shrub, and the tallest specimens reach about one zhàng [approx. 3 meters] in height. Its leaves are as large as those of the paulownia tree, with five or seven pointed lobes; they wither in winter and flourish in summer. It begins to flower in mid-autumn; the blossoms resemble those of the tree peony [mǔdān] and herbaceous peony [sháoyào], and occur in red, white, yellow, and double-petaled [qiānyè] forms. The flowers are exceptionally frost-hardy and do not fall. The plant bears no fruit. Mountain-dwelling people use the bark to make rope. In Sichuan and Guangdong there is a color-changing Jù Shuāng flower: when it first opens it is white, by the following day it is somewhat red, and by the day after that it is deep red, so that at any one time flowers of several colors appear together on the plant. Flowers are gathered at the time of frost; leaves are gathered after the frost. Both are shade-dried before use in medicine.

**Leaves and Flowers**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Slightly acrid [xīn], balanced [píng], non-toxic.

**[Indications]** Clears the lungs and cools the blood; disperses heat and resolves toxins; treats all manner of large and small abscesses [yōng] and swellings [jū], toxic swellings, and malignant sores; reduces swelling, drains pus, and relieves pain. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elaboration]** Li Shizhen states: The flowers and leaves of Mù Fúróng have a balanced qi — neither cold nor hot; the flavor is mildly acrid and the nature is slippery, mucilaginous, and adhesive. Their efficacy in treating abscesses and swellings is truly remarkable. Contemporary physicians specializing in sores [yáng yī] have given it secret proprietary names such as Qīngliáng Gāo [Cool and Refreshing Plaster], Qīnglù Sǎn [Clear Dew Powder], and Tiějū Sǎn [Iron-Binding Powder] — all of these are simply this same drug. The formula treats all abscesses [yōng] and swellings [jū] of the back, breast abscesses [rǔ yōng], and malignant sores, regardless of whether they have yet formed or already formed, already ulcerated or not yet ulcerated. One uses Fúróng leaves, or the bark of the root, or the flowers — either freshly ground or dried and powdered — mixed with honey and applied in a ring around the swelling, leaving an opening at the head; when it dries, it should be renewed frequently. At the onset [of a lesion], the patient immediately feels a cooling sensation; pain ceases and the swelling subsides. When the lesion has already formed, the pus is gathered and the toxin expelled. When the lesion has already ulcerated, the pus drains and the wound closes easily. The effects are beyond description. Adding freshly ground red adzuki bean [chì xiǎodòu] powder is even more effective.

segments.title_inferred: Mountain Camellia (Shancha) — Flower and Seed Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰其葉類茗，又可作飲，故得茶名。

【集解】時珍曰︰山茶產南方。樹生，高者丈許，枝幹交加。葉頗似茶葉，而濃硬有棱，中闊頭尖，面綠背淡。深冬開花，紅瓣黃蕊。《格古論》云︰花有數種︰寶珠者，花簇如珠，最勝。海榴茶花蒂青，石榴茶中有碎花，躑躅茶花如杜鵑花，宮粉茶、串珠茶皆粉紅色。又有一捻紅、千葉紅、千葉白等名，不可勝數，葉各小異。或云亦有黃色者。《虞衡志》廣中有南山茶，花大倍中州者，色微淡，葉薄有毛。結實如梨，大如拳，中有數核，如肥皂子大。周定王《救荒本草》云︰山茶嫩葉炸熟水淘可食，亦可蒸晒作飲。

花

【氣味】缺。

【主治】吐血衄血，腸風下血，並用紅者為末，入童溺、薑汁及酒調服，可代郁金（震亨）。湯火傷灼，研末，麻油調塗。

子
婦人發髮，研……
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Name:** Shizhen states: Its leaves resemble those of the tea plant [茗], and they can likewise be used to make a beverage; hence it acquired the name 'cha' [tea].

**Collected Commentaries:** Shizhen states: Mountain camellia [山茶] is produced in the southern regions. It grows as a tree, reaching a height of up to one zhang [approx. 3 metres] for the tallest specimens, with branches and trunk intertwining. The leaves closely resemble tea leaves but are thicker, stiffer, and ridged, broad in the middle and tapering to a point at the tip, green on the upper surface and pale beneath. Flowers open in the depths of winter, with red petals and yellow stamens. The 'Gegu Lun' [格古論, 'Discourse on the Investigation of Antiquities'] states: there are several varieties of flower. The 'Baozhu' [Precious Pearl] type, whose flower clusters resemble pearls, is the finest. The 'Hailiucha' [Sea-Pomegranate Camellia] has a greenish calyx; the 'Shiliucha' [Pomegranate Camellia] bears small, clustered petals within; the 'Zhizhicha' [Rhododendron Camellia] has flowers resembling azalea blooms; the 'Gongfencha' [Palace Pink Camellia] and 'Chuanzhicha' [Strung-Pearl Camellia] are both pink. There are further varieties named 'Yinianhong' [One-Pinch Red], 'Qianyehong' [Thousand-Petal Red], 'Qianyebai' [Thousand-Petal White], and so on — too many to enumerate — each with slight differences in leaf. Some report that yellow-flowered specimens also exist. The 'Yuhengzhi' [虞衡志] records that in the Guang [Guangdong/Guangxi] region there is a 'Southern Mountain Camellia' whose flowers are twice the size of those found in the central provinces, with a slightly paler colour; the leaves are thin and covered in fine hairs. It bears fruit resembling a pear, as large as a fist, containing several seeds inside, each approximately the size of a gleditsia seed [皁莢子]. Prince Zhouding's 'Jiuhuang Bencao' [救荒本草, 'Famine Relief Materia Medica'] states: the tender leaves of mountain camellia can be deep-fried, then rinsed in water and eaten; they may also be steamed and sun-dried to make a beverage.

**Flower**

**Flavor and Nature:** [Not recorded / missing from source.]

**Indications and Treatments:**
For vomiting of blood [吐血], nosebleed [衄血], and intestinal wind with bloody stool [腸風下血]: use the red-flowered variety, ground into powder, mixed with boys' urine [童溺], ginger juice, and wine, and taken orally. It may substitute for curcuma [郁金] in this application. (Attributed to Zhu Zhenheng [震亨].)
For scalds and burns from hot water or fire: grind into powder and mix with sesame oil [麻油] to apply topically.

**Seed**
For [women's hair-related condition — text is incomplete]: grind [the seed] … [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-entry; likely describes a preparation for hair treatment]

segments.title_inferred: Làméi (Wintersweet): Names, Varieties, and Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】黃梅花。

時珍曰︰此物本非梅類，因其與梅同時，香又相近，色似蜜蠟，故得此名。

【集解】時珍曰︰蠟梅小樹，叢枝尖葉。種凡三種︰以子種出不經接者，臘月開小花而香淡，名狗蠅梅；經接而花疏，開時含口者，名磬口梅；花密而香濃，色深黃如紫檀者，名檀香梅，最佳。結實如垂鈴，尖長寸余，子在其中。其樹皮浸水磨墨，有光采。

花

【氣味】辛，溫，無毒。

【主治】解暑生津（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**[Names]** Yellow Plum Blossom [Huángméi Huā].

Li Shizhen states: This plant is not truly a member of the plum [méi] family, but because it blooms at the same time as the plum, its fragrance is similar, and its color resembles beeswax [mìlà], it received this name.

**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Làméi is a small tree with clustered branches and pointed leaves. There are altogether three varieties: those grown from seed without grafting bloom with small flowers in the twelfth lunar month [làyuè] and have a light fragrance — these are called Gǒuyíng Méi ["Dog-fly Plum"]; those that have been grafted but bloom sparsely, with flowers that remain half-closed at the mouth when open, are called Qìngkǒu Méi ["Chime-mouth Plum"]; those with dense flowers, a rich fragrance, and a deep yellow color resembling zitan [rosewood/Pterocarpus santalinus], are called Tánxiāng Méi ["Sandalwood-fragrance Plum"] — this last is the finest. The fruit hangs like a drooping bell, pointed and over an inch in length, with seeds inside. The bark of the tree, when soaked in water and used to grind ink, imparts a lustrous sheen.

**Flowers**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Acrid [xīn], warm, non-toxic.

**[Primary Indications]** Relieves summerheat and promotes the generation of fluids [jiěshǔ shēngjīn]. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Fúniú Flower (Buxus-spine flower / Damnacanthus): Uses for Wind-Dampness and Pain
segments.original_text:
【釋名】隔虎刺花（未詳）。

【集解】頌曰︰伏牛花生蜀地，所在皆有，今惟益州蜀地有之，似黃柏葉而不光。莖赤有刺。開花淡黃色作穗，似杏花而小，三月採，陰乾。又睦州所生虎刺，云凌冬不凋，彼人無時采根、葉，治風腫疾。

花

【氣味】苦、甘，平，無毒。

【主治】久風濕痺，四肢拘攣，骨肉疼痛。

作湯，治風眩頭痛，五痔下血（《開寶》）。
一切腫痛風疾，細銼焙研，每服一錢匕，用溫酒調下（頌）。

【發明】時珍曰；伏牛花治風濕有名，而用者頗少。楊子建《護命方》有伏牛花散，治男女一切頭風，發作有時，甚則大腑熱秘。用伏牛花、山茵陳、桑寄生、白牽牛、川芎、白僵蠶、蠍梢各二錢，荊芥穗四錢，為末。每服二錢，水煎一沸，連滓服。

根、葉、枝
segments.translation:
**[Names]** Also called Géhǔcìhuā [Hedge Tiger-Thorn Flower] (origin of name unclear).

**[Collected Commentaries]** Su Song [Sòng] states: Fúniú flower grows in the Shu region [Sichuan]; it is found in many places, but today it is found only in Yizhou [Chengdu area] in Shu. Its leaves resemble those of Huángbò [Phellodendron] but are not glossy. The stems are red and bear thorns. It produces pale yellow flowers in spikes, resembling apricot blossoms but smaller. It is collected in the third month [March–April] and dried in the shade. There is also a Tiger-Thorn [Hǔcì] growing in Muzhou, said to remain evergreen through winter; the local people collect the roots and leaves at any season to treat wind-swelling ailments.

**Flower**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Bitter, sweet; balanced [ping]; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Chronic wind-dampness impediment [fēngshī bì], contracture of the four limbs, and aching pain in the bones and flesh.

Prepared as a decoction [tāng], it treats wind-dizziness [fēngxuàn], headache, and rectal bleeding from the five types of hemorrhoids [wǔzhì]. (Source: *Kāibǎo [Bencao]*)

For all swelling pain and wind ailments: finely chop, dry-roast, and grind into powder; take one qiánbǐ [approx. 1 qian, ~3–4 g] per dose, stirred into warm wine. (Source: Su Song)

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: Fúniú flower is well known for treating wind-dampness, yet it is seldom used in practice. Yang Zijian's *Hùmìng Fāng* [Formulary for Protecting Life] contains a formula called Fúniú Flower Powder [伏牛花散], used to treat all manner of head-wind [tóufēng] in men and women, attacks that occur at irregular intervals, and in severe cases, heat-constipation of the large intestine. The formula uses: Fúniú flower, mountain yinchen [Artemisia capillaris], Sāng jìshēng [Loranthus/mistletoe], white morning glory seeds [Bái qiānniú], Chuān xiōng [Ligusticum chuanxiong], white stiffened silkworm [Bái jiāngcán], and scorpion tail [Xiē shāo], each two qian; Jīngjié spike [Schizonepeta tenuifolia] four qian. Grind to a powder. Take two qian per dose, brought to a single boil in water, and swallowed together with the dregs.

**Roots, Leaves, and Branches** [entry continues — text not provided in this segment]

segments.title_inferred: Mìméng Flower (Buddleja officinalis) — Medicinal Uses and Description
segments.original_text:
【釋名】水錦花（《炮炙論》）。

時珍曰︰其花繁密蒙茸如簇錦，故名。

【集解】頌曰︰密蒙花，蜀中州郡皆有之。樹高丈餘。葉似冬青葉而濃，背白有細毛，又似橘葉。花微紫色。二月、三月採花，曝乾用。

宗奭曰︰利州甚多。葉冬不凋，亦不似冬青，柔而不光潔，不深綠。其花細碎，數十房成一朵，冬生春開。

花

【氣味】甘，平、微寒，無毒。

【主治】青盲膚翳，赤澀多眵淚，消目中赤脈，小兒麩豆及疳氣攻眼（《開寶》），羞明怕日（劉守真）。入肝經氣、血分，潤肝燥。
segments.translation:
**[Explanatory Names]** Water-brocade Flower [水錦花] (from the *Paozhi Lun* [Treatise on Preparation]).

Li Shizhen states: Its blossoms are densely clustered and fluffy, like gathered brocade, hence the name [mìméng, 'dense and lush'].

**[Collected Commentaries]** Su Song states: Mìméng flower is found throughout the prefectures and districts of Shu [modern Sichuan]. The tree grows to more than one zhang [approx. 3+ metres] in height. The leaves resemble those of winter-green [冬青, Ilex] but are thicker, with a white underside covered in fine hairs; they also resemble tangerine leaves. The flowers are a pale purple. Flowers are gathered in the second and third months [of the lunar calendar] and dried in the sun for use.

Kou Zongshi states: The plant is found in great abundance in Lizhou. Its leaves do not wither in winter, yet they do not truly resemble those of winter-green — they are soft rather than glossy and smooth, and not a deep green. The flowers are fine and delicate; tens of florets form a single cluster. They emerge in winter and open in spring.

**Flower**

**[Qi and Flavour]** Sweet; balanced [平], slightly cold; non-toxic.

**[Main Treatments]** Blue-blindness [青盲, amaurosis] and superficial eye-screen [膚翳, corneal opacity]; redness, roughness, and excessive discharge with tearing of the eyes; disperses red veins in the eye; in children: pox-related eye affections [麩豆] and *gan* [疳, nutritional-deficiency syndrome] qi attacking the eyes (from the *Kaibao [Bencao]*); photophobia and sensitivity to sunlight (Liu Shouzhen). [The flower] enters the qi and blood aspects of the Liver channel; moistens Liver dryness.

segments.title_inferred: Cotton (Mùmián) — Tree and Herbaceous Forms
segments.original_text:
【釋名】古貝（《綱目》）、古終。

時珍曰：木綿有二種：似木者名古貝，似草者名古終。或作吉貝者，乃古貝之訛也。梵書謂之婆，又曰迦羅婆劫。

【集解】時珍曰：木綿有草、木二種。交廣木綿，樹大如抱。其枝似桐。其葉大，如胡桃葉。入秋開花，紅如山茶花，黃蕊，花片極濃，為房甚繁，逼側相比。結實大如拳，實中有白綿，綿中有子。今人謂之斑枝花，訛為攀枝花。李延壽《南史》所謂林邑諸國出古貝花，中如鵝毳，抽其緒，紡為布；張勃《吳錄》所謂交州、永昌木綿樹高過屋，有十餘年不換者，實大如杯，花中綿軟白，可為絮及毛布者，皆指似木之木綿也。江南、淮北所種木綿，四月下種，莖弱如蔓，高者四、五尺，葉有三尖如楓葉，入秋開花黃色，如葵花而小，亦有紅紫者，結實大如桃，中有白綿，綿中有子，大如梧子，亦有紫綿者，八月採，謂之綿花；李延壽《南史》所謂高昌國有草，實如繭，中絲為細，名曰白疊，取以為帛，甚軟白；沈懷遠《南越志》所謂桂州出古終藤，結實如鵝毳，核如珠，治出其核，紡如絲綿，染為斑布者，皆指似草之木綿也。此種出南番，宋末始入江南，今則遍及江北與中州矣。不蠶而綿，不麻而布，利被天下，其益大哉。又《南越志》言：南詔諸蠻不養蠶，惟收娑羅木子中白絮，紉為絲，織為幅，名娑羅籠段。祝穆《方輿志》言：平緬出娑羅樹，大者高三、五丈，結子有綿，紉綿織為白氈兜羅錦。此亦斑枝花之類，各方稱呼不同耳。

白綿及布

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。
辛，熱，微毒。

【主治】血崩金瘡，燒灰用（時珍）。

子油（用兩瓶合燒取瀝。）
惡瘡疥癬。燃燈，損目（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Also known as Gǔbèi [古貝] (Bencao Gangmu) and Gǔzhōng [古終].

Li Shizhen states: There are two kinds of mùmián [cotton/kapok]: the tree-form is called Gǔbèi, and the herb-form is called Gǔzhōng. The variant spelling Jíbèi [吉貝] is a corruption of Gǔbèi. Sanskrit texts call it [pō-pó / bā], and also Jiāluó-pójié [迦羅婆劫].

**Collected Explanations**
Li Shizhen states: There are two kinds of mùmián — the herb-form and the tree-form.

The mùmián of Jiao[zhou] and Guang[zhou] is a tree as large as one can wrap one's arms around. Its branches resemble the paulownia. Its leaves are large, like walnut leaves. In autumn it flowers, red as camellia blossoms, with yellow stamens; the petals are very thick, and the flower clusters are extremely dense, pressed close together. The fruits are as large as a fist; inside the fruit is white floss [bái mián], and within the floss are seeds. People today call it bānzhīhuā [斑枝花], sometimes corrupted to pānzhīhuā [攀枝花]. Li Yanshou's Nanshi [History of the Southern Dynasties] states that the countries of Linyi and others produce the Gǔbèi flower, whose interior is like goose down; one draws out the fibers and spins them into cloth. Zhang Bo's Wulu [Records of Wu] states that in Jiaozhou and Yongchang the mùmián trees grow taller than houses; some go more than ten years without being replaced; the fruits are as large as cups; the floss within the flowers is soft and white, and can be used for wadding and woolen cloth — all of these refer to the tree-form of mùmián.

The mùmián cultivated in Jiangnan and north of the Huai River is sown in the fourth month. The stems are slender and vine-like, growing four to five feet tall at most. The leaves have three points like maple leaves. In autumn it flowers yellow, smaller than a mallow blossom, though red and purple varieties also exist. The fruits are as large as peaches; inside is white floss, and within the floss are seeds as large as parasol-tree seeds [wútóng seeds]; purple-floss varieties also exist. It is harvested in the eighth month and is called miánhuā [綿花, cotton flower]. Li Yanshou's Nanshi states that in the kingdom of Gaochang there is a plant whose fruits resemble cocoons, with fine silk-like fibers inside, called báidié [白疊, white cotton]; cloth made from it is very soft and white. Shen Huaiyuan's Nanyue Zhi [Records of Southern Yue] states that Guizhou produces the Gǔzhōng vine, whose fruits resemble goose down, with pits like beads; after the pits are removed, the fibers are spun like silk-floss and dyed into patterned cloth — all of these refer to the herb-form of mùmián.

This variety originated in the southern barbarian lands and only entered Jiangnan at the end of the Song dynasty; today it has spread throughout Jiangbei and the Central Plains. It produces floss without silkworms, and cloth without hemp — its benefits extend across the world; truly its value is immense.

The Nanyue Zhi also states: The various Man [barbarian] peoples of Nanzhao do not raise silkworms; they collect only the white floss from the seeds of the suōluó tree [娑羅木], twist it into thread, and weave it into cloth, called suōluólóngduàn [娑羅籠段]. Zhu Mu's Fangyu Zhi [Gazetteer of Regions] states: Pingmian [in present-day Burma] produces the suōluó tree; the largest grow thirty to fifty feet tall; the seeds bear floss, which is twisted and woven into white felt and dūluójǐn [兜羅錦, duola brocade]. These too are of the same class as bānzhīhuā, merely called by different names in different regions.

**White Floss and Cloth made from it**

*Flavor and Nature:* Sweet, warm, non-toxic.
[The seed oil is:] Acrid, hot, slightly toxic.

*Primary Therapeutic Uses:*
- Uterine flooding [blood collapse, 血崩] and sword/metal wounds [金瘡]: burn to ash and apply. (Li Shizhen)

**Seed Oil** (obtained by placing [seeds] between two bottles and burning to collect the drippings):
- Malignant sores [惡瘡], scabies [疥], and ringworm [癬].
- When burned as lamp oil, it damages the eyes. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Zuò Wood (Chisel-Handle Tree) — Bark Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】鑿子木。

時珍曰︰此木堅韌，可為鑿柄，故俗名鑿子木。方書皆作柞木，蓋昧此義也。柞乃橡櫟之名，非此木也。

【集解】藏器曰︰柞木生南方，細葉，今之作梳者是也。

時珍曰︰此木處處山中有之，高者丈餘。葉小而有細齒，光滑而韌。其木及葉丫皆有針刺，經冬不凋。五月開碎白花，不結子。其木心理皆白色。

木皮

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。時珍曰︰酸，澀。

【主治】黃膽病，燒末，水服方寸匕，日三（藏器）。治鼠瘻難產，催生利竅，腫毒癰疽（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** [Also called] Záozi Wood [Chisel-Handle Wood].

Li Shizhen says: This wood is hard and tough, suitable for making chisel handles; hence its common name Záozi Wood [Chisel-Handle Wood]. Medical texts all write it as Zuò Wood (柞木), but this misses the original meaning. Zuò (柞) is actually the name for oak [Quercus species], not this tree.

**Collected Explanations:** [Chen] Cangqi says: Zuò Wood grows in the southern regions; its leaves are small and fine, and it is the wood presently used to make combs.

Li Shizhen says: This tree is found throughout mountain regions everywhere, and the taller specimens exceed one zhàng [approx. 3 meters] in height. Its leaves are small with fine teeth, smooth, and tough. Both the wood and the leaf axils bear needle-like thorns. It does not shed its leaves in winter. In the fifth month it produces tiny white flowers and does not bear fruit. The heartwood and grain of the wood are entirely white.

**Bark of the Wood**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter, balanced [neutral], non-toxic. Li Shizhen says: [Also] sour and astringent.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Jaundice [huángdǎn disease]: burn [the bark] to powder and take one fāngcùn-bǐ [approx. 1–2 g] dissolved in water, three times daily. (Cangqi)
- Scrofula [rat sores / lǔ fistulas], difficult labor — to promote parturition and open the passages; swollen toxins, carbuncles, and abscesses. (Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Evergreen Tree (Budiao Mu) — Monograph
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰：生太白山岩谷。樹高二、三尺，葉似槐，莖赤有毛如棠梨，四時不凋。

【氣味】苦，溫，無毒。

【主治】調中補衰，治腰腳，去風氣，卻老變白（藏器）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Commentaries】Chen Cangqi states: It grows in the rocky valleys of Mount Taibai. The tree stands two to three chi [roughly 2–3 feet] tall; its leaves resemble those of the scholar tree [Sophora japonica]; its stems are red and bear hairs like those of the crabapple [Malus/Pyrus species]; it does not shed its leaves in any of the four seasons.

【Qi and Flavor】Bitter; warm; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】Regulates and harmonizes the middle [digestive system] and supplements debility; treats pain and weakness of the lower back and legs; dispels wind-qi [rheumatic or arthritic conditions]; retards aging and reverses whitening of the hair. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Maizimu / Selling-Child Wood (賣子木)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】買子木。

【集解】恭曰︰賣子木出嶺南、邛州山谷中。其葉似柿。

頌曰︰今惟川西、渠州歲貢，作買子木。木高五、七尺，徑寸許。春生嫩枝條，葉尖，長一、二寸，俱青綠色，枝梢淡紫色。四、五月開碎花，百十枝圍攢作大朵，焦紅色。隨花便生子如椒目，在花瓣中黑而光潔，每株花裁三、五大朵爾。五月採其枝葉用。

時珍曰︰《宋史》渠州貢買子木並子，則子亦當與枝葉同功，而本草缺載，無從考訪。

木

【氣味】甘、微鹹，平，無毒。

【主治】折傷血內溜，續絕補骨髓，止痛安胎（《唐本》）。
segments.translation:
**Explanations of Names:** Also written as 買子木 (Maizimu, 'Buying-Child Wood').

**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Maizimu grows in the mountain valleys of Lingnan and Qiongzhou. Its leaves resemble those of the persimmon.

Su Song states: Nowadays it is only Chuanxi [western Sichuan] and Quzhou that present it as an annual tribute, written as 買子木. The tree grows five to seven feet tall, with a trunk diameter of about one cun [~3 cm]. In spring it puts forth tender shoots and branches; the leaves are pointed, one to two cun long, all of a blue-green color, while the tips of the branches are a pale purple. In the fourth and fifth months [May–June] it bears small flowers, with a hundred or more branchlets clustered together into large compound clusters of a scorched-red color. Fruits form immediately after flowering, resembling pepper seeds [椒目], black and glossy within the petals; each plant bears only three to five large flower clusters. The branches and leaves are collected for use in the fifth month [June].

Li Shizhen states: The Song History records that Quzhou submitted 買子木along with its seeds as tribute; thus the seeds ought to share the same medicinal properties as the branches and leaves. However, the materia medica records make no mention of the seeds, and there is no further source from which to investigate the matter.

**Wood**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, slightly salty; neutral; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** Internal bleeding from fractures and traumatic injuries [血內溜]; reconnecting severed [tendons and bones] and replenishing the bone marrow; relieving pain; and calming the fetus [i.e., preventing miscarriage]. (From the *Tang Bencao* [Tang Materia Medica].)

segments.title_inferred: Mù Tiān Liǎo (Wood Polygonum / Actinidia polygama) — Branches, Leaves, Root, and Fruit
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰：其樹高而味辛如蓼，故名。又馬蓼亦名天蓼而物異。

【集解】恭曰：木天蓼所在皆有，生山谷中。今安州、申州作藤蔓，葉似柘，花白，子如棗許，無定形，中瓤似茄子，味辛，啖之以當薑、蓼。

藏器曰：木蓼，今時所用出山南鳳州。樹高如冬青，不凋。不當以藤天蓼為注，既云木蓼，豈是藤生？自有藤蓼耳。藤蓼生江南、淮南山中，藤著樹生，葉如梨，光而薄，子如棗，即蘇恭以為木天蓼者。又有小天蓼，生天目山、四明山，樹如梔子，冬月不凋，野獸食之。是有三天蓼，俱能逐風，而小者為勝。

頌曰：木天蓼今出信陽。木高二、三丈。三月、四月開花似柘花。五月採子，子作球形似麻，子可藏作果食。蘇恭所說自是藤天蓼也。

時珍曰：天蓼雖有三種，而功用仿佛，蓋一類也。其子可為燭，其芽可食。故陸機云：木蓼為燭，明如胡麻。薛田《詠蜀詩》有「地丁葉嫩和嵐采，天蓼芽新入粉煎」之句。

枝葉

【氣味】辛，溫，有小毒。
甘，溫，無毒。
苦、辛，微熱，無毒。

【主治】症結積聚，風勞虛冷，細切釀酒飲（《唐本》）。
一切風虛羸冷，手足疼痺，無論老幼輕重，浸酒及煮汁服之。十許日，覺皮膚間風出如蟲行（藏器）。
賊風口面喎斜，冷癖氣塊，女子虛勞（甄權）。

根
風蟲牙痛，搗丸塞之，連易四、五次，除根。勿咽汁（時珍，出《普濟》）。

【發明】藏器曰：木天蓼出深山中，人云久服損壽，以其逐風損氣故也。藤天蓼、小天蓼三者，俱能逐風。其中優劣，小者為勝。

子
segments.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen says: The tree grows tall and its flavor is pungent [xīn] like polygonum [liǎo], hence the name. Note also that Horse Polygonum [mǎ liǎo] is also called Tiān Liǎo, but it is a different plant.

**Collected Commentaries**
Su Gong says: Mù Tiān Liǎo is found everywhere, growing in mountain valleys. In Anzhou and Shenzhou today it grows as a climbing vine. The leaves resemble those of Cudrania [zhè], the flowers are white, and the fruits are roughly the size of jujubes, irregular in shape; the inner flesh resembles eggplant, the flavor is pungent, and it may be eaten as a substitute for ginger and polygonum.

Chen Cangqi says: Mù Liǎo [Wood Polygonum], as currently used in medicine, comes from Fengzhou in Shannan. The tree grows as tall as Chinese holly [dōngqīng] and does not shed its leaves. It should not be glossed as the same as Vine Tiān Liǎo — if it is called a tree [mù], how could it be a vine? There is a separate Vine Polygonum [téng liǎo]. Vine Polygonum grows in the mountains of Jiangnan and Huainan, climbing trees; its leaves resemble those of the pear, smooth and thin; its fruits are like jujubes. This is what Su Gong took to be Mù Tiān Liǎo. There is also a Small Tiān Liǎo, growing on Mount Tianmu and Mount Siming; the tree resembles gardenia [zhīzǐ] and does not shed its leaves in winter; wild animals eat it. Thus there are three kinds of Tiān Liǎo, all capable of expelling wind [qū fēng], but the small variety is the best.

Su Song says: Mù Tiān Liǎo today comes from Xinyang. The tree reaches two to three zhàng in height. It flowers in the third and fourth months, resembling Cudrania flowers. The fruits are harvested in the fifth month; they are spherical, resembling hemp seeds, and can be preserved and eaten as a fruit. What Su Gong described is clearly Vine Tiān Liǎo.

Li Shizhen says: Although there are three kinds of Tiān Liǎo, their functions are similar — they are of the same general class. The fruits can be used as candles, and the young shoots are edible. Lu Ji accordingly wrote: "Mù Liǎo makes candles that burn as bright as sesame." Xue Tian's poem "Ode to Shu" contains the line: "The tender leaves of dìdīng are gathered with the mountain mist; the fresh buds of Tiān Liǎo are fried in a flour batter."

---
**Branches and Leaves**

*Qi and Flavor:* Pungent, warm, slightly toxic. / Sweet, warm, non-toxic. / Bitter and pungent, slightly heating, non-toxic.

*Indications and Treatments:*
- Abdominal masses [zhèng jié], accumulations and gatherings [jī jù], wind taxation [fēng láo] with vacuity and cold: finely chop and brew into wine for drinking. (*Tang Bencao*)
- All conditions of wind vacuity with emaciation and cold, pain and numbness of the hands and feet, regardless of the patient's age or the severity of the condition: steep in wine or decoct the juice for oral administration. Within approximately ten days, the patient will sense wind leaving the skin as though insects were crawling through it. (Chen Cangqi)
- Deviated mouth and face from malign wind [zéi fēng], cold aggregations [lěng pǐ] and qi lumps, vacuity taxation in women. (Zhen Quan)

---
**Root**

*Treatment:* Toothache due to wind or worms: pound into pills and pack into the tooth cavity; replace four or five times in succession to eradicate [the condition]. Do not swallow the juice. (Li Shizhen, citing the *Puji Fang*)

[Condition treated: dental pain (wind/worm toothache). Preparation: root pounded into pill form, applied topically into the tooth cavity, renewed repeatedly.]

---
**Elucidation**
Chen Cangqi says: Mù Tiān Liǎo comes from deep in the mountains. People say that prolonged use shortens life, because it expels wind but in doing so depletes qi. All three — Mù Tiān Liǎo, Vine Tiān Liǎo, and Small Tiān Liǎo — are capable of expelling wind. Among the three, the small variety is the best.

---
**Fruit [Zǐ]**
*(Entry appears to continue in the source but is not given here.)*

segments.title_inferred: Fangzhang Wood (Staff-Releasing Wood)
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰︰生溫、括、睦、婺諸州山中。樹如木天蓼。老人服之，一月放杖，故以為名。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】一切風血，理腰腳，輕身變白不老，浸酒服之（藏器）。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】Chen Cangqi [藏器] states: It grows in the mountains of Wen, Kuo, Mu, and Wu prefectures. The tree resembles Mu Tianliao [木天蓼, Actinidia polygama]. When elderly people take it, they are able to set aside their walking sticks within one month, hence the name ['Staff-Releasing Wood'].

【Qi and Flavor】Sweet, warm, non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】All wind[-related] and blood[-related] conditions; regulates and strengthens the lower back and legs; lightens the body, turns [the hair] white [i.e., reverses graying], and prevents aging. Prepared by steeping in wine and drinking (Chen Cangqi).

segments.title_inferred: Jiegu Wood (Elderberry): Names, Description, and Medical Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】續骨木（《綱目》）、木蒴藋。

頌曰︰接骨以功而名。花、葉都類蒴藋、陸英、水芹輩，故一名木蒴藋。

【集解】恭曰︰所在皆有之。葉如陸英，花亦相似。但作樹高一、二丈許，木體輕虛無心。斫枝插之便生，人家亦種之。

【氣味】甘、苦，平，無毒。

藏器曰︰搗汁亦吐人，有小毒。

【主治】折傷，續筋骨，除風癢齲齒，可作浴湯（《唐本》）。

根皮︰主痰飲，下水腫及痰瘧，煮汁服之，當利下及吐出。不可多服（藏器）。打傷瘀血，痰瘧，大人七葉，小兒三葉，生搗汁服，取吐（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Names and Appellations**
Also known as Xugu Wood [Bone-Continuing Wood] (Bencao Gangmu) and Mu Shuodi [Tree Elder].

Su Song states: The name 'Jiegu' [Bone-Joining] derives from its therapeutic function. Its flowers and leaves closely resemble those of Shuodi [dwarf elder, Sambucus ebulus], Lu Ying [Chinese elder, Sambucus chinensis], and water parsley [Oenanthe spp.], hence the alternate name Mu Shuodi [Tree Elder].

**Collected Explanations**
Su Gong states: It is found everywhere. The leaves resemble those of Lu Ying, and the flowers are similarly alike. However, it grows as a tree reaching one to two zhang [approximately 3–6 metres] in height, with wood that is light, hollow, and pithy [literally: without a heart]. Cuttings struck from trimmed branches will readily take root, and people also cultivate it in their gardens.

**Flavour and Nature**
Sweet and bitter; neutral [balanced]; non-toxic.

Chen Cangqi states: The juice, when pounded and consumed, can induce vomiting — it has a mild toxicity.

**Indications and Treatments**
Treats fractures and wounds; rejoins sinews and bones; eliminates wind-itch [pruritus] and tooth decay [dental caries]; may be prepared as a bath decoction. (Tang Bencao)

Root bark: Governs phlegm-fluid accumulation [tanyin]; disperses water-swelling [oedema] and phlegm malaria [tanüe]. Boil to extract juice and take orally; it should promote purgation downward and emesis upward. Do not take in excessive amounts. (Chen Cangqi)

For traumatic injury with blood stasis and phlegm malaria: for adults, use seven leaves; for children, use three leaves — pound raw and take the expressed juice orally to induce vomiting. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Lingshumu (Longevity Staff Wood) — Root Bark Monograph
segments.original_text:
【釋名】扶老杖（孟康）、椐。

【集解】藏器曰︰生劍南山谷。圓長皮紫。《漢書》︰孔光年老，賜︰木似竹有節，長不過八、九尺，圍三、四寸，自然有合杖製，不須削理。作杖，令人延年益壽。

時珍曰︰陸氏《詩疏》云︰椐即靈壽也。節中腫，似扶老，即今靈壽也。人以作杖及馬鞭。

弘農郡共北山有之。

根皮

【氣味】苦，平。

【主治】止水（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Explanatory Names:** Fú lǎo zhàng ['staff for supporting the elderly'] (Meng Kang); jū [an archaic plant name].

**Collected Commentaries:** Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: It grows in the mountain valleys of Jiannan [present-day Sichuan/Yunnan region]. The stem is round and elongated, with purple bark. According to the *Book of Han*: when Kong Guang was old, he was granted [a staff of this wood]. The wood resembles bamboo in having nodes; it grows no longer than eight or nine feet, with a girth of three to four inches. It is naturally suited to making a walking staff without need for trimming or shaping. Made into a staff, it causes people to live long and enjoy longevity.

Li Shizhen states: Lu Ji's *Commentary on the Odes* [Shi Shu] says: jū is none other than [lingshòu]. The internodes swell outward, resembling a support for the elderly — this is what is today called língshòu mù. People use it to make staffs and horse whips.

It is found in the northern mountains of Hongnong Commandery.

**Root Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Bitter; balanced [ping, i.e., neutral in temperature].

**Indications:** Stops [pathological] water [i.e., resolves edema/fluid retention] (Chen Cangqi).

segments.title_inferred: Muma (Wood Hemp) — Medicinal Plant Entry
segments.original_text:
【集解】藏器曰：生江南山谷林澤。葉似胡麻相對，山人取以釀酒飲。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】老血，婦人月閉，風氣羸瘦症瘕。久服，令人有子。
segments.translation:
【Collected Explanations】 Chen Cangqi states: It grows in the mountain valleys, forests, and marshes of Jiangnan [the region south of the Yangtze River]. Its leaves resemble those of sesame [Sesamum indicum] and grow in opposite pairs. Mountain dwellers gather it to brew wine for drinking.

【Qi and Flavor】 Sweet; warm; non-toxic.

【Indications and Treatments】 [Used to treat] stagnant old blood, amenorrhea [menstrual closure] in women, wind-qi [wind-related qi disorders] with emaciation, and abdominal masses [zheng-jia accumulations]. Prolonged consumption enables a person to conceive children [promotes fertility].

segments.title_inferred: Dakong (大空) — Root Bark for Killing Lice and Intestinal Worms
segments.original_text:
【集解】恭曰︰大空生襄州，所在山谷中亦有之，秦隴人名獨空。作小樹，抽條高六、七尺。葉似楮，小圓濃。根皮赤色。

時珍曰︰小樹大葉，似桐葉而不尖，深綠而皺紋。根皮虛軟，山人采殺虱極妙。搗葉篩蔬圃中，殺蟲。

根皮

【氣味】辛、苦，平，有小毒。

【主治】殺三蟲。作末和油塗發，蟣虱皆死（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Su Gong states: Dakong [大空, 'Great Hollow'] grows in Xiangzhou [modern Hubei/Henan region] and is also found in mountain valleys throughout various localities. The people of Qin and Long [northwest China] call it Dukong [獨空, 'Solitary Hollow']. It forms a small tree, sending up branches to a height of six or seven chi [roughly 6–7 feet]. The leaves resemble those of paper mulberry [楮, Broussonetia papyrifera], being small, rounded, and thick. The root bark is red in color.

Li Shizhen states: It is a small tree with large leaves resembling those of the paulownia [桐] but not pointed at the tip; they are deep green and wrinkled. The root bark is spongy and soft. Mountain dwellers harvest it and find it extremely effective for killing lice. The leaves, when pounded and sifted over vegetable gardens, kill insects.

**Root Bark**

**Flavor and Nature:** Acrid [辛], bitter [苦]; balanced [平]; mildly toxic [有小毒].

**Principal Treatments:** Kills the three types of intestinal worms [三蟲]. Ground into powder and mixed with oil, then applied to the hair, it kills both nits and lice [蟣虱] alike. (Attributed to Chen Cangqi [藏器], i.e., *Bencao Shiyi*.)

segments.title_inferred: Yi (Black Amber) — Wood Category Four
segments.original_text:
【釋名】瑿珀。

斆曰︰瑿是眾珀之長，故號瑿珀。

時珍曰︰亦作䃜。其色黳黑，故名。

【集解】恭曰︰古來相傳松脂千年為茯苓，又千年為琥珀，又千年為瑿。二物燒之皆有松氣。狀似玄玉而輕。出西戎，而有茯苓處無此物。今西州南三百里黑磧中得者，大則方尺，黑潤而輕，燒之腥臭。高昌人名為木瑿，謂玄玉為石瑿。共州土石間得者，燒作松氣，功同琥珀，見風拆破，不堪為器。恐此二種及琥珀，或非松脂所為也。慎微曰︰《梁公子傳》︰奈公云︰交河之間平磧中，掘深一丈，下有瑿珀，黑逾純漆，或大如車輪。末服，攻婦人小腸癥瘕諸疾。時珍曰︰瑿即琥珀之黑色者，或因土色熏染，或是一種木瀋結成，未必是千年琥珀復化也。《玉策經》言︰松脂千年作茯苓，茯苓千年作琥珀，琥珀千年作石膽，石膽千年作威喜。大抵皆是神異之說，未可深憑。雷斆琥珀下所說諸珀可據。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】補心安神，破血生肌，治婦人癥瘕（《唐本》）。小兒帶之辟惡，磨滴目翳赤障（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names.** Also called Yipò [Yi-amber].

Lei Xiao states: Yi is the foremost among all ambers, hence it is called Yipò.

Li Shizhen states: It is also written as 䃜 [an alternate character]. Its color is jet-black [黳黑], hence the name.

**Collected Explanations.** Su Gong states: It has long been transmitted that pine resin after one thousand years becomes Poria fungus [茯苓], after another thousand years becomes amber [琥珀], and after yet another thousand years becomes Yi. Both substances, when burned, emit a pine-like odor. In form they resemble dark jade [玄玉] but are lighter. Yi comes from the Western Rong [western frontier peoples], and in places where Poria grows, this substance is not found. That which is now obtained in the black gravel desert three hundred li south of Xizhou — at its largest it measures a square foot, is black, lustrous, and light, and when burned emits a rank, foul smell. The people of Gaochang call it Mù-yī [wood-yi], while they call dark jade Shí-yī [stone-yi]. That which is obtained from among the earth and stones of Gongzhou, when burned, gives off a pine-like odor, its efficacy is the same as amber, but it cracks and breaks upon exposure to wind and cannot be fashioned into implements. I suspect that these two varieties, as well as amber itself, may not be derived from pine resin at all. Tang Shenwei states: The Lianggongzi zhuan records that a certain Master Nai said: In the flat gravel plains between the rivers of Jiaohe, if one digs down one zhang [approximately 3 meters], Yi-amber is found below — blacker than pure lacquer, and some pieces as large as a cart wheel. Taken in powdered form, it treats masses [癥] and accumulations [瘕] and various ailments of the small intestine in women.

Li Shizhen states: Yi is simply a black-colored variety of amber. It may have become darkened through being stained by the color of the surrounding soil, or it may be formed from the congealed sap [木瀋] of a particular kind of tree; it is not necessarily amber of one thousand years' age that has transformed further. The Yuce Jing [Classic of Jade Tallies] states: Pine resin after one thousand years becomes Poria fungus; Poria fungus after one thousand years becomes amber; amber after one thousand years becomes bile-stone [石膽]; bile-stone after one thousand years becomes Weixi [威喜, a type of mineral]. These are for the most part miraculous and fantastical claims and cannot be relied upon deeply. The various ambers described by Lei Xiao under the amber entry may be consulted as a credible reference.

**Flavor and Nature.** Sweet; balanced [平]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatment.** Nourishes the heart and calms the spirit; breaks up [stagnant] blood and promotes tissue regeneration; treats abdominal masses and accumulations [癥瘕] in women (Tang Materia Medica [《唐本》]). Worn by children, it wards off malign influences; ground and applied as drops, it treats eye nebulae [目翳] and red visual obstructions [赤障] (Chen Cangqi [藏器]).

segments.title_inferred: Zhu Ling (Polyporus / Pig's Fungus) — Wood Section Four
segments.original_text:
【釋名】豭豬屎（《本經》）、豕橐（《莊子》）、地烏桃（《圖經》）。

弘景曰：其塊黑似豬屎，故以名之。司馬彪注《莊子》云：豕橐一名苓，其根似豬矢。是也。

時珍曰：馬屎曰通，豬屎曰零（即苓字），其塊零落而下故也。

【集解】《別錄》曰：豬苓生衡山山谷，及濟陰冤句。二月、八月採，陰乾。

弘景曰：是楓樹苓，其皮黑色，肉白而實者佳，削去皮用。

頌曰：今蜀州、眉州亦有之。生土底，不必楓根下始有也。

時珍曰：豬苓亦是木之餘氣所結，如鬆之餘氣結茯苓之義。他木皆有，楓木為多耳。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

普曰︰神農︰甘。雷公︰苦，無毒。權曰︰微熱。元素曰︰氣平味甘，氣味俱薄，升而微降，與茯苓同。

杲曰︰淡、甘，平，降也，陽中陰也。好古曰︰甘重於苦，陽也。入足太陽、足少陰經。

【主治】痎瘧，解毒、蠱疰、不祥，利水道。久服，輕身耐老（《本經》）。解傷寒、溫疫、大熱，發汗，主腫脹滿、腹急痛（甄權）。治渴除濕，去心中懊憹（元素）。瀉膀胱（好古）。開腠理，治淋腫腳氣，白濁帶下，妊娠子淋胎腫，小便不利（時珍）。

【發明】頌曰︰張仲景治消渴脈浮、小便不利、微熱者，豬苓散發其汗。病欲飲水而複吐，名為水逆，冬時寒嗽如瘧狀者，亦與豬苓散，此即五苓散也。豬苓、茯苓、朮各三分，澤瀉五分，桂二分，細搗篩，水服方寸匕，日三。多飲暖水，汗出即愈。利水道諸湯劑，無若此快，今人皆用之。

杲曰︰苦以泄滯，甘以助陽，淡以利竅，故能除濕利小便。

宗奭曰︰豬苓行水之功多，久服必損腎氣，昏人目。久服者宜詳審之。

元素曰︰豬苓淡滲，大燥亡津液，無濕癥者勿服之。

時珍曰：豬苓淡滲，氣升而又能降。故能開腠理，利小便，與茯苓同功。但入補藥不如茯苓也。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names**
Aliases: *Jiazhu shi* [boar's dung] (Shennong Bencao Jing), *shi tuo* [pig's sack] (Zhuangzi), *di wu tao* [earth black-peach] (Tujing).

Tao Hongjing [Hongjing] states: Its lumps are black and resemble pig dung, hence the name. Sima Biao's commentary on the Zhuangzi says: '*Shi tuo* is also called *ling*; its roots resemble pig droppings.' This is indeed the same thing.

Li Shizhen [Shizhen] states: Horse dung is called *tong* [通]; pig dung is called *ling* [零, i.e., the character *ling* 苓], because the lumps scatter and fall downward, hence the name.

**Collected Commentaries**
Bielu states: Zhu ling grows in the mountain valleys of Hengshan and in Jiyin Yuanju. It is gathered in the second and eighth months and dried in the shade.

Hongjing states: It is the fungus of the maple tree [楓樹苓]. Those with a black skin and white, solid flesh are the finest; the skin is pared away before use.

Su Song [Song] states: Today it is also found in Shuzhou and Meizhou. It grows underground and need not necessarily grow only beneath maple tree roots.

Shizhen states: Zhu ling is likewise formed from the residual vital energy [餘氣] of trees, in the same way that pine trees' residual energy gives rise to fu ling [Poria cocos]. It can form on other trees as well, but the maple tree is most common.

**Qi and Flavor**
Sweet, neutral, non-toxic.

Zhu Pu [Pu] states — Shennong: sweet. Lei Gong: bitter, non-toxic. [Chen] Quan states: mildly warming. [Zhang] Yuansu states: its qi is neutral and its flavor sweet; both qi and flavor are thin [i.e., not strongly concentrated]; it rises and slightly descends — the same as fu ling.

Li Gao [Gao] states: bland and sweet, neutral; it descends; it is yin within yang. Wang Haogu [Haogu] states: sweetness predominates over bitterness; it is yang. It enters the foot taiyang and foot shaoyin channels.

**Indications and Treatments**
— Malarial fevers [痎瘧], detoxification, [treatment of] gu-poisoning [蠱疰] and inauspicious conditions, and promoting the flow of the water pathways. Long-term use lightens the body and retards aging. (Shennong Bencao Jing)
— Resolving cold-damage [傷寒] and warm-epidemic [溫疫] fevers, high fever, inducing sweating; governing swelling and abdominal distension, and acute abdominal pain. (Zhen Quan)
— Treating thirst and eliminating dampness; relieving a distressed, vexed sensation in the heart [心中懊憹]. (Zhang Yuansu)
— Draining the bladder. (Wang Haogu)
— Opening the interstices of the skin and flesh [腠理]; treating strangury [淋] with swelling, leg qi [腳氣, beriberi], white turbid discharge and leukorrhea [白濁帶下], urinary strangury and fetal edema in pregnancy, and difficult urination [小便不利]. (Li Shizhen)

**Elaborations and Discussion**
Su Song states: Zhang Zhongjing, in treating wasting-thirst [消渴] with a floating pulse, difficult urination, and mild fever, used Zhu Ling Powder [豬苓散] to induce sweating. When a patient desires water but vomits it up — a condition called 'water reversal' [水逆] — or when there is a cold cough in winter resembling malarial episodes, he also administered Zhu Ling Powder, which is in fact the Five-Ling Powder [五苓散]. The formula: zhu ling, fu ling, and atractylodes [朮], three parts each; ze xie [Alisma], five parts; gui [cinnamon], two parts. Pound finely and sieve; take one square-cun spoonful [方寸匕] with water, three times daily. Drink plenty of warm water; once sweating occurs, recovery follows. Among all decoctions that promote the water pathways, none acts as swiftly as this; it is in common use today.

Li Gao states: Its bitterness drains stagnation, its sweetness supports yang, its blandness opens the orifices — thus it eliminates dampness and promotes urination.

Kou Zongshi [Zongshi] states: Zhu ling has great power in moving water. Long-term use will inevitably damage kidney qi and dim the eyes. Those who use it long-term should deliberate carefully.

Zhang Yuansu states: Zhu ling is bland and percolating [淡滲]; it greatly dries out and depletes fluids. Those who have no dampness-pattern [濕癥] should not take it.

Shizhen states: Zhu ling is bland and percolating; its qi rises yet can also descend. Thus it opens the interstices of skin and flesh and promotes urination — its function is the same as fu ling. However, as an ingredient in tonifying formulas it is inferior to fu ling.

segments.title_inferred: Mulberry Mistletoe (Sang Shang Ji Sheng) — Parasitic Plant Entry, Chapter Mu Zhi Si
segments.original_text:
【釋名】寄屑（《本經》）、寓木（《本經》）、時珍曰︰此物寄寓他木而生，如鳥立於上，故曰寄生、寓木、蔦木。俗呼為寄生草。《東方朔傳》云︰

【集解】《別錄》曰︰桑上寄生，生弘農川谷桑樹上。三月三日采莖

弘景曰︰寄生松上、楊上、楓上皆有，形類是一般，但根津所因處為異，則各隨其樹名之。生樹枝間，根在枝節之內。葉圓青赤，濃澤易折。旁自生枝節。冬夏生，四月花白。五月實赤，大如小豆。處處皆有，以出彭城者為勝。俗呼為續斷用之，而《本經》續斷別在上品，主療不同，市人混雜無識者。

恭曰︰此多生楓、槲、櫸柳、水楊等樹上。葉無陰陽，如細柳葉而濃脆。莖粗短。子黃色，大如小棗。惟虢州有桑上者，子汁甚粘，核大似小豆，九月始熟，黃色。陶言五月實赤，大如小豆，蓋未見也。江南人相承用其莖為續斷，殊不相關。

保升曰︰諸樹多有寄生，莖、葉並相似，云是烏鳥食一物子，糞落樹上，感氣而生。葉如橘而濃軟，莖如槐而肥脆。處處雖有，須桑上者佳。然非自采，即難以別。可斷莖視之，色深黃者為驗。又《圖經》云︰葉似龍膽而濃闊。莖短似雞腳，作樹形。三月、四月花，黃白色。六月、七月結子，黃綠色，如小豆，以汁稠粘者良也。

大明曰︰人多收櫸樹上者為桑寄生。桑上極少，縱有，形與櫸上者亦不同。次即楓樹上者，力與櫸樹上者相同，黃色。七月、八月採。

宗奭曰︰桑寄生皆言處處有之。從官南北，處處難得。豈歲歲斫踐之，苦不能生耶？抑方宜不同耶？若以為鳥食物子落枝節間感氣而生，則麥當生麥，谷當生谷，不當生此一物也。自是感造化之氣，別是一物。古人惟取桑上者，是假其氣爾。第以難得真者，真者下咽，必驗如

【氣味】苦，平，無毒。

《別錄》曰︰甘，無毒。
甘，平，無毒。

【主治】腰痛，小兒背強，癰腫，充肌膚，堅發齒，長須眉，安胎（《本經》）。去女子崩中內傷不足，產後余疾，下乳汁，主金瘡，去痺（《別錄》）。助筋骨，益血脈（大明）。主懷妊漏血不止，令胎牢固（甄權）。
明目，輕身，通神（《本經》）。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Jixie [寄屑, 'lodging shavings'] (Shennong Bencao Jing); Yumu [寓木, 'guest wood'] (Shennong Bencao Jing). Li Shizhen states: This plant lodges and grows upon other trees, like a bird perched above, hence it is called Jisheng ['parasitic growth'], Yumu ['guest wood'], and Diaonu [蔦木, 'clinging wood']. Commonly it is called Jisheng Cao ['mistletoe herb']. The Dongfang Shuo Zhuan states: [text missing/incomplete in source].

**[Collected Commentaries]**
The Mingyi Bielu states: Mulberry mistletoe [Sang Shang Jisheng] grows on mulberry trees in the river valleys of Hongnong. The stems are collected on the third day of the third month.

Tao Hongjing states: Mistletoe is found on pine, poplar, and maple trees as well; the forms are generally similar, but they differ according to the host tree from which they draw their sap, and each is named after its respective host tree. It grows between branches, with roots embedded within the branch nodes. The leaves are round, greenish-red, thick, lustrous, and easily broken. Side branches grow spontaneously from the nodes. It grows through winter and summer; flowers appear in the fourth month and are white. Fruits ripen in the fifth month and are red, about the size of small beans [adzuki beans]. It is found everywhere, but the variety from Pengcheng is considered superior. People commonly call it Xuduan [續斷, 'Dipsacus'] and use it as such, but in the Shennong Bencao Jing, Xuduan is separately listed in the upper grade [of herbs] and has different therapeutic indications. Merchants mix them together without discernment.

Su Gong states: This plant most commonly grows on maple, oak [Quercus dentata], Zelkova-willow [Juglans-willow type], and water-poplar trees. The leaves have no distinction of upper and lower surface [i.e., are similar on both sides], resembling fine willow leaves but thicker and more brittle. The stems are thick and short. The fruits are yellow, about the size of small jujubes. Only in Guozhou is there a variety that grows on mulberry trees; its fruit juice is very sticky, the seed is as large as a small bean, and it ripens in the ninth month, being yellow in colour. Tao's claim that the fruits are red in the fifth month and the size of small beans is evidently based on not having seen the actual plant. People in Jiangnan have traditionally used its stems as Xuduan, which is altogether incorrect.

Han Baosheng states: Mistletoe is found on many kinds of trees, and the stems and leaves are all similar. It is said that birds eat the seeds of a certain plant and their droppings fall onto trees, where the plant germinates from the vital emanation [qi] of the host. The leaves resemble those of the tangerine but are thicker and softer; the stems resemble those of the pagoda tree [Sophora japonica] but are plumper and more brittle. Although it is found everywhere, only the variety growing on mulberry is considered good. However, unless one gathers it personally, it is difficult to distinguish. One may cut the stem and examine it: a deep yellow colour is the distinguishing mark. The Tujing [Illustrated Classic] further states: the leaves resemble those of Gentiana [longdan, 龍膽] but are thicker and broader. The stem is short and resembles a chicken's foot, giving it a tree-like form. It flowers in the third and fourth months with yellow-white blossoms. Fruits form in the sixth and seventh months, are yellow-green, about the size of small beans; those with thick and sticky juice are considered best.

Da Ming [Ri Huazi] states: People commonly collect specimens growing on Zelkova trees and pass them off as mulberry mistletoe. Those actually found on mulberry are extremely rare, and even when found, their form differs from those on Zelkova trees. Second in quality are those on maple trees, which are equal in efficacy to those on Zelkova and are yellow in colour. They are harvested in the seventh and eighth months.

Kou Zongshi states: It is universally claimed that mulberry mistletoe is found everywhere. Yet in all my travels in official service north and south, I have found it nowhere easy to obtain. Could it be that annual cutting and trampling prevents it from growing? Or that regional conditions differ? If one accepts the explanation that birds drop seeds into branch nodes where the plant then grows from absorbed qi, then wheat ought to produce wheat, and millet ought to produce millet — not this single distinct plant. Rather, it absorbs the vital transformative qi [of heaven and earth] and is a distinct thing of its own. The ancients selected only that growing on mulberry, to borrow the qi of that tree. Precisely because the genuine article is hard to obtain — when the genuine article is swallowed, the effect is certain, like... [text breaks off].

**[Qi and Flavour]**
Bitter, balanced [ping], non-toxic.
The Mingyi Bielu states: Sweet, non-toxic.
[Also recorded as:] Sweet, balanced, non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Lumbar pain, stiffness of the back in children, abscesses and swellings [yongzhong], enriching the skin and flesh, strengthening the hair and teeth, promoting growth of beard and eyebrows, calming the fetus [Shennong Bencao Jing].
