Soak it in pure bitter wine [苦酒, i.e., strong vinegar or sour wine] for three days, then steam and sun-dry it one hundred times in succession, and store it. When traveling far, consuming one meal of it per day allows one to go ten days [without other food]; if one doubles the portion, one will not feel hungry for four hundred and ninety days. Another formula: Take one dou of rice and three jin of red halloysite [赤石脂, chi shi zhi]; soak in water and set in a warm place for one or two days until a blue-white mold [coating] appears on the surface; pound into pills the size of a plum [李]. Take three pills daily; one will similarly not feel hungry.

Zhang Yuxi [掌禹錫] states: According to the *Lingbao Wufu Jing* [《靈寶五符經》, Scripture of the Five Talismans of the Numinous Treasure], it is white xian rice [白鮮米] that is steamed and sun-dried nine times to make grain-abstinence provisions — whereas this formula uses green millet rice, for which I have not found the original source.

segments.title_inferred: Millet (粟米) — Flavor, Properties, Medicinal Uses, and Related Products
segments.original_text:
【氣味】鹹，微寒，無毒。時珍曰：鹹、淡。

宗奭曰：生者難化。熟者滯氣，隔食，生蟲。

藏器曰：胃冷者不宜多食。粟浸水至敗者，損人。

瑞曰：與杏仁同食，令人吐瀉。雁食粟，翼重不能飛。

【主治】養腎氣，去脾胃中熱，益氣。陳者：苦，寒。治胃熱消渴，利小便（《別錄》）。止痢，壓丹石熱（孟詵）。水煮服，治熱腹痛及鼻衄。為粉，和水濾汁，解諸毒，治霍亂及轉筋入腹，又治卒得鬼打（藏器）。解小麥毒，發熱（士良）。治反胃熱痢。煮粥食，益丹田（《日用》）。

【發明】弘景曰：陳粟乃三、五年者，尤解煩悶，服食家亦將食之。

宗奭曰：粟米利小便，故能益脾胃。

震亨曰：粟屬水與土。陳者最硬難化，得漿水乃化也。

時珍曰：粟之味鹹淡，氣寒下滲，腎之谷也，腎病宜食之。虛熱消渴泄痢，皆腎病也。滲利小便，所以泄腎邪也。降胃火，故脾胃之病宜食之。

霍亂卒熱，心煩渴，飲數升立瘥。臭泔：止消渴，尤良（蘇恭）。酸泔及澱：洗皮膚瘙疥，殺蟲。飲之，主五痔。和臭樗皮煎服，治小兒疳痢（藏器）。痔漏脫肛，和諸藥薰之（時珍）。

粟奴
利小腸，除煩懣（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：粟奴，即粟苗成穗時生黑煤者。古方不用。《聖惠》治小腸結澀不通，心煩悶亂，有粟奴湯：用粟奴、苦竹須、小豆葉、炙甘草各一兩，燈心十寸，蔥白五寸，銅錢七文，水煎分服。取效乃止。

粟廩米
見後陳廩米下。

粟米
見後米下。

粟糗
見後下。
segments.translation:
**[Flavor and Qi]** Salty, slightly cold, non-toxic. Li Shizhen states: salty and bland.

Kou Zongshi states: When raw, it is difficult to digest. When cooked, it may stagnate qi, obstruct food [digestion], and generate worms.

Chen Cangqi states: Those with cold stomachs should not eat it in excess. Millet soaked in water until spoiled is harmful to people.

Wu Rui states: Eaten together with apricot kernels [xing ren], it causes vomiting and diarrhea. When wild geese eat millet, their wings become heavy and they cannot fly.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Nourishes kidney qi, removes heat from the spleen and stomach, and supplements qi. Aged millet [chen zhe]: bitter, cold. Treats stomach-heat wasting-thirst [xiao ke / diabetes-like condition] and promotes urination (Mingyi Bielu). Stops dysentery and suppresses the heat from mineral elixirs [dan shi] (Meng Shen). Decocted in water and taken orally, treats hot abdominal pain and nosebleed [epistaxis]. Ground into powder and filtered with water into a liquid, it resolves various poisons, treats cholera [huo luan] and cramps extending into the abdomen, and also treats sudden assault by malevolent spirits [gui da] (Chen Cangqi). Resolves toxicity from wheat and treats fever (Shi Liang). Treats regurgitation [fan wei] and heat dysentery. Cooked into porridge and eaten, it nourishes the cinnabar field [dan tian] (Riyon Bencao).

**[Elaboration / Faming]**
Tao Hongjing states: Aged millet, meaning that stored for three to five years, is especially effective at relieving vexation and oppression; those who practice dietary cultivation [fushi] also eat it.

Kou Zongshi states: Millet promotes urination, and thereby benefits the spleen and stomach.

Zhu Zhenheng states: Millet belongs to the phases of Water and Earth. Aged millet is the hardest and most difficult to digest; it is only broken down upon contact with fermented liquid [jiang shui].

Li Shizhen states: The flavor of millet is salty and bland; its qi is cold and percolating downward — it is the grain of the kidney. Those with kidney disease should eat it. Deficiency-heat wasting-thirst [xiao ke] and diarrhea-dysentery are all kidney diseases. By percolating and promoting urination, it drains pathogenic kidney qi. It also descends stomach fire, and therefore diseases of the spleen and stomach are also amenable to its consumption.

[Regarding millet water wash / soaking liquid:] For sudden heat in cholera [huo luan], with vexing thirst, drinking several sheng [of this liquid] brings immediate recovery. Foul millet washing water [chou gan]: especially effective at stopping wasting-thirst (Su Gong). Sour millet washing water and its sediment [suan gan ji dian]: used to wash itchy skin and scabies, and to kill parasites. Drunk, it treats the five types of hemorrhoids [wu zhi]. Decocted with the bark of foul chun tree [chou chu pi, i.e., Ailanthus altissima] and taken orally, it treats childhood gan-dysentery [gan li] (Chen Cangqi). For hemorrhoidal fistulas and rectal prolapse, combined with various drugs and used as a fumigant (Li Shizhen).

**Millet Smut [Su Nu]**
Benefits the small intestine and relieves vexation and oppression (Li Shizhen).

**[Elaboration]**
Li Shizhen states: Millet smut [su nu] is the black sooty growth that forms on millet stalks when they are forming ears. It is not used in ancient formulas. The Shenghuifang [Shenghui Formulary] includes a formula called 'Millet Smut Decoction' [su nu tang] for treating obstructed small intestine with inability to pass [stool or urine], accompanied by vexing agitation of the heart-mind: use millet smut, bitter bamboo rootlets [ku zhu xu], small red bean leaves [xiao dou ye], honey-roasted licorice root [zhi gancao], one liang each; rush pith [deng xin] ten cun; scallion whites [cong bai] five cun; seven copper coins [tong qian]; decoct in water, divide into doses and take. Stop once efficacy is achieved.

**Granary-stored Millet [Su Lin Mi]**
See later entry under Aged Granary Rice [Chen Lin Mi].

**Millet [Su Mi / Bai Mi]**
See later entry under Rice.

**Parched Millet Flour [Su Qiu]**
See later entry.

segments.title_inferred: Shu (Glutinous Millet) — Grains, Chapter 2
segments.original_text:
【釋名】眾（音終。《爾雅》）、糯秫（《唐本》）、糯粟（《唐本》）、黃糯。

時珍曰：秫字篆文，象其禾體柔弱之形，俗呼糯粟是矣。北人呼為黃糯，亦曰黃米。釀酒劣於糯也。

【集解】恭曰：秫是稻秫也。今人呼粟糯為秫。北土多以釀酒，而汁少於黍米。凡黍、稷、粟、秫、粳、糯，三谷皆有粳、糯也。

禹錫曰：秫米似黍米而粒小，可作酒。

宗奭曰：秫米，初搗出淡黃白色，亦如糯，不堪作飯，最粘，故宜作酒。

時珍曰：秫即粱米、粟米之粘者。有赤、白、黃三色，皆可釀酒、熬糖、作糕食之。蘇頌《圖經》謂秫為黍之粘者，許慎《說文》謂秫為稷之粘者，崔豹《古今注》謂秫為稻之粘者，皆誤也。惟蘇恭以粟、秫分粳、糯，孫炎注《爾雅》謂秫為粘粟者，得之。
秫米（即黃米）

【氣味】甘，微寒，無毒。

詵曰：性平。不可常食，擁五臟氣，動風，迷悶人。

時珍曰：按：《養生集》云：味酸性熱，粘滯，易成黃積病，小兒不宜多食。

【主治】寒熱，利大腸，療漆瘡（《別錄》）。治筋骨攣急，殺瘡疥毒熱。生搗，和雞子白，敷毒腫，良（孟詵）。主犬咬，凍瘡，嚼敷之（《日華》）。治肺瘧，及陽盛陰虛，夜不得眠，及食鵝鴨成症，妊娠下黃汁（時珍）。煮湯，洗風（孟詵）。

【發明】弘景曰：北人以此米作酒煮糖，肥軟易消。方藥不正用，惟嚼以塗漆瘡及釀諸藥醪爾。

時珍曰：秫者，肺之谷也，肺病宜食之。故能去寒熱，利大腸。大腸者肺之合，而肺病多作皮寒熱也。《千金》治肺瘧方用之，取此義也。《靈樞經》岐伯治陽盛陰虛，夜不得瞑，半夏湯中用之，取其益陰氣而利大腸也。大腸利則陽不盛矣。方見半夏條。又《異苑》云：宋元嘉中，有人食鴨成症瘕。醫以秫米研粉調水服之。須臾煩躁，吐出一鴨雛而瘥也。

《千金方》治食鴨肉成病，胸滿面赤，不能食，以秫米湯一盞飲之。
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**

Also known as: Zhòng [眾, pronounced 'zhōng'; from the *Erya*], Nuòshú [糯秫; from the *Tang Bencao*], Nuòsù [糯粟; from the *Tang Bencao*], and Huángnuò [黃糯, 'yellow glutinous grain'].

Li Shizhen states: The seal-script form of the character *shú* [秫] resembles the pliant, soft body of a grain plant — precisely what the common name 'glutinous millet' [nuòsù] conveys. Northerners call it Huángnuò ('yellow glutinous grain') or Huángmǐ ('yellow rice'). For brewing wine, it is inferior to [rice-based] nuò [糯].

**Collected Commentaries**

Su Gong states: Shú is a kind of glutinous grain. Today people call glutinous millet shú. In the northern lands it is widely used for brewing wine, though it yields less liquid than black millet [shǔ]. Among the grains — black millet, panicled millet, foxtail millet, glutinous millet, round-grained non-glutinous rice, and glutinous rice — all three grain types have both a non-glutinous [jīng, 粳] and a glutinous [nuò, 糯] variety.

Yu Xi states: Shú grain resembles black millet [shǔ] but has smaller kernels, and can be used to make wine.

Zou Zongshi states: When shú is first pounded, it is a pale yellowish-white color, similar to glutinous rice [nuò]; it is not suitable for making cooked rice as it is extremely sticky, and is therefore best used for brewing wine.

Li Shizhen states: Shú is simply the glutinous variety of sorghum [liáng] or foxtail millet [sù]. It comes in three colors — red, white, and yellow — all of which can be used to brew wine, boil into syrup, or make cakes for eating. Su Song's *Illustrated Classic* [*Tujing*] identifies shú as the glutinous variety of black millet [shǔ]; Xu Shen's *Shuowen* identifies it as the glutinous variety of panicled millet [jì]; and Cui Bao's *Gujin Zhu* identifies it as the glutinous variety of rice — all three are incorrect. Only Su Gong's distinction between non-glutinous and glutinous forms of foxtail millet [sù], and Sun Yan's commentary on the *Erya* identifying shú as 'sticky millet,' are correct.

*Shú grain (that is, yellow rice)*

**Flavor and Nature**

Sweet, slightly cold, non-toxic.

Meng Shen states: Its nature is balanced [píng]. It should not be eaten regularly, as it obstructs the qi of the five viscera, stirs up wind, and causes confusion and oppression in a person.

Li Shizhen states: According to the *Yangsheng Ji* [Collection on Nourishing Life]: its flavor is sour and its nature is hot; it is sticky and cloying, and readily causes jaundice-accumulation disease [huángjí bìng]. Children should not eat it in excess.

**Indications and Treatments**

- Alternating chills and fever [hánrè]; promotes movement of the large intestine; treats lacquer-rash [qīchuāng] (*Bielu*).
- Treats cramping and spasms of the sinews and bones; eliminates the toxic heat of sores and scabies. Pound it fresh, mix with egg white, and apply to toxic swellings — effective (Meng Shen).
- Governs dog bites and frostbite: chew and apply topically (*Rihua*).
- Treats pulmonary malaria [fèi nüè]; excess yang with deficient yin causing inability to sleep at night; abdominal masses [zhèng] caused by eating goose or duck; and yellow vaginal discharge during pregnancy (Li Shizhen).
- Boil into a decoction and wash [affected areas] for wind-related conditions (Meng Shen).

**Elucidation**

Tao Hongjing states: Northerners use this grain to make wine and boil syrup [táng]; it is rich, soft, and easy to digest. It is not commonly used in formal medicine — only chewed and applied to lacquer-rash, or used to brew medicated wines [yào láo].

Li Shizhen states: Shú is the grain of the lung; those with lung disease should eat it. Hence it can dispel alternating chills and fever and promote movement of the large intestine. The large intestine is paired with [and corresponds to] the lung, and lung disease commonly manifests as chills and fever of the skin. This is why the *Qianjin* [*Essential Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold*] uses it in prescriptions for pulmonary malaria — drawing on exactly this principle. In the *Lingshu Jing*, Qi Bo uses it in the Pinellia Decoction [Bànxià Tāng] for the treatment of excess yang with deficient yin causing inability to sleep, taking advantage of its ability to nourish yin qi and promote movement of the large intestine. When the large intestine moves freely, yang will no longer be in excess. The formula appears under the entry for pinellia [bànxià]. Furthermore, the *Yiyuan* [Garden of Strange Tales] records that during the Yuanjia reign period of the [Liu] Song dynasty, a person developed an abdominal mass [zhèng jiǎ] from eating duck. A physician ground shú grain into powder, mixed it with water, and administered it. Shortly afterward the patient became agitated and vomited out a duckling [embryo], whereupon he recovered.

The *Qianjin Fang* [*Essential Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold*] treats illness caused by eating duck meat — characterized by fullness in the chest, flushing of the face, and inability to eat — by having the patient drink one cup of shú grain decoction.

segments.title_inferred: Cānzǐ (Finger Millet / Dragon-Claw Millet)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】龍爪粟、鴨爪稗。

時珍曰︰乃不粘之稱也。又不實之貌也。龍爪、鴨爪，象其穗歧之形。

【集解】周定王曰︰子生水田中及下濕地。葉似稻，但差短。梢頭結穗，仿佛稗子穗。其子如黍粒大，茶褐色。搗米，煮粥、炊飯、磨面皆宜。

時珍曰︰子，山東、河南亦五月種之。苗如茭黍，八、九月抽莖，有三棱，如水中草之莖。開細花，簇簇結穗如粟穗，而分數歧，如鷹爪之狀。內有細子如黍粒而細，赤色。其稃甚薄，其味粗澀。

【氣味】甘，澀，無毒。

【主治】補中益氣，濃腸胃，濟飢。
segments.translation:
**Names and Appellations**
Also known as: Dragon-Claw Millet [龍爪粟] and Duck-Claw Barnyard Grass [鴨爪稗].

Li Shizhen states: The name [參子, Cānzǐ] denotes a grain that is non-glutinous [not sticky]. It also suggests an unfilled or sparse appearance. The names 'Dragon-Claw' and 'Duck-Claw' are derived from the forking, claw-like shape of the panicles.

**Collected Commentaries**
Prince Zhouding [周定王] states: Cānzǐ grows in paddy fields and low-lying wet ground. Its leaves resemble those of rice, though somewhat shorter. It forms panicles at the tip of the stem, resembling the panicles of barnyard grass [稗]. Its seeds are about the size of millet grains [黍粒] and are tea-brown in color. The grain may be hulled and used to cook porridge, steam rice, or grind into flour — all are suitable.

Li Shizhen states: Cānzǐ is also sown in May in Shandong and Henan. The seedlings resemble wild rice [茭黍]. In the eighth and ninth months, stems emerge with three ridges [三棱], similar to the stems of aquatic grasses. It bears tiny flowers that cluster together, forming panicles resembling those of foxtail millet [粟], branching into several forks in the shape of an eagle's talons. Within are small seeds, finer than millet grains, and red in color. The hulls [稃] are very thin, and the flavor is coarse and astringent.

**Flavor and Nature**
Sweet, astringent; non-toxic.

**Principal Indications and Treatments**
Supplements the center [中焥] and augments [vital] qi; enriches and strengthens the stomach and intestines; relieves hunger.

segments.title_inferred: Bai (Barnyard Grass / Wild Millet) — Grains, Chapter 2
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰稗乃禾之卑賤者也，故字從卑。

【集解】弘景曰︰稗子亦可食。又有烏禾，生野中如稗，荒年可代糧而殺蟲，煮以沃地，螻、蚓皆死。

藏器曰︰稗有二種︰一種黃白色，一種紫黑色。紫黑者似芑有毛，北人呼為烏禾。

時珍曰︰稗處處野生，最能亂苗。其莖葉穗粒並如黍稷。一斗可得米三升。故曰︰五穀不熟，不如稗。苗似稗而穗如粟，有紫毛，即烏禾也。《爾雅》謂之迭（音迭）。周定王曰︰稗有水稗、旱稗。水稗生田中。旱稗苗葉似子，色深綠，根下葉帶紫色。梢頭出扁穗，結子如黍粒，茶褐色，味微苦，性溫。以煮粥、炊飯、磨面食之皆宜。

稗米
【氣味】辛、甘、苦，微寒，無毒。
穎曰︰辛、脆。
【主治】作飯食，益氣宜脾，故曹植有芳菰精稗之稱（時珍）。

苗根
金瘡及傷損，血出不已。搗敷或研末摻之即止，甚驗（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen states: Bai [稗] is the lowliest of the grain grasses; hence the character is formed with the element 'bei' [卑, meaning low or base].

**Collected Commentaries**
Tao Hongjing states: The seeds of bai are also edible. There is also a plant called Wu He [烏禾, 'dark grain'], which grows wild and resembles bai. In years of famine it can substitute for food, and it kills insects: when its water of decoction is poured on the ground, mole crickets and earthworms all die.

Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] states: There are two varieties of bai — one is yellow-white in color, and one is purple-black. The purple-black variety resembles qi [芑, a type of millet] and has hair [bristles]; people of the north call it Wu He.

Li Shizhen states: Bai grows wild everywhere and is especially prone to becoming a weed among crop seedlings. Its stems, leaves, ears, and grains all resemble those of shu [黍, broomcorn millet] and ji [稷, foxtail millet]. From one dou [a dry measure] one can obtain three sheng of rice [husked grain]. Hence the saying: 'When the five grains fail to ripen, they are no better than bai.' The plant whose seedlings resemble bai but whose ear resembles millet [su, 粟] and which has purple bristles — that is Wu He. The Erya refers to it as 迭 (pronounced 'die'). King Zhoudingwang states: there are water-bai [水稗] and dry-land bai [旱稗]. Water-bai grows in paddies. The seedlings and leaves of dry-land bai resemble [ILLEGIBLE: one character missing, likely a comparison plant], deep green in color, with the leaves near the root tinged purple. At the tip it puts forth a flat ear, bearing seeds like broomcorn millet grains, tea-brown in color, slightly bitter in flavor, and warm in nature [xing]. It is suitable for cooking into porridge, steaming as rice, or grinding into flour for food.

**Bai Grain [稗米]**
*Flavor and Nature:* Acrid [xin], sweet, and bitter; slightly cold; non-toxic.
Ying [穎] states: Acrid and crisp.
*Indications and Treatments:* When eaten as cooked rice, it replenishes qi and benefits the spleen. Hence Cao Zhi [the poet] praised it in his phrase 'fragrant water-oats and refined bai.' (Li Shizhen)

**Seedlings and Roots**
*Condition treated:* Metal-edge wounds [jin chuang, 金瘡] and injuries with incessant bleeding.
*Preparation:* Pound fresh and apply as a poultice, or grind to powder and dust onto the wound — bleeding stops immediately. Highly efficacious. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Wild Foxtail Grass (Yě láng wěi cǎo) — Grain Chapter Two
segments.original_text:
【釋名】稂（音郎）、童粱（《爾雅》）、野狼茅（《爾雅》）、孟（《爾雅》）、宿田翁（《詩疏》）、守田（《詩疏》）。

時珍曰︰野狼尾，其穗象形也。秀而不成，嶷然在田，故有宿田、守田之稱。

【集解】藏器曰︰野狼尾生澤地，似茅作穗。《廣志》云︰子可作黍食。《爾雅》云︰孟，野狼尾。似茅，可以覆屋，是也。

時珍曰︰野狼尾莖、葉、穗、粒並如粟，而穗色紫黃，有毛。荒年亦可采食。許慎《說文》云︰禾粟之穗，生而不成者，謂之稂。其秀而不實者，名狗尾草，見草部。

米

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】作飯食之，令人不飢（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Nomenclature**

Alternate names: Láng [稂, pronounced láng]; Tóng liáng [童粱] (as written in the *Erya*); Yě láng máo [野狼茅, 'wild wolf grass'] (*Erya*); Mèng [孟] (*Erya*); Sù tián wēng [宿田翁, 'old one dwelling in the field'] (*Shī shū*); Shǒu tián [守田, 'guardian of the field'] (*Shī shū*).

Li Shizhen states: The name 'wild wolf's tail' [野狼尾] is derived from the shape of its spike. Because it heads out but does not fully ripen, standing solitary and unmoved in the field, it has received the names 'field-dweller' [宿田] and 'field-guardian' [守田].

**Collected Commentaries**

Chen Cangqi states: Wild wolf's tail [野狼尾] grows in marshy ground and resembles cogon grass [máo, 茅] in forming spikes. The *Guangzhi* records: its seeds can be cooked and eaten in the manner of millet [shǔ, 黍]. The *Erya* states: Mèng [孟] is wild wolf's tail; it resembles cogon grass and can be used for thatching roofs — this is indeed the same plant.

Li Shizhen states: The stems, leaves, spikes, and grains of wild wolf's tail all resemble those of millet [sù, 粟], but the spike is purplish-yellow in color and bears hairs. In years of famine it may also be gathered and eaten. Xu Shen's *Shuowen* states: the spike of a grain or millet plant that grows but does not mature is called láng [稂]. That which heads out but bears no grain is called dog's-tail grass [gǒu wěi cǎo, 狗尾草]; see the Grass section.

**Grain [Mǐ, 米]**

**Flavor and Nature**

Sweet; balanced [píng, 平]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**

Cooked into a rice [or grain porridge] and eaten, it prevents hunger. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Peng Grass Seeds (Broom-plant / Tumbleweed Seeds)
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰︰陳藏器《本草》載蓬草子，不具形狀。珍按︰蓬類不一︰有雕蓬，即菰草也，見菰米下；有黍蓬，即青科也；又有黃蓬草、飛蓬草。不識陳氏所指果何蓬也？以理推之，非黃蓬即青柯爾。黃蓬草生湖澤中，葉如菰蒲，秋月結實成穗，子細如雕胡米。飢年人采食之，須浸洗曝舂，乃不苦澀。青科西南夷人種之，葉如茭黍，秋月結實成穗，有子如赤黍而細，其稃甚薄，曝舂炊食。又粟類有七棱青科、八棱青科，麥類有青稞、黃稞，皆非此類，乃物異名同也。其飛蓬乃藜蒿之類，末大本小，風易拔之，故號飛蓬。子如灰菜子，亦可濟荒。又《魏略》云︰鮑出遇飢歲，采蓬實，日得數斗，為母作食。《西京雜記》云︰宮中正月上辰，出池邊盥濯，食蓬餌，以祓邪氣。此皆不知所采乃何蓬也？大抵三種蓬子，亦不甚相遠。

子

【氣味】酸、澀，平，無毒。

【主治】作飯食之，益飢，無異粳米（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Chen Zangqi's *Bencao* [*Bencao Shiyi*] records 'peng grass seeds' [蓬草子] but gives no description of their appearance. I, Shizhen, note: the category of 'peng' [broom-plant / rush-like grasses] is not uniform. There is 'diao-peng' [雕蓬], which is the same as gu-grass [菰草; wild rice], discussed under the entry for gu-mi [菰米]. There is 'shu-peng' [黍蓬], which is the same as qing-ke [青科]. There are also huang-peng grass [黃蓬草] and fei-peng grass [飛蓬草, tumbleweed]. I cannot determine which peng Chen [Zangqi] was actually referring to. Reasoning it out, it must be either huang-peng or qing-ke.

Huang-peng grass [黃蓬草] grows in lakes and marshes. Its leaves resemble those of gu-pu [菰蒲; wild rice and cattail]. In autumn it forms seeds in spikes; the seeds are as fine as diao-hu-mi [雕胡米; wild rice grain]. In years of famine, people gather and eat them. They must first be soaked, washed, sun-dried, and hulled by pounding [曝舂], so as to remove their bitterness and astringency.

Qing-ke [青科] is cultivated by the peoples of the southwestern [ethnic minority] regions. Its leaves resemble those of jiao-shu [茭黍; wild rice / water oats]. In autumn it forms seeds in spikes; the seeds resemble red millet [赤黍] but are smaller, with very thin husks [稃]. It is sun-dried, hulled by pounding, and cooked for eating.

Among millet-type [粟類] plants there is also 'seven-ridged qing-ke' [七棱青科] and 'eight-ridged qing-ke' [八棱青科], and among wheat-type [麥類] plants there is qing-ke barley [青稞] and huang-ke [黃稞]—these are all of a different kind, being different things that share a similar name.

As for fei-peng [飛蓬, tumbleweeds], these belong to the category of li-hao [藜蒿; goosefoot/mugwort type plants]. Their tops are large and their bases small; the wind easily uproots them, hence the name 'flying peng' [飛蓬]. Their seeds resemble those of hui-cai [灰菜; Chenopodium album, lamb's quarters] and can also be used to relieve famine.

Furthermore, the *Wei Lüe* [《魏略》, *Brief Records of Wei*] states: 'Bao Chu, in a year of famine, gathered peng seeds; each day he obtained several dou [bushels], and made food from them for his mother.' The *Xijing Zaji* [《西京雜記》, *Miscellaneous Records of the Western Capital*] states: 'In the palace, on the first chen-day [辰日] of the first month, [people] went to the pool-side to wash their hands and faces, ate peng cakes [蓬餌], and thereby expelled evil influences [祓邪氣].' In none of these cases do we know which peng was actually gathered. On the whole, the seeds of the three types of peng [discussed above] are not very different from one another.

**Seeds [子]**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Sour and astringent; neutral [平]; non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatment]** Cooked into a rice-like food [作飯] and eaten, it relieves hunger, and is no different from [ordinary] polished rice [粳米]. (Chen Zangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Huangcao (皇草) — a wild grain plant of abandoned paddies
segments.original_text:
【釋名】皇（《爾雅》）、守田（同上）、守氣（同）。

時珍曰︰皇、䅌，音相近也。

【集解】藏器曰︰䅌草生水田中，苗似小麥而小。四月熟，可作飯。

時珍曰︰《爾雅》︰皇，守田。郭璞云︰一名守氣，生廢田中，似燕麥，子如雕胡，可食。

米

【氣味】甘，寒，無毒。

【主治】作飯，去熱，利腸胃，益氣力。久食，不飢（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Huáng [皇] (from the *Erya*); Shǒutián [守田, 'Guardian of the Fields'] (ibid.); Shǒuqì [守氣, 'Guardian of the Qi'] (ibid.).

Li Shizhen remarks: The names Huáng [皇] and Suì [䅌] are close in pronunciation.

**Collected Explanations**
Chen Cangqi states: Suì grass [䅌草] grows in irrigated rice paddies. Its seedlings resemble wheat but are smaller. It ripens in the fourth month and can be cooked into rice.

Li Shizhen states: The *Erya* records: 'Huáng is Shǒutián.' Guo Pu [郭璞] comments: 'It is also called Shǒuqì; it grows in abandoned fields, resembles wild oat [燕麥], and its grains are like those of water-oat [雕胡; i.e., Manchurian wild rice], and are edible.'

**Grain [米]**

*Qi and Flavor:* Sweet; cold; non-toxic.

*Principal Treatments:* When cooked as a grain dish, it dispels heat, benefits the intestines and stomach, and augments qi and physical strength. Prolonged consumption prevents hunger. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Zirán Gǔ / Yǔ Yúliáng — a coastal grass grain
segments.original_text:
【釋名】自然谷（《海藥》）、禹余糧。

【集解】藏器曰︰《博物志》云︰東海洲上有草名曰䅟。有實，食之如大麥。七月熟，民斂獲至冬乃訖。呼為自然谷，亦曰禹余糧。此非石之禹余糧也。

曰︰䅟實如球子，八月收之。彼民常食，中國未曾見也。

時珍曰︰按︰《方孝孺集》有海米行，蓋亦䅟草之類也。其詩云︰海邊有草名海米，大非蓬蒿小非薺。婦女攜籃晝作群，采摘仍於海中洗。歸來滌釜燒松枝，煮米為飯充朝飢。莫辭苦澀咽不下，性命聊假須臾時。

子

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】不飢，輕身（藏器）。補虛羸損乏，溫腸胃，止嘔逆。久食健人（李珣）。
segments.translation:
**Names:** Zìrán Gǔ [Natural Grain] (from *Hǎi Yào* [Materia Medica of Sea Drugs]); Yǔ Yúliáng [Yü's Leftover Provisions].

**Collected Commentaries:**

Zàngqì [Chén Cángqì] states: The *Bówù Zhì* [Record of Diverse Matters] says: On the islands of the Eastern Sea there is a grass called 䅟 [a grain grass]. It bears seeds that taste like barley. It ripens in the seventh month, and the people gather and harvest it through the winter until it is finished. They call it 'Natural Grain,' and also 'Yǔ Yúliáng.' This is not the mineral Yǔ Yúliáng [limonite/goethite].

[Lǐ Xún] states: The seeds of 䅟 are like small balls and are collected in the eighth month. The local people eat them regularly; they have never been seen in China [the interior].

Shízhēn [Lǐ Shízhēn] states: According to the collected works of Fāng Xiàorú, there is a poem titled 'Ballad of Sea Rice' (*Hǎimǐ Xíng*), which apparently also refers to this type of 䅟 grass. The poem reads: 'By the sea grows a grass called sea rice — larger than mugwort, smaller than shepherd's purse. Women carrying baskets gather in groups by day, picking and then washing it in the sea. Returning home, they rinse the pot and burn pine branches, cooking the grain into rice to satisfy the morning hunger. Do not complain of the bitterness and astringency that is hard to swallow — for a little while it sustains life.'

**Seeds**

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet; balanced [neither warm nor cold]; non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:** Alleviates hunger; lightens the body (Zàngqì). Supplements deficiency, emaciation, and depletion; warms the stomach and intestines; stops vomiting and counterflow. Prolonged consumption strengthens the person (Lǐ Xún).

segments.title_inferred: Afurong (Opium): Entry from Grains, Chapter Two
segments.original_text:
【釋名】阿片。

時珍曰︰俗作鴉片，名義未詳。或云︰阿，方音稱我也。以其花色似芙蓉而得此名。

【集解】時珍曰︰阿芙蓉前代罕聞，近方有用者，云是罌粟花之津液也。罌粟結青苞時，午後以大針刺其外面青皮，勿損裡面硬皮，或三、五處，次早津出，以竹刀刮，收入瓷器，陰乾用之。故今市者猶有苞片在內。王氏《醫林集要》言是天方國種紅罌粟花，不令水淹頭，七、八月花謝後，刺青皮取之者。案︰此花五月實枯，安得七、八月後尚有青皮？或方土不同乎？

【氣味】酸，澀，溫，微毒。

【主治】瀉痢脫肛不止，能澀丈夫精氣（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰俗人房中術用之。京師售一粒金丹，云通治百病，皆方伎家之術耳。
segments.translation:
**[Clarification of Names]** Also called Āpiàn [阿片].

Li Shizhen states: The popular written form is 'Yāpiàn' [鴉片; opium]. The meaning of the name is not entirely clear. Some say that 'ā' [阿] is a regional pronunciation for 'I/me' [我], and that the plant received this name because its flower resembles the hibiscus [芙蓉, Furong].

**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: Afurong [阿芙蓉; opium] was rarely heard of in earlier generations; only recently have there been prescriptions making use of it. It is said to be the fluid [津液] of the poppy [罌粟] flower. When the poppy has formed its green seed-pods, one uses a large needle in the afternoon to pierce the outer green skin, taking care not to damage the inner hard skin, pricking three to five places. By the following morning, the fluid has seeped out; it is then scraped off with a bamboo knife, collected into a porcelain vessel, and dried in the shade for use. This is why specimens sold in today's markets still have fragments of the pod-skin within them. Wang's 'Collected Essentials of the Medical Grove' [醫林集要] states that it comes from the red poppies cultivated in the land of Tianfang [天方國; Arabia], that the flower heads must not be submerged in water, and that after the flowers fall in the seventh or eighth month, the green skin is pierced to collect it. However, I note: this flower's fruit dries out by the fifth month — how could there still be green skin after the seventh or eighth month? Perhaps conditions differ by region.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Sour, astringent, warm; slightly toxic.

**[Primary Indications]** Treats incessant diarrhea and dysentery [瀉痢] with prolapsed rectum [脫肛]; it can astringe [澀] a man's seminal essence [精氣]. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elaboration]** Li Shizhen states: Common people use it in the bedroom arts [房中術]. In the capital, a pill called 'One-Pellet Gold Elixir' [一粒金丹] is sold, claimed to universally treat a hundred ailments — but all of this is merely the craft of itinerant practitioners and conjurers.

segments.title_inferred: Soybean Sprout [Dàdòu Huángjuǎn] — Germinated Black Soybean
segments.original_text:
【釋名】豆蘖。

弘景曰︰黑大豆為蘖芽，生五寸長，便乾之，名為黃卷，用之熬過，服食所須。

時珍曰︰一法︰壬癸日以井華水浸大豆，候生芽，取皮，陰乾用。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

普曰︰得前胡、杏子、牡蠣、烏喙、天雄、鼠屎，共蜜和良。惡海藻、龍膽。

【主治】濕痹，筋攣膝痛（《本經》）。五臟不足，胃氣結積，益氣止痛，去黑皯，潤肌膚皮毛（《別錄》）。破婦人惡血（孟詵。頌曰：古方蓐婦藥中多用之）。宜腎（思邈）。除胃中積熱，消水病脹滿（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Names:** [Also called] dòu niè [bean sprout/malt].

Tao Hongjing states: Black soybeans are allowed to germinate [as sprouts]; once the sprouts have grown to about five cun [approx. 12 cm] in length, they are dried. This product is called huángjuǎn [yellow rolls/curls]. It is parched [熬] before use and is a requirement in dietary supplement [服食] preparations.

Li Shizhen states: One method is to soak the soybeans in clear well water drawn on rén and guǐ days [i.e., days associated with water in the sexagenary cycle], wait until sprouts emerge, remove the husks, and dry in the shade before use.

**Qi and Flavor:** Sweet; balanced [平]; non-toxic.

Zhen Quan [or Wu Pu] states: It combines well with honey when used together with Bupleurum [前胡, Bupleurum spp.], apricot kernels [杏子, Prunus armeniaca], oyster shell [牡蠣, Ostrea spp.], wū huì [烏喙, Aconitum carmichaelii, the lateral root], tiān xióng [天雄, Aconitum carmichaelii, the main root], and rat droppings [鼠屎]. It is antagonistic to [惡] seaweed [海藻, Sargassum spp.] and gentian [龍膽, Gentiana spp.].

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Damp impediment [濕痹], sinew cramps [筋攣], and knee pain (Shennong Bencao Jing).
- Insufficiency of the five zang-organs, stagnation and accumulation of stomach qi, boosting qi and relieving pain, removing dark facial blemishes [黑皯], and moistening the skin and body hair (Mingyi Bielu).
- Breaking up pathological blood [惡血] in women (Meng Shen; Su Song notes: ancient formulas for women in the postpartum period [蓐婦] frequently used this ingredient).
- Beneficial to the kidneys (Sun Simiao).
- Eliminating accumulated heat in the stomach and dispersing water-disease [水病] with abdominal distension and fullness (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Yellow Soybean (Grain Chapter Three)
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰︰大豆有黑、青、黃、白、斑數色，惟黑者入藥，而黃、白豆炒食作腐豆葉而大，結角比黑豆角稍肥大，其莢、葉嫩時可食，甘美。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

時珍曰︰生溫，炒熱微毒。多食，壅氣生痰動嗽，令人身重，發面黃瘡疥。
辛、甘，熱，微毒。

【主治】寬中下氣，利大腸，消水脹腫毒（寧原）。研末，熟水和，塗痘後癰（時珍）。
塗瘡疥，解發（時珍）。

秸
燒灰，入點痣、去惡肉藥（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: Soybeans come in several colors — black, green, yellow, white, and mottled — but only the black variety is used in medicine. The yellow and white beans are eaten roasted or made into bean curd [tofu]. The leaves of this plant are large; the pods are slightly plumper and larger than those of the black soybean. The pods and young leaves are edible and have a sweet, pleasant flavor.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet; warm; non-toxic.

Li Shizhen states: When raw, [the bean is] warm [in nature]; when roasted, it becomes hot and mildly toxic. Eaten in excess, it obstructs [the flow of] qi, generates phlegm, and provokes coughing; it makes the body feel heavy and causes yellow sores [jaundice-related skin lesions] and scabies on the face.
[Also classified as:] Acrid and sweet; hot; mildly toxic.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Relaxes the middle [jiao], moves qi downward, benefits the large intestine, and disperses water accumulation, swelling, and toxic swellings. (Ning Yuan)
- Ground into powder, mixed with hot water, and applied topically to post-pox carbuncles [secondary infections after smallpox]. (Li Shizhen)
- Applied to sores and scabies; resolves [drug-induced or pathogenic] eruptions. (Li Shizhen)

**Stalk [秸, dried soybean straw]:**
Burned to ash, the ash is incorporated into preparations for marking and removing moles [nevi] and excising malignant or necrotic flesh. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Fubei (Adzuki Bean Flower / 腐婢) — Grain Chapter Three
segments.original_text:
【集解】《別錄》曰：腐婢生漢中，小豆花也。七月採之，陰乾四十日。

弘景曰：花與實異用，故不同品。方家不用。未解何故有腐婢之名？《本經》不言是小豆花，《別錄》乃云，未審是否？今海邊有小樹，狀如梔子，莖條多曲，氣似腐臭。土人呼爲腐婢，療瘧有效。以酒漬皮服，療心腹疾。此當是真，此條應入木部也。

恭曰：腐婢，相承以爲葛花。葛花消酒大勝，而小豆全無此效，當以葛花爲真。

禹錫曰：按：《別本》云：小豆花亦有腐氣。與葛花同服，飲酒不醉。與《本經》治酒病相合。陶、蘇二說並非。

甄權曰：腐婢即赤小豆花也。

頌曰：海邊小樹、葛花、赤小豆花，三物皆有腐婢之名，名同物異也。

宗奭曰：腐婢既在穀部，豆花爲是，不必多辯。

時珍曰：葛花已見本條。小豆能利小便，治熱中，下氣止渴，與腐婢主療相同，其爲豆花無疑。但小豆有數種，甄氏《藥性論》獨指爲赤小豆，今姑從之。

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。

【主治】瘧，寒熱邪氣，泄痢，陰不起。止消渴。病酒頭痛（《本經》。《心鏡》云：上証，用花同豉汁五味，煮羹食之）。消酒毒，明目，下水氣，治小兒丹毒熱腫，散氣滿不能食，煮一頓食之（《藥性》）。治熱中積熱，痔漏下血（時珍。《宣明》葛花丸中用之）。
segments.translation:
**[Collected Explanations]**

*Mingyi Bielu* states: Fubei [腐婢] grows in Hanzhong; it is the flower of the small bean [xiaodou]. It is gathered in the seventh month and dried in the shade for forty days.

[Tao] Hongjing states: The flower and the fruit have different medicinal uses and therefore belong to different categories. Physicians do not use it [the flower]. I cannot determine why it bears the name 'fubei' [lit. 'rotten maidservant']. The *Shennong Bencao Jing* [Classic of Materia Medica] does not say it is the small bean flower; it is the *Bielu* that claims so, and I am uncertain whether this is correct. There is currently a small tree growing near the seashore that resembles the gardenia [zhizi] in form, with many curved stems and branches and a fetid, rotten smell. The local people call it fubei; it is effective in treating malaria [nüe]. The bark soaked in wine and taken orally treats ailments of the heart and abdomen. This plant is likely the true fubei, and this entry ought to be placed in the Wood section.

[Su] Gong states: By long-standing convention, fubei has been understood to be the flower of kudzu [ge, *Pueraria*]. The kudzu flower is far superior in dispelling the effects of alcohol, whereas the small bean has no such efficacy at all; therefore the kudzu flower should be considered the true fubei.

Yu Xi states: According to an alternate edition [of the classics], the small bean flower also has a fetid odor. When taken together with kudzu flower, it prevents intoxication from drinking wine. This accords with the *Bencao Jing*'s indication of treating alcohol-related illness. The views of both Tao [Hongjing] and Su [Gong] are incorrect.

Zhen Quan states: Fubei is precisely the flower of the red small bean [chixiaodou].

[Su] Song states: The seashore small tree, the kudzu flower, and the red small bean flower — all three bear the name fubei; the name is shared but the substances are different.

[Kou] Zongshi states: Since fubei appears in the Grain section, the bean flower is the correct identification; there is no need for further debate.

[Li] Shizhen states: The kudzu flower has already been discussed in its own entry. The small bean can promote urination, treat heat accumulation in the interior [re zhong], direct qi downward, and relieve thirst — indications that coincide with those of fubei — and there is no doubt that it refers to the bean flower. However, there are several varieties of small bean; Zhen [Quan]'s *Yaoxing Lun* [Treatise on the Properties of Medicines] specifically identifies it as the red small bean [chixiaodou], and for now I will follow that identification.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Acrid [xin], balanced [ping], non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
Malaria [nüe]; cold and heat [alternating] pathogenic qi; diarrhea and dysentery [xieli]; failure of the male organ to become erect. Relieves wasting-thirst [xiao ke / diabetes-like syndrome]. Headache due to alcohol intoxication (*Bencao Jing*. The *Xinjing* [Heart Mirror] states: for the above conditions, use the flower together with fermented bean sauce [dou chi], juice, and five seasonings, cooked into a broth and eaten). Dissipates alcohol toxicity; brightens the eyes; moves water-qi downward; treats erysipelas with fever and swelling in children [xiao er dan du re zhong]; disperses qi fullness with inability to eat — boil and consume in a single serving (*Yaoxing* [Treatise on Properties]). Treats accumulated interior heat [re zhong ji re] and hemorrhoidal bleeding [zhi lou xia xue] (Li Shizhen; used in the Ge Hua Wan [Kudzu Flower Pill] formula from the *Xuanming* [Formulary]).

segments.title_inferred: White Bean (Bai Dou / Fan Dou)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】飯豆。

【集解】詵曰︰白豆苗，嫩者可作菜食，生食亦妙。

穎曰︰浙東一種味甚勝，用以作醬、作腐極佳。北方水白豆，相似而不及也。

原曰︰白豆即飯豆也，粥飯皆可拌食。

時珍曰︰飯豆，小豆之白者也，亦有土黃色者。豆大如綠豆而長。四、五月種之。苗葉似赤小豆而略大，可食，莢亦似小豆。一種豆，葉如大豆，可作飯、作腐，亦其類也。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。原曰︰鹹，平。

【主治】補五臟，調中，助十二經脈（孟詵）。暖腸胃（《日華》）。殺鬼氣。腎之谷，腎病宜食之（思邈）。

葉
煮食，利五臟，下氣（《日華》）。
segments.translation:
**[Names]** Also called Fan Dou [Rice Bean / Meal Bean].

**[Collected Commentaries]**
Meng Shen [詵] states: The young shoots of the white bean may be eaten as a vegetable; eating them raw is also excellent.

Ying [穎] states: A variety from eastern Zhejiang has a particularly fine flavor and is excellent for making sauce [醬] and bean curd [腐]. The northern water-white bean is similar but inferior.

Yuan [原] states: The white bean is none other than the fan dou [rice/meal bean]; it can be mixed into porridge or cooked rice and eaten.

Li Shizhen [時珍] states: The fan dou is the white variety of the small bean [小豆]; there are also earth-yellow colored ones. The bean is the size of the mung bean but longer. It is sown in the fourth and fifth months. The seedlings and leaves resemble those of the red adzuki bean [赤小豆] but are slightly larger and are edible; the pods are also similar to the small bean. There is another variety whose leaves resemble those of the soybean [大豆]; it can be made into cooked rice or bean curd, and belongs to the same category.

**[Qi and Flavor]** Sweet, neutral [平], non-toxic. Yuan states: Salty, neutral.

**[Indications / Main Treatments]**
- Tonifies the five viscera [五臟], harmonizes the middle [調中], and supports the twelve channels [十二經脈]. (Meng Shen)
- Warms the intestines and stomach. (*Rihua* [日華本草])
- Dispels [lit. kills] ghost qi [鬼氣; malignant/pestilential influences].
- It is the grain of the kidney; those with kidney disease should eat it. (Sun Simiao [思邈])

**Leaves**
Boiled and eaten: benefits the five viscera and descends qi [下氣]. (*Rihua*)

segments.title_inferred: Lü Dou – Wild Black Soybean (Grain Chapter III)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰乃自生稻名也。此豆原是野生，故名。今人亦種之於下地矣。

【集解】藏器曰︰豆生田野，小而黑，堪作醬。《爾雅》戎菽一名驢豆，古名豆，是也。
瑞曰︰豆，即黑豆中最細者。

時珍曰︰此即黑小豆也。小科細粒，霜後乃熟。陳氏指爲戎菽，誤矣。《爾雅》亦無此文。戎菽乃胡豆。豆乃鹿豆，見菜部。並四月熟。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

【主治】去賊風風痺，婦人產後冷血，炒令焦黑，及熱投酒中，漸漸飲之（藏器）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of the Name**
Li Shizhen states: [Lü dou (穭豆)] is the name given to rice that grows by itself [i.e., volunteer plants]. This bean originally grew wild, hence the name. Nowadays people also cultivate it in low-lying fields.

**Collected Commentaries**
Chen Cangqi [藏器] states: This bean grows in fields and wilderness; it is small and black, and is suitable for making [fermented] bean paste [醬]. In the *Erya*, 'Rong shu' [戎菽] is also called 'lü dou' [驢豆]; the ancient name 'dou' refers to this plant.
Wu Rui [瑞] states: Lü dou is simply the finest and smallest variety among black beans.

Li Shizhen states: This is the small black bean [黑小豆]. It is a small plant with fine, tiny grains, and it ripens only after the first frost. Master Chen's identification of it as 'Rong shu' is mistaken. Furthermore, no such text exists in the *Erya*. 'Rong shu' is in fact the foreign pea [胡豆]. 'Lü dou' [鹿豆, deer bean] is a separate plant; see the Vegetables section. Both [Rong shu and lu dou] ripen in the fourth month.

**Flavor and Nature**
Sweet, warm, non-toxic.

**Indications and Treatments**
Eliminating bandit-wind [賊風, a type of pathogenic wind] and wind-impediment [風痺, wind-type painful obstruction]; treating cold blood [cold stagnation in the blood] in women after childbirth. [Method:] Stir-fry the beans until they are scorched black, then while still hot, steep them in wine, and drink the preparation gradually. (Chen Cangqi)

segments.title_inferred: Wandou (Pea) — Grains, Chapter Three
segments.original_text:
【釋名】胡豆（《拾遺》）、戎菽（《爾雅》）、回鶻豆（《遼志》。《飲膳正要》作回回豆。回回，即回鶻也）、畢豆（《唐史》。崔寔《月令》作𧰆豆）、青小豆（《千金》）、青斑豆（《別錄》）、麻累。

時珍曰︰胡豆，豌豆也。其苗柔弱宛宛，故得豌名。種出胡戎，嫩時青色，老則斑麻，故有胡、戎、青斑、麻累諸名。陳藏器《拾遺》雖有胡豆，但云苗似豆，生田野間，米中往往有之。然豌豆、蠶豆皆有胡豆之名。陳氏所云，蓋豌豆也。豌豆之粒小，故米中有之。《爾雅》︰戎菽謂之荏菽。《管子》︰山戎出荏菽，布之天下。並注云︰即胡豆也。《唐史》︰畢豆出自西戎回鶻地面。
張揖《廣雅》︰畢豆、豌豆，留豆也。《別錄》序例云︰丸藥如胡豆大者，即青斑豆也。孫思邈《千金方》云︰青小豆一名胡豆，一名麻累。《鄴中記》云︰石虎諱胡，改胡豆爲國豆。此數說，皆指豌豆也。蓋古昔呼豌豆爲胡豆，今則蜀人專呼蠶豆爲胡豆，而豌豆名胡豆，人不知矣。又鄉人亦呼豌豆大者爲淮豆，蓋回鶻音相近也。

【集解】時珍曰︰豌豆種出西胡，今北土甚多。八、九月下種，苗生柔弱如蔓，有鬚。葉似蒺藜葉，兩兩對生，嫩時可食。三、四月開小花如蛾形，淡紫色。結莢長寸許，子圓如藥丸，亦似甘草子。出胡地者大如杏仁。煮、炒皆佳，磨粉面甚白細膩。百谷之中，最爲先登。又有野豌豆，粒小不堪，惟苗可茹，名翹搖，見菜部。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。思邈曰︰甘、鹹，溫、平，澀。
瑞曰︰多食發氣病。

【主治】消渴，淡煮食之，良（藏器）。治寒熱熱中，除吐逆，止泄痢下，利小便、腹脹滿（思邈）。調營衛，益中平氣。煮食，下乳汁。可作醬用（瑞）。煮飲，殺鬼毒心病，解乳石毒發。研末，塗癰腫痘瘡。作澡豆，去䵟，令人面光澤（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰豌豆屬土，故其所主病多系脾胃。元時飲膳，每用此豆搗去皮，同羊肉治食，云補中益氣。今爲日用之物，而唐、宋本草見遺，可謂缺典矣。《千金》、《外台》洗面澡豆方，盛用畢豆面，亦取其白膩耳。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**

Alternate names: Hudou [foreign bean] (*Shiyi* [Supplement to Materia Medica]); Rongshu [Rong legume] (*Erya*); Huihu Bean (*Liaozhi* [Liao Annals]; the *Yinshan Zhengyao* [Dietary Principles] writes it as Huihui Bean — Huihui being the same as Huihu [Uyghur]); Bidou (*Tang History*; Cui Shi's *Yueling* [Monthly Ordinances] writes it as 𧰆豆 [an archaic character]); Qing Xiaodou [small green bean] (*Qianjin* [Essential Formulas Worth a Thousand Gold]); Qingban Dou [blue-speckled bean] (*Bielu* [Supplementary Records]); and Malei.

Li Shizhen states: The hudou is the wandou [pea]. Its seedlings are delicate and trailing [wan-wan, supple and drooping], hence the name wan. The variety originates from the Hu and Rong [northern and western barbarian] regions; when tender the seeds are green, and when mature they become mottled and hemp-colored — hence the various names hu, rong, qingban [blue-speckled], and malei [hemp-like clustering]. Chen Cangqi's *Shiyi* does include an entry for hudou, but only says its seedlings resemble bean plants and it grows wild in fields, often found among milled grain. However, both wandou and cándou [broad bean] carry the name hudou. What Chen describes is most likely wandou, since wandou grains are small and thus appear among milled grain. The *Erya* states: 'Rongshu is called Renshu.' The *Guanzi* states: 'The Mountain Rong [tribes] produced renshu and spread it throughout the realm,' with commentary noting this is the hudou. The *Tang History* records: 'Bidou originates from the western Rong Huihu [Uyghur] territories.'

Zhang Ji's *Guangya* [Expanded Elegance] states: 'Bidou and wandou are liudou [留豆].' The prefatory notes to the *Bielu* state: 'Pills the size of hudou refer to qingban dou.' Sun Simiao's *Qianjin Fang* states: 'Qing xiaodou is also named hudou and also named malei.' The *Ye Zhong Ji* [Records of Ye] states: 'Shi Hu, who avoided the character hu as a taboo [it was homophonous with his personal name], renamed hudou as guodou [state bean].' All of these references point to wandou. In ancient times wandou was universally called hudou; nowadays, people in Shu [Sichuan] exclusively call cándou [broad bean] hudou, and the old name hudou for wandou has been largely forgotten. Furthermore, rural people also call the larger variety of wandou 'Huai Bean' [淮豆], likely because the sound of 'Huai' approximates the sound of 'Huihu.'

**[Comprehensive Explanation]**

Li Shizhen states: Wandou originates from the western Hu regions and is now very widespread in northern China. Seeds are sown in the eighth and ninth months. The seedlings grow soft and vine-like, with tendrils. The leaves resemble those of tribulus [ji li, *Tribulus terrestris*], growing in opposite pairs; when tender they may be eaten. In the third and fourth months, small moth-shaped flowers bloom in pale purple. The pods grow to about one cun [approximately 3 cm] in length; the seeds are round like medicinal pills, also resembling the seeds of licorice [*Glycyrrhiza* spp.]. Those from Hu regions are as large as apricot kernels. They are excellent boiled or stir-fried; when ground into flour the meal is very white, fine, and smooth. Among all the hundred grains, it is the earliest to ripen. There is also a wild pea [ye wandou], whose grains are small and of poor quality; only the shoots are edible — it is called qiao yao [翹搖] and is described in the Vegetables section.

**[Qi and Flavor]**

Sweet, balanced [ping], non-toxic. Sun Simiao states: Sweet, salty, warm-balanced, and astringent.
Wu Rui states: Consuming in excess causes qi-disorder diseases [bloating and flatulence].

**[Indications and Treatments]**

- **Wasting-thirst [xiaoke; diabetes-like condition]**: Boil plainly and eat — highly effective. (Chen Cangqi)
- **Alternating cold and heat, interior heat, vomiting and counterflow, stopping dysenteric diarrhea, promoting urination, and abdominal fullness and distension**. (Sun Simiao)
- **Regulating the ying [nutritive] and wei [defensive] qi, supplementing the center and balancing qi; boiled and eaten, it promotes the flow of breast milk; may also be made into paste/sauce [jiang]**. (Wu Rui)
- **When decocted as a drink, it counters malignant toxin [gui du] affecting the heart, and resolves toxicity from mineral drug reactions [ru shi du, i.e., stalactite/mineral elixir poisoning]; ground into powder and applied topically to treat carbuncles, swellings, and pox sores; made into a 'bath-bean' [zao dou] scrub to remove facial dark spots [dai], making the complexion luminous and smooth**. (Li Shizhen)

**[Elaboration]**

Li Shizhen states: Wandou belongs to the Earth phase [属土], and therefore the diseases it principally treats are mostly related to the spleen and stomach. During the Yuan dynasty, court dietary preparations frequently used these beans — pounded with the skin removed and cooked together with mutton — said to supplement the center and augment qi. Today it is an everyday food, yet it was omitted from the Tang and Song materia medica compendia — truly a regrettable lacuna in those records. The face-washing bath-bean formulas in the *Qianjin* and *Waitai* [Secret Essentials from the Imperial Library] make extensive use of bidou [pea] flour, also on account of its whiteness and smooth texture.

segments.title_inferred: Broad Bean (Cándòu) — Grains, Chapter Three
segments.original_text:
【釋名】胡豆。

時珍曰︰豆莢狀如老蠶，故名。王禎《農書》謂其蠶時始熟故名，亦通。吳瑞《本草》以此爲豌豆，誤矣。此豆種亦自西胡來，雖與豌豆同名、同時種，而形性迥別。《太平御覽》云︰張騫使外國，得胡豆種歸。指此也。今蜀人呼此爲胡豆，而豌豆不復名胡豆矣。

【集解】時珍曰︰蠶豆南土種之，蜀中尤多。八月下種，冬生嫩苗可茹。方莖中空。葉狀如匙頭，本圓末尖，面綠背白，柔濃，一枝三葉。二月開花如蛾狀，紫白色，又如豇豆花。結角連綴如大豆，頗似蠶形。蜀人收其子以備荒歉。

【氣味】甘、微辛，平，無毒。
苦、微甘，溫。

【主治】快胃，和臟腑（汪穎）。
酒醉不省，油鹽炒熟，煮湯灌之，效（穎）。

【發明】時珍曰︰蠶豆本草失載。萬表《積善堂方》言︰一女子誤吞針入腹，諸醫不能治。一人教令煮蠶豆同韭菜食之，針自大便同出。此亦可驗其性之利臟腑也。

苗
segments.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]** Also called Húdòu [Foreign/Western Bean].

Li Shizhen states: The bean pod resembles a mature silkworm in shape, hence the name [cándòu, 'silkworm bean']. Wang Zhen's *Nóngshū* [Agricultural Treatise] says it is named so because it ripens at the time of silkworm-rearing, which is also a plausible explanation. Wu Rui's *Běncǎo* mistakenly identifies this plant as the garden pea [wāndòu], which is incorrect. This bean also originates from the Western Regions [Hú], and although it shares a name and planting season with the garden pea, the two differ greatly in form and character. The *Tàipíng Yùlǎn* [Imperial Readings of the Taiping Era] records that Zhang Qian, on his mission to foreign lands, brought back seeds of the Húdòu. That record refers to this very plant. Today the people of Sichuan call this bean 'húdòu,' while the garden pea is no longer referred to by that name.

**[Collected Explanations]** Li Shizhen states: The broad bean is cultivated in the southern lands, and is especially abundant in Sichuan. Seeds are sown in the eighth month [lunar]; in winter the tender seedlings emerge and may be eaten as vegetables. The stem is square and hollow within. The leaves are shaped like the head of a spoon — rounded at the base and tapering to a point — green on the upper surface and white on the underside, soft and thick, with three leaflets per branch. In the second month [lunar] flowers open in the shape of a moth, purple-white in colour, resembling also the flowers of the cowpea [jiāngdòu]. The pods form in clusters like those of the soybean and bear a distinct resemblance to a silkworm in shape. The people of Sichuan collect the seeds as a reserve against famine and shortage.

**[Flavour and Nature]** Sweet, slightly acrid [xīn], balanced [píng], non-toxic.
[Also recorded as:] Bitter, slightly sweet, warm.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Invigorates the stomach and harmonises the viscera and bowels. (Wang Ying)
- For unconsciousness due to drunkenness: stir-fry the beans in oil and salt until fully cooked, then boil into a broth and pour it into the patient's mouth; effective. (Wang Ying)

**[Elucidation]** Li Shizhen states: The broad bean was omitted from earlier materia medica works. Wan Biao's *Jīshàntáng Fāng* [Prescriptions of the Hall of Accumulated Virtue] records the following case: a woman accidentally swallowed a needle, and no physician could treat her. Someone advised her to cook broad beans together with Chinese chives [jiǔcài] and eat them; the needle subsequently passed out with the stool. This case further corroborates the bean's property of benefiting and unobstructing the viscera and bowels.

**[Sprouts/Seedlings]** [Entry continues — text truncated at 苗]

segments.title_inferred: Cowpea (Jiangdou)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】豆夅、豆雙。音絳雙。【時珍曰】：此豆紅色居多，莢必雙生，故有豇、豆夅、豆雙之名。《廣雅》指爲胡豆，誤矣。

【氣味】甘、鹹，平，無毒。

【主治】理中益氣，補腎健胃，和五臟，調營衛，生精髓，止消渴、吐逆泄痢，小便數，解鼠莽毒（時珍）。
segments.translation:
【Explanatory Names】 Also written as 豆夅 and 豆雙 (pronounced jiàng and shuāng). [Li] Shizhen states: This bean is most commonly red in color, and its pods invariably grow in pairs; hence it bears the names jiāng [豇], 豆夅, and 豆雙. The Guangya [a classical dictionary] identifies it as húdòu [a type of foreign bean], which is incorrect.

【Flavor and Nature】 Sweet and salty; balanced [neutral in temperature]; non-toxic.

【Primary Treatments】 Regulates the middle [burner] and replenishes qi; tonifies the kidneys and strengthens the stomach; harmonizes the five viscera; regulates the ying [nutritive] and wei [defensive] qi; generates essence and marrow; stops wasting-thirst [消渴, diabetes-like condition], retching and counterflow, diarrhea and dysentery, and frequent urination; counteracts the toxicity of shǔmǎng [鼠莽, a poisonous plant, likely Gelsemium elegans or similar]. (Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Hyacinth Bean / Flat Bean (Biandou) — Grains, Chapter 3
segments.original_text:
【釋名】沿籬豆（俗）、蛾眉豆。

時珍曰：扁，本作扁，莢形扁也。沿籬，蔓延也。蛾眉，象豆脊白路之形也。

【集解】弘景曰：扁豆人家種之於籬垣，其莢蒸食甚美。

頌曰：蔓延而上，大葉細花，花有紫、白二色，莢生花下。其實有黑、白二種，白者溫而黑者小冷，入藥用白者。黑者名鵲豆，蓋以其黑間有白道，如鵲羽也。

時珍曰：扁豆，二月下種，蔓生延纏。葉大如杯，團而有尖。其花狀如小蛾，有翅尾形。其莢凡十餘樣，或長或團，或如龍爪、虎爪，或如豬耳、刀鐮，種種不同，皆累累成枝。白露後實更繁衍，嫩時可充蔬食茶料，老則收子煮食。子有黑、白、赤、斑四色。一種莢硬不堪食。惟豆子粗圓而色白者可入藥，本草不分別，亦缺文也。

白扁豆

【氣味】甘，微溫，無毒。

詵曰：微寒，患冷人勿食。

弘景曰：患寒熱者不可食。

【主治】和中，下氣（《別錄》）。補五臟，主嘔逆。久服頭不白（孟詵）。療霍亂吐利不止，研末和醋服之（同上）。行風氣，治女子帶下，解酒毒、河豚魚毒（蘇頌）。解一切草木毒，生嚼及煮汁飲，取效（甄權）。止泄痢，消暑，暖脾胃，除濕熱，止消渴（時珍）。
女子赤白帶下，乾末，米飲服之（蘇頌）。焙研服，治崩帶。作餛飩食，治泄痢。擂水飲，解中一切藥毒垂死。功同扁豆（時珍）。
霍亂吐下不止（《別錄》）。吐利後轉筋，生搗一把，入少酢絞汁服，立瘥。醋炙研服，治瘕疾（孟詵）。杵敷蛇咬（大明）。

藤
霍亂，同蘆葦、人參、倉米等分，煎服（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：硬殼白扁豆，其子充實，白而微黃，其氣腥香，其性溫平，得乎中和，脾之穀也。入太陰氣分，通利三焦，能化清降濁，故專治中宮之病，消暑除濕而解毒也。其軟殼及黑鵲色者，其性微涼，但可供食，亦調脾胃。
segments.translation:
**[Names]** Yanlídòu (common name: 'fence-climbing bean'); Éméi dòu ('moth-eyebrow bean').

Li Shizhen says: The character biǎn [扁] originally meant 'flat,' referring to the flat shape of the pod. 'Yanlí' means climbing along a fence (i.e., a trailing vine). 'Éméi' describes the white ridge running along the back of the bean, resembling the shape of a moth's eyebrow.

**[Collected Explanations]** Hongjing [Tao Hongjing] says: The flat bean [biandou] is cultivated by households on fences and trellises; its pods are delicious when steamed.

Su Song says: It climbs upward on its vines, with large leaves and small flowers. The flowers are of two colors, purple and white, and the pods form below the flowers. The seeds come in two kinds, black and white. The white variety is warming [in nature] and the black is slightly cooling; it is the white variety that is used medicinally. The black kind is called quèdòu ('magpie bean'), because its black surface is streaked with white lines, resembling the feathers of a magpie.

Li Shizhen says: The flat bean is sown in the second month; it grows as a trailing vine, twisting and climbing. Its leaves are as large as a cup, rounded but pointed at the tip. The flowers resemble small moths, with wing and tail shapes. The pods come in more than ten varieties — some long, some rounded, some like a dragon's claw or tiger's claw, some like a pig's ear or a sickle blade — each differing from the others, and all growing in clusters along the branches. After the White Dew [solar term, early September], the fruits become even more abundant. When young, the pods can be used as a vegetable or as an accompaniment to tea; when mature, the seeds are gathered and cooked for eating. The seeds come in four colors: black, white, red, and mottled. One variety has pods so hard they cannot be eaten. Only the seeds that are thick, round, and white in color are suitable for medicinal use. The materia medica texts do not make this distinction, which is an omission.

---

**White Flat Bean [Bái Biǎndòu]**

**[Flavor and Nature]** Sweet, slightly warm, non-toxic.

Meng Shen says: Slightly cold; those suffering from cold conditions should not eat it.

Tao Hongjing says: Those suffering from alternating chills and fever should not eat it.

**[Indications and Treatments]**
- Harmonizes the middle [burner] and descends qi (*Bielu*).
- Supplements the five viscera; governs vomiting and counterflow. Long-term consumption prevents the hair from turning white (Meng Shen).
- Treats incessant vomiting and diarrhea in cholera [huoluan]: grind to powder and take with vinegar (same source).
- Moves wind-qi; treats vaginal discharge [daixia] in women; resolves alcohol toxicity and pufferfish [fugu] poisoning (Su Song).
- Resolves all herb and plant toxins: chew raw or drink the decoction to achieve effect (Zhen Quan).
- Stops diarrhea and dysentery; disperses summer-heat; warms the spleen and stomach; eliminates damp-heat; stops wasting-thirst [xiaoke] (Li Shizhen).

**[Pod preparations]**
- For red and white vaginal discharge in women: take dried powder suspended in rice water (Su Song).
- Roast, grind, and take orally to treat uterine flooding and vaginal discharge [chongdai].
- Made into wonton dumplings and eaten to treat diarrhea and dysentery.
- Pounded and the liquid drunk to resolve near-fatal poisoning by any drug or substance. Efficacy equivalent to the bean itself (Li Shizhen).

**[Flower preparations]**
- Incessant vomiting and diarrhea in cholera (*Bielu*).
- For muscle cramps [zhuanjin] following vomiting and diarrhea: pound a handful of fresh flowers, add a small amount of vinegar, wring out the juice and drink — recovery is immediate.
- Roast with vinegar, grind, and take orally to treat abdominal masses [zhajia] (Meng Shen).
- Pound and apply topically for snakebite (Daming).

**[Vine preparations]**
- For cholera: take in equal parts with reed [lúwěi], ginseng [rénshēn], and aged rice [cāngjǐ], decocted and drunk (Li Shizhen).

**[Elaboration]** Li Shizhen says: The white flat bean with a hard shell has full, plump seeds — white with a faint yellow tinge, with a raw, faintly fragrant odor. Its nature is warm and balanced, embodying the quality of centrality and harmony; it is the grain [corresponding to] the spleen. It enters the qi aspect of the Taiyin [spleen channel], opens and regulates the Triple Burner, and is capable of transforming the clear and descending the turbid. For this reason it specializes in treating diseases of the Middle Palace [the spleen and stomach], dispersing summer-heat, eliminating dampness, and resolving toxins. The soft-shelled variety and the black-magpie-colored variety are slightly cooling in nature; they may be consumed as food and also regulate the spleen and stomach.

segments.title_inferred: Sword Bean (Knife Bean) — Grain Chapter Three
segments.original_text:
【釋名】挾劍豆。

時珍曰：以莢形命名也。案：段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：樂浪有挾劍豆，莢生橫斜，如人挾劍。即此豆也。

【集解】穎曰：刀豆長尺許，可入醬用。

時珍曰：刀豆，人多種之。三月下種，蔓生引一、二丈，葉如豇豆葉而稍長大，五、六、七月開紫花如蛾形。結莢，長者近尺，微似皂莢，扁而劍脊，三棱宛然。嫩時煮食、醬食、蜜煎皆佳。老則收子，子大如拇指頭，淡紅色。同豬肉、雞肉煮食，尤美。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】溫中下氣，利腸胃，止呃逆，益腎補元（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰：刀豆本草失載，惟近時小書載其暖而補元陽也。又有人病後呃逆不止，聲聞鄰家。或令取刀豆子燒存性，白湯調服二錢即止。此亦取其下氣歸元，而逆自止也。
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate name: Jiā jiàn dòu [Sword-carrying Bean].

Li Shizhen states: The name derives from the shape of the pod. According to Duan Chengshi's *Youyang Zazu*: 'In Lelang there is a sword-carrying bean whose pods grow obliquely, like a person carrying a sword at the side.' This is the very same bean.

**Collected Commentaries**
Ying [i.e., Wang Ying] states: The sword bean grows to about one chi [approximately one foot] in length and can be used in pickling sauces.

Li Shizhen states: The sword bean is widely cultivated. Seeds are sown in the third month; the vine climbs one to two zhang [roughly ten to twenty feet]. Its leaves resemble those of the cowpea [vigna] but are somewhat longer and larger. In the fifth, sixth, and seventh months, it produces purple flowers shaped like moths. The pods that form can reach nearly one chi in length; they somewhat resemble the honey locust pod [皂莢], being flat with a ridge along the spine, clearly three-angled. When tender, they are excellent boiled, pickled in sauce, or candied with honey. When mature, the seeds are harvested; they are as large as a thumb-tip and pale red in color. They are especially delicious when cooked together with pork or chicken.

**Flavor and Nature**
Sweet; neutral [balanced in temperature]; non-toxic.

**Principal Indications and Treatments**
Warms the center [middle burner] and directs qi downward; benefits the intestines and stomach; stops hiccoughs [呃逆, counterflow qi]; tonifies the kidneys and replenishes primordial [yuan] qi. (Li Shizhen)

**Elucidation**
Li Shizhen states: The sword bean was omitted from earlier materia medica works; only in recent minor writings is it recorded that it warms and supplements primordial yang [元陽]. There was also a case of a person who, after illness, suffered incessant hiccoughing so loud it could be heard in the neighboring house. Someone instructed that sword bean seeds be taken and roasted to ash while retaining their nature [燒存性, i.e., charred but not fully consumed to ash], then mixed with plain hot water and taken in a dose of two qian [approximately 7.5 g]. The hiccoughing stopped immediately. This too makes use of the bean's property of directing qi downward and returning it to its origin [歸元], whereupon the counterflow naturally ceases.

segments.title_inferred: Li Dou (Mucuna Bean) — Names, Descriptions, Properties, and Uses
segments.original_text:
【釋名】狸豆（《綱目》）、虎豆。

藏器曰︰豆子作狸首文，故名。

時珍曰︰黎亦黑色也。此豆莢老則黑色，有毛露筋，如虎、狸指爪，其子亦有點，如虎、狸之斑，煮之汁黑，故有諸名。

【集解】藏器曰︰黎豆生江南，蔓如葛，子如皂莢子，作狸首文。人炒食之，別無功用。陶氏注蚺蛇膽云如黎豆者，即此也。《爾雅》云︰諸慮一名虎涉。又注根云︰苗如豆。

《爾雅》︰攝，虎蘽。郭璞注云︰江東呼蘽爲藤，似葛而粗大。纏蔓林樹，莢有毛刺。一名豆搜，今虎豆也，千歲蘽是矣。

時珍曰︰《爾雅》虎蘽，即狸豆也。古人謂藤爲蘽，後人訛蘽爲狸矣。《爾雅》山蘽、虎蘽，原是二種。陳氏合而爲一，謂諸慮一名虎涉，又以爲千歲蘽，並誤矣。千歲蘽見草部。狸豆野生，山人亦有種之者。三月下種生蔓。其葉如豇豆葉，但文理偏斜。六、七月開花成簇，紫色，狀如扁豆花。一枝結莢十餘，長三、四寸，大如拇指，有白茸毛。老則黑而露筋，宛如乾熊指爪之狀。其子大如刀豆子，淡紫色，有斑點如狸文。煮去黑汁，同豬、雞肉再煮食，味乃佳。

【氣味】甘、微苦，溫，有小毒。多食令人悶。

【主治】溫中，益氣（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**[Names and Appellations]**
Also known as Li Dou [Raccoon-dog Bean] (from the *Bencao Gangmu*) and Hu Dou [Tiger Bean].

Chen Cangqi [Zangqi] says: The seeds bear markings resembling the face of a raccoon-dog [li], hence the name.

Li Shizhen says: "Li" [黎] also means black in color. When the pods of this bean are old, they turn black, with exposed fibers and hairs resembling the claws of tigers and raccoon-dogs. The seeds are spotted like the markings of tigers and raccoon-dogs, and the water turns black when boiled — hence all these names.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Chen Cangqi says: Li Dou grows in Jiangnan [south of the Yangtze River]. Its vines resemble kudzu [ge], and its seeds resemble those of the honey locust [zaojia], bearing raccoon-dog-face markings. People roast and eat them; there are no other noted uses. Tao [Hongjing]'s annotation on the bile of the python [rán shé dǎn] mentions something resembling Li Dou — that is precisely this plant. The *Erya* states: "Zhulü" is another name for "Hushe [Tiger-ford]." Another annotation on the root [gen] states: the seedlings resemble beans.

The *Erya* records: "She, Hulei [Tiger Vine]." Guo Pu's commentary states: In the Jiangdong region, "lei" is called "teng" [vine]. It resembles kudzu but is coarser and larger, wrapping around forest trees; its pods have hairy bristles. It is also called "Dousou"; this is the present-day Hu Dou [Tiger Bean], which is the same as the Qiansui Lei [Thousand-Year Vine].

Li Shizhen says: The "Hulei" [Tiger Vine] of the *Erya* is the same as Li Dou [Raccoon-dog Bean]. The ancients used "lei" to mean vine; later people corrupted "lei" [蘽] into "li" [狸, raccoon-dog]. In the *Erya*, "Shanlei" [Mountain Vine] and "Hulei" [Tiger Vine] are originally two different plants. Chen [Cangqi] conflated them into one, claiming that "Zhulü" is another name for "Hushe," and further identifying it as the Qiansui Lei — both of these are errors. The Qiansui Lei is found in the Herbs section [of this work]. Li Dou grows wild, though mountain-dwelling people also cultivate it. Seeds are sown in the third month and the vines grow out. The leaves resemble those of cowpea [jiangdou] but the veining runs obliquely. In the sixth and seventh months flowers bloom in clusters, purple in color, shaped like the flowers of hyacinth bean [biandou]. Each stem produces over ten pods, three to four inches long, as thick as a thumb, covered in white downy hair. When old, the pods turn black with exposed fibers, looking exactly like the dried claws of a bear. The seeds are as large as sword bean [dao dou] seeds, pale purple in color, with spots resembling the markings of a raccoon-dog. Boil them and discard the black water; then cook again together with pork or chicken — only then is the flavor good.

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Sweet, slightly bitter; warm in nature; mildly toxic. Eating too much causes oppression [discomfort] in the chest.

**[Indications and Treatment]**
Warms the center [middle jiao], supplements qi. (Li Shizhen)

segments.title_inferred: Bean Yellow (Fermented Black Soybean Powder)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】時珍曰︰造法︰用黑豆一斗蒸熟，鋪席上，以蒿覆之，如造醬法，待上黃，取出晒乾，搗末收用。

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。

詵曰︰忌豬肉。

【主治】濕痺膝痛，五臟不足氣，胃氣結積，壯氣力，潤肌膚，益顏色，填骨髓，補虛損，能食，肥健人。以煉豬脂和丸，每服百丸，神驗秘方也。肥人勿服（詵。出《延年秘錄》方）。生嚼塗陰癢汗出（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Explanation of Name:** Li Shizhen states: The method of preparation is as follows — take one dou [approx. 10 liters] of black soybeans, steam them until cooked, spread them out on a mat, and cover with artemisia [wormwood], following the method for making fermented paste [jiang]. Wait until a yellow [mold] appears on the surface, then remove them, dry in the sun, pound into a powder, and store for use.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet, warm, non-toxic.

Meng Shen states: Pork is contraindicated when taking this.

**Indications and Treatments:**
- Damp impediment [shi bi] with knee pain
- Insufficiency of qi in the five viscera
- Stagnant accumulation of stomach qi
- Strengthens qi and physical power
- Moistens the skin
- Improves complexion
- Fills the bone marrow
- Supplements vacuity and depletion [xu sun]
- Promotes appetite
- Makes the body robust and healthy

Formulated into pills with refined lard, taken at one hundred pills per dose, this is a wondrously effective secret formula. Obese persons should not take it. (Meng Shen; sourced from the formula in the *Yannian Milu* [Secret Records for Prolonging Life].)

Chewing it raw and applying it topically treats itching of the genitals with sweating. (Li Shizhen.)

segments.title_inferred: Tofu (Bean Curd)
segments.original_text:
【集解】時珍曰︰豆腐之法，始於漢淮南王劉安。凡黑豆、黃豆及白豆、泥豆、豌豆、綠豆之類，皆可爲之。造法︰水浸磨碎，濾去滓，煎成，以鹽鹵汁或山礬葉或酸漿、醋澱就釜收之。又有入缸內，以石膏末收者。大抵得鹹、苦、酸、辛之物，皆可收斂爾。其面上凝結者，揭取晾乾，名豆腐皮，入饌甚佳也。

【氣味】甘、鹹，寒，有小毒。原曰︰性平。

頌曰︰寒而動氣。

瑞曰︰發腎氣、瘡疥、頭風，杏仁可解。

時珍曰︰按︰《延壽書》云︰有人好食豆腐中毒，醫不能治。作腐家言︰萊菔入湯中則腐不成。遂以萊菔湯下藥而愈。大抵暑月恐有人汗，尤宜慎之。

【主治】寬中益氣，和脾胃，消脹滿，下大腸濁氣（寧原）。清熱散血（時珍）。
segments.translation:
**Collected Explanations:** Li Shizhen states: The method of making tofu [bean curd] originated with Liu An, the Prince of Huainan [淮南王] of the Han dynasty. Black soybeans, yellow soybeans, white beans, mud beans [泥豆], peas, mung beans, and similar legumes can all be used to make it. The method of preparation is as follows: soak the beans in water, grind them into a fine paste, filter out the dregs, boil the liquid, and then coagulate it in the pot using brine [鹽鹵汁], or the leaves of alum-plant [山礬葉], or sour slurry [酸漿], or vinegar lees [醋澱]. There is also a method of pouring the liquid into a vat and coagulating it with powdered gypsum [石膏末]. In general, salty, bitter, sour, and pungent substances can all cause it to coagulate and set. The layer that congeals on the surface, when lifted off and dried, is called tofu skin [豆腐皮]; it is an excellent ingredient in dishes.

**Flavor and Nature:** Sweet and salty; cold [in thermal nature]; mildly toxic. [Ning] Yuan states: Its nature is neutral [平].

[Su] Song states: It is cold and stirs up [i.e., disturbs] the qi.

[Wu] Rui states: It can provoke kidney qi [disorders], sores and scabies [瘡疥], and head-wind [頭風]; apricot kernels [杏仁] can counteract these effects.

Li Shizhen states: According to the *Yanshou Shu* [Book of Prolonging Life]: A man who habitually ate tofu was poisoned by it, and physicians could not cure him. A tofu maker remarked that when radish [萊菔] is added to the soaking water, the tofu will not set. Thereupon, the physicians administered medicine with a radish decoction, and the man recovered. In the summer months, one should be especially cautious, as the tofu may have been contaminated by the sweat of the maker.

**Indications and Therapeutic Uses:** Loosens the middle [burner] and replenishes qi; harmonizes the spleen and stomach; disperses abdominal distension and fullness; expels turbid qi from the large intestine (Ning Yuan). Clears heat and disperses blood [stasis] (Li Shizhen).

segments.title_inferred: Aged Granary Rice (Chen Lin Mi)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】陳倉米（古名）、老米（俗名）、火米。

時珍︰有屋曰廩，無屋曰倉，皆官積也。方曰倉，圓曰囷，皆私積也。老亦陳也。火米有三，有火蒸治成者，有火燒治成者，又有畬田火米，與此不同。

【集解】弘景曰︰陳廩米即粳米久入倉陳赤者。以廩軍人，故曰廩爾。方中多用之。人以作醋，勝於新粳米也。

藏器曰︰廩米，吳人以粟爲良，漢地以粳爲善。亦猶吳鄭縞，貴遠賤近之意。確論其功，粟當居前。

宗奭曰︰諸家注說不言是粳是粟，然二米陳者性皆冷，煎煮亦無膏膩，頻食令人自利，與經說稍戾。

時珍曰︰廩米，北人多用粟，南人多用粳及秈，並水浸蒸晒爲之，亦有火燒過治成者。入倉陳久，皆氣過色變，故古人謂之紅粟紅腐，陳陳相因也。

【氣味】鹹、酸，溫，無毒。

藏器曰︰廩米熱食即熱，冷食即冷，假以火氣也，體自溫平。同馬肉飧，發痼疾。

時珍曰︰廩米年久，其性多涼，但炒食則溫爾，豈有熱食即熱者乎？

【主治】下氣，除煩渴，調胃止泄（《別錄》）。補五臟，澀腸胃（《日華》）。暖脾，去憊氣，宜作湯食（士良）。炊飯食，止痢，補中益氣，堅筋骨，通血脈，起陽道。以飯和酢搗封毒腫惡瘡，立瘥。北人以飯置瓮中，水浸令酸，食之，暖五臟六腑之氣。研取汁服，去卒心痛（孟詵）。寬中消食。多食易飢（寧原）。調腸胃，利小便，止渴除熱（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰陳倉米煮汁不渾，初時氣味俱盡，故沖淡可以養胃。古人多以煮汁煎藥，亦取其調腸胃、利小便、去濕熱之功也。《千金方》治洞注下利，炒此米研末飲服者，亦取此義。《日華子》謂其澀腸胃，寇氏謂其冷利，皆非中論。
segments.translation:
**Names and Nomenclature**
Alternate names: Chén cāng mǐ [aged granary rice] (ancient name); lǎo mǐ [old rice] (common name); huǒ mǐ [fire rice].

Li Shizhen notes: A roofed storage structure is called a lǐn [廩; covered granary], while one without a roof is called a cāng [倉; open granary]; both are government stores. Square ones are called cāng and round ones are called qūn [囷]; both are private stores. Lǎo [old] likewise means aged. There are three kinds of huǒ mǐ [fire rice]: one processed by steaming with fire, one processed by burning with fire, and another that is the fire rice of slash-and-burn [畬田] fields — this last is different from the present article.

**Collected Commentaries**
Tao Hongjing states: Aged granary rice is simply polished non-glutinous rice [粳米] that has been stored in a granary for a long time until it turns reddish. It was used to ration soldiers, hence the name lǐn [granary ration]. It is widely used in medicinal formulae. People also use it to make vinegar, and it surpasses fresh polished rice for that purpose.

Chen Cangqi states: For granary rice, the people of Wu [Jiangnan region] consider millet [粟] superior, while those of the Han [northern Chinese] lands favor polished non-glutinous rice [粳]. This is rather like the silk of Wu and Zheng — one values what is distant and disdains what is near. If one truly weighs their efficacy, millet should be ranked first.

Kou Zongshi states: The various commentators do not specify whether this is polished non-glutinous rice or millet; however, both types of aged rice are cool in nature, produce no rich or unctuous quality when boiled, and frequent consumption causes loose stools — which is slightly at odds with what the canonical texts say.

Li Shizhen states: For granary rice, people in the north mostly use millet, while those in the south mostly use polished non-glutinous rice [粳] or indica rice [秈], all of which are soaked in water, steamed, and sun-dried; some versions are also processed by fire-roasting. Once stored in a granary for a long time, the qi dissipates and the colour changes, which is why the ancients called it 'red grain and red rottenness' [紅粟紅腐] — the stale accumulating upon the stale.

**Flavour and Nature**
Salty and sour; warm; non-toxic.

Chen Cangqi states: Aged granary rice is warming when eaten hot and cooling when eaten cold, borrowing its quality from the heat or cold of preparation; in its own nature it is warm and balanced. Eaten together with horse meat, it can trigger chronic or deep-seated ailments [痼疾].

Li Shizhen states: Granary rice that has been aged for many years is predominantly cool in nature; it is only warm when stir-fried and eaten — how could eating it hot make it hot?

**Indications and Treatments**
- Descends qi, relieves vexation and thirst, regulates the stomach, and stops diarrhoea. (*Mingyi Bielu*)
- Supplements the five viscera; astringes [i.e., firms] the intestines and stomach. (*Rihua Bencao*)
- Warms the spleen, dispels fatigue qi [憊氣]; best taken as a soup or congee. (Shi Liang)
- Cooked as rice and eaten: stops dysentery, supplements the centre and boosts qi, strengthens sinews and bones, opens the blood vessels, and arouses [strengthens] the yang pathway [起陽道]. Mixed with vinegar [酢], pounded and applied as a poultice, it seals toxic swellings and malignant sores, bringing rapid cure. The northern people place cooked rice in a jar and soak it in water until it turns sour; eating this warms the qi of the five viscera and six bowels. Ground and the juice taken orally, it relieves sudden heart pain. (Meng Shen)
- Relaxes the centre and aids digestion; frequent consumption causes easy hunger. (Ning Yuan)
- Regulates the intestines and stomach, promotes urination, quenches thirst, and eliminates heat. (Li Shizhen)

**Elucidation**
Li Shizhen states: When aged granary rice is boiled, the resulting liquid is not turbid. Because both its qi and flavour have been largely exhausted over time, it is mild and bland, and thus suitable for nourishing the stomach. The ancients frequently used its boiled liquid as a vehicle for decocting medicines, prizing its functions of regulating the intestines and stomach, promoting urination, and dispelling damp-heat. The *Qianjin Fang* [Essential Prescriptions Worth a Thousand Gold] uses this rice stir-fried, ground into powder, and taken as a drink to treat torrential diarrhoea with incessant discharge [洞注下利] — drawing on the same principle. The claim of *Rihua Bencao* that it astringes the intestines and stomach, and Kou Zongshi's claim that it is cold and causes loose stools, are both not well-reasoned positions.

segments.title_inferred: Qingjing Dried Stone Xin Rice / Wufan (Black Rice of Southern Candle Plant)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】烏飯。

頌曰︰按︰陶隱居《登真隱訣》載︰太極真人青精乾石飯法。𩛓音信。𩛓之爲言飧也，謂以酒、蜜、藥草輩溲而曝之也。亦作𨑏。凡內外諸書並無此字，惟施於此飯之名耳。陳藏器《本草》名烏飯。

【集解】頌曰︰《登真隱訣》載南燭草木名狀，注見木部本條下。其作飯法︰以生白粳米一斛五斗舂治，淅取一斛二斗。用南燭木葉五斤，燥者三斤亦可，雜莖皮煮取汁，極令清冷，以溲米，米釋炊之。從四月至八月末，用新生葉，色皆深；九月至三月，用宿葉，色皆淺，可隨時進退其斤兩。又采軟枝莖皮，於石臼中搗碎。假令四、五月中作，可用十許斤熟舂，以斛二斗湯浸染得一斛也。比來只以水漬一、二宿，不必用湯，漉而炊之，初米正作綠色，蒸過便如紺色。若色不好，亦可淘去，更以新汁漬之。洒濩皆用此汁，惟令飯作正青色乃止。高格曝乾，當三蒸曝，每蒸輒以葉汁溲令浥。每日可服二升，勿復血食。填胃補髓，消滅三蟲。《上元寶經》云：子服草木之王，氣與神通；子食青燭之津，命不復殞。此之謂也。今茅山道士亦作此飯，或以寄遠。重蒸過食之，甚香甘也。

藏器曰︰烏飯法︰取南燭莖葉搗碎，漬汁浸粳米九浸，九蒸九曝，米粒緊小，黑如瑿珠，袋盛，可以適遠方也。

時珍曰︰此飯乃仙家服食之法，而今之釋家多於四月八日造之，以供佛耳。造者又入柿葉、白楊葉數十枝以助色，或又加生鐵一塊者，止知取其上色，不知乃服食家所忌也。

【氣味】甘，平，無毒。

【主治】日進一合，不飢，益顏色，堅筋骨，能行（藏器）。益腸胃，補髓，滅三蟲，久服變白，卻老。（蘇頌《出太極》）
segments.translation:
**[Synonyms]** Wufan [Black Rice].

Su Song states: According to Tao Hongjing's [Tao Yinju's] *Dengzhen Yinjue* [Secret Instructions for Ascending to Perfection], it records the method of the Taiji Perfected Person's Qingjing Dried Stone Xin [𩛓] Rice. The character 𩛓 is pronounced 'xin.' 𩛓 means something like 'soaked meal' [飧, sun], referring to food moistened with wine, honey, and medicinal herbs and then sun-dried. It is also written as 𨑏. This character appears in no internal or external books whatsoever and is used solely as the name of this particular rice preparation. Chen Cangqi's *Bencao* [Materia Medica] calls it Wufan [Black Rice].

**[Collected Explanations]** Su Song states: The *Dengzhen Yinjue* records the name and appearance of the plant Nanzhu [Southern Candle; *Vaccinium bracteatum*]; see the annotation in the Wood section under its own entry. The method for making the rice is as follows: take one hu five dou of raw white japonica rice [*jingmi*], hull and wash it to yield one hu two dou. Use five jin of fresh Nanzhu leaves — three jin of dried leaves will also suffice — together with stems and bark, boil to extract the juice, and let it become thoroughly clear and cold. Use this to soak the rice until it softens, then cook it. From the fourth month through the end of the eighth month, use newly grown leaves, which yield a deeper colour; from the ninth month through the third month, use old leaves, which yield a lighter colour — the quantity in jin may be adjusted accordingly with the season. Also gather soft branch stems and bark, pound them to pieces in a stone mortar. If preparing the rice in the fourth or fifth month, approximately ten or so jin may be used, well-pounded; steeping with one hu two dou of hot water will yield one hu of dyed [juice]. In recent practice, it suffices to soak [the plant material] in cold water for one or two nights without using hot water; strain and cook the rice. Initially the rice takes on a green colour; after steaming it becomes dark blue-purple [紺色, gàn sè, a deep purplish-blue]. If the colour is not satisfactory, the rice may be rinsed and steeped again in fresh juice. All rinsing and steeping should use this juice, continuing until the rice achieves a true blue-green [qing] colour. Dry on an elevated rack in the sun; it should undergo three cycles of steaming and sun-drying, and at each steaming the rice should be moistened with leaf juice until just damp. One may consume two sheng daily; meat and blood foods should no longer be eaten. It fills the stomach, nourishes the marrow, and eliminates the Three Worms [三蟲, i.e., intestinal parasites]. The *Shangyuan Baojing* [Precious Scripture of the Upper Prime] says: 'He who consumes the king of herbs and trees, his breath communicates with the divine; he who eats the essence of the Green Candle, his life shall no longer perish.' This is precisely what is meant. Today the Daoist priests of Maoshan also prepare this rice and sometimes send it to distant places. Steamed again and eaten, it is extremely fragrant and sweet.

Chen Cangqi states: The method for Wufan [Black Rice]: take the stems and leaves of Nanzhu, pound them to pieces, steep to extract the juice, and soak japonica rice in it — nine soakings, nine steamings, nine sun-dryings. The rice grains become firm and small, black as jet [瑿] pearls. Stored in a bag, it can be taken on long journeys to distant places.

Li Shizhen states: This rice is a method of dietary cultivation [服食] practiced by Daoist immortalists. Today Buddhist monks frequently prepare it on the eighth day of the fourth month to offer to the Buddha. Those who make it also add several dozen sprigs of persimmon leaves [柿葉] and white poplar leaves [白楊葉] to enhance the colour; some even add a piece of raw iron [生鐵]. They know only that these things improve the colour, not that raw iron is precisely what those who practice dietary cultivation must avoid.

**[Flavour and Nature]** Sweet, neutral, non-toxic.

**[Indications and Treatment]** Taking one ge daily prevents hunger, improves complexion, strengthens sinews and bones, and enables walking (Chen Cangqi). Benefits the intestines and stomach, nourishes the marrow, eliminates the Three Worms; taken over a long period, it reverses whitening of the hair and wards off old age. (Su Song, citing the *Taiji* scriptures.)

segments.title_inferred: Congee/Porridge (Grains, Chapter Four)
segments.original_text:
【釋名】糜。

時珍曰︰粥字象米在釜中相屬之形。《釋名》云︰煮米爲糜，使糜爛也。粥濁於糜，育育然也。厚曰饘，薄曰酏。

小麥粥

【氣味】甘，溫，無毒。
甘，溫、平，無毒。

【主治】止消渴煩熱（時珍）。

寒食粥（用杏仁和諸花作之）
咳嗽下血，氣調中（藏器）

糯米、秫米、黍米粥
益氣，治脾胃虛寒，泄痢吐逆，小兒痘瘡白色（時珍）。

粳米、秈米、粟米、粱米粥
利小便，止煩渴，養脾胃（時珍）。

【發明】時珍曰︰按︰羅天益《寶鑒》云︰粳、粟米粥，氣薄味淡，陽中之陰也。所以淡滲下行，能利小便。韓𢘅《醫通》云︰一人病淋，素不服藥。予令專啖粟米粥，絕去他味。旬餘減，月餘痊。此五穀治病之理也。又張耒《粥記》云︰每晨起，食粥一大碗。空腹胃虛，穀氣便作，所補不細。又極柔膩，與腸胃相得，最爲飲食之良。妙齊和尚說︰山中僧，每將旦一粥，甚系利害。如不食，則終日覺臟腑燥涸。蓋粥能暢胃氣，生津液也。大抵養生求安樂，亦無深遠難知之事，不過寢食之間爾。故作此勸人每日食粥，勿大笑也。又《蘇軾帖》云︰夜飢甚。吳子野勸食白粥，云能推陳致新，利膈益胃。粥既快美，粥後一覺，妙不可言也。此皆著粥之有益如此。諸谷作粥，詳見本條。古方有用藥物、粳、粟、粱米作粥，治病甚多。今略取其可常食者，集於下方，以備參考云。

赤小豆粥 利小便，消水腫腳氣，辟邪癘。

綠豆粥 解熱毒，止煩渴。

御米粥 治反胃，利大腸。

薏苡仁粥 除濕熱，利
segments.translation:
**[Explanation of Names]** Also called mí [糜, "mush"].

Li Shizhen states: The character zhōu [粥, "porridge/congee"] graphically depicts rice (米) within a cauldron (釜), connected together. The *Shiming* ["Explanation of Names"] says: cooking rice until it becomes mí means making it soft and disintegrated [糜爛]. Zhōu is thicker and murkier than mí, with a full, swelling quality. A thick preparation is called zhān [饘]; a thin one is called yǐ [酏].

---

**Wheat Congee [小麥粥]**

**[Qi and Flavor]** Sweet, warm, non-toxic. [Also noted as:] sweet, warm to neutral, non-toxic.

**[Primary Treatments]** Stops wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě] and feverish agitation. (Li Shizhen)

---

**Cold Food Festival Congee [寒食粥]** (made with apricot kernels and various flowers)

Treats coughing with expectoration of blood, regulates qi and harmonizes the middle [burner]. (Chen Cangqi)

---

**Glutinous Rice, Broomcorn Millet, and Panicum Millet Congee [糯米、秫米、黍米粥]**

Supplements qi; treats cold deficiency of the spleen and stomach, diarrhea and dysentery, vomiting and counterflow, and white-colored pox sores in children. (Li Shizhen)

---

**Polished Non-glutinous Rice, Indica Rice, Foxtail Millet, and Panicled Millet Congee [粳米、秈米、粟米、粱米粥]**

Promotes urination, stops vexing thirst, and nourishes the spleen and stomach. (Li Shizhen)

---

**[Elaboration / Discoveries]**

Li Shizhen states: According to Luo Tianyi's *Precious Mirror* [《寶鑒》]: congee made from polished non-glutinous rice and foxtail millet has thin qi and bland flavor — it is yin within yang. For this reason it percolates downward through bland seepage and is able to promote urination. Han Mao's *Comprehensive Medicine* [《醫通》] records: A man suffered from strangury [urinary blockage] and had never taken medicine. I instructed him to eat only foxtail millet congee, completely cutting out all other foods. After ten days or more his condition lessened; after a month or more he was cured. This illustrates the principle of the five grains treating disease.

Furthermore, Zhang Lei's *Notes on Congee* [《粥記》] states: Every morning upon rising, eat one large bowl of congee. With the stomach empty and the digestive organ vacuous, the grain's qi immediately takes effect, and the replenishment it provides is not slight. Moreover, congee is extremely soft and smooth, in harmony with the intestines and stomach, making it the finest of all foods and drink. The monk Miaoqi said: The mountain monks eat one serving of congee each morning before dawn — it is a matter of great consequence.
