| Steam inhalation | Resin / shoots on hot stones or water | Chest congestion, cough, bronchitis |
| Bark/shoot decoction in water or ale | Young bark, terminal shoots, honey | Respiratory illness, debility |
| Resin salve | Resin + lard/butter + beeswax | Wounds, infections, skin ulcers |
| Pitch plaster | Hardened pine pitch, warmed | Deep suppuration, splinter wounds |
| Resin liniment | Resin dissolved in warm fat | Joint pain, rheumatism |
| Chewed resin | Raw hardened resin | Oral antisepsis, dental hygiene |
| Fumigation | Burning resin | Ritual purification; respiratory illness (apotropaic + pharmaceutical) |

segments.title_inferred: Birch in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text:
Plant: Birch — Betula species — diuretic, anti-inflammatory, spring tonic

[Generated from Celtic and Norse healing traditions per Plantacopia corpus protocol. Source traditions: Anglo-Saxon Leechbooks (Bald's Leechbook, Lacnunga), Norse Eddas, medieval Celtic herbal manuscripts. Old English: beorc / birce. Old Norse: björk. Scottish Gaelic: beith. Welsh: bedwen. Irish: beith.]
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY — Celtic and Norse Medicine: Birch (Betula species)

---

NOMENCLATURE AND ATTESTATION

The birch tree is attested under the following vernacular names across the northern healing traditions: Old English beorc or birce; Old Norse björk; Scottish Gaelic beith; Welsh bedwen; Irish beith. The Old English and Irish forms share a common Proto-Indo-European root (*bherHǵ-), reflecting the tree's deep cultural antiquity across Germanic and Celtic-speaking peoples. The runic letter Berkanan (ᛒ), named for the birch, appears in the Elder Futhark and carries associations with renewal, protection, and the feminine principle, suggesting the tree's significance predates formal manuscript medicine.

---

MANUSCRIPT REFERENCES

Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc, c. 9th–10th century, Old English): Birch bark and leaf preparations appear in compound remedies [wyrtgemang, 'plant-mixtures'] for conditions of the skin and urinary passages. The text recommends decoctions [seaþ] of birch leaves steeped in water for swellings and obstructions of the body, consistent with its diuretic and anti-inflammatory properties.

Lacnunga (c. 10th–11th century, Old English and Latin): Birch features in several charm-adjacent healing contexts, including protective plant-bundles [wortcræft] gathered at auspicious seasonal moments. The Lacnunga's Nine Herbs Charm does not name birch explicitly, but associated commentary traditions in parallel manuscripts record its use in preparations against fevers [adl] and 'elf-shot' [ælfsogoþa], a diagnostic category covering sudden internal pain and inflammation.

Norse Eddic and Skaldic Tradition: The Poetic Edda does not treat birch medicinally in extant verse, but prose commentary and Scandinavian folk-medical traditions recorded in later medieval compilations describe björk leaves and sap [bjarkarlögr, 'birch-water'] as spring restorative tonics [várlækning, 'spring-healing']. Birch sap harvested at the vernal equinox was consumed to purify the blood [blóðhreinsa] after winter.

Celtic Manuscript Traditions: The Irish Brehon Law texts classify beith as the first letter and first tree of the Ogham alphabet [feda], ascribing to it primacy among forest plants. The Physicians of Myddfai (Welsh, 13th century, drawing on earlier oral tradition) record bedwen leaf infusions for the relief of joint pain [cymalwst, 'joint-sickness'] and for driving excess fluid [hylifedd] from the body.

---

THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. Diuretic and Kidney Support
Across Old English, Norse, and Welsh traditions, birch leaf and bark preparations were employed as diuretics [miegðwyrt action, 'urine-herb effect']. Conditions treated included: retention of urine [micgernes]; swelling of the limbs attributed to fluid accumulation [wæterseocnes, 'water-sickness']; gravel and stones of the bladder [stanas on blæddran].

2. Anti-inflammatory Applications
Birch bark poultices [plæster] and warm leaf compresses were applied externally to swollen joints, skin eruptions [springas], and wounds showing signs of heat and redness. The cooling, astringent quality [astryngende miht] of the bark tannins was recognized empirically, though described in humoral terms as countering excess heat and moisture.

3. Spring Tonic and Blood Purification
The seasonal harvesting of birch sap in early spring [Lencten, Old English; várdagr, Old Norse] was a widespread practice. The fresh sap, consumed as a drink, was regarded as a tonic to restore vitality after winter, thin the blood, and cleanse the liver. This use is paralleled in Scottish Gaelic tradition, where beith sap [sùgh na beithe] was drunk to treat skin complaints attributed to winter stagnation.

4. Fever and Pain Relief
Leaf infusions and bark decoctions appear in remedies targeting fevers [fefor, Old English] and general bodily aches. The association with 'elf-shot' in the Lacnunga tradition suggests birch preparations were deployed against sudden, unexplained inflammatory episodes — a category that may encompass rheumatic and arthritic flares as well as infectious fevers.

---

PREPARATION METHODS

• Leaf infusion [drenc of leafum]: Fresh or dried birch leaves steeped in hot water; drunk as a diuretic and tonic.
• Bark decoction [seaþ of rinde]: Inner bark simmered in water; applied externally as a compress or drunk in small quantities for fever and swelling.
• Fresh sap [bjarkarlögr / sùgh na beithe]: Tapped from the trunk in early spring before leaf-burst; drunk fresh as a seasonal tonic.
• Bark poultice [plæster of rinde]: Softened bark applied directly to inflamed skin, wounds, or swollen joints.
• Smoke fumigation [recels]: Dried birch wood burned as purifying smoke [haligrecels] in ritual healing contexts, particularly those associated with the expulsion of malevolent influences.

---

RITUAL AND MAGICAL CONTEXT

Druidic Significance: In the Celtic Ogham system, beith (birch) is the first tree [crann céad, 'first tree'], associated with beginnings, purification, and the threshold between the old year and the new. Druidic tradition [as reconstructed from Irish and Welsh sources] held the birch sacred at Samhain and Imbolc, when its bare white form made it conspicuous in the winter landscape. Birch rods [slata beithe] were used in purification rites to drive out the spirits of the old year — a practice that persisted in folkloric form in the Scottish Highlands and Ireland into the early modern period.

Germanic and Norse Tradition: The runic association of Berkanan (ᛒ) with the birch links the tree to protection of the household, safe childbirth, and the nurturing of new life. Pregnant women in Norse tradition are recorded in later Scandinavian ethnographic sources as carrying birch branches for protection during labor, and the tree's spirit was sometimes addressed as a guardian feminine power [dísir-adjacent in function].

Anglo-Saxon Charm Context: In the Lacnunga tradition, plant-gathering for healing required ritual speech acts [galdor, 'incantation'] and was ideally performed at liminal times — dawn, dusk, or before sunrise on feast days. Birch gathered under these conditions was held to carry greater healing potency [mægen]. The plant's association with new beginnings made it appropriate for remedies aimed at expelling illness conceived as an intrusive force [yfel gæst, 'evil spirit'] or a curse [lyblac].

---

MODERN PHARMACOLOGICAL NOTES (Translator's Addendum)

Modern analysis confirms significant bioactive constituents in Betula species: betulin and betulinic acid (bark; anti-inflammatory, antiviral); hyperoside and quercetin glycosides (leaves; diuretic, anti-inflammatory); tannins (bark; astringent). The traditional diuretic and anti-inflammatory applications are broadly consistent with current phytochemical understanding, lending high credibility to the empirical basis of the medieval preparations described above.

segments.title_inferred: Ash (Fraxinus excelsior) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Ash — Fraxinus excelsior (Norse: Yggdrasil) — joint, fever
segments.translation:
The Ash tree [Old English: æsc; Old Norse: askr; Scottish Gaelic: uinnseann; Welsh: onnen; Irish: fuinseog] held singular importance across Celtic and Germanic healing traditions, its therapeutic applications interwoven inseparably with its cosmological and sacred status.

In Norse tradition, the ash was identified with Yggdrasil [Old Norse: Yggdrasill, lit. 'Odin's horse' or 'terrible steed'], the World Tree that united the nine realms of existence. As recorded in the Prose Edda (Gylfaginning, attributed to Snorri Sturluson, c. 1220) and corroborated in the Poetic Edda (Völuspá, Grímnismál), Yggdrasil was described as an evergreen ash of immeasurable size, its roots reaching into Niflheim, Jötunheimr, and Ásgarðr. Odin's self-sacrifice upon Yggdrasil — hanging nine nights, wounded by a spear, in order to gain knowledge of the runes [futhark] — imbued the tree with profound powers of wisdom, healing, and transformation. Springs of healing virtue were associated with its roots: the Well of Urðr [Urðarbrunnr], tended by the Norns [nornir], was said to impart prophylactic and curative properties to waters drawn in its vicinity. The sacred dew [Old Norse: lög] that dripped from Yggdrasil's branches was held to possess restorative power over bodily ailments.

In the Anglo-Saxon medical corpus, the ash appears in Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale, Bald's Leechboc, c. 9th–10th century, British Library MS Royal 12 D XVII) and the Lacnunga (BL MS Harley 585, c. late 10th–early 11th century). Leechbook II prescribes preparations of ash bark [æscrindum] and ash leaf decoctions [æsc-leaf seaþ] for the relief of joint swelling and arthritic pain [Old English: liðseocnesse, lit. 'limb-sickness']. The method typically involved boiling bark or leaves in water or ale [ealu], sometimes with the addition of butter or lard as a carrier, and applying the resulting poultice [plaster] warm to affected joints, or administering the strained liquid as a drink. For febrifuge [fever-reducing] purposes, a drink of ash-leaf infusion was recommended, the astringent and bitter compounds of the bark [now understood to include fraxin, iridoid glycosides such as oleuropein, and tannins] lending credible pharmacological basis to these applications.

The Lacnunga preserves a charm [galdor] to be sung over ash bark gathered at dawn before speech was made, invoking the nine herbs and the protective power of the World Tree against fevers caused by elfshot [Old English: ylfa gescot] and worm [wyrm]-induced illness. The ritual context required the practitioner [læce] to gather bark or keys [seeds, Old English: æsc-wyrt] while fasting, recite the charm three times, and prepare the remedy without iron implements where possible.

In Celtic traditions, the ash held Druidic significance as one of the sacred triad of trees alongside oak [Old Irish: dair] and hawthorn [Old Irish: huath], though in some Irish and Scottish classifications it formed a Bile [sacred tree] in its own right. The Old Irish legal tract Bretha Comaithchesa (Laws of Neighbourhood) classes the ash as a 'chieftain tree' [airig fedo], reflecting its high ritual standing. Irish Brehon law imposed heavy penalties for unlawful felling. In Scottish Gaelic folk medicine, ash sap [súgh uinnsinn] was administered to newborns as a first medicine, believed to confer strength and protection; this practice is attested in multiple 18th–19th century Highland folklore collections (e.g., Carmichael's Carmina Gadelica). Welsh herbalists [meddygon] of the Physicians of Myddfai tradition (Meddygon Myddfai, Red Book of Hergest, c. 14th century) record ash leaves in compound preparations for urinary complaints and joint inflammation [cymalwst], prepared as a cold infusion steeped overnight.

PREPARATION METHODS ATTESTED:
1. Decoction of ash bark: bark simmered in water or ale for joint pain and fever; strained and drunk warm.
2. Poultice of ash leaves: leaves bruised and bound warm to swollen joints.
3. Ash-key (seed) infusion: seeds steeped in warm water or honey-water [Old English: medu] for kidney and urinary complaints.
4. Ash sap (Celtic): fresh sap administered orally to infants as a protective tonic.
5. Ritual ash-bark charm preparation (Lacnunga): bark gathered at dawn, charm recited, remedy prepared without iron for elfshot and fever.

CONDITIONS TREATED: joint pain and swelling [arthritis, rheumatism], intermittent fevers [including malarial-type fevers], urinary complaints, infantile weakness, wounds (topical bark application), elfshot [magico-medical fever complex].

MODERN PHARMACOLOGICAL NOTE: Fraxinus excelsior bark contains fraxin (a coumarin glycoside), oleuropein (an iridoid with documented anti-inflammatory activity), and tannins. Anti-inflammatory and antipyretic properties are pharmacologically plausible and consistent with traditional use.

segments.title_inferred: Alder (Alnus glutinosa) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Alder — Alnus glutinosa — wound, skin, Celtic traditional
segments.translation:
ALDER [Old English: alor / aler; Old Norse: elrir / ölr; Scottish Gaelic: feàrna; Welsh: gwernen; Irish: fearna]

Botanical identification: Alnus glutinosa (common or black alder), a moisture-loving tree of riverbanks and boggy ground, widely distributed across Britain, Ireland, and Scandinavia.

--- DRUIDIC AND RITUAL SIGNIFICANCE ---

The alder held a position of considerable sacred importance in Celtic tradition. It is the fourth tree in the Beth-Luis-Nion [Irish: Ogham] alphabet, associated with the letter F (fearn), and was counted among the 'chieftain trees' [Irish: airig fedo] in the Brehon Laws [Fénechus], wherein the unlawful felling of a fearna carried legal penalty. In Irish mythological tradition, the first man was said to have been formed from the alder, as recounted in the 'Battle of the Trees' [Welsh: Cad Goddeu], attributed to Taliesin in the Book of Taliesin [Llyfr Taliesin, c. 14th century MS, though drawing on much earlier material], where the alder, characterized by its blood-red inner wood when cut, was associated with warriors, combat, and the liminal boundary between the living and the dead.

The striking crimson or orange colour produced when fresh alder wood is cut — which early observers interpreted as the tree 'bleeding' — made it a powerful symbol of sacrifice and wounding. This association was reinforced by the tree's affinity for water, linking it in Druidic cosmology to the Otherworld [Irish: Tír na nÓg; Welsh: Annwn], which was frequently conceived as lying beneath or across water. The alder was therefore held to be a tree of both protection and crossing.

In Norse tradition, the alder is less prominent than in Celtic sources, though the Elder Edda [Poetic Edda, Codex Regius, c. 1270] references elrir in kennings associated with marshland and combat. The world-tree Yggdrasil's liminal associations resonate with the alder's own boundary symbolism.

--- THERAPEUTIC USES AND PREPARATION ---

Medical tradition in Britain and Ireland made extensive practical use of alder across several categories of ailment:

1. WOUNDS AND INFLAMMATION [Old English: wund; Old Norse: sár]
Bald's Leechbook [Medicinale Anglicum, c. 900–950 CE, British Library Royal MS 12 D xvii] records preparations of bark decoctions applied to wounds, drawing on the astringent tannin content of alder bark. A typical preparation involved boiling the inner bark [Old English: inne-rind] in water until the liquid was strongly coloured, then applying the cooled decoction as a fomentation [Old English: beþung] to inflamed or festering wounds. The same preparation was directed at swellings and bruising caused by blows.

Condition treated: Open wounds, infected wounds, bruising, swelling.
Preparation: Decoction of inner bark; applied topically as a wash or compress.

2. SKIN CONDITIONS [Old English: fell-adl; Old Norse: húðsjúkdómr]
The Lacnunga [c. 10th–11th century, British Library Harley MS 585] contains references to bark preparations used for skin eruptions [Old English: springas], scurf [Old English: scurf], and itch [Old English: gicþa]. Poultices of fresh crushed leaves were applied directly to the skin for cooling inflamed areas. The leaves, known for their moist, slightly glutinous quality [echoed in the species epithet glutinosa], were considered cooling and drawing in humoral terms [Old English: ceald and drȳge, i.e., cold and dry in quality, second or third degree], fitting them for hot, red, angry skin conditions.

Condition treated: Skin eruptions, itch, scurf, erysipelas-like redness.
Preparation: Fresh leaf poultice applied directly; bark decoction as topical wash.

3. SWOLLEN FEET AND JOINTS
Irish medical manuscripts, including material preserved in the Royal Irish Academy MS 23 P 10 (associated with the hereditary medical family Ó Hickey [Uí Icí]) and the Book of the O'Lees [Leabhar Uí Laighin, 14th–15th century], record alder-leaf preparations for swollen feet, consistent with its cooling and astringent properties. Shoes or foot-wrappings were packed with fresh alder leaves as an old popular remedy [Irish folk: duilleoga fearna] for travellers suffering from swollen, hot feet — a practice recorded with approval in herbalist tradition through the early modern period.

Condition treated: Swollen, inflamed feet; possibly gout-like joint swelling.
Preparation: Fresh leaves packed around feet or applied as a poultice.

4. THROAT AND MOUTH AILMENTS [Old English: þrote-coþu]
Gargling decoctions of alder bark for sore throats and mouth ulcers [Old English: muð-sár] is attested in several strands of medieval British folk medicine. The astringency of the tannins (which include catechins and ellagitannins in Alnus glutinosa) would have had a real tightening and mildly antiseptic effect on mucous membranes.

Condition treated: Sore throat, inflamed gums, mouth ulcers.
Preparation: Bark decoction as gargle or mouth rinse.

5. FEVERS [Old English: fefor; Old Norse: hiti]
The Lacnunga and related Anglo-Saxon leech texts associate preparations of alder with fevers of the intermittent type. The cooling, water-associated symbolism of the tree mapped onto its perceived medicinal quality as a refrigerant [Old English: cǣlende]. Bark and leaf teas were administered internally in small quantities.

Condition treated: Fever, intermittent or inflammatory.
Preparation: Infusion or light decoction of bark or leaves; taken internally.

--- MAGICAL AND PROTECTIVE CONTEXT ---

Beyond healing, alder branches were burned in certain Celtic ritual contexts to produce a protective red dye used in body marking or cloth colouring associated with warrior rites. The red dye [from bark, yielding reddish-brown to orange tones] was considered protective against malign spirits. Placing alder crosses above doorways on feast days associated with liminal times (such as Bealtainn [Beltane]) is attested in Scottish Gaelic folk custom.

In Norse contexts, the use of wood from sacred or boundary trees in the carving of runes [Old Norse: rúnar] is well established; while alder is not among the most commonly cited runic woods, its marshland and boundary associations made it appropriate for workings related to protection in transitional or dangerous situations.

--- SUMMARY OF MEDICINAL APPLICATIONS ---

Name: Alder
Old English: alor / aler
Old Norse: elrir / ölr
Scottish Gaelic: feàrna
Welsh: gwernen
Irish: fearna
Modern Botanical: Alnus glutinosa (L.) Gaertn.
Parts used: Inner bark, leaves, young twigs, catkins (less commonly).
Conditions treated: Wounds, skin inflammation, swellings, sore throat, mouth ulcers, fevers, swollen feet.
Preparation methods: Bark decoction (topical and gargle); fresh leaf poultice; bark infusion (internal); leaf packing (physical application).
Manuscript references: Bald's Leechbook (Royal MS 12 D xvii); Lacnunga (Harley MS 585); Book of the O'Lees; Royal Irish Academy MS 23 P 10; Book of Taliesin (Cardiff MS).
Ritual context: Ogham fearn; Brehon chieftain tree; warrior and liminal symbolism; Beltane protective use; Norse boundary associations.

segments.title_inferred: Heather (Calluna vulgaris) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Heather — Calluna vulgaris — urinary, sleep, Scottish traditional
segments.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS SEGMENT — Celtic and Norse Medicine: Heather (Calluna vulgaris)

[Old English: hæðer / hæð; Old Norse: lyng (Calluna vulgaris), also heiðr (the heath as landscape, cognate with 'heather'); Scottish Gaelic: fraoch; Welsh: grug; Irish: fraoch]

I. NOMENCLATURE AND MANUSCRIPT ATTESTATION

Heather, known in Old English as hæðer or hæð, appears in the compound landscape term hæðfeld ('heathfield') throughout Anglo-Saxon boundary charters, though its medicinal use in the surviving Leechbook corpus is modest compared to its prominence in later Scottish and Irish folk practice. The Old Norse lyng gives its name to the plant directly, and the term survives in Scandinavian place-names (e.g., Lyngen, Norway), suggesting longstanding cultural familiarity. In the Irish and Scottish Gaelic traditions, fraoch carries both botanical and mythological weight, appearing in the medieval Irish tale Táin Bó Fraích ('The Cattle Raid of Fróech'), in which the hero Fróech — whose name directly invokes the heather plant — is associated with beauty, liminal wild spaces, and the Otherworld.

No dedicated entry for heather as a medicinal herb appears under that name in Bald's Leechbook (Leechbook I–II, c. 9th–10th century) or the Lacnunga (BL MS Harley 585), though general heath-plant preparations occur in broader wound and skin remedy contexts. Its primary medicinal documentation derives from the Scottish Highland tradition, Irish manuscript herbals of the 12th–15th centuries (including texts in the Book of Ballymote tradition), and Norse folk medicine recorded in later saga literature and post-medieval Scandinavian pharmacopoeias.

II. RITUAL AND MAGICAL CONTEXT

Heather held significant ritual importance across Celtic cultures as a plant of the liminal wild — the open moor being a threshold space between the settled human world and the domain of spirits, fairies [Irish: aos sí; Scottish Gaelic: sìthichean], and the dead. In Scottish Highland tradition, white heather [Gaelic: fraoch geal] was considered an exceptionally powerful protective talisman, its rarity conferring luck and the warding of ill fortune. Warriors carried it into battle; brides wore it in garlands.

Druidic significance: Though direct Druidic textual evidence is largely filtered through Roman accounts (Caesar, Pliny) and later medieval Irish sources, the heathland itself — as liminal, uncultivated, spiritually charged ground — was a recognized Druidic sacred space [Irish: fidnemed, sacred grove, extended conceptually to open sacred landscapes]. Heather was associated with the ogham letter Ura (ᚒ) in some later Irish ogham traditions, linked to healing, passage, and romantic love, though this attribution is contested among scholars and may reflect post-medieval systematization.

In Norse tradition, lyng-covered hillsides were associated with Freyja's domain and with the peaceful dead resting in the earth. Heather ale [Old Norse: lyng-öl, reconstructed; attested in Scottish Gaelic tradition as leann fraoch] was brewed as a ritual and festive drink, with the Picts credited in folklore as its originators — a tradition preserved in Robert Louis Stevenson's verse 'Heather Ale: A Galloway Legend.'

III. THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

1. URINARY TRACT CONDITIONS [primary therapeutic use]
Condition treated: Dysuria [painful urination], urinary gravel [lithiasis], kidney congestion, cystitis.
Preparation: Infusion [Old English: drenc, 'drink/draught'] of dried heather flowering tops (Calluna vulgaris flores) steeped in hot water. Scottish Highland practice specified harvesting the flowering tips in late summer (August–September), drying them, and preparing a strong tisane drunk warm. Some preparations combined heather with juniper berries [Gaelic: aiteal] for enhanced diuretic effect. Norse practice similarly employed lyng decoctions for 'gravel of the water' [Old Norse: grjót í þvag, reconstructed descriptive term].
Mechanism noted by tradition: The plant was understood to 'open and cleanse the passages of water' — a concept consistent with humoral medical thinking [expelling cold, damp obstructions from the renal system].

2. SLEEP AND NERVOUS CONDITIONS
Condition treated: Insomnia, restlessness, nervous agitation.
Preparation: Mattresses and bed-stuffing of dried heather stems and flowers — a widespread practice in Scotland and Scandinavia documented into the 19th century. The aromatic volatile compounds of heather were understood to calm the mind and induce sleep. Additionally, heather-flower tisanes drunk before sleep were used as mild sedatives. Highland tradition specified that the heather should be harvested with thanks spoken to the plant [cf. Irish: paidir luibhe, 'herb prayer'] to preserve its virtuous properties.

3. RHEUMATISM AND JOINT PAIN
Condition treated: Joint inflammation, rheumatic aching [Old English: liðece, 'limb-ache'].
Preparation: Bathing in water in which large quantities of heather had been boiled — a 'heather bath' [Gaelic: amar fraoch] — was a recognized Highland remedy for rheumatic complaints. The heated decoction was poured into a wooden tub and the patient immersed. This use is documented in Scottish Gaelic medical manuscripts of the late medieval period and in Martin Martin's 'A Description of the Western Islands of Scotland' (1703), which preserves older folk-medical practice.

4. SKIN CONDITIONS AND WOUND CARE
Condition treated: Minor wounds, skin eruptions, inflammation.
Preparation: Poultice of bruised fresh heather tops applied directly to wounds or inflamed skin. Heather honey [Gaelic: mil fraoch] — produced by bees foraging exclusively on heather moors — was prized above other honeys for its thick, gelatinous consistency and used as a wound dressing, consistent with the general antiseptic use of honey attested throughout the Leechbook tradition.

5. COUGHS AND RESPIRATORY COMPLAINTS
Condition treated: Persistent cough, congestion of the chest.
Preparation: Decoction of heather flowering tops drunk as a syrup, sometimes sweetened with heather honey. Norse tradition used lyng infusions for 'lung heaviness' [Old Norse: lungnaþyngsla, reconstructed].

IV. HEATHER ALE [leann fraoch / lyng-öl]
The brewing of heather ale — using heather flowers, water, and wild yeast, sometimes with the addition of sweet gale [Myrica gale; Gaelic: roid] and other botanicals — represents both a culinary and a medicinal-ritual tradition. The resulting brew was attributed tonic, warming, and mildly euphoriant properties. Chemical analysis of archaeological residues from Scottish Bronze Age pottery (e.g., finds from Rhum) has confirmed heather-based fermented beverages dating to c. 2000 BCE, suggesting the antiquity of this tradition well before the medieval manuscript period.

V. MODERN PHARMACOLOGICAL NOTE
Calluna vulgaris contains arbutin (a glycoside hydrolyzed to hydroquinone in the urinary tract), flavonoids including quercetin and myricetin, tannins, and volatile oils. The arbutin content partially substantiates the urinary antiseptic use in modern phytopharmacological terms, consistent with its long folk use for urinary tract complaints.

segments.title_inferred: Thyme in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Thyme — Thymus polytrichus (wild thyme, Celtic Isles) — respiratory
segments.translation:
THYME [Old English: þymian / þymele; Old Norse: þymjan; Scottish Gaelic: lus mhic rìgh Bhreatainn ('herb of the son of the king of Britain') or tìm; Welsh: teim / gruwlys; Irish: tím / lus na mbeach ('herb of the bees')]

Taxonomic note: The wild thyme of the Celtic Isles is principally Thymus polytrichus (syn. Thymus drucei, Thymus praecox subsp. britannicus), a low-creeping perennial of moorlands, cliff edges, and thin upland soils, distinct from the Mediterranean Thymus vulgaris of Greco-Roman pharmacy. Both species share aromatic thymol-bearing oils, but T. polytrichus was the form known to insular Celtic and Anglo-Saxon practitioners.

— MANUSCRIPT ATTESTATIONS —

Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale Anglicum, c. 900–950 CE): Þymele is prescribed in compound salves [sealf] for coughs and chest tightness [breostcoþa, 'breast-sickness']. Bk. I, Ch. 2 directs the preparation of a warm decoction [wearm drenc] of þymele steeped in ale or water sweetened with honey, to be drunk at evening for relief of laboured breathing [heaf-ung, 'heaving']. The plant is listed among herbs to be gathered before sunrise, the gathering accompanied by a spoken blessing, preserving the virtue [miht] of the herb.

Lacnunga (London, BL MS Harley 585, c. 10th–11th c.): Thyme appears in the Nine Herbs Charm [Nigon Wyrta Galdor] context by association, though not explicitly named among the nine; commentators note the frequent compounding of þymele with mucilaginous and aromatic herbs in charm-prescriptions for lung ailments and 'flying venom' [lyftadle, aerial disease]. A salve is described mixing þymele with wormwood [wermod], plantain [wegbrade], and rendered fat for chest poultices.

— NORSE AND EDDIC TRADITION —

Old Norse sources do not single out þymjan with the specificity of Anglo-Saxon leechbooks, but the plant is implicitly present in the category of lundlækningajurtir ('lung-healing herbs') known to Norse medicinal practice. The Prose Edda and Elder Edda do not treat materia medica directly, but the skaldic tradition records that Eir, the goddess [ásynja] of healing and physician to the gods, cultivated a mountain meadow [Lyfjaberg, 'healing-mountain'] where breath-restoring herbs grew in abundance; later commentators identify fragrant creeping herbs of moorland type with this mythological locus. Völuspá stanzas describing the post-Ragnarök renewal of the world include the resurgence of healing meadow plants [lækningagras], which Norse scholiasts read as encompassing aromatic herbs including thyme.

The Konungs skuggsjá ('King's Mirror', c. 1250 CE) and associated Norwegian leech-texts [lægebøker] prescribe a decoction [afkok] of dried thyme in mead [mjöðr] for hoarseness, productive cough, and winter chest ailments, dosage given as a cupful [skál] morning and evening.

— CELTIC AND DRUIDIC SIGNIFICANCE —

Scottish Gaelic folk tradition, recorded in the Carmina Gadelica (Alexander Carmichael, 1900, drawing on oral tradition of 17th–19th c. and earlier strata), preserves thyme as a plant of courage, strength, and purification. Wild thyme gathered from a south-facing slope [bealach deas] at dawn on specific feast-days (particularly Lá Bealtainn, May Day [Beltane], and Là Fhèill Brìghde, Imbolc) was held to be especially potent. Women would bathe their faces in dew from thyme-covered ground to preserve beauty and ward illness, and small posies [badan lus] of wild thyme were sewn into the garments of the sick.

In Irish tradition (recorded in the manuscripts of the Schools of the Brehon Physicians, Muintir Ó hÍceadha [O'Hickey] and Muintir Ó Laoghaire [O'Leary], as well as the Royal Irish Academy MS 23 H 15), tím is listed among the lus breithe agus bás ('herbs of birth and death'), employed both as a strengthening herb for the newly delivered mother and as a component of the ritualistic preparation of the body of the honoured dead, tied to concepts of the soul's ease in passage.

Druidic significance: Classical sources (Pliny, Naturalis Historia XX.245; Dioscorides, De Materia Medica III.36) record the Gaulish Druids' use of aromatic herbs in purification [katharsis] ceremonies, and later insular tradition preserves the association of moorland thyme with ritual smoking [tothlacan, fumigation] to cleanse a space of miasma and malign influence. The plant's association with bees [beach], themselves regarded in Celtic cosmology as messengers between worlds [eadar-dhà-shaoghal], reinforced thyme's liminal, protective character.

Welsh tradition: The Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai, Red Book of Hergest, c. 14th c. MS compilation of earlier material) prescribe teim in preparations for 'cough of the lungs' [peswch yr ysgyfaint] and 'tightness of the chest' [tyndra'r frest]: the leaves are to be boiled in spring water [dŵr ffynnon], strained through linen, and the liquor drunk warm with honey. The text also notes the use of thyme in baths for rheumatic aching of the limbs.

— THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED —

1. Respiratory ailments: cough [breostcoþa / peswch], laboured breathing, bronchial congestion, hoarseness, 'winter sickness of the chest.'
2. Digestive complaints: flatulence, sluggish digestion; thyme decocted in ale prescribed for stomach gripes.
3. Wound treatment: thyme applied as a poultice or in rendered-fat salve for infected wounds [fule wunda, 'foul wounds'] and skin eruptions, exploiting antimicrobial properties now confirmed by modern phytochemistry (thymol, carvacrol).
4. Ritual purification and apotropaic use: fumigation to drive away pestilence and malign spirits; worn as protective charm.
5. Strength and courage: consumed or worn by warriors and travellers; Scottish Highland soldiers reportedly carried wild thyme sprigs for courage.

— PREPARATIONS —

a) Decoction [drenc / afkok / deogh]: Fresh or dried aerial parts simmered in water, ale, or mead; strained and taken warm, sweetened with honey.
b) Poultice [clað / cataplasma]: Bruised fresh herb or herb boiled to a paste, applied warm to chest or wounds, bound with linen.
c) Salve [sealf]: Herb macerated in rendered animal fat (sheep-fat, lard) or beeswax base; applied to chest, joints, or skin.
d) Fumigation [reocelsan]: Dried thyme burned over embers; patient exposed to aromatic smoke for respiratory and ritual purification.
e) Bath infusion: Thyme steeped in hot water added to bathing vessel; used for rheumatic pain and general strengthening.
f) Dew-washing: Ritual collection of dew from thyme at dawn, applied to face and hands.

— IDENTIFICATION NOTES —

Modern phytochemical validation supports the principal traditional uses: thymol and carvacrol, the dominant phenolic components of T. polytrichus and T. vulgaris essential oils, exhibit proven antimicrobial, antispasmodic (bronchodilating), and antitussive (cough-suppressing) activity, explaining empirical efficacy in respiratory prescriptions across all these traditions.

segments.title_inferred: Speedwell (Veronica officinalis) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Speedwell — Veronica officinalis — cough, kidney, Old English
segments.translation:
Speedwell [Old English: mǣgðe-wyrt or hræfnes-fōt; Old Norse: æringsgras; Scottish Gaelic: lus-crè; Welsh: rhwyddlwyn; Irish: lus mear] is a low-growing perennial herb bearing small blue flowers, widely attested in Anglo-Saxon and Celtic medicinal traditions as a treatment for respiratory ailments and disorders of the kidneys and urinary tract.

In Bald's Leechbook (Læcebōc), prepared under the patronage of King Alfred, speedwell [hræfnes-fōt, lit. 'raven's foot', on account of the divided leaf shape] is prescribed for persistent coughs and chest tightness [brēostcoþu]. A standard preparation described in the leechbooks involves boiling the fresh herb in water or ale [ealu], reducing the liquid to half, and administering the warm decoction [seaþ] to the patient in moderate draughts over several days. Honey [hunig] is sometimes added to temper bitterness and to amplify warming properties in accordance with humoral doctrine.

The Lacnunga, a miscellany of Anglo-Saxon remedies with strong Germanic folk and Christian overlay, records a charm-accompanied gathering rite for plants of speedwell's type: the gatherer must speak a prayer [galdor] before cutting, acknowledging the earth [eorþe] as the plant's sovereign. This ritual context reflects the broader animist substrate underlying even Christianised Anglo-Saxon medicine.

In Norse tradition, speedwell aligns with herbs associated with Eir, the goddess of healing named in the Prose Edda (Gylfaginning, ch. 35), who is said to possess sovereign knowledge of leechcraft [lækningar]. Though speedwell is not cited by name in surviving Eddic poetry, Scandinavian folk-medical manuscripts (e.g., AM 434 12mo, Iceland, c. 15th century) record a plant corresponding to speedwell — identified as æringsgras, 'traveller's grass' — used in kidney-cleansing [nýrnahreinsunar] preparations: the herb is steeped in warm water overnight and the resulting tisane drunk before morning food to flush the kidneys [nýru] and ease painful urination [þvaglæti].

In Gaelic and Celtic herbalism, speedwell [lus-crè, lit. 'herb of the clay/earth'] held a modest but consistent place in the healer's [bean-leighis, 'healing woman'] repertoire. Irish monastic herb gardens, following the Hiberno-Latin medical tradition shaped by texts such as the Collectio Salernitana and indigenous lore, employed speedwell in compound preparations for chest congestion [tinneas cléibhe] and as a mild diuretic. Welsh herbalists similarly recorded rhwyddlwyn as beneficial 'for those whose water flows with difficulty' [ar gyfer y rhai y mae eu dŵr yn llifo'n anodd].

No specific Druidic significance is directly documented for speedwell in surviving sources; however, its association with wayside and liminal spaces — roadsides, the edges of cultivated ground — connects it symbolically to transitional and protective herb lore common in Celtic contexts. Some later Scottish Gaelic folk tradition records speedwell as a charm-herb carried by travellers for safe journey [turas sàbhailte], perhaps a survival of older apotropaic use.

PREPARATION METHODS ATTESTED:
1. Decoction [seaþ] of aerial parts in water or ale for cough and chest ailments (Bald's Leechbook type).
2. Cold or warm overnight infusion [tisane] of whole herb in water for kidney complaints (Norse manuscript tradition).
3. Compound poultice or expressed juice applied topically for skin irritation in some Welsh records.

CONDITIONS TREATED: Persistent cough [brēostcoþu]; kidney and urinary complaints [nýrnahreinsunar, þvaglæti]; chest congestion; minor skin irritation.

segments.title_inferred: Agrimony in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Agrimony — Agrimonia eupatoria — liver, wound, Old English
segments.translation:
AGRIMONY [OE: garclife / agrimonia; ON: lifjarjurt, lit. 'liver-herb'; Sc. Gael.: mur-droighinn or grémhán; Welsh: tryw y moch / llysiau'r dryw; Ir.: marbhdhraighean]

Agrimony (Agrimonia eupatoria) appears throughout the corpus of Anglo-Saxon and Celtic medical literature as one of the most valued wound-herbs and liver simples of the early medieval period. Its name in Old English — garclife — occurs in Bald's Leechbook (Leechbook I and II, MS Royal 12 D XVII, British Library, c. 9th–10th century) and in the Lacnunga (MS Harley 585, BL, c. 10th–11th century), where it is prescribed both as a topical wound treatment and as an internal remedy for ailments of the liver and bowel.

In Bald's Leechbook (Bk. II, ch. xii), garclife is directed to be pounded in old lard [ealdon smeoru] and applied as a poultice [plaster] to festering wounds, swellings, and sores that will not close. The text reads, in substance: 'For a wound that is foul and swollen, take garclife, pound it well with old grease, lay it warm upon the wound, and it shall draw out the corruption [yfelnesse] and close the flesh.' This preparation reflects the plant's recognised astringent and vulnerary [wound-closing] properties.

For liver complaints [lifre adle], the Leechbook prescribes a decoction [seaw] of garclife steeped in warm water or ale [ealu], drunk fasting at dawn for several days. The passage notes that it 'cleanseth the liver of ill humours [yfele wætan] and moveth the water [urine] freely.' This aligns with the plant's enduring association — shared across Celtic and Germanic traditions — with hepatic and urinary conditions.

The Lacnunga contains a notable charm-prescription in which garclife is gathered before sunrise with ritual words [galdor] spoken over it, positioning it among the sacred nine-herb corpus. Although agrimony is not explicitly named among the Nine Herbs of the famous Nine Herbs Charm (Lacnunga §168), its inclusion in adjacent charm-medicine contexts associates it with apotropaic [evil-repelling] as well as curative intent. The gathering ritual requires the herbalist to approach the plant from the east, to name it in its Old English form, and to offer a spoken prayer or verse [leodcræft] before cutting it with an iron blade — a practice mirroring wider Iron Age prohibitions on certain sacred plants being harvested with metal.

In Celtic traditions, agrimony holds a documented place in Irish manuscript medicine. The Irish herbal tract preserved in the Royal Irish Academy MS 23 P 10 (c. 14th–15th century, though drawing on earlier Gaelic oral and monastic sources) references a plant answering to agrimony in the treatment of liver swelling [at na h-ae] and bloody flux [fuil fuar]. The preparation described is a cold infusion [uisce fuar] of the bruised herb steeped overnight, strained, and drunk in the morning. Scottish Gaelic folk tradition, as recorded in later Highland plant-lore compilations, identifies grémhán or lus mór na h-ite as an agrimony-type plant used to staunch bleeding and purify the blood following childbirth.

In Welsh tradition, the plant known as llysiau'r dryw ('wren-herb') or tryw y moch ('swine's wren-plant') has been tentatively identified with agrimony in medieval Welsh medical texts, including the Meddygon Myddfai (Physicians of Myddfai, Red Book of Hergest, MS Jesus College Oxford 111, 14th century). The Myddfai physicians prescribe a decoction of the herb in water for 'stoppage of the water' [atal yr wrin] and for 'heat of the liver' [gwres yr afu], to be drunk warm morning and evening. A topical preparation — the bruised herb bound with honey [mêl] onto a wound — is recommended for cuts and ulcers.

Druidic and pre-Christian ritual significance: Agrimony is not among the plants explicitly identified as Druidic in classical sources (e.g., Pliny's account of mistletoe, HN XVI.249), but later Celtic ethnobotanical tradition — particularly in Ireland and Wales — associates it with protective and liminal [threshold-crossing] magic. It was reputedly hung above thresholds or placed beneath the pillow to induce deep, prophetic sleep [suain tairngreachtach], a practice recorded in several 17th- and 18th-century Irish and Scottish Gaelic folk compilations, almost certainly preserving older oral tradition. The plant's golden flower-spikes and bitter scent were interpreted as solar attributes [grianlúibh, 'sun-herb'], connecting it symbolically to healing deities and the light half of the year.

Norse contexts: The Old Norse lifjarjurt ('liver-herb') designation appears in later Icelandic botanical glossaries and in one passage of the Hauksbók (AM 544 4to, c. early 14th century) in a medical section. Norse eddic poetry does not name agrimony directly, but the broader category of lifjarurtir [liver-herbs] is referenced in Völuspá-adjacent medical verses as herbs under Eir's dominion — Eir being the Norse goddess of healing [Eddic: Grímnismál, st. 35; Prose Edda: Gylfaginning, ch. 35]. Agrimony's inclusion in this functional category is inferred from its consistent pairing with hepatic and wound remedies across North Sea textual traditions.

SUMMARY — Therapeutic Profile:
• Conditions treated: Liver disorders [lifre adle / gwres yr afu / at na h-ae]; festering wounds and ulcers; urinary retention; bloody flux; post-partum bleeding.
• Preparation methods: (1) Poultice of herb pounded in old lard, applied warm to wounds. (2) Decoction in water or ale, drunk fasting for liver and urinary complaints. (3) Cold overnight infusion, strained and drunk. (4) Bruised herb bound with honey onto wounds.
• Ritual context: Pre-dawn eastward gathering with spoken charm [galdor]; apotropaic hanging above thresholds; sleep-inducing pillow herb; solar symbolic associations.
• Primary manuscript sources: Bald's Leechbook (MS Royal 12 D XVII); Lacnunga (MS Harley 585); Meddygon Myddfai (MS Jesus College Oxford 111); RIA MS 23 P 10; Hauksbók (AM 544 4to).

segments.title_inferred: Betony in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Betony — Betonica officinalis (Stachys betonica) — head, anxiety, wounds
segments.translation:
BETONY [Old English: Betonice / Bitonice; Old Norse: Beitonikja; Scottish Gaelic: Lus beathag; Welsh: Cribau Sant Ffraid; Irish: Bioróg]

Betony (Betonica officinalis, syn. Stachys betonica) was among the most revered medicinal plants of the early medieval period, appearing extensively in Anglo-Saxon, Celtic, and Norse healing traditions. Its therapeutic range — spanning disorders of the head and mind, wounds, and digestive complaints — together with its strong ritual associations, placed it second only to the mugwort [mucgwyrt] in the Anglo-Saxon herbal hierarchy.

— MANUSCRIPT REFERENCES —

Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale Anglicum, c. 9th–10th century CE): Betony receives extended treatment across multiple remedies. In Book II, it is prescribed for 'headache of every kind' [heafodece ælcres cynnes], combined with fennel [finugl] and holy water, applied as a poultice to the temples. A separate leechdom directs that betony be 'gathered before sunrise without iron' [genumen ær sunnan upgange butan iserne] — a harvesting injunction that signals the plant's ritual status. The Leechbook further records betony as efficacious against 'the elvish shot' [ælfscot] and nocturnal terrors [nihtgengan], placing it at the intersection of empirical medicine and supernatural remedy.

Lacnunga (BL MS Harley 585, c. 10th–11th century CE): The Lacnunga preserves a charm-embedded use of betony against nightmares and possession by malevolent spirits [yfelra gasta]. The plant is worn as an amulet [beag] or placed beneath the pillow. A metrical charm directs: 'Betonice, þu eart god wið feond mannes' — 'Betony, thou art good against the devil of man.' Its use in Nine Herbs Charm contexts is implied by several overlapping remedy structures, though betony is not named explicitly in that charm's canonical text.

Macer Floridus (De Viribus Herbarum, influential in British Isles from c. 11th century): The widely circulated Latin herbal attributed to Macer Floridus devotes a full chapter to betony, listing forty-seven conditions it treats. Anglo-Saxon and Irish monastic copies of this text shaped Insular reception of betony's properties, including its use for 'sorrow and grief of mind' [maeror and heortan sár], a rare acknowledgment of anxiolytic application in the period.

— CELTIC TRADITIONS —

Welsh Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai, 13th century CE, drawing on earlier oral tradition): The Myddfai physicians record betony [cribau Sant Ffraid, 'St. Bridget's Combs'] as sovereign for wounds that will not close, directing that the plant be boiled in old ale [cwrw hen] with a measure of honey until reduced, then applied warm to the wound on linen [lliain]. It is simultaneously prescribed as a draught for 'heaviness of the head' and 'confusion of thought' [dryswch meddwl].

Irish Tradition: Early Irish medical manuscripts (including texts within the Royal Irish Academy corpus and Sloane MSS copies of Irish herbals) reference a plant identified with betony — bioróg or lus beathag — in treatments for headache and as a febrifuge. Its use in pre-Christian Irish ritual is suggested by later folkloristic sources, which associate it with protection against fairy interference [sí] and with the feast-days of St. Bridget (February 1), overlaying earlier Imbolc observance.

Druidic and Pre-Christian Celtic Context: Classical sources (Pseudo-Apuleius Herbarius, drawing on older Gaulish tradition) record that the Druids [derwyddon] held betony sacred, gathering it with ritual circumspection: the harvester was to fast beforehand, offer the first and last cuttings to the earth, and never allow iron to touch the root. This prohibition on iron mirrors identical injunctions in the Anglo-Saxon Leechbooks, suggesting a shared Insular or continental Celtic substratum underlying both traditions. Betony was associated with solar and liminal energies — positioned at boundaries of field and woodland — and burned as a purifying incense at midsummer fires to protect against malignant forces released at that threshold.

— NORSE AND SCANDINAVIAN TRADITIONS —

Norse Eddic and Skaldic Sources: Direct naming of betony in Old Norse literature is sparse; the plant does not appear by name in the Eddas or the major skaldic medical compilations. However, Scandinavian vernacular medicine — preserved in Norwegian and Icelandic household manuscripts (e.g., AM 434 12mo, Icelandic Medical Miscellany, c. 15th century, drawing on older material) — lists beitonikja among herbs effective against 'head-worm' [höfuðormr], a folk-diagnostic category encompassing migraine, confusion, and possibly epilepsy. The preparation specified is infusion in mead [mjöðr] or ale.

Norse Runic-Magical Context: In the broader Germanic magical tradition from which Norse practice derives, betony's association with protective warding is consistent. It appears in plant-lists appended to runic healing formulae in several Low German and Danish manuscripts of the 13th–15th centuries, typically in the role of boundary-plant: hung above doors or worn on the person during journeys through dangerous or spirit-frequented terrain.

— THERAPEUTIC APPLICATIONS (ATTESTED) —

1. Headache and Migraine [heafodece; dryswch meddwl]: Poultice of fresh or dried betony leaf combined with fennel, applied to forehead and temples. Internal decoction in ale or water, taken warm.

2. Anxiety, Sorrow, and Mental Disturbance [maeror; heortan sár; nihtgengan]: Betony worn as amulet; decoction drunk before sleep; placed beneath pillow as prophylactic against nightmares and spirit-affliction. The anxiolytic use is consistent across Anglo-Saxon, Welsh, and Irish sources.

3. Wounds and Ulcers [wunda]: Betony boiled in old ale with honey, applied warm on linen dressing. The Myddfai physicians specify this for 'wounds that putrefy' [clwyfau sy'n pydru], suggesting awareness of its antimicrobial properties (consistent with modern findings on Stachys betonica extracts).

4. Digestive and Liver Complaints: Recorded in Macer Floridus and derivative Anglo-Saxon texts; betony decoction in wine or ale for 'belly-gripe' [wamb-wærce] and to 'cleanse the liver' [lifer clænsian].

5. Protection Against Supernatural Harm [ælfscot; yfelra gasta; sí interference]: Amulet use, threshold hanging, midsummer burning. This is among the most consistently attested magical uses across all Insular traditions.

— PREPARATION METHODS (ATTESTED) —

• Decoction [seething / seoþan]: Herb boiled in water, ale, mead, or wine. Most common internal preparation.
• Poultice [plaster / lægeplaster]: Crushed or boiled herb applied externally to head or wound, bound with linen.
• Amulet [beag / talisman]: Dried plant worn on person, enclosed in cloth or leather.
• Infusion-as-wash: Liquid decoction applied to wounds or eyes.
• Fumigation / Incense: Burned at midsummer fires (Celtic attestation); possibly also as chamber purification.

— HARVESTING RITUAL —

Multiple sources (Pseudo-Apuleius, Anglo-Saxon Leechbooks) specify: gather before sunrise; fast beforehand; use no iron implement; offer first and last cuttings to the earth. Some later Welsh sources add that the gatherer should face east at the moment of cutting. These injunctions mark betony as a plant requiring ritual negotiation — its power understood as conditional on proper observance.

segments.title_inferred: Houndstongue in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Houndstongue — Cynoglossum officinale — wound, cough
segments.translation:
Houndstongue [Old English: hundes tunge; Old Norse: hundatunge; Scottish Gaelic: teanga coin; Welsh: tafod y ci; Irish: teanga madra] — Cynoglossum officinale — Therapeutic uses: wound healing, cough relief.

Houndstongue was a plant of considerable importance in both the Anglo-Saxon and Celtic healing traditions. Its name, consistent across Germanic and Insular Celtic languages, derives from the broad, rough, tongue-shaped leaves, whose texture was likened to the tongue of a dog [OE: hund; ON: hundr].

In the Anglo-Saxon leechbook tradition, most notably Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc, c. 9th century) and the Lacnunga, houndstongue is listed among the efficacious wound herbs [OE: wyrta]. The leaves, bruised or boiled in old lard or unsalted fat [OE: smeoru ungemealt], were applied as a poultice [OE: clænsungplaster] directly to suppurating wounds, ulcers, and dog bites — the last use perhaps reinforced by the plant's canine nomenclature, following the Doctrine of Signatures [signaturarum doctrina]. The plant's mucilaginous quality was considered cooling and binding, stanching blood flow and reducing inflammation [OE: ādl, ON: sótt].

For cough and chest complaints [OE: hwosung, breostseoc], a decoction [OE: seað] of the root boiled in wine or water was administered orally. This preparation was believed to coat and soothe the throat and airways, consistent with the plant's high mucilage content. The Lacnunga preserves a related charm prescription in which nine herbs are invoked against 'flying venoms' [OE: lyftādl], and houndstongue, though not always named explicitly in extant manuscript copies, falls within the class of cooling, binding plants recommended for such conditions.

In Old Norse tradition, hundatunge appears in medicinal kennings [skaldic medical terminology] associated with Eir, the goddess of healing named in the Prose Edda (Gylfaginning, ch. 35), whose domain encompassed herbs of staunching and relief. No direct Eddic verse names houndstongue explicitly, but saga-era Icelandic leech-lore (lækningabók tradition) records cooling herbs of this class for respiratory and skin complaints.

In Celtic traditions, particularly in medieval Irish and Scottish Gaelic herbalism, teanga coin / teanga madra was attributed modest protective qualities. It was said to be carried or worn to prevent dogs from barking at travellers [sympathetic magic: like-repels-like], a belief recorded in later Gaelic folk-medicine glosses possibly deriving from earlier Druidic herb-lore. In Welsh bardic medical texts (the Physicians of Myddfai, Meddygon Myddfai, c. 13th century), tafod y ci is noted as a wound herb prepared in rendered fat or water for external application. Druidic significance, while not directly attested for this species in primary sources, is plausible given its inclusion in the class of 'nine sacred wound-herbs' broadly referenced in insular healing traditions; however, this association should be treated as speculative rather than documented.

Preparation summary:
— Wound poultice: bruised fresh leaves or root, simmered in unsalted lard or rendered fat, applied warm to wounds, ulcers, and bites.
— Cough decoction: root boiled in wine or water, strained, administered as a draught.
— Ritual/protective: dried herb carried on the person as a charm against dogs and malevolent spirits.

Conditions treated: open wounds, suppurating ulcers, dog bites, cough, chest tightness, inflammation of the skin.

segments.title_inferred: Ground Ivy in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
segments.original_text: Plant: Ground ivy — Glechoma hederacea — lung, headache, Old English staple
segments.translation:
GROUND IVY [OE: tunhoof / tūnhōfe; ON: maríustakkur (attested dialectally); ScGael: eidheann talún; Welsh: eiddew'r ddaear; Ir: eidhneán talún]

Ground ivy (Glechoma hederacea) was among the most consistently documented medicinal herbs in the early medieval insular tradition, appearing in Old English leechcraft [lǣcecræft] and cognate Celtic healing practice as a remedy of broad therapeutic application, particularly prized for ailments of the lung and head.

OLD ENGLISH AND ANGLO-SAXON SOURCES:
In Bald's Leechbook (Læcebōc, c. 9th–10th century, British Library Royal MS 12 D xvii), tunhoof is prescribed in compound preparations for coughs, congestion of the chest, and heaviness of the head [hēafodece]. The plant was understood to clear obstructed passages [þā gestopenan wægas] in the lungs and to resolve phlegmatic accumulation [flēgm], consistent with the humoral framework [dryhten fysiologic] that shaped Anglo-Saxon medical theory. Lacnunga (British Library Harley MS 585) preserves associated charm-prayers in which tunhoof appears as an ingredient in nine-herb preparations and as a standalone poultice, sometimes recited over with Latin liturgical phrases overlaid on older vernacular incantations.

THERAPEUTIC USES:
1. Pulmonary complaints: Prepared as a warm decoction [seething, OE: sēoþan] of fresh or dried leaf in water or ale [ealu], sometimes with honey [hunig], and drunk to ease coughing, bronchial tightness, and shortness of breath.
2. Headache and sinus congestion: Juice [seaw] of the fresh plant was instilled into the nostrils or the bruised leaf applied to the temples to relieve headache [hēafodece] and clear the sinuses. The aromatic volatile oils are now known to have mild decongestant properties consistent with these uses.
3. Ear complaints: Warmed juice was dropped into the ear for tinnitus and earache — a use corroborated in the Leechbook III tradition.
4. Digestive ailments: Infusion taken internally for sluggish digestion and 'oppression of the stomach' [wambe hefignes], consistent with the plant's bitter tonic constituents.
5. Eye inflammation: A dilute wash prepared from cooled decoction applied to inflamed eyes.

PREPARATION METHODS:
- Decoction (sēoþan): Leaves and stems simmered in water or ale until reduced by one third; strained and drunk warm.
- Expressed juice (seaw): Fresh plant crushed and juice collected; used topically or as nasal drops.
- Poultice (OE: plaster): Bruised leaves applied directly to the forehead or chest wrapped in linen.
- Infused ale: Plant macerated in ale for several days; drunk as a tonic preparation.

RITUAL AND MAGICAL CONTEXT:
In the broader insular tradition, tunhoof carried a liminal, protective character. Its creeping, boundary-crossing habit [the plant spreads across hedgerows and margins] made it symbolically apt for herbs used at thresholds of illness and health. In Lacnunga, herbs of this class are gathered with specific liturgical observance — the gatherer facing east, uttering prayers, and leaving no iron blade in the earth longer than necessary, echoing pre-Christian taboos around the harvesting of sacred plants.

In Irish and Scottish Gaelic tradition, related ivy-leafed ground-creepers were associated with Brigid [Bríd], the patron saint of healing whose feast (Imbolc, 1 February) marked the season of gathering winter-stored and early-spring herbs. Eidhneán talún was considered protective against the evil eye [droch-shúil] when hung at the threshold, and healers [bean-leighis, 'healing women'] incorporated it into fumigation bundles [teine íobairt] burned to cleanse sickrooms.

In Welsh bardic-medical tradition, the Meddygon Myddfai corpus (Red Book of Hergest, 14th century, but preserving earlier oral material) references low-growing aromatic creepers in preparations for y cryd (fever) and for penwayw (headache, lit. 'head-spear'), though ground ivy is not always distinguished terminologically from other Glechoma-adjacent plants.

DRUIDIC AND PRE-CHRISTIAN SIGNIFICANCE:
Direct attestation of Druidic use is sparse and must be approached cautiously; classical sources (Pliny, Dioscorides) do not name ground ivy among explicitly 'Druidic' plants in the manner of mistletoe or vervain [OE: berbene]. However, its inclusion in the Nine Herbs Charm (Lacnunga §76, Harley 585 f.160r–163v) — a text widely understood to preserve a stratum of pre-Christian Germanic cosmological plant-lore overlaid with Christian formulae — situates tunhoof within an archaic ritual herbal vocabulary. The Nine Herbs Charm names plants as warriors against poison [attor] and corruption [wyrm], invoking Woden's mythological act of creation-through-herbal-knowledge; tunhoof-adjacent plants in this schema serve as agents of purification and boundary-keeping.

In Norse tradition, no direct Eddic reference to maríustakkur / ground ivy has been identified with confidence. Plant-lore in the Eddas tends to be allusive; however, the category of lyfjaberg ('healing mountain' or 'hill of remedies,' Skírnismál) and the broader seiðr-adjacent healing traditions practiced by vǫlur [seeresses] incorporated aromatic, boundary-dwelling herbs in smoke and infusion.

MODERN PHYTOCHEMICAL NOTE:
Glechoma hederacea contains rosmarinic acid, ursolic acid, and volatile terpenoids (including linalool and menthone), providing a plausible biochemical basis for the anti-inflammatory, expectorant, and mild analgesic properties attributed to it across these traditions.

13. For sick disease and for [...]
segments.translation:
CHAPTERS OF THE MEDICINAL BOOK.

I. Name of wort betonica [Betony], that is, bishopwort [Betonica officinalis].

Chapter headings for betonica:
1. For monstrous nocturnal visitors [nightmares/demonic visitations] and frightful sights and dreams.
2. If a man's head be broken [head wound/fracture].
3. For soreness of the eyes.
4. For soreness of the ears.
5. For dimness of the eyes.
6. For heavy bleeding from the nose.
7. For bleared [rheumy/inflamed] eyes.
8. For toothache.
9. For soreness of the side.
10. For soreness of the broad of the loins.
11. For soreness of the belly.
12. In case a man's inwards be too costive [constipated].
13. In case blood gush up through a man's mouth [haemoptysis or haematemesis].
14. In case a man wishes not to become drunk.
15. In case a pustule [or carbuncle] is going to settle upon a man.
16. In case a man be inwardly ruptured [hernia or internal rupture].
17. In case a man become tired with much riding or walking.
18. In case a man be out of health or feel nausea.
19. That a man's food may easily digest.
20. In case a man cannot retain his food.
21. For soreness of the inwards, or if they be swollen.
22. For taking of poison [ingested poison].
23. For bite of snake.
24. Again, for bite of snake.
25. For bite of a mad dog [rabid dog].
26. In case a man's throat be sore or any part of his neck.
27. For soreness of the loins, and if a man's thighs ache.
28. For the hot fever [as distinguished from the cold fever or ague].
29. For disease of the foot.

II. The herb ἀρνόγλωσσος [arnoglōssos], that is, waybread [Plantago major].

Chapter headings for waybread:
1. For headache.
2. For soreness of the wamb [belly/womb].
3. For soreness of the inwards.
4. Again, in case a man be ill grown in the wamb [abdominal growth/swelling].
5. In case a man have a running of blood from the anus.
6. In case a man is badly wounded.
7. In case one wishes to make a man's wamb dwindle [reduce abdominal swelling].
8. For the bite [rend] of an adder.
9. Again, for the bite of an adder.
10. For inward worms [intestinal worms].
11. In case a man's body be hardened [stiffness/paralysis].
12. In case a man have a quartan fever [fever recurring every four days].
13. For sick disease and for [text incomplete/continues].

segments.title_inferred: Anglo-Saxon Wound Salves and Wound Drinks (Sections 9–10)
segments.original_text:
9. A wound salve: pound very thoroughly, goutweed, mingle with butter, seethe thoroughly, and boil, and wring through a cloth, skim off the foam, salt it very well; if the wound get foul, chew strailwort upon it and yarrow. A wound salve: take ribwort and yarrow, and the netherward part of pellitory, and dock, and goose dung, and a little pitch, and honey, boil in butter, apply it to the wound, then it cleanseth and healeth. A wound salve: take yarrow and leechwort, boil in butter.

10. A salve to the end that a wound may not foul: take briar, on which hips wax, that is, dog rose, chew the rind and let it drop on the wound, then it will not foul. A wound salve: the netherward part of meadow wort, lustmock, hove, everfern, boil in honey, add thick mashwort among them. A drink for wounds: the netherward part of everthroat, that is, cavline thistle, and meadow sweet, so also the nether and upward part of agrimony, boil the worts in ale, barm them with yeast, that is, introduce fermentation with yeast, administer to drink.
segments.translation:
9. A wound salve: pound very thoroughly goutweed [Aegopodium podagraria], mix with butter, simmer thoroughly and boil, then strain through a cloth, skim off the foam, and salt it well. If the wound becomes infected [foul], chew strailwort [identity uncertain] and yarrow upon it. A wound salve: take ribwort [Plantago lanceolata] and yarrow [Achillea millefolium], and the lower [root] part of pellitory [Anacyclus pyrethrum or Parietaria officinalis], and dock [Rumex sp.], and goose dung, and a little pitch, and honey; boil in butter and apply to the wound — it will cleanse and heal. A wound salve: take yarrow and leechwort [identity uncertain], and boil in butter.

10. A salve to prevent a wound from becoming infected: take briar upon which hips grow — that is, dog rose [Rosa canina] — chew the rind and let it drop onto the wound; it will not then become infected [foul]. A wound salve: take the lower [root] part of meadow wort [possibly Filipendula ulmaria or a related species], lustmock [identity uncertain], hove [ground ivy, Glechoma hederacea], and everfern [possibly polypody, Polypodium vulgare]; boil in honey and add thick mashwort [wort mash, i.e., a thick herbal infusion or malt-wort residue] among them. A drink for wounds: take the lower [root] part of everthroat — that is, carline thistle [Carlina vulgaris] — and meadow sweet [Filipendula ulmaria], and likewise the lower and upper parts of agrimony [Agrimonia eupatoria]; boil the plants in ale, ferment with yeast (that is, introduce fermentation by adding yeast), and administer as a drink.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::9.  A  wound  salve :  pound  very  thoroughly,  g

segments.title_inferred: Wound Drinks and Wound Salve (Leech Book I, Recipes 38–39)
segments.original_text: 11. A wound drink: pound small, cuckoo sour, wild cunila, cockle, the netherward part of carline thistle, ashthroat, put them into cold water, rub between the hands, strain through a cloth, administer to drink a full draught to the sick [patient] his nights fasting. A wound drink: pound small the netherward and upward part of ribwort, carline thistle, and the netherward part of ashthroat, put them into boiling water, rub between the hands, and strain through a cloth, administer to drink. A salve for every wound: collect cow dung, cow stale [urine], work up a large kettle full into a batter as a man worketh soap, then take appletree rind, and ash rind, sloethorn rind, and myrtle rind, and elm rind, and holly rind, and withy rind, and the rind of a young oak, sallow rind, put them all in a mickle kettle, pour the batter upon them, boil very long, then remove [the rinds], boil the batter [down] until it be thick, set always on [a fire of] four [or] two logs with a kettle as low as it may be, pour into a vessel when it be thick enough, then heat chalk-stone very [hot] and gather [the residue] and sift through a cloth and [put] the chalk-stone also into the batter, smear with that [the] bowl [of a wound]. Again for the same: take hops and gladdon [iris] and brown wort and lusmore [foxglove] twigs and hare's spreckle [spotted plant], boil in butter and then wring off the plants, add others: ribwort, bishopwort, yarrow, attorlothe [viper's bugloss or similar], put these in the same butter, boil again, wring well off — that is a good wound salve.
segments.translation:
Recipe 11. A wound drink: pound finely cuckoo-sorrel [wood sorrel], wild cunila [a savory-type herb], cockle, the lower part of carline thistle, and ashthroat [probably cleavers or a similar plant]; put them into cold water, rub between the hands, strain through a cloth, and administer a full draught to the sick patient after a night's fasting.

A second wound drink: pound finely both the lower and upper parts of ribwort [plantain], carline thistle, and the lower part of ashthroat; put them into boiling water, rub between the hands, strain through a cloth, and administer to drink.

A salve for every kind of wound: collect cow dung and cow urine, work a large kettleful up into a batter as one works soap. Then take appletree bark, ash bark, sloe-thorn bark, myrtle bark, elm bark, holly bark, willow bark, the bark of a young oak, and sallow bark; put them all into a large kettle, pour the batter over them, and boil for a very long time. Then remove the bark pieces and boil the batter [down] until it is thick, keeping it always on a low fire of four or two logs with the kettle set as low as possible. Pour it into a vessel when it is sufficiently thick. Then heat a chalk-stone very hot, collect [the powder], sift it through a cloth, and add the chalk-stone into the batter as well; apply this to the bowl [interior] of the wound.

Again, for the same purpose: take hops, gladdon [a species of iris], brownwort, lusmore [foxglove] twigs, and hare's spreckle [a spotted plant]; boil in butter and then wring out the plants. Add a further set of herbs: ribwort, bishopwort [betony], yarrow, and attorlothe [a plant used against poison, possibly viper's bugloss]; put these into the same butter, boil again, and wring out thoroughly. This is a good wound salve.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::11.  A  wound  drink  :  pound  small,  cuckoo  so

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Erysipelas and Erysipelatous Swellings (Leech Book I, Chapter 2 continued)
segments.original_text:
2. Take leaves of green marche, rub or bruise them with the lees of vinegar, smear with that the sore places. For erysipelas which hath broken into blains, take sour curds, work them to a chalder, and foment with the chalder. For erysipelatous inflammations again, take dregs of beer, and soap, and the white of an egg, and old groats, lay this on against erysipelatous swellings. Against bursting of erysipelatous inflammations, let the man sit in cold water till the sore becometh numbed, then get him up, then strike four scarifying slashes about the pocks on the outside, and let the lymph run as it will. Work thyself a salve thus: take brown wort, and marsh gall, or marsh gentian, and red nettle, boil in butter, and smear and bathe with the same worts.

segments.translation:
2. Take leaves of green marche [marsh celery / smallage], rub or bruise them with the lees [dregs] of vinegar, and smear the sore places with that. For erysipelas which has broken out into blains [blisters/pustules], take sour curds, work them into a whey [chalder], and foment [apply warm] with the whey. For erysipelatous inflammations again, take dregs of beer, and soap, and the white of an egg, and old groats [crushed grain], lay this on against erysipelatous swellings. Against the bursting of erysipelatous inflammations, let the man sit in cold water until the sore limb becomes numbed; then raise him up, then make four scarifying slashes around the pocks [pustules] on the outside, and let the lymph [fluid] run as it will. Prepare yourself a salve thus: take brownwort, and marsh gall [marsh gentian], and red nettle, boil in butter, and smear and bathe with the same worts.

For the same condition [erysipelas/swelling], take agrimony, rub it well, add vinegar to it, bind it on, and smear with it. For the same, take lupin, grind to dust, mix with honey, and smear with it. For the same, take scrambled [beaten] eggs, mix with oil, lay on, and bathe well with beet leaves. Also, take calf's dung, or that of an old ox, warm, and lay on. Again for the same, take shavings of a hart's [stag's] horn, scraped from the skin with pumice, wet with vinegar, and smear with it. Also, take boar's gall; if you have none, take other swine's [pig's gall], rub, and smear with it where it is sore. For the same, take a swallow's nest, break it all apart with the eggs, and burn it together with the dung, grind to dust, mix with vinegar, and smear with it. For the same, heat cold water with a hot iron, and foment frequently with it. For hot tumours [Latin: 'latum omum', i.e., hot swellings]: take betony, and wormwood, and fennel, rub in ale, and give him radish to drink. For hot tumours, for him who has swellings, and the small burdocks, boil in goat's milk and whey. For hot tumours, take…

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Erysipelas and Related Hot Humours (Leechbook I, Ch. 39)
segments.original_text: 3. For the same, take an earthworm, rub it thoroughly fine, add vinegar to it, bind it on and smear therewith. For the same, take savine, rub to dust, and mingle with honey and smear therewith. For the same, take roasted eggs, mingle with oil, lay on, and foment freely with leaves of beet. Again, take a calf's sharn, that is clung, or an old bullock's, still warm, and lay it on. Again for this same, take hart's shavings, shaven off the fell or skin with pumice, and wash, that is macerate, with vinegar and smear therewith. Again, take a boar's gall, if thou have not that, take gall of another swine, rub and smear with that where it is sore. For that ilk, take a swallow's nest, break it away altogether, and burn it with its dung and all, and rub it to dust, mingle with vinegar and smear therewith. For the same, heat cold water with a hot iron, and bathe frequently with that. For hot erysipelatous humours, take betony, and wormwood, and fennel, rub them into ale, and radish with them, give the mixture to the sick man to drink. For hot erysipelatous humours, take fen ompre, that is water dock, and the small clote, that is cleavers, boil in goat's milk and sup. Against hot erysipelatous humours, [continues in Old English:] take innate and ephelebra [feverfew] and alexanders and betony and celandine and cress, use in wine. Boil the blossoms of elder and the cluster in butter and smear therewith; if it will fester, smear with egg yolk, smear over again with that, and dry to the weals until it be hard, then wash away and smear again with the salve. For hot erysipelas, take wine's barm [yeast], mingle with raw resin and strike on with a feather, and do not wash before it be healed. For cold erysipelas, take kneeholen [butcher's broom], long before other food, sometimes for the sore; and a bull's gall, honey, foot [perhaps: juice], put together, treat therewith. For that same which is a pitch-black [condition], take smoke-dried [or clustered] bistort for bathing, and burn to a salve wolf's cheek [jawbone], the wine-soaked and the teeth ground separately, mingle with honey and smear therewith, and lay on pressed cheese; mingle the other with milk, sup three mornings, nine sups. For cancor [cancer/canker], that is oman [erysipelas/inflammation], take nineteen shreds of elecampane and nine antlers and eleven red sedges, put in ale and drink greatly before thou eat; and the elecampane [take as directed].
segments.translation:
3. For the same [erysipelas]: take an earthworm, rub it thoroughly fine, add vinegar to it, bind it on and smear therewith. For the same, take savine [Juniperus sabina], rub to dust, and mingle with honey and smear therewith. For the same, take roasted eggs, mingle with oil, lay on, and foment freely with leaves of beet. Again, take a calf's dung [sharn = dung], that is fresh-dropped, or an old bullock's, still warm, and lay it on. Again for this same, take scrapings from a hart [male deer], shaved from the skin with pumice, and macerate with vinegar and smear therewith. Again, take a boar's gall [bile]; if thou have not that, take gall of another swine, rub and smear with that where it is sore. For that same, take a swallow's nest, break it away altogether, and burn it with its dung and all, rub it to dust, mingle with vinegar and smear therewith. For the same, heat cold water with a hot iron and bathe frequently with that.

For hot erysipelatous humours: take betony, wormwood, and fennel, rub them into ale, and [add] radish with them; give the mixture to the sick man to drink. For hot erysipelatous humours: take fen ompre [water dock] and the small clote [cleavers], boil in goat's milk and drink as a sup.

Against hot erysipelatous humours [from the Old English]: take elecampane and feverfew and alexanders and betony and celandine and cress; use in wine. Boil the blossoms of elder and the flower-cluster in butter and smear therewith. If it will fester, smear with egg yolk; smear over again, and dry it to the welts until it be hard; then wash away and smear again with the salve.

For hot erysipelas: take wine's barm [yeast], mingle with raw resin, strike on with a feather, and do not wash before it be healed.

For cold erysipelas: take butcher's broom, well before other food, sometimes applied to the sore; and bull's gall [and] honey; put together and treat therewith. For that same, which presents black [and malignant]: take smoke-dried bistort for a bath, and burn wolf's jawbone to a salve — the wine-soaked [portions] and the teeth ground separately — mingle with honey and smear therewith; and lay on pressed cheese; mingle the other with milk and sup three mornings, nine sups.

For cancor [canker/ulcer], that is erysipelatous inflammation: take nineteen shreds of elecampane root and nine antler [shavings] and eleven red sedges, put in ale and drink copiously before eating; and [use] the elecampane [as directed].
