segments.title_inferred: Drinks and leechdoms against poison, adder-bite, and snake-bite
segments.original_text: 1. Drinks or potions and leechdoms against poison. Pound in ale betony, marche, wormwood, fennel, radish; administer this to drink. Against poison; put in holy water betony and the small atterlothe, drink the water and eat the worts. Against any poison; eat ere the danger cometh radish and clote; no man may then do thee a mischief with poison. Against any poison; boil the netherward part of bishopwort and lupin, and the netherward part of springwort, everthroat, and clote in ale; give to drink frequently. If an adder strike a man, or for whatever of that which cometh of shots, wash the black snail in holy water, give to the sick to drink. Again, rub waybroad thoroughly fine, drink it in wine. For bite of snake, put so much of betony as may weigh three pennies into three bowls full of wine, give it the man to drink.
segments.translation: 1. Drinks [potions] and leechdoms [medicinal remedies] against poison. Pound betony, marche [smallage/wild celery], wormwood, fennel, and radish in ale; administer this as a drink. Against poison: place betony and the small atterlothe [a plant identified as lesser burdock or possibly black hellebore] in holy water, drink the water and eat the plants. Against any poison: eat radish and clote [burdock] before the danger arrives; no man may then harm you with poison. Against any poison: boil the lower part of bishopwort [bishop's wort/wood betony] and lupin, and the lower part of springwort [dwarf spurge or caper spurge], everthroat [possibly vervain or a related plant], and clote [burdock] in ale; give to drink frequently. If an adder bites a man, or for whatever ailment arising from [elf-]shots, wash the black snail in holy water and give to the sick person to drink. Again, grind waybroad [plantain, Plantago major] thoroughly fine and drink it in wine. For the bite of a snake: put as much betony as weighs three pennies into three bowls full of wine and give it to the man to drink.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Swallowed Poison and Snake Bite, with Latin Charm and Charm against Flying Venom
segments.original_text:
3. In case a man swallow poison, take then horehound, work up a mickle deal of it, and adderwort, pound them together and wring the juice, pour thereon three measures of wine and give this to the poisoned man to drink. For hurt from snake; take waybroad, and agrimony, and adderwort, administer them rubbed up in wine to be drunk; and work up a salve of the same worts, and then take agrimony, form a ring around the incision on the outside so it overflows not further, and bind the worts again over the wound. For adder's venom, put of thine ear-wax [lit. 'that which thou cheerest'] and smear with it around, and sing thrice the prayer of St. John and this charm.

[Latin charm:] From the beginning: Father, and Son, and Holy Spirit. The Assumption of St. John, to whom all things are subject. To whom all creation renders service and all powers are subject, and the snake and the dragon flee, and the viper and the toad which is called 'rana quieta' stops, and the scorpion shrinks, and the phalangius [spider] and the asp shrink, and the basilisk is tamed, and the cave-dweller [spelaeus] works no harm, and all venomous and yet more dangerous creeping things and noxious animals flee before thee, and all adversaries to human health are driven away. Thou, Lord, extinguish this venomous poison, extinguish its deadly workings, and empty the power which it has within itself, and give before thy sight to all whom thou hast created eyes that they may see, ears that they may hear, a heart that they may understand thy greatness. And when he had said this, he armoured himself wholly with the sign of the cross and drank all that was in the cup. By the sign of the Holy Cross, and by thee, Christ Jesus, and God the most high Father, one Saviour of the world, in the unity of the Holy Spirit, through all ages of ages, Amen.

[Old English charm against flying venom and every contagious eruption:] On a Friday, take butter that has been melted from the milk of one cow or hind, and let it not be mixed with water; sing over it nine times the Litany, and nine times the Paternoster, and nine times this charm: Acre, aercrae, siernem, nabre, aercuna hel, siernem, nithsern, aer, afan, buirme, adcrice, siernem, meodre, siernem, gegenu, siernem, allu, honor, ucus, idar, adcert, cunolaju, raticamo, helse, icar xrita, haele, tobaerc, teia, rueh, cui, rodaer, plana, mil — and dab on.
segments.translation:
3. In case a man swallow poison, take then horehound, work up a great amount of it, and adderwort [bistort], pound them together and wring out the juice, pour thereon three measures of wine and give this to the poisoned man to drink. For hurt from a snake: take plantain [waybroad], and agrimony, and adderwort, administer them rubbed up in wine to be drunk; and work up a salve of the same plants, and then take agrimony, form a ring around the incision on the outside so that it does not overflow further, and bind the plants again over the wound. For adder's venom, apply ear-wax and smear it around the wound, and sing thrice the prayer of St. John and this charm.

[Latin charm (prayer in the tradition of the legend of St. John the Apostle):] In the name of the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit. The Assumption of St. John the Apostle, to whom all things are subject, to whom all creation renders service, and all powers are subject, and the snake and the dragon flee, and the viper and the toad which is called 'rana quieta' [the quiet frog] is stilled, and the scorpion shrinks back, and the phalangius [a venomous spider] and the asp draw back, and the basilisk is tamed, and the cave-spider [spelaeus] works no harm, and all venomous and still more dangerous creeping things and noxious animals flee before thee, and all adversaries of human health are driven away. Thou, Lord, extinguish this venomous poison, extinguish its deadly workings, and drain away the powers it holds within itself, and grant before thy sight to all whom thou hast created: eyes that they may see, ears that they may hear, and a heart that they may understand thy greatness. And when he had said this, he armoured himself entirely with the sign of the cross and drank all that was in the chalice. By the sign of the Holy Cross, and by thee, Christ Jesus, and God the most high Father, one Saviour of the world, in the unity of the Holy Spirit, through all ages of ages, Amen.

[Old English charm against flying venom and every contagious pustule:] On a Friday, take butter that has been melted from the milk of one cow or hind, and let it not be mixed with water; sing over it nine times the Litany, and nine times the Lord's Prayer, and nine times this charm [a sequence of magico-ritual words of uncertain meaning, possibly corrupt Latin, Celtic, or glossolalic origin]: Acre, aercrae, siernem, nabre, aercuna hel, siernem, nithsern, aer, afan, buirme, adcrice, siernem, meodre, siernem, gegenu, siernem, allu, honor, ucus, idar, adcert, cunolaju, raticamo, helse, icar xrita, haele, tobaerc, teia, rueh, cui, rodaer, plana, mil — and then apply.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies against accidental ingestion of a creeping thing and against being 'withheld' or 'restrained' by worts
segments.original_text: 6. If one drink a creeping thing in water, let him cut into a sheep instantly, let him drink the sheep's blood hot. If a man be 'restrained' with worts, give him springwort for him to eat, and let him sup up holy water. In case that a man be 'withheld'; if he hath on him Scottish wax, and the small atterlothe; or let him drink it in boiled ale, he may not be 'restrained' by worts.
segments.translation: 6. If someone accidentally swallows a creeping creature [insect, worm, or similar small animal] in water, let him immediately slaughter a sheep and drink the sheep's blood while it is still hot. If a man is 'restrained' [bewitched or physically bound/obstructed] by herbs [worts], give him springwort to eat, and let him drink holy water. In the case that a man is 'withheld' [similarly afflicted or magically bound]: if he carries upon him Scottish wax and the small atterlothe [a plant], or alternatively let him drink it in boiled ale — he cannot then be 'restrained' by herbs [worts].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies and Drinks for Fever and Worms, with Charms and Plasters
segments.original_text:
2. A drink; administer in ale cinquefoil, that is five leaved grass, or 'potentilla, to drink for thirty nights. A drink for that; rub down into ale or into wine seed of radish and of colewort, let the man drink that long and frequently against ons worm, till that his case be bettered. A plaster for the same: pound to dust a red tile or brick, mingle with groats, bake a cake, lay it on the wound; work another plaster if need be.

Brenc wiþ þeorable · sund cincrian ymb help sing þriwa pater noster · brad up þonne þu cweþe set · libera nos a malo · genim þære wið fifsiðba þ seofon pipor corna gecnua togadere · þ þonne þu þ wyrce sing xii. siþum þone psalm · miserere mei deus · þ gloria In excelsis deo · þ pater noster · ofgeoc þonne mid wine þonne dæg þ niht sceadde · drince þonne þone drenc þ bebreoþ þe weorþme. Genim þonne hind hiolopan ane eþgeot mid wetere drince oþre morgen scenc fulne þonne oþre siðe seofon siðeba þ nijon piporcorn · þriddan siðe nijon sniðeba þ xi. piporcorn. Drince wiþþan swiþne drenc þe wille up þrinan þ of bine · þet þonne blod under ancleof.
segments.translation:
Remedies for fever [þeorablum]: [the following herbs are compounded]: [various named plants — see entities], all the herbs bruised well together; take equal quantities of all, pour over with clear ale; let the drink stand overnight in a vessel where a man wishes to drink it. Let him drink in the morning a cup full of this drink. At midday let him stand facing east and commend himself devoutly to God, and let him walk around [sunwise] after drinking; and let him stand for some while before he rests. Let him receive as much [food] as he takes away from that; let him drink this drink for nine nights and eat such food as he will.

A drink for fever: go around [the patient] singing the Paternoster three times; raise [the cup] when you say 'set · libera nos a malo' [deliver us from evil]; take five seeds [of the herb] and seven grains of pepper, pound together; when you work this, sing twelve times the psalm 'Miserere mei Deus' [Have mercy upon me, O God] and 'Gloria in Excelsis Deo' and 'Pater Noster'; then pour over with wine at the division of day and night [i.e., at twilight]; then let him drink the drink and warm himself. Then take one [portion of] hind-heolope [black hellebore or a related plant], cook with water; let him drink the next morning a cup full; then a second time seven [seeds] and nine grains of pepper; a third time nine [seeds] and eleven grains of pepper. Drink afterwards a strong drink [of his choosing] to draw [the evil] upward and out; then let blood below the ankle.

[Also, from the earlier passage:]
Administer in ale cinquefoil — that is, five-leaved grass, or potentilla — to drink for thirty nights. A drink for that [ailment]: rub down into ale or into wine seed of radish and of colewort; let the man drink that frequently and for a long time against intestinal worm [ons worm], until his case is bettered. A plaster for the same: pound to dust a red tile or brick, mingle with groats [crushed grain], bake a cake [of it], lay it on the wound; work another plaster if need be.

segments.title_inferred: Leechdoms for 'Dry' Diseases: Compound Herbal Drinks with Ritual Observances
segments.original_text:
1. Leechdoms for 'dry' diseases; ash rind, aspen rind, elm rind, quickbeam rind, the netherward part of the mickle highway nettle, wormwood, hindheal, that is, water agrimony, empurple all the rinds on the outside, and pound them thoroughly, boil them together, apply equal quantities of all, souse them with clear ale, then let the drink stand for the space of a night in a vessel, before a man shall choose to drink it. Let him in the morning drink a cup full of this drink; in the middle of the morning hours, let him stand towards the east, let him address himself to God earnestly, and let him sign himself with the sign of the cross, let him also turn himself about as the sun goeth from east to south and west; after the drink let him next go and stand some while ere he repose himself; let him pour as much liquid into the vessel as he removes from it: let him drink this potion for nine nights and eat what meat he will. A drink for the 'dry' disease; delve about sour ompre, that is, sorrel dock, sing thrice the Pater noster, jerk it up, then while thou sayest sed libera nos a malo, take five slices of it and seven pepper corns, bray them together, and while thou be working it, sing twelve times the psalm Miserere mei, deus, and Gloria in excelsis deo, and the Pater noster, then pour the stuff all over with wine, when day and night divide, then drink the dose and wrap thyself up warm. Then take hindheal alone, souse it with water, drink the next morning a cup full, then the next time seven slices and nine pepper corns, the third time nine slices and eleven pepper corns; afterwards drink a strong potion which will run up and adown; then let blood below the ancle.

[Old English (Lacnunga/Leechbook)]: bpenc þis þeopable mnie healfpubu 7 bulenþfan þa linalan · hninon þync · habiþpexan moþopeapb · þealpynt moþopeapbe þecnua þonne ealle topomne pypce him to bjience bo on pyliþt ealo · oþþe on beop hat þtanban nihterne · bpince þonne þpilce nijon mopjenaþ · nime þy teoþan mopjne þæp bpincef tpa bleba pulle · bepylle on ane 7 þa pyjita þien mib aþeol þuph cþaþ apete tip þæpе hio eoþþan hpiman ne mæje oþ þæt hit mon bpincan mæje · þonne þu hit jebjuincen hæbbe beþpeoh [fol. 45a]
segments.translation:
1. Leechdoms [remedies] for 'dry' diseases [likely a category of wasting or febrile illness]: [take] ash rind, aspen rind, elm rind, quickbeam [rowan] rind, the lower part of the great wayside nettle, wormwood, hindheal — that is, water agrimony — purple [bruise/char] all the rinds on the outside, and pound them thoroughly, boil them together, apply equal quantities of all, steep them in clear ale, then let the drink stand overnight in a vessel before a man shall drink it. Let him drink a cupful in the morning; in the middle of the morning hours, let him stand towards the east, address himself to God earnestly, sign himself with the sign of the cross, and turn himself about as the sun goes from east to south and west; after the drink, let him stand a while before he lies down; let him pour as much liquid back into the vessel as he takes out. Let him drink this potion for nine nights and eat whatever food he wishes.

A drink for the 'dry' disease: dig around sour ompre — that is, sorrel dock — sing the Pater noster thrice, pull it up while saying 'sed libera nos a malo' [but deliver us from evil]; take five slices of it and seven pepper corns, pound them together, and while working them, sing twelve times the psalm Miserere mei Deus [Have mercy on me, O God], and Gloria in excelsis Deo, and the Pater noster; then pour wine over the whole preparation at the moment when day and night divide [i.e., at twilight or midnight]; then drink the dose and wrap yourself up warmly. Then take hindheal alone, steep it in water, drink a cupful the next morning; the next time [take] seven slices and nine pepper corns; the third time nine slices and eleven pepper corns; afterwards drink a strong potion that will work both upward and downward [i.e., an emetic and purgative]; then let blood below the ankle.

[Old English continuation, fol. 45a]: [Directions continue, indicating the preparation of a similar compound drink with related herbs — including what appear to be related bark medicines — boiled together and made into a drink with light ale or beer, left to stand overnight; the patient is to drink it for nine mornings; on the tenth morning take two full cups of the drink, boil it down to one, and strain the herbs through a cloth; set it to stand until it cools enough to drink; when you have drunk it, [continue treatment — text breaks off at fol. 45a].]
segments._label: cockayne_v2::1.  Leechdoms  for    '"dry"  diseases;'^  ash  ri

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for the 'Dry' Disease: drinks, salves, and topical treatments
segments.original_text:
2. A drink against the "dry" disease; take liekl [some] balm and the small bulentse, thunderwort, the nether part of Calaminth, the nether leaf of woodwax, the netherward part of wallwort, nepeta. Then pound all together, work it for him (the patient) for a drink, put it into foreign ale or beer, let it stand for the space of a night, then let him drink such drink for nine mornings, take on the tenth morning two cups full of the drink, boil them both in one, and let the worts be therewith, strain through a cloth, set it up where it may not touch the earth, till that a man may drink it; when thou have drunken it, wrap thee up warm; lie on the side to which the pain is incident, if it be in the inwards, then this drink will drive it out. A salve against the "dry" disease; take garlic and great wort, wormwood, a plant of nettle, pound small, and along with it harts grease, that it may be such as dough is, place it then on a linen cloth, then warm both the body and the salve at the fire; when thou wilt smear the body or the spot where the disease may be, follow up the patient with this salve and with this drink. A drink for the "dry" disease; dry wormwood, radish, wallwort [Sambucus ebulus], of all these equal quantities, put into ale, rub the herbs down well, the man should have the liquid stand at first for three nights before he drink it, and subsequently let him drink it for about seven nights, let him let blood under the ancle, let him drink the drink straight on for fourteen nights; let him next let blood under the other ancle. Drink the dose for thirty nights in all, a good cup full at nine A.M. or when thou wilt go to bed. For a "dry" worm in the foot; take the red nettle, pound it, add water to it, lay it on a hot stone, make it froth, bind it on the foot for the space of a night. Again, a salve; pound oats, lay on. For the "dry" rot in the foot, triturate wallwort into sweetened wine, and mastic and pepper; let him drink that.

[Old English text:] One herb for nine ailments: take wallwort and clufsung and kneehollow and egolaspe and camomile and lungwort, nine leaves of bishopwort, and attorlathe and red nettle, and red rose and wormwood and yarrow, and horehound and bolemart, and brewer's ale; do all these worts into wylisc [Welsh/foreign] ale and drink then nine days and let blood. Against severe swelling, work for a drink: alexanders, fennel, wormwood, two of kneehollow.
segments.translation:
2. A drink against the 'dry' disease [a wasting or drying condition, possibly dropsy, arthritis, or a rheumatic ailment]: take liekl [balm, possibly lemon balm, Melissa officinalis] and the small bulentse [identity uncertain], thunderwort [Sempervivum tectorum, houseleek], the lower part of calamint [Calamintha spp.], the lower leaf of woodwax [Genista tinctoria, dyer's greenweed], the lower part of wallwort [Sambucus ebulus, dwarf elder], and nepeta [catmint, Nepeta cataria]. Pound all together, prepare it as a drink for the patient, put it into foreign [imported] ale or beer, let it stand overnight, then let him drink such drink for nine mornings. On the tenth morning take two cups full of the drink, boil them together in one vessel, with the plant material included, strain through a cloth, set it somewhere it does not touch the earth until it is cool enough to drink. When you have drunk it, wrap yourself up warmly; lie on the side on which the pain is present. If the disease is in the internal organs, this drink will drive it out.

A salve against the 'dry' disease: take garlic and great wort [possibly burdock or plantain], wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], and a plant of nettle [Urtica spp.]; pound small, and mix with it hart's grease [rendered fat of a male deer], until it is like dough. Spread it on a linen cloth, then warm both the body and the salve at the fire. When you wish to apply it to the body or to the spot where the disease is, treat the patient with this salve together with the drink described above.

Another drink for the 'dry' disease: dry wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], radish [Raphanus sativus], wallwort [Sambucus ebulus] — equal quantities of all these — put into ale and rub the herbs down well. The liquid should stand for three nights before he drinks it; then let him drink it for about seven nights. Let him let blood from beneath the ankle, and drink the drink continuously for fourteen nights; then let him next let blood from beneath the other ankle. Drink the full dose for thirty nights in total, a good cupful at nine in the morning or when going to bed.

For a 'dry' worm [parasitic lesion or ulcer] in the foot: take red nettle [Urtica dioica or Lamium purpureum], pound it, add water, lay it on a hot stone, make it froth, bind it on the foot overnight.

Again, a salve [for the same]: pound oats, apply.

For 'dry' rot in the foot: triturate wallwort [Sambucus ebulus] into sweetened wine with mastic and pepper; let him drink that.

[From the Old English:] One herb for nine ailments: take wallwort and clufsung [clove-like herb, possibly clove-wort or similar], kneehollow [Ruscus aculeatus, butcher's broom], egolaspe [identity uncertain], camomile [Anthemis/Chamaemelum nobile], lungwort [Pulmonaria officinalis or Sticta pulmonaria], nine leaves of bishopwort [Stachys betonica, betony], attorlathe [Plantago spp. or Conyza, a 'poison-loather'], red nettle, red rose [Rosa spp.], wormwood, yarrow [Achillea millefolium], horehound [Marrubium vulgare], bolemart [identity uncertain], and brewer's ale. Put all these worts into wylisc [Welsh or foreign] ale, drink for nine days, and let blood.

Against severe swelling, prepare a drink: alexanders [Smyrnium olusatrum], fennel [Foeniculum vulgare], wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], two [parts] of kneehollow [Ruscus aculeatus].
segments._label: cockayne_v2::2.  A  drink   against   the   "  dry  "   disease

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for intestinal worms and the small (hair) worm
segments.original_text:
2. For worms again; mingle and rub up with honey a hard and very dry goats tord, administer it to be drunk, that will drive them away. Against worms which ail a man within, again; seethe in water radish to the third part, mingle with wine, give to drink. Again for that; put goats gall on wool, lay and bind it on the navel. For that ilk; mingle with honey, mint well triturated, work it into a little bolus, make him swallow it. Again, give for three days to drink oil and of vinegar an equal quantity. Again, everthroat, marehe, betony, nepeta, githcorn; boil them in wine. For worms which are troublesome within; give to drink a wort drink of contre and of parsnip. A salve; let him eat celandine; let him boil brownwort in inspissated wine, then add thereto ship tar and sulfur; smear therewith.

xlix.
For the small worm; the forepart of a twig of withe wind, and the tallow dock, not the red one, and this coarse salt beaten together very small and little butter.
segments.translation:
2. For worms again: mix and rub up with honey a hard and very dry goat's dung [tord = dung/excrement], administer it to be drunk; that will drive them away. Against worms which afflict a man internally, again: boil radish in water down to one-third, mix with wine, give to drink. Again for that: put goat's gall [bile] on wool, lay and bind it on the navel. For that same ailment: mix with honey mint well ground [triturated], work it into a small bolus [pill], make the patient swallow it. Again, give for three days to drink equal quantities of oil and vinegar. Again: everthroat [a plant, possibly cleavers/Galium aparine], marehe [marsh plant, possibly water-parsley or similar], betony, nepeta [catnip/catmint], githcorn [corn cockle or similar]; boil them in wine. For worms which are troublesome within: give to drink an infusion [wort drink] of contre [identity uncertain] and of parsnip. A salve: let the patient eat celandine; let him boil brownwort in thickened [inspissated] wine, then add thereto ship's tar and sulfur; smear therewith.

Chapter xlix.
For the small worm [hair worm]: the tip [forepart] of a twig of withe wind [bindweed], and tallow dock [possibly Rumex obtusifolius, the broad-leaved dock, not the red dock], and coarse salt beaten together very fine, and a little butter.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Hand-Worms, Dew-Worms, Flesh-Worms, and Internal Worms (Leech Book I, chapters 50–52)
segments.original_text:
1. For hand worms and dew worms; take dock or clotes, such as would swim, mingle the roots with cream and with salt, let it stand for three nights, and on the fourth day smear therewith the sore places.

[Old English, fol. 47a–47b:]
Wið pyrum hond ete: genim mersc meær geallan ⁊ reade netlan ⁊ reade boccan ⁊ smele clifan; wyrc on cu buteran þonne fio sealf gelbben sie, wipþum; nim þonne sealtes þrys men læad on hweor coldum e, ⁊ inne und; wyrc mid laþan ymb niht smire mid. Wið bearwyrme læppe on þar col cele und wætre læppe on swa hat swa he hardost mæge. Wið bearwyrme: sume nimaþ wearm cwead monnes þynne dribað neahterne on; sume swines lungen wearme. Wið hond wyrme: nim scirsceare ⁊ swefl ⁊ pipor ⁊ hwit sealt; meng tosomne smire und. Weax sealf wið wyrme: weax sealf; butere pipor hwit sealt; meng tosomne smire und.

.LI.
Wið wyrmum þe mannes flæsc etaþ: genim geallan þone wagan; cnua on niþe ealo ær þon hit asripen wæ; fol. 47b. wele þ ofer pyllo drincan þreo niht. Eft genim grundswelgan þe on eorþan weaxeþ ⁊ sceapes smeru meng tosomne gelice fela lege on. Eft genim beren ear besenj lege on swa hat ⁊ hat wætres laþa on. Wið flæsc wyrmum genim monnes snian þa leap gerel togedre gebrisid on þærfe gecuna þonne lege on swa þu hatost mæge aþresnan.

.LII.
Wið lusum acriud ⁊ hwon wermod gecnua on ealu wele drincan. Wið lusum cwic seolfor ⁊ eald butere an penig seolfres ⁊ tu penig wæge butran meng on aþrot eal tosomne.
segments.translation:
**Chapter 50 (remedy 1). For hand-worms and dew-worms [skin parasites or eruptions]:**
Take dock or burdock [clotes], such as would float [i.e., fresh/light roots], mingle the roots with cream and with salt; let it stand for three nights, and on the fourth day smear the sore places therewith.

**For hand-worms that eat [i.e., cause itching/eruption on] the hand:**
Take marsh-mallow gall [or: marsh-mallow bile], red nettle, red dock, and small cleavers; work [these] into cow-butter; when the salve is prepared, add [pepper?]; then take three men's measure of salt in cold water [i.e., a cold brine], and apply inwardly [?]; make [the salve] with lye; smear on at night. For a bear-worm [intestinal parasite]: apply cold to cool it, and pour on water as hot as the patient can bear. For a bear-worm: some take warm human excrement and drip it on overnight; some [use] warm swine's lung. For hand-worm: take scissors-herb [scirsceare, possibly germander or a cutting herb], sulphur, pepper, and white salt; mingle together and smear on the wound. Wax salve against worms: wax salve — butter, pepper, white salt; mingle together and smear on.

**Chapter 51. Against worms that eat a man's flesh [flesh-worms]:**
Take gall of the wagon [? — possibly ox-gall or a plant identifier]; pound it into weak ale before it has fermented; give that mixture to drink for three nights over a pillow [i.e., at bedtime]. Again: take groundsel that grows in the earth and sheep's fat; mingle together in equal parts and lay on. Again: take a barley ear, immerse and lay on as hot as possible, and pour on hot water. For flesh-worms: take a man's [urine? — text uncertain], the leaves gathered together, bruised in need; pound them, then lay on as hot as you can endure.

**Chapter 52. Against lice:**
Bruise/pound [acrimony? — acriud] and a little wormwood into ale; give to drink. Against lice: [take] quicksilver and old butter — one penny-weight of silver and two pennies' weight of butter; mix together into a complete [paste] and rub on thoroughly.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::1.  For  hand  worms-  and  dew  worms  ;  t-ake

segments.title_inferred: Remedies against worms, flesh worms, and lice (Leechbook sections li–lvi)
segments.original_text:
2. If a worm eat the hand; take marsh marigold and red nettle, and red dock, and the small bur, boil in cows butter; when the salve is sodden, then further take of salt three parts, shed thereupon, shake together, and smear therewith; lather with soap, about night time smear therewith. Against a dew worm; let the man step upon a hot coal, let him cool the foot with water; let him step upon it as hot as he hottest may. For a dew worm, some take warm thin ordure of man, they bind it on for the space of a night; some take a swines lung warm. Against a hand worm; take ship tar, and sulfur, and pepper, and white salt, mingle them together, smear therewith. A wax salve against a worm; a wax salve; butter, pepper, white salt, mingle them together, smear therewith.

li.
Against worms which eat a mans flesh; pound into new ale, before it be strained, the party coloured ram gall, give the running over to drink for three nights. Again, take groundsel which waxeth on the earth, and sheeps grease, mingle them together, alike much in quantity, lay on. Again, take an ear of beer or barley, singe it, lay it on so hot, and hot water, leave it on. Against flesh worms; take mans sorrel, boil the leaves together, spread them out on the grass, then pound them, lay them on, as thou hottest may endure them.

lii.
Against lice; pound in ale oak rind and a little wormwood, give to the lousy one to drink. Against lice; quicksilver and old butter; one pennyweight of quicksilver and two of butter; mingle all together in a brazen vessel.

.LIII.
Vis fmejca [Against burning/itching]: eat fresh cheese and bread of fine rye. If a mans head bone burn, to allay it, put thereon with pepper.

.LIIII.
For a worm-eaten body and for cruelty [severe affliction] of worms; take elderberry rind from the north side below the bark, elecampane root, dock root, worm-ash [ash of worms], pepper, lovage, swertia [or gentian], oil and horse grease to boot, and cress juice — of all these equal quantities, and of the barks likewise equal quantities; mingle all cold together so that it may smear well the diseased parts; smear therewith by night and in the morning each.

.LV.
For a diseased [inflamed/erupted] body; broom, cress, yarrow — boil in butter and in lard; smear therewith.

.LVI.
A bath for a diseased body; gather the great fern downward [lower fronds], and [continue as fragment].
segments.translation:
2. If a worm [parasitic or festering lesion] should eat the hand: take marsh marigold and red nettle, and red dock, and the small bur [burdock or similar], boil in cow's butter; when the salve is cooked, then additionally take three parts of salt, pour it thereon, shake together, and smear therewith; lather with soap; smear therewith about nighttime. Against a dew worm [ringworm or similar skin condition]: let the man step upon a hot coal, then cool the foot with water; let him step upon it as hot as he can bear. For a dew worm: some take warm thin ordure [excrement] of man and bind it on for the space of a night; some take a swine's lung while still warm. Against a hand worm: take ship's tar, and sulphur, and pepper, and white salt; mingle them together and smear therewith. A wax salve against a worm: a wax salve of butter, pepper, and white salt; mingle them together and smear therewith.

li.
Against worms that eat a man's flesh: pound into new ale, before it is strained, the party-coloured [variegated] ram's gall; give the liquor that runs over to drink for three nights. Again: take groundsel that grows on the earth, and sheep's grease; mingle them together in equal quantity and lay on. Again: take an ear of barley [or beer grain], singe it, lay it on as hot as may be, with hot water, and leave it on. Against flesh worms: take common sorrel ["man's sorrel"]; boil the leaves together, spread them out on the grass, then pound them and lay them on as hot as thou canst endure.

lii.
Against lice: pound oak rind and a little wormwood in ale; give to the lousy person to drink. Against lice: quicksilver and old butter — one pennyweight of quicksilver and two of butter; mingle all together in a brazen vessel.

liii.
For a burning [or itching condition of the skin]: eat fresh cheese and bread of rye. If a man's skull bone burns [is inflamed], to allay it, apply pepper thereon.

liv.
For a worm-eaten body and for severely worm-afflicted [cruelty of worms] lesions: take elderberry rind from the north side below the tree, elecampane root, dock root, ash of worms [calcined preparation], pepper, lovage, gentian [swertia], oil and horse grease in addition, and cress juice — of all these in equal quantities, and likewise equal quantities of the barks; mingle all cold together so that it may smear well all the diseased parts; smear therewith at night and each morning.

lv.
For a diseased [inflamed or erupted] body: broom, watercress, yarrow — boil in butter and in lard; smear therewith.

lvi.
A bath for a diseased body: gather great fern [bracken] from the lower growth, and [text continues as fragment — remainder not fully legible in this segment].

segments.title_inferred: Salves for a Stricken Body, Fig Disease, and Wound Treatments (Leechbook I, Chapters 2, 57–58)
segments.original_text:
2. For a stricken body, a salve; helenium thoroughly sodden, and the netherward part of hammersedge, and old lard, pound all together, warm through a cloth at the fire, smear therewith; then scarify continually about the bruise for seven nights, set a horn upon the open scarifications, smear with the black salve, be it for a night, be it for two, as need be, and as they be open.

LVII.
For the disease called fig, a drink and a salve; let him boil worm wort in milk and drink it. A salve; pound glass to dust, add a drop of honey, leech the wound therewith.

LVIII.
[For wound-salve and wound swellings: work it from the lower part of nettle, and from hemlock, and from the cloven wound-wort, and from the small moorwort; boil all four in butter and in sheep's grease until enough; pound again the same plants into the salve, and sheep's tallow, and garlic, and crow-leek, and leek, and salt; mix well, put on a cloth, warm greatly at the fire, smear therewith.

Wound-salve: outer bark of crab-apple, nettle cropped, two wound-worts, elder bark, waybread, southernwood, bishopwort, lower part of bolt, grease-wort, salt, sheep's tallow, and sheep's grease. For a wound swelling: take crow-leek, radish, elecampane, cloven wound-wort; pound all the plants very well, lay on.

Wound-salve: marrow of a hart, iris tar, and beaten pepper, and sheep's tallow.

[For the black blain, give the man to eat two clusters or three of the plant which is in three ways called mixen-plant.] (marginal addition, later hand)]
segments.translation:
2. For a bruised or injured body, a salve: take helenium [elecampane] thoroughly boiled, and the lower (root) part of hammersedge [sedge/Carex sp.], and old lard; pound all together, warm through a cloth at the fire, and smear therewith. Then scarify [scratch/incise the skin] continually around the bruise for seven nights; set a cupping horn upon the open scarifications, smear with the black salve — do this for a night, or for two, as need requires, and while the scarifications remain open.

Chapter 57.
For the disease called 'fig' [a rectal or anal condition, possibly hemorrhoids or anal fistula], a drink and a salve: let the patient boil worm-wort [artemisia/wormwood] in milk and drink it. For the salve: pound glass to dust, add a drop of honey, and treat the wound therewith.

Chapter 58.
For a wound-salve and wound-swellings: prepare it from the lower part of the nettle, and from hemlock, and from the cloven wound-wort, and from the small moorwort; boil all four together in butter and in sheep's grease until sufficient; then pound again the same plants into the salve, and add sheep's tallow, garlic, crow-leek, leek, and salt; mix well, place on a cloth, warm thoroughly at the fire, and smear therewith.

A wound-salve: [made from] outer bark of crab-apple, cropped nettle, two wound-worts, elder bark, waybread [plantain], southernwood, bishopwort, the lower part of bolt [burdock?], grease-wort, salt, sheep's tallow, and sheep's grease. For a wound-swelling: take crow-leek, radish, elecampane, and cloven wound-wort; pound all the plants very well, and lay on [the wound].

A wound-salve: marrow of a hart [stag], iris [extract], and beaten pepper, and sheep's tallow.

[For the black blain [pustule/carbuncle], give the man two or three clusters of the plant which is called in three ways 'mixen-plant' [possibly Chenopodium/goosefoot or Mercurialis] to eat.] (This last entry is a marginal addition in a different, later hand.)

segments.title_inferred: Salve for wens and wen boils
segments.original_text: 1. For a wen salve and for wen boils; work the salve of the netherward part of nettle and of henbane, and of the wenwort which has cloves or bulbed roots, and of the small moorwort, boil all four in butter and in sheep's grease till there be enough, pound again the same worts in the salve, and ship tar, and garlic, and cropleek, and sedgeleek, and salt, mingle well, put on a cloth, warm thoroughly at the fire, smear therewith.
segments.translation: 1. For a salve against wens [cysts/swellings] and wen boils: prepare the salve from the lower part of nettle and of henbane, and of the wenwort [wen-wort] which has cloves or bulbed roots, and of the small moorwort; boil all four in butter and in sheep's fat until sufficient [in quantity]; pound again the same herbs into the salve, and [add] ship's tar, and garlic, and cropleek, and sedgeleek, and salt; mix well, apply to a cloth, warm thoroughly at the fire, and smear therewith.

segments.translation:
Chapter LIX.

Against palsy: take a cup full of boiling water, another of oil, and of white salt so much as one may pick up with four fingers; shake together until it be all one. Drink all of this by drops, rest awhile, poke thy finger into the gullet, spew up again all and more if thou may; then in the morning let blood from the arm, or from the neck, as much as he may bear; and scarify, and let him put something on; then after all smear with hot oil and let him taste a trifle of salt. Employ gladden [iris] and everfern [polypody fern] picked high up on the tree, and cover over all the scarifications with soft [nesh] wool when they have been smeared.

Against 'neurisn' [a pain condition, possibly neuralgia or a rheumatic ailment]: put bone-wort [comfrey or pellitory] into sour cream and into honey; mingle together with this the yolk of an egg; smear therewith. Again, pound up earthworms and apply them.

Chapter LX.

For a burn, make a salve: take goat's tallow and wheat straw, burn to dust, mix this dust with butter, put it on a pan over fire, render [melt] very well, strain through a cloth, smear therewith.

For a burn, take the lower part of fennel [or smallage], beat with old lard and lay on. Again, take lily and yarrow, boil in butter, smear therewith. For the same, boil ribwort [plantain] in butter and smear therewith.

For the same, boil yarrow in butter and smear therewith.

For the same, boil cuckoo-flower [lady's smock] in sheep's fat and extract [press out], and put everfern [polypody] in honey or in wax. For the same, apply egg-white frequently.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for racking pain and synovia of the joints
segments.original_text: 1. Against racking pain in the joints, pound lithwort with honey, or chew it and lay it on. Again, burn thoroughly the head bone or skull of a wolf and pound it small, sift it through a cloth, put it on the wound. Against pain in the joints, pound wormwood with tar and fen cress, wring out the juice, mingle together, stick the residue upon the joint where the sore is, bind it on fast. For the synovia of the joints, silver weed, brown wort, and the little harewort, it oftenest waxeth in a garden, it hath white blossoms, pound the three worts, mingle them, that is a good salve. With many men the synovia of the joints oozeth out, wring on the spot the juice of an apple, and shave very small some shavings of horn, crumble them on the wound within it, remove that and ever apply the same anew. For the synovia of the joints, burn lithwort, houndshéad, and pound them up with roasted apple; mingle all that together, apply it. Again, take a sour apple, roast and lay it on; apply groats over above the apple.
segments.translation: 1. Against racking pain in the joints: pound lithwort [a plant remedy] with honey, or chew it and lay it on [the affected area]. Again, burn thoroughly the head-bone or skull of a wolf, pound it fine, sift it through a cloth, and apply it to the wound. Against pain in the joints: pound wormwood with tar and fen cress, wring out the juice, mix them together, press the residue upon the joint where the soreness is, and bind it on firmly. For the synovia [synovial fluid / joint fluid] of the joints: [use] silverweed, brownwort, and the little harewort — this last most often grows in a garden and has white blossoms — pound the three herbs, mix them together; that is a good salve. With many men the synovia of the joints oozes out: wring the juice of an apple directly onto the spot, and shave very fine some shavings of horn, crumble them into the wound upon it, remove [the dressing] and always apply the same treatment anew. For the synovia of the joints: burn lithwort [Sambucus, i.e. elder, per marginal note] and houndshead, then pound them together with roasted apple; mix all of that together and apply it. Again, take a sour apple, roast it and lay it on; apply groats [coarsely ground grain] on top of the apple.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::1.  Against  racking  pain  iu  the  joints,  poun

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for synovia of the joints and fever (Leech Book I, ch. 62)
segments.original_text:
2. For the synovia of the joints, take maythe, mingle it with honey, apply it to the wound and bind it fast. For the secretion of the joints, take oak rind and dry it and work it to a fine flour or smede, and further sloethorn rind, the netherward part of it, sift them through a cloth, and shed that on [the wound].

If the joint ooze outward, take merce [smallage/wild celery] the lower part, and honey and the fine flour of wheaten bread and pigeon fennel, mingle together, lay on. Again take meadowwort the lower part, pound it small, mingle with honey, lay on that it be bathed.

If the joint ooze outward, take vinegar and sour crumbs of barley bread and [pepper?] mingle together, bind on that [cloth] which is with vinegar or with sour ale. If the joint ooze outward, take wormwood and pound it, put it in tar, smear on and bind on the joint.

LXII. Against fever disease: elecampane, horehound, waybroad [plantain], pound in ale, let stand two nights, give to drink. Against fever again, drink betony much and eat three shavings. Again a drink in clear ale: wormwood, horehound, betony, bishopwort, fen mint, bogen, the clove-wort, feverwort [wormwood?], marrowdie; drink thirty days. A drink against that: betony, springwort, wolfsbane [attorlathe], barberry, eorothroat [boar's throat], centaury, dry throat, wormwood. Against third-day fever [tertian fever], drink in warm water betony ten portions before one wishes. Against fourth-day fever [quartan fever], drink waybroad juice in sweet water two hours before the fever is to come to him. Against every day's fever, drink in cold water betony the weight of a penny, another such of waybroad.

Against fever again, horehound taken separately in drink is helpful. Against spring disease [lencten-adle]: wormwood, boar's throat, elecampane [elechtre], waybroad, ribwort, chervil, wolfsbane [attorlathe], pepper-wort, alexanders, bishopwort, hyssop, sage, carsuc, work to [a preparation]...
segments.translation:
2. For the synovia [joint fluid / joint swelling] of the joints: take maythe [mayweed/chamomile], mingle it with honey, apply it to the wound and bind it fast.

For the secretion [discharge] of the joints: take oak bark [rind] and dry it and work it to a fine flour or fine meal [smede], and furthermore sloe-thorn bark [rind], the lower/inner part of it; sift them through a cloth and sprinkle that upon [the wound].

If the joint ooze outward [exude]: take smallage [merce / wild celery], the lower part, and honey and the fine flour of wheaten bread and pigeon-fennel [perhaps fennel], mingle together, lay on. Again, take meadowwort, the lower part, pound it small, mingle with honey, lay on so that it be well bathed [with it].

If the joint ooze outward: take vinegar and sour crumbs of barley bread and [possibly pepper or rye], mingle together, bind on [the cloth] soaked with vinegar or with sour ale. If the joint ooze outward: take wormwood and pound it, put it in tar [pitch], smear on and bind on the joint.

Chapter LXII. Against fever illness: elecampane, horehound, plantain [waybroad] — pound in ale, let stand two nights, give to drink. Against fever again: drink betony copiously and eat three shavings [scrapings]. Again, a drink in clear ale: wormwood, horehound, betony, bishopwort [betony-related herb], fen mint, bogen [unidentified], clove-wort, feverwort, marrowdie — drink for thirty days. A drink against that [fever]: betony, springwort, attorlathe [wolf's bane / birthwort], barberry, eorothroat [boar's throat, possibly elecampane variant], centaury, dry-throat [unidentified], wormwood. Against tertian fever [third-day fever]: drink betony in warm water — ten portions — before one expects [the fever] to come. Against quartan fever [fourth-day fever]: drink plantain juice in sweet water two hours before the fever is expected. Against quotidian fever [every-day fever]: drink in cold water betony of the weight of one penny, and another such [weight] of plantain.

Against fever again: horehound taken alone as a drink is helpful. Against the spring sickness [lenten disease, perhaps a seasonal epidemic condition]: wormwood, boar's throat, elecampane, plantain, ribwort, chervil, wolfsbane [attorlathe], pepperwort, alexanders, bishopwort, hyssop, sage, carsuc [cress?] — work into [a preparation, text cuts off]...
segments._label: cockayne_v2::2.  For  the  synovia  of  the  joints,  take  may

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Fever: General, Tertian, Quartan, and Quotidian
segments.original_text: 1. For fever disease; pound in ale lupins, githrife, waybroad, let it stand for two nights, administer to drink. For fever again; let him drink betony much, and eat three bits of it. Again, drink in clear ale wormwood, githrife, betony, bishopwort, fen mint, rosemary, the clove rooted wenwort, marrubium, drink for thirty days. A drink for that, betony, springwort, attorlothe, vervain, everthroat, houndstongue, dwarf dwosle, wormwood. For a tertian fever, let the sick drink in warm water ten sups of betony, when the fever is approaching. For a quartan fever, let him drink juice of waybroad in sweetened water two hours before the fever will to him. For a quotidian fever, let him drink in cold water so much of the dust of betony as may weigh a penny; as much more of waybroad.
segments.translation: 1. For fever disease [febris]: pound lupins, githrife [gyðrife], and waybroad [plantain] in ale; let it stand for two nights, then administer as a drink. For fever again: let the patient drink much betony and eat three morsels of it. Again: drink in clear ale wormwood, githrife, betony, bishopwort [bishop's wort / wood betony or marsh woundwort], fen mint, rosemary, the clove-rooted wenwort [wart-wort], marrubium [white horehound]; drink this for thirty days. Another drink for the same: betony, springwort, attorlothe [viper's bugloss or similar antidotal herb], vervain, everthroat [possibly cleavers or another strangle-wort], houndstongue, dwarf dwosle [dwarf thyme or pennyroyal], wormwood. For a tertian fever [recurring every third day]: let the sick person drink ten sups of betony in warm water when the fever is coming on. For a quartan fever [recurring every fourth day]: let him drink the juice of waybroad in sweetened water two hours before the fever is due to strike. For a quotidian fever [daily recurring fever]: let him drink in cold water as much powdered betony as would weigh a penny-weight, and an equal amount of waybroad.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Fever and Demonic Possession (Leech Book I, Chapters 62–63)
segments.original_text:
2. For fever again it helpeth, to drink marrubium alone. For lent addle, or typhus fever, work to a drink wormwood, everthroat, lupin, waybroad, ribwort, chervil, attorlothe, feverfue, alexanders, bishopwort, lovage.

[Old English and Latin charm text, fol. 51b:]
Boil in foreign ale, add holy water and spring wort; one shall write on a housel-dish and wash the writing off into the drink with holy water and sing over it:
[Latin charm]: In principio erat verbum et verbum erat apud deum et deus erat verbum. Hoc erat in principio apud deum; omnia per ipsum facta sunt. [Then wash the writing with holy water off the dish into the drink.] Then say Credo and Pater Noster and this lay: Beati... [sing over] the cup with the twelve prayer-psalms. 'Adiuro vos, frigora et febres, per deum patrem omnipotentem, et per eius filium Iesum Christum, per ascensum et descensum Salvatoris nostri, ut recedatis de hoc famulo dei et de corpusculo eius, quem dominus noster illuminare instituit. Vincit nos leo de tribu Iuda, radix David. Vincit vos qui vinci non potest. + Christus natus + Christus passus + Christus venturus + Agios + Agios + Agios + Sanctus + Sanctus + Sanctus. In die salutifero incedens squalidus urbes, oppida, rura, vicos, castra, castella peragrans, omnia defulsit sanabat corpora morbis.' [Then rub on the forehead the sign of the cross over other curses gathered for men.]

LXIII. Against a person bewitched by enemies; when the devil seizes the man outright or afflicts him inwardly with disease: [prepare] a spirit-drink [of] elecampane, bishopwort, henbane, cropleac [garlic-leek]; pound to a powder, add ale to moisten; let stand overnight; add fifty libcorns [peppercorns?] and holy water; a drink for a person bewitched by fiends, to drink from a church bell; [also add] yew, [illegible/glaer?], yarrow, elecampane, betony, attorlathe, cassock, fennel... [continues] hage [haw], cassock, in foreign ale; add holy water and springwort.
segments.translation:
2. For fever once more it is helpful to drink horehound alone. For sluggish-fever [lent addle] or typhus fever, prepare as a drink: wormwood, everthroat [possibly cleavers or a similar herb], lupin, plantain [waybroad], ribwort plantain, chervil, attorlothe [viper's bugloss or similar], feverfew, alexanders, bishopwort [wood betony or marsh mallow], lovage.

[Rubric, fol. 51b — Head Indic.:] Boil in foreign [imported] ale; add holy water and springwort; one shall write [a charm] on a communion dish [housel-dish] and wash the written characters off into the drink with holy water, and sing over it:

[Latin charm text:] 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and God was the Word. This was in the beginning with God; all things were made through him.' Then wash the writing with holy water from the dish into the drink. Then say the Creed and the Our Father and this lay: [verses from Sedulius, Carmen Paschale, Book III, 23, intertwined with Bede, Ecclesiastical History III.xxviii.] Sing over the cup, accompanied by the twelve prayer-psalms: 'I adjure you, chills and fevers, by God the Almighty Father, and by his son Jesus Christ, by the ascent and descent of our Saviour, that you depart from this servant of God and from his little body, which our Lord has ordained to enlighten. The lion of the tribe of Judah, the root of David, overcomes us. He who cannot be conquered conquers you. + Christ is born + Christ has suffered + Christ is to come + Holy + Holy + Holy + Sacred + Sacred + Sacred. Passing on the day of salvation through squalid cities, towns, countryside, villages, fortresses, and strongholds, he freed all bodies from their diseases.' Then make the sign of the cross on the forehead, surpassing all other spells gathered for men.

Chapter LXIII. Against a person bewitched by evil beings — when the devil seizes the man outright or afflicts him inwardly with disease: [Prepare] a spirit-drink [from] elecampane, bishopwort, henbane, cropleac [a type of garlic]; pound these to a powder, add ale to moisten, let it stand overnight; add fifty libcorns [possibly peppercorns or grains of pepper] and holy water. [This is] a drink for a person afflicted by fiends, to be drunk from a church bell. [Also add:] yew, [glaer — possibly iris/gladden root], yarrow, elecampane, betony, attorlathe, cassock [possibly sedge or a similar plant], fennel... haw [hage], cassock, [prepared] in foreign ale; add holy water and springwort.

segments.title_inferred: Exorcism and Emetic Remedies for the Demoniac (Fiend-Sick Man)
segments.original_text:
8. A man shall write this upon the sacramental paten, and wash it off into the drink with holy water, and sing over it .... In the beginning, etc. (John i. 1.) Then wash the writing with holy water off the dish into the drink, then sing the Credo, and the Paternoster, and this lay, Beati immaculati, the psalm; with the twelve prayer psalms, I adjure you, etc. And let each of the two men then sip thrice of the water so prepared.

Inde salutiferis incedens gressibus urbes,
Oppida, rura, casas, vicos, castella peragrans
Omnia depulsis sanabat corpora morbis.
SEDULIUS.

LXIII.
For a fiend sick man, or demoniac, when a devil possesses the man or controls him from within with disease; a spew drink, or emetic: lupin, bishop wort, henbane, cropleek; pound these together, add ale for a liquid, let it stand for a night, add fifty libcorns, or cathartic grains, and holy water. A drink for a fiend sick man, to be drunk out of a church bell: githrife, cynoglossum, yarrow, lupin, betony, attorlothe, cassock, flower de luce, fennel, church lichen, lichen, of

[Old English:] cipcijan, crifcef mseles raisan, lupeftice, gepyrc þone brence of hluttrum ealaþ gefinge feofon msepyan ofer þam pyrtum bo gapleac and halig pæter to and brype on ælcne brincan þone brenc þe he brincan pille eft and singe þone sealm bean Inmaculati and exupsat and Saluum me fac deus and þonne drince þone brenc of ciricbellan and se mseppe preoft him singe æfter þam brence þis ofer-domne Sancte pater omnipotens. Piþ bræcfeocum men: cost, gotwode, eluhtpe, betonice, attorlabe, cropleac, holecepfan, hope, pinul; asinge mon msepan ofer pyrce of pylifcum ealoþ and of halig pætepe. Drince þisne brenc þes gehpilcum nipe nijon morgenan and nane oþpe pæran þæt þicce and stille fie, and ælmepsan pelle and him opena god geornlice bidde. Piþ peben heorte: bisceoppyrt, elehcpe, banpyrt, eoporpearn, gifþripe, heahhiologje þonne dæg scade and niht þonne sing þu on ciricean letanias þæt is þara haligra naman, and pater noster mid þy sange þu ga þæt þu sie æt þam pyrtum and þriwa ymbga and þonne þu hie in me gang eft to ciricean mid þy ilcan sange, and gesing .xii. msepan ofer and ofer ealle þa brencas þe to þære adle belimpað on weorþmynde þara twelfa apostola.

LXIV.
Piþ ælcpe ypelpe leodrunan and piþ ælfpri[en]...
segments.translation:
8. [An exorcism:] A man shall write this upon the sacramental paten [communion plate], and wash it off into the drink with holy water, and sing over it: 'In the beginning,' etc. (John 1:1). Then wash the writing with holy water off the dish into the drink; then sing the Creed [Credo], and the Lord's Prayer [Paternoster], and this psalm-verse, 'Beati immaculati' [Psalm 119], with the twelve prayer psalms, 'I adjure you,' etc. And let each of the two men [the leech and the sick man] then sip thrice of the water so prepared.

[Latin verse from Sedulius:]
'Thence, proceeding with health-bringing steps through cities,
Travelling through towns, countryside, cottages, villages, and forts,
He healed all bodies, driving away their diseases.'
—SEDULIUS.

LXIII.
For a fiend-sick man [demoniac], when a devil possesses the man or controls him from within with disease: a spew-drink [emetic]: lupin, bishop-wort [bishopweed], henbane, cropleek [a species of leek]; pound these together, add ale as a liquid, let it stand for a night, add fifty libcorns [cathartic grains] and holy water.

A drink for a fiend-sick man, to be drunk out of a church bell: githrife [possibly a type of plant], cynoglossum [hound's tongue], yarrow, lupin, betony, attorlothe [an antidote plant, possibly betony or elecampane], cassock [possibly wild valerian], flower-de-luce [iris], fennel, church lichen, lichen — [text continues in Old English, here translated:]

Of church vessels [cipicijan], of the sign of the cross [crifcef mæles], of lovage [lupestice]; prepare the drink from clear ale; let seven masses be sung over the plants; add garlic [gapleac] and holy water, and drop into each drink — the drink that he wishes to drink — and again sing the psalm 'Beati immaculati,' and 'Exurge' [Arise, O God], and 'Salvum me fac Deus' [Save me, O God]; and then let him drink the drink from the church bell, and let the mass-priest sing over him after the drink this [prayer]: 'Sancte Pater Omnipotens' [Holy Father Almighty].

For a man sick with vomiting [bræcfeocum]: costmary [cost], gotweed [gotwode, possibly ground elder], elecampane [eluhtpe], betony, attorlathe [antidote plant], cropleek, hollowstem [holecepfan, possibly alexanders or hogweed], hops [hope], fennel [pinul]; let a mass be sung over the plants, [prepared] from Welsh ale [pylisc ealaþ] and from holy water. Let him drink this drink for nine mornings anew, and let no other [liquid] be given that is thick and still; and let him give alms and earnestly pray to the merciful God.

For a heavy [troubled] heart: bishop-wort [bisceoppyrt], elecampane, bonewort [banpyrt, possibly Solomon's seal or comfrey], hart's-tongue fern [eoforþearn], githrife, and [a plant illegible, possibly horehound] — when day separates from night; then sing in the church litanies, that is, the names of the holy saints, and the Lord's Prayer; go thus singing to where you are at the plants, and go around them three times, and then as you put them [in water] go again to the church with the same song; and sing twelve masses over all the drinks that pertain to that disease, in honour of the twelve apostles.

LXIV.
Against every evil enchantment [leodrunan, 'song-rune'] and against elf-[ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]...

segments.title_inferred: Charms, runes, and remedies against elf-sickness, hag-riding, and disease in men and cattle
segments.original_text:
16.

Caiacus + + + + + • Lucas • + • Iohannes. Intercedite pro me • Tecon • Leleloth • Patron • abissus uos.

Eft gocund gewed • In nomine Domini fit benedictum • byrmonice • beromcen • et habet in vestimento et in femore suo • scriptum Rex regum et Dominus dominantium. Eft gocund gewed. In nomine fit benedictum. M M M R M P • N G • L T X X M R F P N + • P T X.

One shall, writing, first write and put the word writing on the swollen breast and not go therein nor write thereon; and also writing wife on done:
EMMANUEL • BYRNICE • NOSTER ASTER.
segments.translation:
16.

[Charm invocation:] Caiacus + + + + + • Lucas • + • Iohannes [i.e., the evangelists Mark, Luke, and John]. Intercede for me. Tecon • Leleloth • Patron • let the abyss [take] you.

Again, a good remedy: In the name of the Lord, let it be blessed. [Anglo-Saxon:] Byrmonic, Beromcen [herb names or charm words]; and he shall have written on his garment and on his thigh: 'King of kings and Lord of lords' [cf. Revelation xix]. Again, a good remedy: In the name [of the Lord] let it be blessed. [Runic/cryptic sequence:] M M M R M P • N G • L T X X M R F P N + • P T X.

[Anglo-Saxon directions:] One shall, while writing, first write and then place that word, writing it upon the swollen [afflicted] breast, and not let it go in thereon nor write thereon; and also, writing [the charm] upon [the patient]:
EMMANUEL • BYRNICE • NOSTER ASTER.

[Continuation of remedies, rendered from the English lower portion of the page:]
Dust [of the prescribed herbs] into milk; drip thrice some holy water upon them; administer this to drink at three hours — at undern (nine in the morning), at midday, or at the ninth hour (three in the afternoon). If the disease be on cattle, pour that same dust into the mouth with holy water.

A salve: Boil lupin, hedgerife [hedge-mustard or ground-ivy], bishopwort [wood betony or bishop's weed], red maythe [red chamomile], harmala [wild rue, Peganum harmala], cropleek, and salt, in butter to a salve; smear it on the head and the breast.

A drink: Put into holy water — viper's bugloss, alexanders, rue, lupins, hedgerife, bishopwort, maythe, cropleek, harmala, and the wenwort which hath knees [knotted figwort or knotweed].

If a mare [nightmare-demon] or hag ride a man, take lupins and garlic and betony and frankincense; bind them on a fawn's skin; let a man have the worts on him and let him go into his home.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies against mental vacancy, folly, bewitched food/drink, and dog-bite (Leech Book I, Chapters 65–68 excerpt)
segments.original_text:
1. Again, a drink against lent addle or iijplivs; feverfue, the herb rams gall [Menyanthes trifoliata], fennel, waybroad; let a man sing many masses over the worts, souse them with ale, add holy water, boil very thoroughly, let the man drink a great cup full, as hot as he may, before the disorder will be on him; say the names of the four gospellers, and a charm, and a prayer, etc. Again, a divine prayer, etc., þine hand vexeth, thine hand vexeth.

Again, a man shall in silence write this, and silently put these words on the left breast, and let him not go in doors with that writing, nor bear it in doors. And also in silence put this on, Emmanuel, veronica.

Ch. lxvi. Against mental vacancy and against folly; put into ale bishopwort, lupins, betony, the southern or Italian fennel, nepte, water agrimony, cockle, marche, then let the man drink. For idiotcy and folly, put into ale, cassia, and lupins, bishopwort, Alexandrian [herb], zedoary, fieldmore, and holy water, then let him drink.

Ch. lxvii. Against bewitched [taken/spoiled] meat; take lupins, lay them under the altar, sing nine masses over them; that shall be against taken [bewitched] meat; lay it under the vessel [vat] that thou wilt milk into. If ale be turned [spoiled], take the lupins, lay them on the four sides of the building and over the door and under the threshold, and under the ale-vat; put the wort into the ale with holy water. If meat be spoiled and the milk bewitched, whether fresh, wild [i.e. from wild animals], or brewed, hallow the worts, put them under the vat and under the door; put lupins and cleavers and betony and bishopwort.

Ch. lxviii. Against the case when a hound has bitten a man, which is a very dangerous wound: draw out the blood with three cuts near the front; throw it then over the way away, then there will be no evil. Again, cut one cut over the wound, pound mugwort, lay it on; there will be no evil to him. Against the bite of a spider [gongelwæpsan], take the nether [lower] part—
segments.translation:
1. Again, a drink against epilepsy [lent addle] or a related convulsive condition [iijplivs, possibly 'triplus' or a corrupt term for a type of seizure]: feverfew, the herb bogbean [Menyanthes trifoliata, here called 'rams gall'], fennel, plantain [waybroad]; let a man sing many masses over the plants, steep them in ale, add holy water, boil very thoroughly, let the man drink a great cupful as hot as he may, before the attack comes upon him; say the names of the four evangelists, and a charm, and a prayer, etc. Again, a divine prayer: 'Thine hand vexeth, thine hand vexeth.'

Again, a man shall in silence write this [charm], and silently place these words against his left breast, and let him not go indoors bearing that writing, nor carry it indoors. And also in silence put this on: Emmanuel, Veronica [i.e. the image on the kerchief].

Chapter 66. Against mental vacancy and against folly: put into ale bishopwort [Betonica officinalis / Stachys betonica], lupins [Lupinus spp.], betony, the southern or Italian fennel [Foeniculum vulgare], nepte [catmint, Nepeta cataria], water agrimony [Bidens tripartita or Eupatorium cannabinum], cockle [Agrostemma githago], marche [smallage / wild celery, Apium graveolens], then let the man drink. For idiocy and folly, put into ale: cassia, and lupins, bishopwort, Alexandrian herb [possibly Smyrnium olusatrum], zedoary [Curcuma zedoaria], fieldmore [possibly Peucedanum palustre or similar], and holy water, then let him drink.

Chapter 67. Against bewitched [supernaturally spoiled] meat: take lupins, lay them under the altar, sing nine masses over them; this shall work against bewitched meat; lay them under the vessel into which you wish to milk. If ale has turned [gone bad], take the lupins, lay them at the four corners of the building and over the door and under the threshold and under the ale-vat; put the plant into the ale with holy water. If food is spoiled and milk is bewitched — whether fresh, from wild animals, or prepared — hallow the plants, put them under the vat and under the door; add lupins and cleavers [Galium aparine] and betony and bishopwort.

Chapter 68. Against a hound having bitten a man, which is a very dangerous wound: by three cuts near the front, let the blood flow; throw it then away over a path, and then there will be no evil [i.e., no ill effect]. Again, cut one incision over the wound, pound mugwort [Artemisia vulgaris], lay it on; there will be no evil to him. Against the bite of a spider [gongelwæpsan, literally 'going-wasp', possibly spider or harvest-mite], take the lower [nether] part—[text continues]
segments._label: cockayne_v2::1.  Again,  a  drink  against  lent  addle  or  ii

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Spoilt Provisions, Spider Bites, and Related Ailments (Leech Book I, sections LXVII–LXX)
segments.original_text:
2. If meat be spoilt, and a good quantity of milken food, or a milking, or brewing, hallow the worts, put them into and under the vat, and under the door; use lupins, and clifwort, and betony, and bishopwort.

LXVIII.
In case that a hunting spider bite a man, that is the stronger spider, strike three scarifications near, in a direction from the bite, let the blood run into a green spoon of hazel wood, then throw it over the road away; then no harm will come of it. Again, strike a scarification on the wound; pound leechwort; lay it on, no harm will happen to the man. Against bite of a weaving spider, take the netherward part of seferthe, and lichen from the blackthorn, dry it to dust, moisten with honey, tend the wound therewith. Against bite of hunting spider, black snails fried in a hot pan and rubbed to dust, and pepper, and betony, let the man eat the dust and drink it and lay it on.

Against hunting spider bite: take the lower part of cottuc, lay it on the wound. Again, strike five scarifications, one on the bite and four around it, throw [blood] with a stick, dripping, over the crossways.

LXIX.
Against bite of a mad dog: agrimony and waybread, mix with honey and egg-white, tend the wound with it. Against dog's wound: fox's clote [burdock], groundsel, boil in butter, smear with it. Again, pound betony, lay it on the bite. Again, pound waybread, lay it on. Again, two onions or three, seething, scorched in ashes, mix with grease and honey, lay on. Again, burn a swine's jaw-bone to ashes, scatter on. Again, take waybread root, pound with grease, put on the wound; it will then drive the venom away.

LXX.
If a man be too loose [suffering from looseness of the bowels], boil hindheoloru [hindberry/raspberry] in Welsh ale, drink at night fasting. If a man be too constrained [costive], boil the same plant in milk, then it will loosen you. Boil in ewe's milk again hindheoloru, Alexandrian laurel, a plant called forewater-palm; then it will be equally dear to the liver.
segments.translation:
2. If meat be spoilt, and a good quantity of dairy food, or a milking, or a brewing [is at risk], hallow the medicinal herbs [worts], place them into and under the vat, and under the door; use lupins, and clifwort, and betony, and bishopwort.

Section LXVIII.
In the event that a hunting spider (that is, the stronger kind of spider) bites a man: make three scarifications [small incisions] close to the bite and directed away from it; let the blood run into a fresh green hazel-wood spoon, then throw it away over the road; then no harm will come of it. Again, make a scarification on the wound; pound leechwort [a medicinal herb]; lay it on; no harm will befall the man. Against the bite of a weaving spider: take the lower portion of seferthe [unidentified plant], and lichen from the blackthorn; dry to a powder; moisten with honey; treat the wound with this. Against the bite of a hunting spider: fry black snails in a hot pan, rub them to a powder, mix with pepper and betony; let the man eat the powder, drink it, and also apply it to the wound.

Against a hunting spider bite: take the lower part of cottuc [mallow], lay it on the wound. Again, make five scarifications — one directly on the bite and four around it — and throw the blood with a stick, letting it drip, over a crossroads.

Section LXIX.
Against the bite of a mad dog: mix agrimony and waybread [plantain] with honey and egg-white; treat the wound with this preparation. Against a dog-bite wound: boil fox's clote [burdock] and groundsel in butter; smear on. Again, pound betony, lay it on the bite. Again, pound waybread [plantain], lay it on. Again, take two or three onions, cook them and scorch them in ashes, mix with grease and honey, lay on. Again, burn a pig's jawbone to ashes and scatter the ash on [the wound]. Again, take a waybread [plantain] root, pound with grease, apply to the wound; this will drive the venom away.

Section LXX.
If a man suffer from looseness of the bowels [diarrhea]: boil hindheoloru [hindberry/raspberry] in Welsh ale [a type of ale]; drink at night on an empty stomach. If a man be too costive [constipated]: boil the same plant in milk; this will loosen him. Boil again in ewe's milk: hindheoloru [raspberry], Alexandrian laurel, and the plant called forewater-palm [identity uncertain]; then it will be equally beneficial to the liver.
