segments.title_inferred: Leech Book I, Chapters 71–72: Treatments for the Dorsal Muscle, Broken Heel Sinew, and Rules for Bloodletting by Season and Moon
segments.original_text:
LXXII. Ch. lxxi.

For the dorsal muscle, seethe in oil and in wax, rue so green, smear the dorsal muscle therewith. Again, take goats hair, make it smoke under the breech up against the dorsal muscle. If a heel sinew be broken, take Fornets palm, seethe it in water, foment the limb therewith, and wash the limb therewith; and work a salve of butter, smear after the fomentation.

Lxxii.

On what season bloodletting is to be foregone, on what to be practised. Bloodletting is to be foregone fifteen nights ere Lammas, and after it for five and thirty nights, since then all venomous things fly and much injure men. Leeches who were wisest, have taught, that in that month no man should either drink a potion drink, nor anywhere weaken his body, except there were a necessity for it; and that in that case, he during the middle of the day should remain within, since the lyft or air is then most mingled and impure. The Romans for this reason, and all south folk, wrought to themselves earth houses, for the boiling heat and venomousness of the lyft. Also leeches say that blossomed worts are then best to work, either for drinks, or for salves, or for dust. Here is set forth how a man shall forego bloodletting on each of the six fives in the month, and when it is best. Leeches teach that no man on the five nights old moon, and again on the ten nights old, and fifteen nights old, and twenty, and five and twenty, and on the thirty nights old moon should let blood; but between each of those six fives. And there is no bloodletting time so good as in early spring, when the evil humours that were drunk in during winter are gathered together; and on the Kalends of April most of all, when trees and plants first shoot forth, then grows the evil fleshy growth and evil blood in the hollows of the body. If a man's blood be evilly swollen, take then a decoction of cress, boil in water and foment therewith, and scrape downward, lay on. If thou wilt stop blood flowing in a cut, take some charcoal ground to dust, scatter on the wound. Take rye straw again and barley, burn to dust [powder].
segments.translation:
Chapter 71.

For the dorsal muscle [back muscle]: seethe rue, while still green, in oil and in wax, and smear the dorsal muscle therewith. Again, take goat's hair, make it smoke under the breech [buttocks/perineum] up against the dorsal muscle. If a heel sinew be broken, take Fornet's palm [an unidentified plant], seethe it in water, foment the limb therewith, and wash the limb therewith; and work a salve of butter, smear [it on] after the fomentation.

Chapter 72.

On what season bloodletting is to be foregone and on what [season] it is to be practised. Bloodletting is to be foregone for fifteen nights before Lammas [August 1st] and after it for thirty-five nights, since at that time all venomous creatures fly about and greatly injure men. The wisest leeches [physicians] have taught that in that month no man should drink a medicinal potion, nor in any way weaken his body, unless there were necessity for it; and in that case he should remain indoors during the middle of the day, since the lyft [air/atmosphere] is then most mingled and impure. The Romans for this reason, and all southern peoples, constructed for themselves underground houses, because of the scorching heat and venomousness of the lyft. The leeches also say that blossomed herbs are then best to prepare, whether for drinks, or for salves, or for powders. Here it is set forth how a man shall avoid bloodletting on each of the six groups of five [nights] in the month, and when it is best [to let blood]. Leeches teach that no man should let blood on the five-nights-old moon, nor again on the ten-nights-old, nor the fifteen-nights-old, nor the twenty, nor the twenty-five, nor the thirty-nights-old moon; but [only] between each of those six groups of five. And there is no bloodletting time so good as early spring, when the evil humours that were ingested during winter are gathered together; and on the Kalends of April [April 1st] most of all, when trees and plants first shoot upward — then the evil fleshy growth and evil blood increase in the cavities of the body. If a man's blood be evilly swollen [congested/inflamed], then take a decoction of cress, boil it in water and foment therewith, and scrape downward [toward the extremities], and lay it on. If thou wilt stop blood from flowing in a wound, take some charcoal ground to dust and scatter it on the wound. Take rye straw again and barley, burn to dust [powder].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Hair Loss, Baldness, Mange in Horses, and Related Ailments
segments.original_text:
1. If a mans hair fall off, work him a salve, take the mickle wolfs bane, and vipers bugloss, and the netherward part of burdock, and ferdwort, work the salve out of that wort, and out of all these, and out of that butter on which no water hath come. If hair fall off, boil the polypody fern, and foment the head with that, so warm. In case that a man be bald, Plinius, the mickle leech, saith this leechdom: take dead bees, burn them to ashes, and linseed also, add oil upon that, seethe very long over gledes, then strain, wring out, and take leaves of willow, pound them, pour the juice into the oil, boil again for a while on gledes, strain them, smear therewith after the bath.

[Old English continuation:]
Deaþ bona þis þon · felijeð leaf pylle on wætere þweah mid þy tep þu hit smepupe · þa leap cnua swa jefoden wruþ on niht on oþ þ hio fie · bruje þ þu mæje smeppan æfter mid þære sealpe bo swa xxx. nihta lenj jif his þearf lie. Wiþ þon þe hwer ne weaxe fol. 58a. lemetwan rejpu jenim mid smit on þa stofe ne cymþ þær næppe ænij weax up; Gif hær to þicce lie jenim swealpan jebærn under cyrelan to ahsan þ þet sceadan þa ahsan on.

LXXXVIII.
Wiþ horses hreopole · nim þa hæranwyrt cnua wel jemenj þonne wiþ frescre buteran jyl swiþe on butian bo on þ hors swa hit hatost mæje smipe ælce dæje bo simle þa sealpe on · jif sio hreopolie micel jenim hland jehæst mid stanum þweah mid þy hlonbe swa hatum þ hors þonne hit drije fie smipe mid þiere sealpe lacua nine. Eft jenim rinjan sealtes þweah mid þy · þ þonne drije fie smipe mid pipres smejipe. Jif hors jeallede fie · nim seþelwerjunj ryrt · þ jorþorman · þ mæjeþan jecnua wel bo buteran to pring wrungendre þurh claþ bo hwit sealt on hwer siþþe lacna þone jeallan mid. Þis horses jeallan nim æccþrotan þ jorwoþan neowearde þ bojen eac swa cnua tosomne syl on wyle þ on buteran afeoh þurh claþ smipe mid.

Gif hors fie ofscoten oþþe oþer neat nim omppan fol. 58b. jieb þ scottisc weax jesinge mon xii. siþþan ofer þ bo halijwæter on þ hors oþþe on swa hwilc neat swa hit fie hafa þe þa wyrte simle mid.

Wiþ þon ilcan nim tobreceendre næble eaje scinje hindan on þone byrelan ne biþ nan teona.
segments.translation:
1. If a man's hair falls off, work him a salve: take the great wolf's bane [aconite], and viper's bugloss, and the lower/root part of burdock, and ferdwort [herb uncertain]; work the salve from that plant and from all these, and from butter upon which no water has come [i.e., clarified/unsalted butter]. If hair falls off, boil the polypody fern and foment [bathe] the head with that, warm. In case a man be bald, Pliny, the great physician [leech], gives this remedy: take dead bees, burn them to ashes, and linseed likewise; add oil to that, and boil very long over coals [gledes]; then strain and wring out; then take willow leaves, pound them, and pour the juice into the oil; boil again for a while on the coals; strain; smear therewith after the bath.

[Old English text, translated:]
Beyond this, concerning that [condition]: take leaves of willowherb [?], boil in water, wash with it [the affected area], while you smear it; pound the leaves thus prepared; apply in the night until it [the affected skin] be dry, so that you may smear afterward with the salve; do so thirty nights long if need be. Against [the condition] that hair may not grow [i.e., to prevent regrowth in unwanted places]: take soot [or ashes of a certain preparation] and apply with it to the place; no hair will ever grow there again. If hair be too thick: take a swallow, burn it under a tile [or earthen cover] to ashes, and scatter those ashes on [the head/scalp].

LXXXVIII.
For mange [hreopol, i.e., scab/mange] in a horse: take the herb hare-wort [hæranwyrt, uncertain], pound it well, mix it then with fresh butter, boil vigorously in butter; apply it to the horse as hot as can be borne; smear every day, always applying the salve. If the mange be great [severe], take urine [hland], heat it with stones, wash the horse with that urine as hot as possible; when it is dry, smear with the salve as the remedy requires. Again, take brine [salted water], wash with it; and when dry, smear with pepper ointment [pipres smeþe]. If a horse has galls [sores/swellings from harness]: take agrimony [?seþelwerjung-wyrt], and groundsel [gorþorman?], and mayweed [mægeþan]; pound them well; add butter; wring out through a cloth; add white salt at each treatment; treat the gall therewith. For horse galls [another remedy]: take sorrel [æcþrote?] and the upper part of groundsel [gorwoþan neowearde], and also ivy [bojen]; pound together; boil in wine and in butter; strain through a cloth; smear therewith.

If a horse or other animal is elf-shot [ofscoten, i.e., afflicted by sudden mysterious illness]: take [a certain herb, name ILLEGIBLE: text damaged], and Scottish wax; let a man sing [i.e., chant or recite] twelve times over it, and put holy water on the horse or on whatever animal it may be; let him who [administers the remedy] always keep the plant with him [as a charm].

For the same [elf-shot]: take a broken needle; sing [chant] over it from behind; thrust it into the [animal's] hide; there will be no harm.

segments.title_inferred: Veterinary remedies for horse leprosy and galls
segments.original_text: 1. For a horses leprosy, take the harewort, pound it well, then mingle with fresh butter, boil thoroughly in butter, put it on the horse as hot as possible, smear every day, always apply the salve. If the leprosy be mickle, take piss, heat it with stones, wash the horse with the piss so hot; when it is dry, smear with the salve, apply also leechdoms inwardly. Again, take runnings of salt, heat them, wash with that, and when it is dry, smear with fishes grease. If a horse be galled, take stichwort, and goutweed, and maythe, pound well, add butter, wring it wetting it through a cloth, add white salt, shake thoroughly, leech the gall therewith. For a horses gall, take ash-throat, and the upward part of goutweed, and rosemary also, pound together, boil in fat and in butter, strain through a cloth, smear therewith.
segments.translation: 1. For a horse's leprosy [scabrous or mange-like skin condition], take harewort, pound it well, then mix with fresh butter, boil thoroughly in butter, apply it to the horse as hot as possible, smear every day, and always keep the salve on. If the leprosy be severe [mickle = great], take urine, heat it with hot stones, wash the horse with the urine while it is hot; when it is dry, smear with the salve, and also apply remedies [leechdoms] internally. Again, take brine [runnings of salt], heat it, wash the horse with that, and when it is dry, smear with fish grease. If a horse be galled [has a sore or abrasion], take stichwort, and goutweed, and maythe [mayweed/chamomile], pound well, add butter, wring the mixture through a cloth, add white salt, shake thoroughly, and treat the gall with it. For a horse's gall, take ash-throat, and the upper part of goutweed, and rosemary as well, pound together, boil in fat and in butter, strain through a cloth, and smear therewith.

segments.original_text:
2. If a horse or other neat be elf shot, take sorrel seed and Scottish wax, let a man sing twelve masses over it, and put holy water on the horse, or on whatsoever neat it be, have the worts always with thee.

3. For the same; take an eye of a broken needle, give the horse a prick with it behind in the barrel, no harm shall come.

[Footnotes: 1. Grease in the legs? 2. The Scottish phrase for this disease; see the Glossary.]

Book II. Contents:

ii. Leechdoms for sore of the maw, in all ten, and if the maw be distended, and what the patient shall eat in that disorder.

vi. Leechdoms for nausea and phlegm that come from the maw, and what the patient shall eat; four remedies.
vii. Leechdoms for a deadened maw, and if it be disordered, and signs of a deadened maw, how it does not digest what he eats; six leechdoms.
viii. Leechdoms for sore and nausea of the maw, such as cannot be healed either with food or with drink, and bitter hiccupping of the throat; four remedies.
ix. Leechdoms for a diseased maw.
x. A leechdom for phlegm and for heating an infirm maw.
xi. A leechdom for a windy and sour distension of the maw.

segments.translation:
2. If a horse or other cattle [neat] be elf-shot [afflicted with a sudden lameness or mysterious ailment attributed to elves], take sorrel seed and Scottish wax [a medicinal wax of Scottish origin], let a man sing twelve masses over it, and put holy water on the horse, or on whatever cattle beast it may be; keep the herbs [worts] always with thee.

3. For the same condition: take the eye [hole] of a broken needle, give the horse a prick with it behind in the barrel [the body cavity/flank], and no harm shall come.

[Editor's footnotes: 1. 'Elf-shot' may refer to 'grease in the legs' [a condition causing swelling in horse legs]. 2. 'Elf-shot' is the Scottish phrase for this disease; see the Glossary.]

Leech Book II — Table of Contents:
i. These remedies [leechdoms] pertain to all disorders of the internal organs [inwards].
ii. Remedies for soreness of the stomach [maw], ten in all, and for when the stomach is distended, and what the patient should eat during that illness.
iii. Remedies for swelling and soreness of the stomach, including how one must let the patient's blood.
iv. Remedies for hard swelling of the stomach, and ointments [smearings/unguents], and what the patient's diet should be.
v. Remedies for distension of the stomach, and what the patient should eat during that illness.
vi. Remedies for nausea and phlegm coming from the stomach, and what the patient should eat; four remedies.
vii. Remedies for a deadened [atonic] stomach, and if it be disordered, and signs of a deadened stomach — how it fails to digest what is eaten; six remedies.
viii. Remedies for soreness and nausea of the stomach that cannot be healed by either food or drink, and for bitter hiccupping of the throat; four remedies.
ix. Remedies for a diseased stomach.
x. A remedy for phlegm and for warming an infirm stomach.
xi. A remedy for windy and sour distension of the stomach.
xii. A remedy for hiccup and for when food will not stay down.
xiii. A remedy for spasm [springiness] of the stomach.
xiv. A remedy for all infirmities of the stomach.
xv. A remedy for spasm of the stomach, when bitterness comes up through the mouth in belching or hiccupping, or when [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off at page end].

segments.title_inferred: Leechdoms for Disorders of the Stomach: Table of Contents (Sections V–IX)
segments.original_text:
V. Leechdoms for puffing up of the maw, and what the patient shall partake of in this disorder.

vi. Leechdoms for want of appetite and for nausea, which cometh of the maw, and what the patient shall eat; four crafts, or skilful recipes.

vii. Leechdoms for deadened maw, and if it have bad lymph, and tokens of deadened maw, how that digests not, which it eateth; six leechdoms.

viii. Leechdoms for sore and want of appetite of the maw, which may be cured neither with meat nor drink, and suffereth bitter risings in the throat; four receipts.

ix. Leechdoms for an inward wound of the maw.
segments.translation:
V. Remedies [leechdoms] for bloating [puffing up] of the stomach [maw], and what the patient shall consume in this condition.

VI. Remedies for loss of appetite and for nausea arising from the stomach, and what the patient shall eat; four skilled recipes [crafts].

VII. Remedies for an atonic [deadened] stomach, and if it contain bad fluid [lymph], and the signs [tokens] of an atonic stomach — namely, that it does not digest what is eaten; six remedies.

VIII. Remedies for pain and loss of appetite of the stomach, which can be relieved neither by food nor drink, and which is accompanied by bitter regurgitation [risings] in the throat; four recipes.

IX. Remedies for an internal wound [ulcer] of the stomach.

segments.title_inferred: Leech Book II: Table of Contents, Chapters XX–XXIX — Liver and Abdominal Disorders
segments.original_text:
XX. Leeches teach this for wound of the liver, when the swelling or matter bursteth forth.

XXI. Leechdoms and tokens of a hardened and puffed up liver in manifold wise, either in the lobes, or in the margins, or in the membranes, or in the hollows, of the liver.

XXII. Leechdoms for the sense of hardness of the liver, and salves, and wort drinks, or if it burst and descend downwards or mounteth up upwards, or if the insensibility and hardness of the liver become too prolonged.

.XXIII. Læcebomaf hyvet him fie to popjanne on lipep able hpaet him fie co healbanne je on læcebomum je on mete · j tacn þ þe fpile þpinan ne mæj ne utypnan on þæpe hppe.

.XXIIII. Læcebomaf · j pyptbjiencaf piþ eallum hpep pæpcum ealjia þpeotyne j pf Lfep peaxe.

.XXV. Læcap eac be eallum pambe coþum j abltim fpeocol tacn funbon j læcebomap j hu mon þa yjelan pætan þæpe pambe lacnian fcyle j þonne abl to þæpe pambe pile pop þæpe yjelan omihtan pætan cneop hatiaþ lenbenu heperaþ jajiaþ þupa lenbena lipan · toje otep betpeox fculdpium utjonj jemenjeb.

.XXVI. Læcebomaf jip fio pamb punb biþ hu f mon onjitan mæje j jelacnian · v. cpæftaf.

Læcebomaf be pambe mipþenlicpe jecynbo oþþe mijfbypbo hu p mon mæje onjitan j jelacnian j be pambe hattpe jecynbo · j be cealbje j pætpe jecynbo j be hattje j bpijje jecynbo j f hæmed þinj ne buje · þyppum lichoman j ne fcejjeþ hatum ne palatum · feopon cpæptap j þæte hæmed þinj fpiþoft eþlaþ þam þe hopn able habbaþ.

segments.translation:
XX. Leeches [physicians] teach this for wound of the liver, when the swelling or matter bursteth forth.

XXI. Leechdoms [medical remedies] and tokens [signs/symptoms] of a hardened and puffed-up liver in manifold ways, whether in the lobes, or in the margins, or in the membranes, or in the hollows of the liver.

XXII. Leechdoms for the sensation of hardness of the liver, and salves, and wort drinks [herbal beverages], or if it burst and descend downwards or mount up upwards, or if the insensibility and hardness of the liver become too prolonged.

XXIII. Leechdoms telling what the sick man hath to forego in liver disorder, and what he hath to hold by, either in leechdoms or in diet [meat], and tokens that the swelling may not dwindle nor run off in the liver.

XXIV. Leechdoms and wort drinks for all liver pains, thirteen in all, and if the liver wax [grow enlarged].

XXV. The Leech Book treats also of all belly [abdominal] complaints and distensions; clear tokens have been found, and leechdoms, and how one must treat the evil humours [ill fluids] of the belly; and when disease settles in the belly on account of the evil, noxious humour, the knees grow hot, the loins oppress and make sore the sides of the loins; [the humour] spreads between the shoulders, going out mixed.

XXVI. Leechdoms for when the belly is wounded — how one may recognise it and heal it; five crafts [methods/chapters].

XXVII. Leechdoms concerning the belly's manifold nature or disorder, how one may recognise and heal it; and concerning the belly's hot nature, and of cold and wet nature, and of hot and dry nature; and that the act of intercourse [sexual thing] may not harm a thin body, and does not injure the hot nor the cold; seven crafts; and that sexual intercourse is most troublesome to those who have a horn complaint [perhaps a hardening or calculus condition].

XXVIII. Leechdoms for when a man's upper gut [upper digestive tract] is filled with evil humour, and concerning a windy belly.

XXIX. Leechdoms for when food is ill digested and turns foul [putrid] and produces evil humour or loose flux [diarrhoea].
segments._label: cockayne_v2::XX.  Leeches  teach  this  for  wound  of  the  li

segments.title_inferred: Table of Contents: Chapters XXX–XXXVI of the Leechbook, covering disorders of the belly, intestines, rectum, spleen, and related conditions
segments.original_text:
XXX. Leechdoms if thou will that thy wamb be always sound, and of disease and sore; and of disease of the wamb and sore of the intestines, and for the moderation of the wamb; sixteen receipts.

XXXI. Leechdoms and symptoms marking of the rope gut and small gut, and of the fæcal discharge; how they suffer unbounded thirst and loss of appetite; and of their complexion or hue, and of the navel, and the dorsal muscles, and rectum, and pit of the belly, and milt, and share or pubes, and how bad leeches ween that that is loin disease or milt wark, and where the wambsick suffer the disorder, and how it is with them, and how a man shall treat them: four methods.

XXXII. Leechdoms how a man shall cure one so afflicted, whether with bloodletting, and salve, and baths, and how to send curatives into the belly. And these leechdoms are efficacious against loin ache, if a man use sand, for dysentery, for diseases of the maw, and gripings, and womens tendernesses, and of the disease where a man would evacuate and is not able (tenesmus), and if the discharge be windy, and watery, and bloody. Twelve methods.

XXXIII. Leechdoms for the perilous disease in which a man casteth from him and speweth, as they say, his excrement through the mouth; and for wounds of the inwards, and sore of the small guts, and for laceration of the inwards, and for inward spasm; and for the wamb which digests late, and the man who is not affected by the leechdoms; there cometh on him dropsy, pain in liver, sore of spleen, retention of urine, inflation of belly, pain in loins, sand and stones wax in the bladder. Thirteen receipts.

[Old English, fol. 61b]
XXXIIII. Leechdoms and by what virtues a man shall give the leechdoms when they are needed for the lungs, and the heart, and the wamb [belly], and the bladder, and the intestines; and how ready [prepared] it be concerning weakness and cold and this late digestion, or if the wamb be overgrown and wounded, and if a man be inwardly bloated, and against inward spasms and itching from below.

XXXV. Leechdoms concerning over-fullness of children and the wamb, and if food digest not well in them, and they spit over [vomit], and if the navel be foul.

XXXVI. Leechdoms concerning milt-wark [spleen disease], and that it be on the left side, and signs of the disease, how uncertain they are, and how long the milt [spleen] be, and of the membrane of the milt on the left side, of the laughter that cometh from the milt; how the milt...
segments.translation:
XXX. Leechdoms [medical remedies] for those who wish that their belly [wamb] be always sound and free of disease and soreness; and for disease of the belly and soreness of the intestines, and for the regulation [tempering] of the belly: sixteen receipts.

XXXI. Leechdoms and diagnostic signs pertaining to the large intestine [rope gut] and small intestine, and of fæcal discharge; how patients suffer extreme thirst and loss of appetite; and concerning their complexion or color, and the navel, and the dorsal muscles, and the rectum, and the pit of the belly, and the spleen [milt], and the groin or pubes; and how incompetent physicians suppose these to be loin disease or spleen-ache; and where those sick in the belly suffer the disorder, and how it manifests in them, and how a man shall treat them: four methods.

XXXII. Leechdoms showing how a man shall cure one so afflicted, whether by bloodletting, salve, or baths, and how to administer curatives internally into the belly. These leechdoms are also efficacious against loin-ache, if a man uses grit [sand passed in urine], for dysentery, for diseases of the stomach [maw], and for gripes, and women's tenderness [pelvic pain], and for the disease in which a man would evacuate but cannot (tenesmus), and if the discharge be windy, watery, or bloody: twelve methods.

XXXIII. Leechdoms for the perilous disease in which a man expels and vomits, as it is called, his excrement through the mouth [intestinal obstruction with feculent vomiting]; and for wounds of the internal organs, and soreness of the small intestines, and for laceration of the internal organs, and for internal spasm; and for a belly that digests slowly; and for the man who is not helped by the leechdoms—upon him cometh dropsy, pain in the liver, soreness of the spleen, retention of urine, inflation of the belly, pain in the loins, and gravel and stones that grow in the bladder: thirteen receipts.

XXXIIII. Leechdoms and by what virtues [properties] a man shall administer the remedies that are needed for the lungs, the heart, the belly, the bladder, and the intestines; and how one should be prepared concerning weakness and cold, and this condition of slow digestion; or if the belly be distended and wounded; and if a man be inwardly bloated; and against internal cramps and itching from below.

XXXV. Leechdoms for over-fullness [overfeeding or indigestion] in children, and of the belly; and if food does not digest properly in them, and they vomit; and if the navel be foul [infected or ulcerated].

XXXVI. Leechdoms concerning spleen-ache [milt-wark], and that it is located on the left side; and the signs of the disease, how uncertain they are; and how large the spleen may be; and concerning the membrane of the spleen on the left side; of the laughter that arises from the spleen [a classical notion that the spleen caused laughter]; how the spleen... [text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: Table of Contents: Leechdoms for Children's Surfeit, Spleen Complaints, Humours, and Related Ailments
segments.original_text:
XXXV. Leechdoms for the overfilling or surfeit of children, and for their wamb, and if their meat digest not well, and if sweat pass from them and stink foully.

XXXVI. Leechdoms of pain in the milt, and that the milt is on the left side, and tokens of the disease, how reckless the sick are, and how long the milt is, and of the film or membrane of the milt on the left side, and of splenetic laughter, which cometh of the milt, how the milt suffereth everything of that which other limbs suffer either hot or cold; and of the bath, and of sexual commerce, and whence the heat cometh and the cold of the milt: eight receipts.

XXXVII. Leechdoms how a man shall tend the man within and without with cold and hot leechdoms, and what meat he is to take, and what he is to forego.

XXXVIII. Leechdoms how a man shall cure the humours and the livid complexion by external applications, and of the evil humours of the milt, and of the lubricity of the humours of the milt.

XXXIX. A leechdom for a windy swollen state of the milt, which cometh of eating of apples, and of nuts, and of peas, and of honey, and which puffeth up throughout the rope gut, and the intestines, and the gastric juice.

[Old English text:]
.XL. Læcedomas be ablawunge and aliehardunge þæs blodes on þam milte.

.XLI. Læcedomas wið þære heardnesse and sare miltes and hu mon niwes spines blædran mid ecede gewylbre gelmescan þa heardnesse and wið eallum mablum þrys criuptas.

.XLII. Læcedomas gif omihtre blod and yfele wætan on þam milte syn þinbende þonne sceal him mon blod læran on þis wisan þe þeos læceboc geþ and be þæs blodes hive.

.XLIII. Læcedomas hwæt him on þære adle to picgeanne sie hwæt to forleanne.

.XLIIII. Læcebom eft þe þe þ yfel uttiht of þam milte swiðe æþele, and þe eac þeah wiþ magan ablawunge and innorþ innescep þa wambe þynnaþ þa oman, bitere hritecetunje awej beþ and breost coþe, and wiþ wæstc, and lifer adle and milte sæstc, and wambe wind eal þa liht.

.XLV. Læcedomas and wiðdrinc wiþ ansvollenum.

.XLVI. Læcedomas wiþ gelipeþerrine sidan wara and tacn wundorlic hwanan he cume and hu sio abl toweard lie, and hu mon þara tilian scyle.

.XLVII. Læcedomas þa þe þynnunge hæbben and fualinge mægen, þam lichoman þe þa hæto medmicle oþþe [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]
segments.translation:
XXXV. Leechdoms [medical remedies] for the overfilling or surfeit of children, and for their belly [wamb], and if their food does not digest well, and if sweat passes from them and stinks foully.

XXXVI. Leechdoms for pain in the milt [spleen], and [a note] that the milt is on the left side, and tokens [signs/symptoms] of the disease, how heedless the sick are, and how long the milt is, and of the film or membrane of the milt on the left side, and of splenetic laughter, which cometh of the milt, how the milt suffereth everything that other limbs suffer whether hot or cold; and of the bath, and of sexual intercourse, and whence the heat cometh and the cold of the milt: eight receipts [recipes/prescriptions].

XXXVII. Leechdoms [explaining] how a man shall tend the patient within and without with cold and hot leechdoms, and what food he is to take, and what he is to forgo.

XXXVIII. Leechdoms [explaining] how a man shall cure the humours and the livid complexion by external applications, and of the evil humours of the milt, and of the fluidity [lubricity] of the humours of the milt.

XXXIX. A leechdom for a windy, swollen state of the milt, which cometh of eating apples, and nuts, and peas, and honey, and which puffeth up throughout the rope gut [large intestine/colon] and the intestines and the gastric juice.

XL. Leechdoms concerning the inflation and hardening of the blood in the milt.

XLI. Leechdoms against the hardness and soreness of the milt, and how one shall apply a freshly prepared pig's bladder [softened] with heated vinegar to ease the hardness, and against all the cramps [pains] of this affliction.

XLII. Leechdoms [for when] impure blood and evil humours are pressing upon the milt — then one shall let blood [perform phlebotomy] from the patient in the manner that this leech-book describes, and concerning the condition of the blood.

XLIII. Leechdoms [advising] what is to be consumed and what is to be avoided during the disease.

XLIV. A leechdom, again, that is very excellent for driving the evil out of the milt, and which is also good against swelling of the stomach; and inwardly it smooths [the gut], thins the belly humours, drives away bitter retching, and [treats] chest disease, and wasting [fever], and liver disease and disease of the milt, and wind of the belly — all these it lightens.

XLV. Leechdoms and a counter-drink [antidote/remedy potion] against swellings.

XLVI. Leechdoms against pain in the side with a slippery [pleuritic?] quality, and a wondrous token [sign] of whence it cometh and how the disease is to be expected, and how one shall treat it.

XLVII. Leechdoms for those who have wasting and the power of putrefaction, for the body wherein the heat is moderate or [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]

segments.title_inferred: On Disorders of the Stomach: Causes, Symptoms, and Dietary Treatment (Leechbook Book II)
segments.original_text:
LEECE BOC.

jecynbon ond on magan ond lila[h]jinn ond on unmobe ond on unsemerpteccunge ond ungepitlico word; þe magas bið neali wiþe heortan ond þære geloðre ond geaborlenge þam brifsigene; of þam cumaþ þa abla swiðolu of þær magan inbyrjan ond on uplum searum pretan atterberende; þonne ga þa wætan þa ypelan weoðrað gegadepode on þone magan ond þær rixiaþ ond scearpunja nimaþ; swiðost on þam monnum þe habbaþ swiþe gewelne ond sarcijenne majan, swa þ he sume fomnunja swelleþ; ne magon abejian þa scarranjan scearpunja þoera ætena sætena; hwilum pyrmas of þam miþerran ond belcum gesecaþ þa neoþerran dælas to þam magan ond eac heortcoþe þyrceað ond angnyssa ond geswoþunga, swa þe hwilum sume men fram þara wyrma plitunje swellaþ ond forweoþrað; for þon þam mannum beah þ him mon on fruman þa mettas giefe þe celunje ond surunje magen habben; swa sindon seppla naes to swete ealles ac surmelsde ond peru ond persucas ond hlaf gedon on ceald water oþþe on wut be þære gelicunje þes magan þe þa ypelan metan sceorpende ond scearpan hæfð; wiþ beah eac on fruman þam þe þa heortcoþe ond sceorp cæpoliaþ selcra georrisc þ him mon lytlum þa mettas selle þa þe late melten; leax ond þa fixas þa þe late meltan, gose, mæple ond swines fet þa þe mægen wiþ habban þam yfelum wætan; ond þonne him wel sie þonne þicge he swetran mettas; ne biþ him nanwuht werse þonne he þa þicge þa þe late melten.
segments.translation:
LEECHBOOK.

[Concerning] natural causes, and [those arising] in the stomach, and lassitude [lit. 'body-aching'], and in despondency [low spirits], and in immoderate excitement of speech; the stomach lies near to the heart and to the gullet [or: the pleasure of it], and [there is] an enlargement toward the diaphragm; from this arise the evil vapours [ill humours] of the stomach, bursting upward and in swelling eruptions bearing poison [noxious matter] upward; then when the evil humours are gathered together in the stomach and hold dominion there, and sharpnesses [acrid sensations] take hold — most severely in those men who have a very moist [humid] and sore stomach, so that it swells up at times — they cannot endure the biting sharpnesses of those foods that have been eaten. At times worms from the middle parts and from the bowels seek out the lower parts toward the stomach, and also cause heartburn [heartache], and oppression and faintings, so that sometimes some men swell up from the stirring of those worms and perish. Therefore it is beneficial for those persons that in the beginning one give them foods that can have a cooling and souring [astringent] quality; such are apples, not altogether too sweet but somewhat sour, and pears, and peaches, and bread put into cold water or into [warm] water according to the disposition of the stomach which has those evil sharp-biting and acrid qualities. It is also beneficial in the beginning for those who suffer heartburn and acrid eructations [sharp belching], that for each meal one give them sparingly of foods that digest slowly: salmon and those fish that digest slowly, goose, sorrel [or: maple-tree related preparation — possibly sorrel], and pig's trotters, which can withstand [resist being broken down by] the evil humour; and when he is well, then let him eat sweeter foods. It will not be at all worse for him when he eats those [foods] that digest slowly.

segments.title_inferred: On wamb [stomach/gut] disease and treatment by bloodletting and herbal drinks
segments.original_text: 2. Of wamb disease, or if the evil humours come from the wamb alone and do not overrun the whole body, that case shall be treated with healing meats alone. If moreover the evil humour from the wamb overrunneth the whole body, this shall be dealt with by means of the stronger remedies: at whiles one shall let him blood from a vein, if there seems to be too much of the blood and of the evil humour, and also give a wort drink; but he shall first be let blood and after that have the wort drink given him.
segments.translation: 2. Concerning disease of the wamb [stomach/belly/gut]: if the evil humours [morbid bodily fluids] arise from the wamb alone and do not spread throughout the whole body, that condition shall be treated with healing foods alone. If, however, the evil humour from the wamb spreads throughout the entire body, this shall be addressed by means of stronger remedies: at times one shall let blood from a vein, if there appears to be an excess of blood and of the evil humour, and shall also administer a wort drink [herbal medicinal beverage]; but the patient shall first be bled and only afterwards have the wort drink administered to him.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for a Sore and Swollen Maw (Stomach)
segments.original_text:
1. For a sore and swollen maw; take oil, and put mastic, and dill, and southern wormwood into the oil, let it stand three nights, and arrange that the worts be sodden in the oil, then put that upon raw fresh wool, smear the maw therewith. Again, for that ilk; take old lard, triturate it in a treen mortar, mingle therewith the white of an egg, put on a cloth and lay on. For a sore maw, again; put the wort into warm oil, which hight fenugreek, and bunches of laurel flowers, and dill; smear the maw with that.

2. For a sore maw; put on a cloth juice of [watercress] and oil. Again, if the maw be swollen or distended; take of Welsh wine and fennel oil, each half, seethe wormwood tops, put into warm oil, smear therewith. Give him then flesh to eat of small creatures, of small birds, roasted and broiled, and manifold apple-kinds, fermented beverages against those that are raw [i.e., undigested], and sodden in vinegar and in grease and in wine well sharp. For a sore maw; rose leaves five, or seven, or nine, and pepper corns the same number, grind small and in warm water give to drink. Again for the same; take of field-wormwood twenty clustered corns and of cummin as much as thou canst take with three forward fingers, crumble then a bowl-full, pound in a mortar, add cold water two good bowls full, give then first the half to drink.

3. This is a fomentation to strengthen the maw and to comfort after purging or after a wortdrench [medicinal drink]; heat thinly sliced bread on old lard if thou have it; if it be summer, to wormwood seed-burrs seethe together, put on a cloth, anoint with oil, lay on the maw; if it be winter thou needest not put the wormwood to it.

[Section III.] For the swelling and soreness of the maw; if the person have the strength, let him blood, then smear him with the oil...
segments.translation:
1. For a sore and swollen maw [stomach]: take oil, and put mastic, and dill, and southern wormwood into the oil; let it stand three nights, and arrange that the herbs be steeped [sodden] in the oil; then apply that upon raw fresh wool and smear the maw therewith. Again, for the same ailment: take old lard, triturate it in a wooden mortar, mix therewith the white of an egg, put it on a cloth and lay it on. For a sore maw, again: put into warm oil the herb called fenugreek, and bunches of laurel flowers, and dill; smear the maw with that.

2. For a sore maw: put upon a cloth the juice of [watercress] and oil, and lay it on. Again, if the maw be swollen or distended: take Welsh wine and fennel oil, each in equal half measure; seethe wormwood tops, put into warm oil, smear therewith. Then give the patient flesh to eat of small creatures, of small birds, roasted and broiled, and various kinds of apples; fermented [acid] drinks against those [foods] that lie undigested, and things sodden in vinegar and in grease and in well-sharp wine. For a sore maw: take five, or seven, or nine rose leaves, and an equal number of pepper corns, grind small, and give to drink in warm water. Again, for the same: take twenty clustered corns of field-wormwood and of cummin as much as thou canst take between three forward fingers; crumble a bowlful, pound in a mortar, add cold water to make two good bowls full, and then give the first half to drink.

3. This is a fomentation to strengthen the maw and to comfort it after a purge or after a medicinal drink [wortdrench]: heat thinly sliced bread in old lard if thou have it; if it be summer, seethe wormwood seed-burrs together with it, put on a cloth, anoint with oil, lay it on the maw; if it be winter, thou needest not add the wormwood.

[Section III.] For the swelling and soreness of the maw: if the person have the strength, let blood from him, then smear him with the oil [recipe follows].

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Hard Swelling of the Maw and Loss of Appetite
segments.original_text:
IV.

For a hard swelling of the maw; give the sick salt meats, and hares and boars flesh, roots of rue, and cresses, and sheer (clear) wine, and easily digested meats, and applications drawing out the hard swelling, and baths; work moist smearings, that is, lotions, of oil and wormwood, and of mastic and wine; bathe him, then smear with that, then overlay with ewes wool, and swathe up; take also mild apples, put them for the space of a night into wine and then seethe them; then sweeten the wash or infusion with virgin honey, and pepper it with twenty peppercorns; then give him in the morning a little bowl full or a spoon full of the thus wrought potion to drink.

A leechdom for swelling of the maw; one shall in the morning hours squeeze hard the mans feet and hands, and one shall bid him cry or sing very loud.

[Old English sections follow, treating nausea/disgust of food coming from the stomach, including remedies with wormwood, threebread, sharp wine, salt meats with vinegar, roasted senna, radish, hot-natured foods, good clear wine, and further remedies using southern cumin dried and ground with vinegar in a mortar, fennel seed, dill, pepper, rue leaves, strained honey, compounded with vinegar to the consistency of prepared electuary, placed in a glass vessel and taken with bread or any food desired, also taken at the lupine and vesper hours; noted to benefit not only loss of appetite but the whole body. Against loss of appetite of food: dissolve asafoetida in water, grind with vinegar, give to drink with flatbread.]
segments.translation:
IV.

For a hard swelling of the stomach [maw]: give the sick person salt meats, and flesh of hare and boar, roots of rue, and cresses, and clear wine, and easily digested foods, and poultices drawing out the hard swelling, and baths. Prepare moist ointments, that is, lotions, of oil and wormwood, and of mastic and wine; bathe him, then anoint him with that, then lay ewes' wool over it and bind it up. Take also mild apples, steep them overnight in wine and then boil them; then sweeten the liquid or infusion with virgin honey, and add twenty peppercorns; then give him in the morning a small bowlful or a spoonful of this prepared potion to drink.

A remedy for swelling of the stomach: in the morning hours one shall squeeze the man's feet and hands firmly, and bid him cry out or sing very loudly.

[Section VI — Against nausea and disgust of food coming from the stomach and thereabouts:] Give him on an empty stomach [at night-fasting] wormwood or threebread [a medicinal bread preparation] steeped in sharp wine; give on an empty stomach; and after that give salt meats prepared with vinegar, and roasted senna and radish to eat, and all foods that have a hot quality, and give whey to drink; and take care that they suffer no irregularity; and give good heated and clear wine to drink at night fasting; and teach him to fast at night on honey; and seek for him a brook to ride along, and on a horse or whatever they can endure.

Again, against loss of appetite for food: take southern cumin, dry it with vinegar, then grind it in a mortar; and fennel seed, and dill — three spoonfuls — grind all together; add three spoonfuls of pepper and seven spoonfuls of rue leaves, and a pound of strained finest honey; mix all thoroughly together; then add vinegar as seems fitting so that it is made to the consistency of a preparation that is compounded to swallow; then put it in a glass vessel, and then with bread or with whatever food you wish teach him to take it and use it — though you may with a spoon take the juice that helps; use it at the lupine hour as well as at the vesper hour. This is not good against loss of appetite for the stomach alone, but for the whole body it is beneficial.

Against loss of appetite for food: dissolve asafoetida in water with pressure [by rubbing]; grind with vinegar; give to drink with flatbread. This [remedy follows].
segments._label: cockayne_v2::IV.   For  a  hard  swelling  of  the  maw  ;  giv

segments.title_inferred: Leech Book II: Remedies for a Deadened or Troubled Stomach (Maw)
segments.original_text:
1548. [For the maw / stomach.] Give him also after his nights fast [nihtnescigum] this: take vinegar mingled with somewhat of gladden [gladene / iris] and of long pepper ten corns or clusters, and mustard; mingle all together and triturate; give after a nights fasting one spoon measure. Then consider thou, notwithstanding, that all the aforenamed leechdoms and the after-written ones shall not be done at one too long season, but must have space and rest between them, whilom two days, whilom three; and when one lets him blood on a vein, on those days let none other leechdom be done to him, except about five days later or more. For a stomach troubled with hiccup [forsogenum magan] or puffed up, take beeves flesh sodden in vinegar and with oil, prepared with salt, and dill, and porrum [leek]; let the sick diet on that for seven days; then that relieves the labouring maw. These are tokens of a deadened maw: what he taketh, that melteth or digesteth not, but the undigested meat passes through the womb [bowels] outward.

[Section VIII.]
For soreness and aversion [unlust] of the maw, of him who can be cured neither with meat nor with drink, and [for] bitter hiccuping: Take centaurian [centaury], that is of a wild sort, some call it hyrde wyrt [shepherd's herb], some earth-gall [earthgall]; scrape a pound [of it].
segments.translation:
1548. [Remedy for the stomach / maw.] Give him also after his night's fast the following: take vinegar mingled with some gladden [iris root] and ten corns or clusters of long pepper, and mustard; mingle all together and triturate; give him, after a night's fasting, a spoon's measure. Bear in mind, however, that all the above-named remedies and the ones written hereafter should not be carried out continuously for too long a period at once, but must have intervals of space and rest between them—sometimes two days, sometimes three. And when one lets him blood from a vein, on those days no other remedy should be applied to him, except about five days later or more.

For a stomach troubled with hiccup or puffed up: take beef-flesh boiled in vinegar and prepared with oil and salt, and dill, and leek; let the sick man diet on that for seven days; that then relieves the labouring stomach. These are the signs of a deadened [paralysed] stomach: what he takes in neither melts [digests] nor is retained, but the undigested food passes downward through the bowels outward.

[Section VIII.]
For soreness and loathing of the stomach in one who can be cured neither with food nor with drink, and for bitter hiccuping: Take centaury—the wild sort; some call it hyrde wyrt [shepherd's herb], some call it earthgall [earth-bile]; scrape a pound of it.

LEECH BOOK. II. 189

wort, some earth gall, rub small a pound of it, and smoke; apply thereto four bowls full of hot water; give it to the sick to drink for three days after his nights fasting. Again, take the upper part of the red nettle, while having seed, wash it clean, and work it up to sup. Again, administer to drink juice of green marche triturated and wrung out, and in the same wise, give him to drink juice of horehound. Again, for sore of maw; rue and mint, dill, dwarf dwosle, agrimony, some call it garcliff, and cress, pound them all in wine or in ale, give of this each day to drink.

ix.

For an inward wound of the maw; take goats milk just when it is milked, administer to be drunk. Some drink for sore of maw warm ewe milk, some the best oil warmed, some mingle that with the goats milk till they spew, that they may spew the more easily.

LEECH BOOK. II. 189

wort, some earth gall — rub small [i.e., grind fine] a pound of it, and smoke [fumigate]; apply thereto four bowls full of hot water; give it to the sick to drink for three days after his nightly fasting. Again, take the upper part of the red nettle while it is in seed, wash it clean, and work it up into a soup [or decoction]. Again, administer to drink the juice of green marche [wild celery or marsh parsley], triturated and wrung out; and in the same manner give him to drink juice of horehound. Again, for soreness of the maw [stomach]: rue and mint, dill, dwarf dwosle [dwarf elder / danewort], agrimony (some call it garcliff), and cress — pound them all in wine or in ale, and give of this to drink each day.

ix.

For an inward wound of the maw: take goat's milk just as it is milked, and administer it to be drunk. Some drink warm ewe's milk for soreness of the maw; some [drink] the best oil warmed; some mingle that [oil] with the goat's milk until they vomit, so that they may vomit the more easily.

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Nausea, Stomach Ailments, Bloating, and Vomiting
segments.original_text:
X.
For nausea and to heat the maw; water sodden on wormwood and on dill, down to the third part, give the man that to drink; it warmeth and hardeneth the maw.

XI.
For puffing up and blowing of the maw; overpour roots of fennel and marehe with clear old wine, and of that give the sick to drink after his nights fast two little bowls full. For a windy puffing up of the maw, to warm the maw, rue and dill, mint and marehe; seethe bundles of them separate in three jugs full of water, and continue seething so that there be only one cup; then administer the water to be drunk.

XII.
For spewing, and in case that a man's meat will not keep down; take sinfulle, rub it fine into sharp wine, give the man a bowl full to drink after evening work. Take, for that ilk, two parts of juice of fennel, one of honey, seethe or boil down till the mixture have the thickness of honey, then give after a night's fast a spoon measure full; that restraineth nausea, that bettereth the lungs, that healeth the liver. For mickle spewing, and in case a man may keep in no meat; take one ounce of dill seed, four [parts] of pepper, three of cumin, rub very fine; then put into water in which mint has been sodden and sour apples or verjuice, upper measure [i.e., heaped], if the man be not in a warm season, with wine, and give to drink when he will not go to fast; and lay outwardly on the maw sodden wood-apple and crumbs of bread and such-like poultices.

XIII.
For warmth/heating of the maw: there is a certain herb kind called lenticulae [lentils], a hundred and twenty times. Again, three spoon measures of sharp sheep's vinegar when he will sleep in the evening.

XIV.
For all ailments of the maw; take the outer roots of fennel, so that there be the most of them; take off from the fennel so much as may be one and a half pound; pour [over them]...
segments.translation:
X.
For nausea and to warm the stomach: boil water with wormwood and dill down to one-third its original volume; give this liquid to the man to drink. It warms and strengthens the stomach.

XI.
For bloating and flatulent distension of the stomach: steep roots of fennel and marsh-mallow [marehe] in clear, aged wine, and give the sick person two small bowlfuls to drink after his overnight fast. For a windy bloating of the stomach, to warm the stomach: take rue, dill, mint, and marsh-mallow; boil bundles of each separately in three jugs of water, continuing to boil until only one cup of liquid remains; then administer this water as a drink.

XII.
For vomiting, and when a man's food will not stay down: take houseleek [sinfulle], rub it fine into sharp [acidic] wine, and give the man a bowlful to drink after the evening's labor. For the same complaint: take two parts of fennel juice and one part of honey; boil these down until the mixture reaches the consistency of honey; then give a spoonful after an overnight fast. This restrains nausea, improves the lungs, and heals the liver. For excessive vomiting, when a man cannot retain any food: take one ounce of dill seed, four [parts] of pepper, three of cumin; grind very fine; then put into water in which mint has been boiled, along with sour apples or verjuice, heaped measure — if the man is not in a warm season [i.e., not feverish], add wine — and give to drink when he is about to break his fast. Also apply externally to the stomach: boiled crab-apple [wood-apple], bread crumbs, and similar poultices.

XIII.
For heating/fever of the stomach: there is an herb called lenticulae [lentils], to be used one hundred and twenty times [i.e., as a frequently repeated remedy]. Also, three spoonfuls of sharp sheep's vinegar taken in the evening when he wishes to sleep.

XIV.
For all ailments of the stomach: take the outer roots of fennel, the most plentiful available; remove from the fennel plant so much as amounts to one and a half pounds; pour [over them — text continues]...
segments._label: cockayne_v2::X.   For  nausea  and  to  heat  the  maw  ;  wate

segments.title_inferred: Old English remedies for stomach ailments: gastric burning, bloating, and heat of the stomach (Leechbook II, Chapters XIV–XVI)
segments.original_text:
1556. Properly περιφαρισμός is of the wamb, or venter, not of the maw; and Aretaeus says as much, Chron. lib. ii., cap. 6. But other authors have the same expression as Alex. Trail.; for instance Caelius Aurelianus, Chron. lib. iii., cap. 2.

[Ch. XIV.]
For that [ailment] pour on [the] open wound/sore: prepare it hot; pound [ingredients] finely with vinegar and seethe; then take out the roots somewhat in the vinegar and wring out of the vinegar cleanly; put then in the vinegar honey with vinegar; put then away [a] good portion thereof into [the] weight of an ounce, more and other such of white pepper and of ameos [Ammi] and of southern wort [southernwood], other [such] portion; mix these lighter ones all together, and then give him three spoonfuls. Do this against stomach burning and thirst. Afterwards mix with the lesser oil [olive oil] and give to drink; that [benefits] the thirsty.

[Ch. XV.]
For that stomach ailment when the womb/belly mounts with bitter belching, or he belches, or there seethes in him in the stomach: take of pepper such as one coin's weight; of hyssop seed such as four coins' weight; other such of cumin; grind all and give in wine [a] spoonful's measure when he will go to sleep. The distension of the maw and the swelling thus comes from the black humors; but take then springs [i.e., cold-spring water or sponges], put in sharp vinegar, season strongly, lay over the stomach when it is such. After that, if there be need, lay other cloths on [in] stronger [preparation] and remove, as is said: one mixed with honey and then make [as] the physicians know [how].

[Ch. XVI.]
This is a recipe for the hot stomach's immoderate leanness/wasting and the over-cooling thereof; [for] the hot stomach's im-[moderation]...

[Scholarly note from editor:] a pound and a half, then pour on of vinegar as much as be a sextarius and a half, then let these stand thus together for three nights; after that seethe the roots somewhat in the vinegar, and [...]
segments.translation:
1556. [Editorial note:] Properly, περιφαρισμός [peripharismos] pertains to the womb [venter/abdomen], not to the stomach [maw]; and Aretaeus says as much, Chronic Diseases, book ii, chapter 6. But other authors use the same expression as Alexander of Tralles; for instance Caelius Aurelianus, Chronic Diseases, book iii, chapter 2.

[Chapter XIV.]
For this [condition, of stomach burning and thirst]: pour [the preparation] onto the open [wound/affected area]; prepare it hot; pound [the ingredients] finely with vinegar, and then take the roots out of the vinegar and wring them out of the vinegar cleanly; then put into the vinegar honey with vinegar; then put away a good portion thereof equal in weight to an ounce, and more, and an equal portion of white pepper and of ameos [bishop's weed / Ammi majus] and of southern wort [southernwood / Artemisia abrotanum], another equal part; mix all these lighter [ingredients] together, and then give him three spoonfuls. Do this for stomach burning and thirst. Afterwards mix with the lesser [i.e., olive] oil and give to drink; this aids the thirsty.

[Chapter XV.]
For that stomach complaint when the belly swells with bitter belching, or he belches, or there is a seething in him in the stomach: take pepper of the weight of one coin; hyssop seed of the weight of four coins; an equal amount of cumin; grind all together and give in wine [by] a spoonful's measure when he wishes to go to sleep. The distension of the stomach and the swelling thus comes from the black humors [black bile]; but take then sponges [or: springs], put them in sharp vinegar, season strongly, and lay them over the stomach when it is in such a state. After that, if there be need, lay other cloths on [the area] in a stronger [preparation] and remove them, as is customary: one [cloth] mixed with honey; and then treat as physicians know how.

[Chapter XVI.]
This is a remedy for the immoderate wasting caused by the hot stomach [i.e., excessive heat of the stomach], and [for] its over-cooling; [for] the hot stomach's im-[moderate condition...]

[Editor's note, continued:] a pound and a half, then pour on of vinegar as much as a sextarius and a half, then let these stand together thus for three nights; after that seethe the roots somewhat in the vinegar, and [text continues].

segments.title_inferred: Leech Book II: On Disorders of the Maw (Stomach) — Symptoms, Diet, and Remedies for Hot and Cold Stomach Ailments
segments.original_text:
1556. I cap. 5; p. 105, ed. 1548; p. 319, ed.

Head [S]iege. 1556, for a few lines only.

Gr. εἰς ἄκρατον, dipped in wine unmixed with water, (as if brandy). Read fpripban?

LEECH BOOK II.

Maw are tokens, when it is vexed with inflammations, Book II. thirst is incident to the man, and oppression, and swoonings, and vacillation of mind, and loss of appetite, and nausea. It is beneficial for him that he should eat bread in cold water or in vinegar, and eggs very hard boiled or roasted, (at nine o'clock in the morning,) and worts, and lactucas, that is lettuces, and mallow, and hens flesh not much sodden, and the extremest parts of the limbs of goose, that is giblets, and fishes which have hard flesh, and periwinkles, and oysters, and others; various sorts of peas, and mild apples, and a bath of sweet fresh waters shall be wrought; a hot bath will not suit him. Tokens of the overcold maw, that the men feel no thirst nor burning heat in the maw, nor is there any warm symptom incident upon them. But they yearn for meats more strongly than is proper, and if in their inwards there lodges any cold humour, then they spew up the filth and are not able to retain the meats which they swallow; and after the spewing soon they pray that somewhat be given them to eat. Those men thou shalt smear with the oil on which wormwood has been sodden. And the thick coagulated and the viscid humours in the maw, and the chilled humours, and the intractable thick viscid foulness, thou shalt warm and thin with the afore named leechdoms. Work then for the sick man a wort drink of the rind of the root of fennel, and let it be very tender, and such that it may weigh six ounces, and one sextarius of vinegar, and three ounces of aloes; then seethe the fennel in the vinegar till it be well sodden, then wring the worts off the vinegar, then add to the vinegar a pound of clean honey, then seethe these together, till it be as thick as honey, then shed the aloes into it, well rubbed up, and give three spoon measures with water; that is good for heart ache and for epilepsy.
segments.translation:
LEECH BOOK II [an Old English medical compilation].

[Chapter 5: On Ailments of the Maw (Stomach)]

[Editorial note: Reference to edition of 1556, cap. 5; p. 105, ed. 1548; p. 319, ed. (1556). The heading 'Siege' appears in the 1556 edition for a few lines only. The Greek phrase εἰς ἄκρατον means 'dipped in wine unmixed with water' (i.e., as if in brandy). Read [fpripban?].]

The signs [tokens] that the maw [stomach] is afflicted with inflammations are: thirst comes upon the man, and oppression, and swoonings [fainting], and vacillation [wandering] of mind, and loss of appetite, and nausea. It is beneficial for him that he should eat bread soaked in cold water or in vinegar, and eggs very hard-boiled or roasted (at the third hour [nine o'clock in the morning]), and pot-herbs [worts], and lactucas — that is, lettuces — and mallow, and hen's flesh not much boiled [sodden], and the outermost parts of the limbs of goose — that is, giblets — and fishes with firm flesh, and periwinkles, and oysters, and others; various sorts of peas, and mild apples. A bath of sweet fresh water shall be prepared; a hot bath will not suit him.

The signs of the over-cold maw: the men feel no thirst nor burning heat in the stomach, nor is there any warm symptom upon them. But they crave food more strongly than is proper, and if in their inward parts there lodges any cold humour, then they vomit up foul matter and are unable to retain the food they have swallowed; and soon after vomiting they beg that something be given them to eat.

Those men you shall anoint with the oil in which wormwood has been boiled. The thick, coagulated, and viscid [sticky] humours in the stomach, the chilled humours, and the intractable thick viscid foulness, you shall warm and thin with the aforementioned leechdoms [remedies].

Then prepare for the sick man a medicinal herb-drink [wort drink] made from the bark of the root of fennel — let it be very fresh and tender, weighing six ounces — and one sextarius [approximately one pint] of vinegar, and three ounces of aloes. Then boil the fennel in the vinegar until it is thoroughly cooked; then strain the herbs from the vinegar; then add to the vinegar a pound of pure honey; then boil these together until the mixture is as thick as honey; then stir in the aloes, well ground [rubbed up], and administer three spoonfuls with water. This is good for heartache [stomach pain / cardiac ailment] and for epilepsy.

segments.title_inferred: On Excessive Appetite, Extreme Hunger, Digestion, and Liver Diseases
segments.original_text:
2. Of the overmickle appetite, when from the same Book II. cold disease of the maw it cometh that the overmickle appetite and greediness ariseth from the foul humour, which cometh from the maw, and the sick are spewing, and, as it were a hound, again soon seek the meats: to them thou shalt give clean and clear wine, and red, much heated; let it not be too sharp; nor let the meat be too sharp, nor too sour, which thou mayst give them, but smooth and fat. If extreme hunger cometh from immoderate heat and tenderness of the maw, so that they are soon in a swoon, if they have not the meat; then, for extreme hunger thou shalt soon treat the man; bind the extremities of his limbs with ligatures, pull his locks for him, and wring his ears, and twitch his whisker, when he is better, give him soon some bread broken in wine, before he take other meats. Give him the meats which are not too soon digested. Beeves flesh, and goats, and harts digests late: bucks is worst, and rams, and bulls, and those of four footed neat which are very old, and fowls which have hard flesh; peacock, swan, duck. To those that have a cold wamb thou shalt give well digesting meats, shell fishes, and young of culvers, hens flesh, and gooses wings; they are the better as they are fatter and fresher. The extremities of the limbs of swine [i.e., pigs' trotters] are easy of digestion, and young beeves, and kids: and sweet wine digests better than the rough.

XVII. For all liver diseases, and of its nature, and increment, and of the six things which work the liver pain, and curing of all these, and plain tokens, either by the urine, or by the loss of appetite, or by the hue of the [skin].

[Old English text:] V nepefeoþan þo htas pp lagppan helc þa lenbenbpaðan · þo is blodes cimber · and blodes hus · and foscor · þonne þara metta meltung biþ and þynnes þa becumaþ on þæb hyger þonne wendaþ hie hiora hiw and cegiaþ on blod · and þa unyefernessa þe þær beoþ hio awyrrþ ut and þæt claene blod gesamnaþ and þurh feower æðra swiðost onsenc to þære heortan and eac geond ealne þone lichoman oþ þa ytmestan limo. Be sex þingum þe þone liferseoc wyrceaþ: ærest geswel þæt is aþundenes þære hyerre; oþer is þies geswelles coberseung; þridde is wund þære lifre; feorþe is welmes hæto mid geselnysse and mid sare geswelle; sixt [...]
segments.translation:
2. On excessive appetite [overmickle appetite]: When, from the same cold disease of the stomach [maw], it comes about that excessive appetite and greediness arise from the foul humour [corrupt bodily fluid] coming from the stomach, and the sick are vomiting and, like a dog, soon seek food again — to such patients you shall give clean, clear wine, red and well heated; let it not be too sharp [acidic]; nor let the food you give them be too sharp or too sour, but smooth and fatty. If extreme hunger comes from immoderate heat and tenderness of the stomach, such that the patient soon falls into a swoon if he lacks food — then for extreme hunger you shall treat the man promptly: bind the extremities of his limbs with ligatures, pull his hair, wring his ears, and twitch his beard/whiskers. When he recovers, give him soon some bread broken in wine before he takes other foods. Give him foods that are not digested too quickly. Beef, goat, and hart [red deer] digest slowly; buck [male deer] is the worst, as are ram, bull, old four-footed cattle, and fowl with hard flesh — peacock, swan, duck. To those with a cold stomach [wamb] you shall give easily digesting foods: shellfish, young pigeons [culvers], hen's flesh, and goose wings; these are better the fatter and fresher they are. The extremities of the limbs of swine [pigs' trotters] are easy of digestion, as are young cattle and kids. Sweet wine digests better than rough [astringent] wine.

XVII. On all liver diseases, their nature, growth, the six things that cause liver pain, their cures, and the plain signs — either by the urine, by loss of appetite, or by the complexion [hue of the skin].

[Old English section, translated:] ...the kidneys [lendenbrædan]: it [the liver] is the vessel of blood, and the house of blood, and the filter [foscor]. When the digestion of foods and their refinement are complete, they come into the liver, and there they change their form and are transformed into blood; and the impurities [uncleannesses] that are there it drives out, and the pure blood it gathers, and sends it most strongly through four veins to the heart, and also throughout the whole body to the outermost limbs. Concerning the six things that cause liver disease: the first is swelling, that is, distension of the liver; the second is the bursting [rupture] of that swelling; the third is a wound of the liver; the fourth is the heat of inflammation [welm] with tenderness and painful swelling; the sixth [sic] [...]
