segments.title_inferred: On the Swollen and Wounded Liver: Tokens, Leechdoms, and Hardening
segments.original_text:
XIX.

Tokens of a swollen and wounded liver; leechdoms for that; and of the hardening of the liver. He who is wounded in the liver, if he be not sooner cured, then arriveth at the disorder in which a man speweth purulent matter. If the man swollen in the liver, or the bloated one, abideth so swollen until the five and twentieth day, so as that the swelling bursteth not, then beginneth the liver to harden; if it bursteth, then is there a wound in the liver.

Tokens of the wound are these; when the wound is bursten out then the outrunning through the wamb [belly/intestines] is as it were bloody water, and his face is red and swollen; and when thou settest thy hand upon the liver then he feeleth very great pain, and the man is very tender, and from that disease cometh full oft water-blisters. Against a swollen sore: at first with poultices and salves shall one treat it; the salve shall be of barley groats sodden in lye and of dove's dung wrought with honey; and then lay the salve on a hot cloth or skin, or smear a blanket therewith; then the swelling will soften straightway and ripen within. Let him drink mulsum [hydromel/mead], that is a mingled drink, every day; and goat's milk sodden, and water in which good herbs have been sodden.

XX.

Leechdoms for the wound of the liver when the swelling is ripened and bursteth. Take goat's milk, as warm as newly milked, give to drink. Add also to the drink viper prepared as physicians know; and when he shall be willing to drink of anything let them call it not water, but [let it be] previously sodden from herbs—in wormwood and in other such things—and such poultices as we wrote before. But one shall first bathe and wash the sore places with warm springs and hot water, and in that water shall be sodden laurel berries and shepherd's purse and earthgall [centaury] and wormwood; with that thou shalt long first heat the sore places and let it steam in. If then the wound of the liver festereth greatly so that he hawks that foul matter out of the mouth, make for him a mingled drink [mulsum]; that is a great deal of boiled water in a good deal of honey.
segments.translation:
XIX.

Signs of a swollen and wounded liver; remedies for that; and concerning the hardening of the liver. He who has a wound in the liver, if he is not cured sooner, then arrives at the condition in which a man vomits purulent matter. If the man who is swollen in the liver, or the bloated one, remains so swollen until the twenty-fifth day without the swelling bursting, then the liver begins to harden; if it bursts, then there is a wound in the liver.

The signs of the wound are these: when the wound has burst outward, then the discharge through the belly runs like bloody water, and his face is red and swollen; and when you place your hand upon the liver then he feels very great pain, and the man is very tender, and from that disease very often come water-blisters. Against a swollen sore: one shall treat it at first with poultices and salves; the salve shall be made of barley groats sodden in lye and of dove's dung worked with honey; then one shall lay the salve on a hot cloth or skin, or smear a blanket with it; then the swelling will soften immediately and ripen within. Let him drink mulsum [honey-water], that is a mingled drink, every day; and goat's milk that has been boiled, and water in which good herbs have been boiled.

XX.

Remedies for the wound of the liver when the swelling has ripened and bursts. Take goat's milk, as warm as newly milked, and give it to drink. Add also to the drink viper prepared as physicians know how; and when he is willing to drink anything, let them not call it water, but [let it be] previously boiled from herbs—in wormwood and in other such things—and use such poultices as we wrote before. But one must first bathe and wash the sore places with warm springs and hot water, and in that water shall be boiled laurel berries, and shepherd's purse, and earthgall [centaury], and wormwood; with that thou shalt long first heat the sore places and let the steam penetrate. If then the wound of the liver festers greatly, so that he coughs that foul matter out of the mouth, prepare for him a mingled drink [mulsum]; that is a large portion of boiled water with a good portion of honey.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::XIX.   Tokens  of  a  swollen  and  wounded  liver

segments.title_inferred: Leechdoms for Abscess of the Liver (Chapter XX–XXI)
segments.original_text:
XX.

Leechdoms for the abscess of the liver, when the purulent swelling bursteth; take goats milk so warm, newly milked, give the patient that to drink. Form also into a potion an adder, wrought so as leeches ken how to work it, and when the sick will to drink anything, let them drink nothing but water previously sodden with worts, on wormwood and on other such, and such onlayings as we before wrote of. But one shall previously bathe and wash the places with warm squirtings and with hot water, and on the water let there be sodden bunches of laurel berries or flowers, and herdwort, that is, earth gall, and wormwood; with these do thou long previously foment the sore places, and make the reek smoke them. If further the wound of the liver be very rotten, so much as that the man breaketh the rotten matter from his mouth, let him work himself a mulled drink, that is, a mickle deal of boiled water in a good deal of honey; from it shall the scum [be frequently skimmed off before one puts it away, until there be none; let it then cool and be drunk].

XXI.

[Old English text — Signs of abscess of the liver, whether in the lobes and sides and hollows. The hardening is made in two ways. One is at first, before any other ailment comes to the liver more easily; the other comes after other ailments of the liver — that is without pain, and when the man eats anything he vomits again and turns his body and has uncontrolled vomiting and the hiccups; and when thou settest thy hands straight upon the liver they are as heavy as stone and it is not sore. If it be thus for a long time, then it changes into unnatural water-collections, all the bloatings and the fluids which lie distributed throughout the body, and they come from hot blood and boiling. So also must one observe in the liver whether the heat and the bloating is in the liver itself, in the hollows, and in the things that are around the liver, and whether it is in the cartilages and lobes that are in the round parts and sides that are in the two halves. When the physician understands that, he may then the more readily find the remedy. These are the signs: if the bloating is so hot in the liver's other [lobe] or hollow, then there is great swelling and fever with choking of the throat and shining [acute] sore to the collarbones, to the shoulder and coughing and narrowness of the chest, and more heaviness than soreness.]
segments.translation:
XX. Remedies for abscess of the liver, when the purulent swelling bursts: take goat's milk still warm, freshly milked, and give the patient that to drink. Also prepare a potion from an adder, prepared as physicians know how to make it. When the sick person wishes to drink anything, let them drink nothing but water previously boiled with herbs — with wormwood and other such plants — and use such poultices [onlayings] as we described before. But one should first bathe and wash the affected places with warm irrigations [squirtings] and with hot water, in which bunches of laurel berries or flowers, and herdwort — that is, earth gall — and wormwood have been boiled; with these foment the sore places at length beforehand, and make the steam/smoke [reek] fumigate them. If furthermore the wound of the liver is very putrid, to such a degree that the man brings up the putrid matter from his mouth, let him prepare for himself a mulled drink — that is, a great quantity of boiled water in a good measure of honey — from which the scum must be frequently skimmed off before one puts it away, until there is none; then let it cool and be drunk.

XXI. [Old English, translated:] Signs of abscess of the liver, whether in the lobes, sides, and hollows. The hardening arises in two ways. One kind occurs at the outset, before any other ailment more easily comes to the liver; the other follows after other ailments of the liver — it is without pain, and when the man eats anything he vomits again, tosses his body, and has uncontrolled vomiting and hiccups; and when thou settest thy hands directly upon the liver, they feel as heavy as stone and it is not sore. If it remains thus for a long time, it then turns into unnatural accumulations of fluid [water-collections], all the bloatings and fluids that lie spread throughout the body coming from hot and boiling blood. So also must one determine in the liver whether the heat and the bloating is in the liver itself, in the hollows, and in the structures surrounding the liver, and whether it is in the cartilages and lobes of the rounded parts and sides, in both halves. When the physician understands this, he may the more readily find the appropriate remedy. These are the signs: if the bloating is intensely hot in the other lobe or hollow of the liver, then there is great swelling and fever with choking of the throat, and sharp [shining/acute] pain extending to the collarbones and to the shoulder, and coughing and tightness of the chest, and more heaviness than soreness.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::XX.   Leechdoms  for  the  abscess  ot  the  liver

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Abscess with Suppuration: Surgical Incision, Drainage, and Diuretic Remedies (Leech Book II, Chapter XXII–XXIII)
segments.original_text:
XXII.

LEECH BOOK. II. 209

every day to drink the wort parsley, and dill, and seed of marche or its roots with honey: if he hath no fever eke that with wine. After that other wort drinks are proper, when the swelling is become an abscess and bursteth, and is becoming more free from soreness, and is passing off downwards through the wamb, and the man pisseth ratten, reckoneth that he then may be hole; then must be given him principally the diuretic drinks, in order that all the mischief through the wamb and through the mie may be done away, lest the man should take to spewing ratten through the mouth; and let him withhold himself somewhat from the bath and from green apples. If however the swelling and the ratten mounteth up to that degree that it seem to thee that a man may cut into it and let it out, then work him a salve first of culvers sharn and the like of that, and previously bathe the places with sousings, with the water, and with the worts which are wrote of before. When thou understandeth that the swelling is growing nesh and mild, then touch thou it with the cutting iron, and cut in a little, and cleverly, even that the blood may come out, lest an evil sinus or pouch descend in thither. Do not let too much blood at one time, lest the sick man become too languid or die; but when thou dost prick or cut it, then have for thyself a linen cloth ready that therewith thou mayst soon bind up the cut; and when thou wilt again let more blood draw the cloth off, let it run by a little at a time till it gets dry; and when the wound is clean, then enlarge it that the thirl or aperture may not be too narrow; but do thou every day syringe through it with a tube,

[Old English, fol. 78b:]
sum þiddan oþlege þe þa wunde clænsien · gif hio wiþon mynstre weorþe clænsian mid hunige · 7 gelæc erc togædere. Gif þonne seo ungefelede aheardung þære liþe to langlum wyrþ · þonne wyrþeþ hio stæcer bollan þone þe mon gelacman ne mæg. 7 him sceal sona on fruman þa ær genemnedun bestungan · ne drince he nipæs naht · 7 gif se þrefsoca mon blodes to fela lifebbe þonne sceal him mon ær eallum oþrum læcedomum blod læcan of þam swiþran earme on þære niþerran æbre · gif þa mon ne mæge þare gerædan þonne sceal mon on þære middel æbre blod lætan · þa þe þær ne boþ on micel eaþerum becumaþ.

.XXIII.
Dyrst him ne to wyrcanne on lifer adle hwæt him ne to healdanne.
segments.translation:
XXII.

LEECH BOOK. II. 209

[Instruct the patient] every day to drink a preparation of the herb parsley, and dill, and the seed of marche [alexanders] or its roots with honey; if he hath no fever, augment that with wine. After that, other herbal drinks are appropriate, when the swelling has become an abscess and bursteth, and is becoming more free from soreness, and is passing downwards through the belly [wamb], and the man passeth matter [ratten = pus/putrid matter], reckon then that he may become whole; then must be given to him principally the diuretic drinks, in order that all the morbid matter through the belly and through the kidneys [mie] may be expelled, lest the man should begin to vomit pus through the mouth; and let him abstain somewhat from the bath and from green apples. If however the swelling and the pus mount up to such a degree that it seem to thee that a man may cut into it and let it out, then prepare for him a salve first of pigeon dung [culvers sharn] and the like thereof, and previously bathe the places with fomentations [sousings], with the water, and with the herbs that are written of before. When thou perceivest that the swelling is growing soft and mild, then touch thou it with the cutting iron [lance/scalpel], and cut in a little, and skilfully, just enough that the blood may come out, lest an evil sinus or pouch form inward. Do not let too much blood at one time, lest the sick man become too faint or die; but when thou dost prick or cut it, then have for thyself a linen cloth ready that therewith thou mayst soon bind up the cut; and when thou wilt again let more blood, draw the cloth off and let it run a little at a time until it runs dry; and when the wound is clean, then enlarge it so that the opening or aperture may not be too narrow; but do thou every day syringe through it with a tube,

[Continuing from Old English:]
and apply some poultices thereupon that shall cleanse the wound; if it be within a smaller space, cleanse it with honey and draw it together again. If however the unfelt [insensible] hardening of the limb be of too long standing, then it becometh a fixed swelling [induration] that one cannot treat medically. And one must from the outset apply the stinging remedies mentioned before; let him drink nothing raw [unprepared]; and if the afflicted man have too much blood, then one must before all other remedies let blood from the right arm in the lower vein; if that cannot be reached, then one must let blood in the median vein, those which are not easily accessible there.

XXIII.
What is not to be done in liver disease; what is to be maintained [i.e., what the patient must observe/avoid].

segments.title_inferred: Leechdoms for Liver Disease (Leech Book I, Chapter 215–216)
segments.original_text:
1548. [Some word, perhaps wyn, is omitted by MS.]

LEECH BOOK I [p. 215]

again in the evening. Let him withhold himself also from vinegar, from the bath, from peas, and beans, and navews, and from the things which work in a man a windy vapour. Again, beat or rub up and sift costmary, fenugreek, pepper, hares treadles, equal quantities of all; put a spoon full of this into wine, and give it to him who is without fever, to drink. To him who hath fever, that is mickle heat and fire, give it in warm water; then let him lie on the right side and lay his right hand stretched out under his head, for half an hour. Again, wortdrinks for liver disease: to two little bowls full of juice of clover mingled with a little honey, add a bowl full of heated wine; give this to be drunk for three days, if anything of evil be on the liver, the drink will cure it. Again, give to drink for four days, three little bowls full of the juice of wild mallow, mingled with two such of water; and if fever disease be on him, the wort drink driveth it away. Again, rub together wine, cummin, and honey, give him this to drink. Again, five and twenty bunches of ivy berries, gathered in the month which we hight in Latin Januarius, and in English the second Yule, and of pepper as much, rub these up with the best wine, and heat it; give it to the sick man, after his nights fasting, to drink. A leechdom again for liver disease: dry clean some twigs or stalks of colewort with the flower heads, burn them to ashes, store the ashes, and when occasion is, put a spoon full of the ashes with eleven ground pepper corns into old very clear wine, then heat it, give to be drunk; the next time nine corns, the third time seven. A leechdom again for liver disease: rub small a bunch of bay berries and twenty pepper corns, put them into a bowl full of old wine, and mingle them together with a glowing [iron].

1548. [Some word, perhaps 'wine' (OE: wyn), is omitted by the manuscript.]

LEECH BOOK I [p. 215]

...again in the evening. Let him also abstain from vinegar, from the bath, from peas, beans, and navews [turnip-like roots, Brassica rapa], and from those things which produce a windy [flatulent] vapour in a man. Again, beat or grind up and sift costmary, fenugreek, pepper, and hare's dung [hares treadles], in equal quantities of all; put a spoonful of this into wine and give it to him who is without fever to drink. To him who hath fever — that is, great heat and fire — give it in warm water; then let him lie on the right side and lay his right hand stretched out under his head for half an hour.

Again, herbal drinks [wortdrinks] for liver disease: to two small bowls full of juice of clover mingled with a little honey, add a bowl full of heated wine; give this to be drunk for three days — if anything evil be upon the liver, the drink will cure it.

Again, give to drink for four days three small bowls full of the juice of wild mallow, mingled with two such measures of water; and if fever disease be upon him, the herbal drink will drive it away.

Again, rub together wine, cumin, and honey, and give him this to drink.

Again, five and twenty clusters of ivy berries, gathered in the month which we call in Latin Januarius [January], and in English the second Yule, and of pepper an equal amount — rub these up with the best wine and heat it; give it to the sick man to drink after his night's fasting.

A leechdom again for liver disease: dry and clean some twigs or stalks of colewort [cabbage/kale] with the flower heads, burn them to ashes, store the ashes, and when occasion arises, put a spoonful of the ashes with eleven ground peppercorns into old, very clear wine, then heat it and give to be drunk; the next time use nine corns, the third time seven.

A leechdom again for liver disease: grind small a bunch of bay berries and twenty peppercorns, put them into a bowl full of old wine, and stir them together with a glowing [iron implement]; give [this] to drink and let [the patient] lie quietly.

Again, woodbine [or vine-creeper]: the green twigs wound upward around [...]

segments.title_inferred: Signs and Remedies for Disorders of the Stomach (Wamb), Leech Book II, Chapter XXV
segments.original_text:
XXV.

Here are plain tokens of disorders and sicknesses of the wamb, and how a man shall cure the evil humours of the wamb. When wamb disease is present then the tokens are; the wamb turneth itself, and is fevered, and feeleth sore when the man eateth meat, and prickings, and loss of appetite for meat. The knees are slow, the loins are heavy, and there are spasms between the shoulders, and all the body by piece meal is heavy, and the feet are tardy, and the muscles of the loins are sore; when a man observes these tokens, then the first leechdom is a day's fasting, that with that he may cleanse the wamb, that it may be the lighter. Well, if the disease be still on the increase, let him fast for two days together if his strength allow; if he cannot, then let one give him some lighter food to taste, such as thin broths and the like. Some, for cleansing of the wamb, seethe nettles in water and in wine and in oil; some use the red nettle twice or thrice; some seethe bear or dock in sweetened wine and give to drink; and if the disease increase and the sick man by morning be touched thereby, then seethe for him stronger herbs and add somewhat of pepper thereto. Let one carefully observe what the outgoing [bowel movement] is — whether much or little or whether there be none at all — and let the leech judge thereby how it seemeth to him what should be done. If it be undigested matter within, as yet unburnt [unconsumed], let one draw it out with certain sinking [downward-acting, laxative] foods, and let it not stay settled within the body; and through gathered undigested matter in the wamb or in the small intestine, there is then no outgoing of the stool, but the stool is corrupted and the stomach is turned and broken, and the head is swollen and sore, and the flanks are blown up, and heat is lost, and great thirst, and all bodily strength is weakened and prostrated. One shall treat such a disease, if there be no fever, with cow's milk or goat's, freshly milked, as a drink. It also helpeth if one heat the milk with river-stones that have been burned [heated in fire], or with hot iron, and give it to drink; and if the patient be a young man and it be the right time and he have strength, one should draw blood copiously from the arm, and after three nights let him drink the milk again.
segments.translation:
Chapter XXV.

Here are clear signs of disorders and sicknesses of the stomach [wamb], and how a man shall cure the evil humours of the stomach. When stomach disease is present, the signs are these: the stomach turns itself, is fevered, and feels sore when the man eats food; there are prickings [sharp pains] and loss of appetite. The knees are sluggish, the loins are heavy, there are spasms between the shoulders, and the whole body is gradually heavy; the feet are slow and the muscles of the loins are sore. When a man observes these signs, the first remedy [leechdom] is a day's fasting, so that by it he may cleanse the stomach and make it lighter. Well, if the disease is still increasing, let him fast for two days together if his strength permits; if he cannot do that, then let someone give him a little light food to taste, such as thin broths and the like. Some, for the cleansing of the stomach, seethe nettles in water and in wine and in oil; some use the red nettle two or three times; some seethe bear's wort [or dock] in sweetened wine and give it to drink. And if the disease increases and the sick man is affected by morning, then seethe for him stronger herbs and add some pepper to it. Let one carefully observe what the evacuations are — whether much or little, or whether there are none at all — and let the physician judge thereby what he thinks should be done. If there is undigested matter within, not yet consumed [broken down], let one draw it out with certain downward-acting [laxative] foods, and let it not settle within the body; for through gathered, undigested matter in the stomach or in the small intestine, there is then no passing of stool, but the stool is putrefied and the stomach is disordered and distraught, and the head is swollen and sore, and the flanks are bloated, and vital heat is lost, and there is great thirst, and the strength of the whole body is weakened and laid low. One should treat such a disease, if there is no fever, with cow's milk or goat's milk drunk freshly milked. It also helps if one heats the milk with river-stones burned [fire-heated] or with hot iron, and gives it to drink. And if the patient is a young man, and the time is right, and he has strength, one should draw blood copiously from the arm, and after three nights let him drink the milk again.

segments.title_inferred: On Poor Digestion, Bad Humours, and Their Treatment by Emesis and Diet
segments.original_text:
XXIX.

In case a "mans" meat doth not well digest, and turneth to evil humour and to excrement, it is good for those "men" that "they" should spew, if it be not too uneasy to "him," irritate him to spew by a wort drink. If there be extreme need that he may be able to spew before meat, let him manage that with sweetened wine. Let him flee the meats which work him mucus, and burnings, and heat in his inside, and which too readily digest: let him take those which work a good juice, and make the wamb nesh. At whiles it is good for him that one should give him light wort drinks, such as are aloes well prepared. The humour, if one doth not get rid of it, worketh not easily cured diseases, that is to say, foot pain, joint pain, loins pain; and often a strong fever cometh on the men who have that disease.
segments.translation:
XXIX.

In the case where a man's food does not digest well, and turns to bad humour [corrupt bodily fluid] and to waste matter, it is beneficial for such men that they should vomit; if this is not too difficult for him, induce him to vomit by means of a herbal [wort] drink. If there is urgent need for him to be able to vomit before eating, let him accomplish this with sweetened wine. Let him avoid the foods which produce in him mucus, burning sensations, and heat in his belly, and those which digest too quickly; let him take instead those which produce a good juice [chyme] and make the stomach [wamb] soft [nesh]. At times it is good for him that one should give him light herbal drinks, such as well-prepared aloes. The humour [corrupt fluid], if one does not purge it, produces diseases not easily cured — namely, pain in the feet, pain in the joints, and pain in the loins; and often a severe fever comes upon the men who suffer from this condition.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::XXIX.   In  case  a  "mans"  meat  doth  not  well

segments.title_inferred: On the Health of the Belly: Remedies for Digestive and Urinary Complaints
segments.original_text: XXX. If thou wish that thy wamb be always sound, then shalt thou thus treat it, if thou wilt. Look to it every day that thy fæcal discharge, and thy mie, be of sound aspect as right is. If the mie be little, seethe marche and fennel, work a good broth, or seethe juice of . . . and of other sweet worts. If the fæcal discharge be too little, take the wort which in southern lands hight turpentine tree, as much of it as the size of an olive; give it the sick when he will go to bed. These worts are also very good for that, and more: mealpe and brassica and the like sodden [boiled] with young swine's flesh; take that broth; and also dead nettle sodden in water and pressed to drink; and also elder's leaf and the broth in the same wise. Some drip leaves when one will go to sleep, such as the size of three beans, to swallow each day; and brews like these, that strengthen — if there be need, they are to be given — chiefly in early spring before the evil humour that is gathered in winter pour itself throughout the other limbs. Many men care not then nor heed what comes from those evil humours: either the half-dead disease [palsy], or wild madness, or the painful disorder that in southern tongue is called tetanus, or head-ailment, or erysipelas [St. Anthony's fire]. Therefore one must first cleanse the evil humours away before the evils come and grow in winter and spread through the limbs. For belly-ache and pain: smear or beat hemlock [?] a bowl-full, and two cups of vinegar, boil together, give to drink fasting to the sick man. Again, lay bruised brooklime chosen upon the navel; it soon stills it. Again, a little dill seed rubbed in water, give to drink. For belly-ache and for inner pain: when for great cold the belly be unruled [disordered], do those things to it that are written above. If there be then a turning of crudity [undigested matter] or a cutting pain, take three drops of laurel berries rubbed, and of cumin, and of wild parsley separate spoonsful, and twenty corns of pepper; rub all together and dry three times in a warm belly; after that take water, rub dill in it, and give those things warm to drink, until the pain be stilled. For the same, take lily sodden in goat's milk; apply [foment] in the southern [manner].
segments.translation: Chapter XXX. If you wish that your belly [wamb] be always sound, you shall treat it thus, if you will. See to it every day that your fæcal discharge and your urine [mie] are of sound appearance, as is proper. If the urine be scanty, boil smallage [marche] and fennel, make a good broth, or boil the juice of [lacuna] and of other sweet herbs [worts]. If the fæcal discharge be too scanty, take the herb which in southern lands is called the turpentine tree, as much as the size of an olive, and give it to the sick person when he wishes to go to bed. These herbs are also very good for that purpose, and more: mercury-herb [mealpe] and brassica [cabbage/kale] and the like, boiled together with the flesh of a young pig; let the patient drink that broth; and also dead nettle boiled in water and pressed out to drink; and also elder leaves and the broth prepared in the same manner. Some drip leaves when one wishes to go to sleep — of such a quantity as three beans — to be swallowed each day; and drinks similar to these, which strengthen the body — if there be need, they are to be given — especially in early spring before the evil humour gathered in winter pours itself throughout the other limbs. Many men of former times did not care nor do they now heed what comes of those evil humours: either the half-death disease [palsy/hemiplegia], or raving madness, or the painful disorder that in the southern tongue is called tetanus, or head-ailment, or erysipelas. Therefore one must first cleanse away the evil humours before the evils come and grow in winter and spread through the limbs. For belly-ache and pain: smear [or beat] hemlock [?] a bowl-full, and two cups of vinegar, boil together, give to the sick man to drink fasting. Again, lay bruised brooklime, well chosen, upon the navel; it soon stills the pain. Again, a little dill seed rubbed [ground] into water, give to drink. For belly-ache and for inner pain: when from great cold the belly is disordered [unruled], do those things to it that are written above. If there be then a churning of undigested matter or a sharp cutting pain, take three drops of laurel berries rubbed, and of cumin, and of wild parsley, each a separate spoonful, and twenty grains of pepper; rub all together, and let it warm three times in a warm belly [i.e., apply with warmth]; after that, take water, rub dill into it, and give those things warm to drink until the pain is stilled. For the same ailment, take lily boiled in goat's milk and apply it in the southern fashion.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::XXX.   If  thou  wish  that  thy  wamb  be  always

segments.title_inferred: Remedies for Stomach Sickness and Bowel Complaints
segments.original_text: 2. For wamb sickness seethe rue in oil, and let the sick swallow it in oil. Again, give him to eat a wild pigeon sodden in vinegar and in water. For wamb sickness, again, let him chew leaves of laurel, and swallow the juice, and let him lay the leaves on his navel. Again, give melted harts marrow in hot water to drink. For moderating the action of the wamb; take beet, delve it up and shake the mould off, do not wash it, but seethe and boil it in a kettle so long, that it be all sodden to pieces, and run thick, then add a little salt, and of honey five spoon measures, of oil one spoon measure, give the man a bowl full. Again, give to the sick to eat, separate, the top of a sodden leek, having a head to it. Again, give him to eat some seed of the red nettle on bread. Again, give him to drink juice of mulberries. Again, let him eat after his night's fasting plum fruits. Again, give him to drink elder rind beaten, as much as may weigh a penny, in a bowl full of cold water.
segments.translation: 2. For stomach [wamb] sickness: boil [seethe] rue in oil, and let the sick person swallow it in the oil. Again, give him to eat a wild pigeon boiled [sodden] in vinegar and water. For stomach sickness, again, let him chew leaves of laurel and swallow the juice, and let him lay the leaves upon his navel. Again, give melted hart's [male deer's] marrow dissolved in hot water to drink. For regulating [moderating] the action of the stomach [wamb]: take beet, dig it up and shake the earth off, do not wash it, but boil it in a kettle for so long that it is entirely dissolved to pieces and becomes thick, then add a little salt, five spoon-measures of honey, and one spoon-measure of oil; give the man a bowl full. Again, give the sick person to eat, separately, the top of a boiled leek that has a head on it. Again, give him to eat some seed of the red nettle on bread. Again, give him to drink juice of mulberries. Again, let him eat plum fruits after his overnight fast. Again, give him to drink beaten elder rind, as much as weighs a penny [a small unit of weight], in a bowl full of cold water.

segments.title_inferred: On diseases of the belly, colon, and small intestines: symptoms and early treatment
segments.original_text:
XXXI. Of wamb sicknesses, and of tokens in the colon and in the small guts. There is a kind of that ilk disease in the wamb, and in the colon, and small guts, of which this will be for a token; that the sick suffer immoderate thirst and loss of appetite for meat, and often they have a flux with a mingled faecal discharge, at whiles hard, at whiles white, at whiles they discharge often in the day and then little at a time, at whiles once and then much; at whiles a desire is upon them to go to stool and to cast the burthen from them, and gladly would they attend to it, but they are not able, they have not the power of digestion, and they drop blood, very much like a broken vessel. Of their hue, or complexion, and of the navel, and of the dorsal muscles, and of the back gut or rectum, and of the lower belly, and the milt, and the share; they are [horr-]

Þa wambseocan men þropiaþ on þam bæcþearme ⁊ on þam niþerran hwrife ⁊ losað him sona sio scern ⁊ cele þropiaþ ⁊ slæp oftogen ⁊ imho ⁊ tiþð innan þone for ⁊ on þæt smæl þearm.

XXXII. Þisse adle fruman mon mæg yfelice gelacnian · on þa ilcan wisan þe þa utyrnendan · ⁊ æfter uneaþ · gif hio biþ unwislice to lange forlæten. On fruman mon sceal dæg oþþe II. togeadere gewæscan ⁊ beþan þa breost mid wine · ⁊ mid ele ⁊ wyrcean onlegenan of rosan ⁊ berenum melpe wiþ win gemengeb ⁊ on hunige gesoben ⁊ mid ele on mortere gesamnod lege ofer þa sceare of þone nafolan ⁊ ofer þa lendeno oþ þone bæcþearm ⁊ þær hit sar lie · læt him blod ryne ⁊ þæt sete þær on oþþe horn ⁊ teo þæt blod ut · ⁊ smere mid ele fol. 87b. ⁊ bewreoh hine wearme for þon þe cile biþ swæðe adle
segments.translation:
XXXI. On diseases of the belly [wamb], and on symptoms in the colon and in the small intestines. There is a certain type of disease of this kind in the belly, and in the colon, and in the small intestines, of which the token [sign/symptom] shall be this: that the sick suffer immoderate thirst and loss of appetite for food, and often they have a flux [diarrhoea] with a mixed faecal discharge — sometimes hard, sometimes white; sometimes they discharge frequently during the day but only a little at a time, sometimes once only but then a great deal; sometimes they have an urge to go to stool and to expel the burden, and they would gladly do so, but they are unable — they have no power of digestion — and they pass blood, very much as from a broken vessel.

As to their colour [complexion], and the navel, and the dorsal muscles, and the back gut [rectum], and the lower belly, and the spleen [milt], and the groin [share]: they become pallid [horribly discoloured], and the whole body wastes; and the sick person has no control over foul odour; and the pain is on the right side, half on the groin, and the belly is much constricted; and also from the navel as far as the spleen, and on the left rib-cartilage; and it reaches the rectum and also the lower gut; and the loins are girt about with great pain. Unwise physicians think it to be a kidney disease [lenben adl] or spleen-ache [miltre wærc], but it is not so. Those who are sick in the kidneys pass blood and gravel in urine, whereas those who have spleen-ache: the spleen presses upon them and becomes hardened on the left side of the flank. Those who are belly-sick suffer in the rectum and in the lower abdomen, and quickly lose control of their bowels, and suffer cold, and sleep is drawn away from them, and they have hiccup, and wind gathers inwardly — in the stomach and in the small intestine.

XXXII. At the beginning this disease may be treated with difficulty in the same manner as dysentery [the outward-running], and afterwards with effort, if it has been unwisely left too long. At the outset one should, for a day or two together, wash and foment the chest with wine and with oil; and make a poultice of roses and barley meal mixed with wine and boiled in honey, and gathered together with oil in a mortar; lay it over the groin from the navel, and over the loins as far as the rectum, and wherever there is pain. Let blood flow from him, and place a cupping vessel [sete] thereon, or a horn, and draw the blood out; and anoint with oil, and wrap him up warmly, for cold is a great aggravator of the disease.
segments._label: cockayne_v2::XXXI.   Of  wamb  sicknesses,  and  of  tokens  in

segments.title_inferred: Mangalya — the auspicious opening and purpose of medicine
segments.original_text: प्रयोजनं चास्य स्वस्थस्य स्वास्थ्यरक्षणमातुरस्य विकारप्रशमनं च।
segments.translation: The purpose of this [Ayurveda] is the maintenance of health in the healthy and the alleviation of disease in the diseased. Charaka opens with this foundational statement establishing Ayurveda's dual mission: preventive and curative medicine. The text positions itself as a complete system — not merely a collection of remedies but a science of life (Ayur = life, Veda = knowledge/science).

segments.title_inferred: Shadvirechanashatashritiya — the 500 purgative plants
segments.original_text: विरेचनोपगानां शतानि पञ्च — पिप्पली, पिप्पलीमूल, चव्य, चित्रक, शृङ्गवेर...
segments.translation: The five hundred [groups of] purgative plants — long pepper, long pepper root, chavya, chitraka, dried ginger root... Charaka Chapter 4 (Shadvirechanashatashritiya) is one of the most important pharmacological chapters in classical Ayurveda, listing plants in 50 groups of 10 by therapeutic action. The groups cover: purgatives (virechana), emetics (vamana), nasal medications (nasya), enemas (basti), and external applications (lepa).

segments.title_inferred: Yajjah Purushiya — the plant kingdom as medicine
segments.original_text: न तत् किञ्चिद् द्रव्यं यद् भेषजं न स्यात्।
segments.translation: There is no substance in the world that cannot be used as a medicine. This foundational Ayurvedic principle — that everything in the natural world has potential therapeutic application — underpins the entire Charaka pharmacopoeia. The implication is that the physician's task is knowledge, not scarcity: every plant, mineral, and animal product is potentially medicinal when properly understood and applied.

segments.title_inferred: Annapanavidhi — therapeutic foods and their properties
segments.original_text: शालयः षष्टिकाश्चैव मुद्गाः सैन्धवमेव च। आमलकी च जीवन्ती च मधुरश्च रसः स्मृतः।
segments.translation: Shali rice (red rice), shashtika rice (60-day rice), green mung beans, rock salt, amalaki (Indian gooseberry), and jivanti — these are the most beneficial foods. The most important Ayurvedic chapter on food-as-medicine. Shali and shashtika rice are consistently ranked as the most therapeutic grains. Mung beans are the most easily digestible legume. Amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica) appears here as a food with extraordinary medicinal properties — one of the three fruits in Triphala and documented for the highest Vitamin C content of any fruit.

segments.title_inferred: Phala Varga — therapeutic fruits
segments.original_text: आमलकानां श्रेष्ठतमं। दाडिमं हृद्यं पाचनं रोचनं।
segments.translation: Among fruits, amalaki is the foremost. Pomegranate (dadima) is cardiotonic, digestive, and appetizing. Charaka's fruit chapter documents the therapeutic properties of the major fruits used in Ayurvedic medicine. Amalaki is ranked supreme among all fruits — later research has confirmed it has one of the highest Vitamin C concentrations of any food. Pomegranate for cardiac conditions connects to the Greek/Roman, Persian, and Tibetan documentation of pomegranate for the same indication.

segments.title_inferred: Harita Varga — therapeutic vegetables and herbs
segments.original_text: शाकानां वास्तूकं श्रेष्ठम्। पटोलं कटुतिक्तं च ज्वरघ्नं।
segments.translation: Among vegetables, vastuka (bathua/goosefoot) is foremost. Patola (pointed gourd) is bitter and pungent, and destroys fever. The Ayurvedic vegetable classification assigns therapeutic primacy to specific plants. Patola for fever connects to the cross-tradition fever treatment documentation. Bitter gourd (karela/bitter melon) is documented extensively — connecting to the Philippine, Tibetan, and West African documentation in the existing archive.

segments.title_inferred: Rasayana — rejuvenation therapy and life extension
segments.original_text: लभते ह्यायुर्मेधां बलं आरोग्यं सुखं च। रसायनं प्रयोक्तव्यं...
segments.translation: One obtains longevity, memory, strength, freedom from disease, and happiness [through rasayana]. Rasayana therapy should be administered... Rasayana (literally 'path of rasa/essence') is Ayurveda's systematic anti-aging and rejuvenation system — the most sophisticated traditional longevity medicine in any world tradition. The primary rasayana plants include: Ashwagandha, Shatavari, Brahmi, Guduchi, and the Triphala combination. All are now extensively studied for adaptogenic and anti-aging properties.

segments.title_inferred: Ashwagandha Rasayana — the premier rejuvenation plant
segments.original_text: अश्वगन्धा बलकरी वृष्या रसायनी। श्रमहारिणी।
segments.translation: Ashwagandha gives strength, is aphrodisiac, is a rasayana, and removes fatigue. The Charaka Samhita establishes ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) as the premier rasayana plant — superior to others for building strength, endurance, and sexual vitality. Specifically recommended for emaciation, general debility, weakness in old age, and for those exhausted by physical labor. The preparation: ashwagandha root powder in milk with ghee and honey.
segments.preparation: Root powder in warm milk with ghee and honey; taken at bedtime
segments.condition: Debility, fatigue, emaciation, weakness, sexual dysfunction, aging

segments.title_inferred: Brahmi Rasayana — cognition and memory
segments.original_text: ब्राह्मी मेध्या स्मृतिकरी आयुष्या रसायनी।
segments.translation: Brahmi enhances intelligence, improves memory, is longevity-promoting, and is a rasayana. Bacopa monnieri (Brahmi) is Ayurveda's primary cognitive medicine — documented by Charaka for memory, learning, intelligence, and longevity. The specific claim that brahmi improves memory in children and reverses cognitive decline in older patients is among the most precisely stated therapeutic claims in the Charaka Samhita. Multiple modern RCTs have since validated these claims.
segments.preparation: Brahmi ghrita (brahmi-infused clarified butter); also brahmi juice with honey
segments.condition: Poor memory, cognitive decline, epilepsy, anxiety, insanity

segments.title_inferred: Jvarachikitsa — fever treatment
segments.original_text: सर्वरोगाणाम् अग्रणी ज्वरः। गुडूची ज्वरघ्नी।
segments.translation: Fever is foremost among all diseases. Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) destroys fever. The Charaka fever chapter documents a hierarchy of fever treatment — from simple fasting and rest to complex multi-herb decoctions. Guduchi is the primary anti-fever plant. The pippali (long pepper) + ginger + black pepper combination (Trikatu) is documented for digestive fever. Neem (nimba) is documented for malarial fever types — one of the earliest documented antimalarial treatments.
segments.preparation: Guduchi decoction; Trikatu (long pepper + ginger + black pepper) for digestive fever
segments.condition: Fever (all types), malarial fever, digestive fever, fever with ama (toxins)

segments.title_inferred: Gulma Chikitsa — abdominal tumors and digestive masses
segments.original_text: हिङ्ग्वादयः गुल्मघ्नाः। अग्निमान्द्यहरा वायुलोमाः।
segments.translation: Asafoetida (hingu) and others destroy abdominal masses. They overcome sluggish digestion and promote downward movement of wind. Asafoetida (Ferula asafoetida) is documented as the primary medicine for gulma — abdominal masses, which may correspond to tumors, cysts, or severe digestive disorders. The antispasmodic and carminative properties of asafoetida are well-established. This chapter also documents long pepper for digestive complaints and haritaki for constipation-related gulma.
segments.preparation: Asafoetida compound preparations in sesame oil; taken after meals
segments.condition: Abdominal masses, digestive disorders, flatulence, constipation

segments.title_inferred: Kushtha Chikitsa — skin disease treatment
segments.original_text: खदिरः कुष्ठघ्नः श्रेष्ठः। तुम्बुरु शिरीषश्च।
segments.translation: Khadira (Acacia catechu) is the foremost destroyer of skin diseases. Tumburu (Zanthoxylum) and shirisha (Albizia lebbeck) also [treat skin diseases]. The Charaka skin disease chapter is remarkable for its specificity — distinguishing 18 types of kushtha (skin conditions ranging from psoriasis to leprosy) and prescribing specific plants for each. Khadira's pharmacological content — catechin and epicatechin — has now been confirmed as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial.
segments.preparation: Khadira bark decoction; Panchatikta ghrita (five bitter plants in ghee)
segments.condition: Psoriasis, leprosy, eczema, vitiligo, all skin conditions (18 types of kushtha)

segments.title_inferred: Rajayakshma Chikitsa — tuberculosis and wasting diseases
segments.original_text: अश्वगन्धा बलादीनां रसायनानि राजयक्ष्मणि। पिप्पली क्षयकासघ्नी।
segments.translation: Ashwagandha and bala and other rasayanas [are used] in tuberculosis/wasting disease. Long pepper destroys wasting cough. Rajayakshma — the 'King of Diseases' — corresponds primarily to tuberculosis and other wasting conditions. The treatment strategy is remarkable: Charaka prescribes nutritive rasayana plants (ashwagandha, bala, shatavari) to rebuild the patient while using antimicrobial plants (long pepper, giloy) to fight the infection. This dual strategy — nutritive support + antimicrobial — anticipates modern TB treatment principles.
segments.preparation: Ashwagandha in milk; Chyavanaprasha (multi-herb amalaki-based preparation)
segments.condition: Tuberculosis, wasting diseases, chronic cough, emaciation

segments.title_inferred: Apasmara Chikitsa — epilepsy treatment
segments.original_text: ब्राह्म्याः सर्पिर्मेध्यं। वचा संज्ञाप्रदा।
segments.translation: Brahmi ghrita (brahmi in ghee) is the cognitive treatment [for epilepsy]. Vacha (Acorus calamus) restores consciousness. The Charaka epilepsy chapter is one of the most pharmacologically interesting — brahmi (Bacopa) and vacha (Acorus) are both documented for neurological conditions. Vacha (calamus root) contains asarone — a compound with documented anticonvulsant activity. The combination of these plants in Saraswatarishta and other classical preparations is still used in Ayurvedic practice today.
segments.preparation: Brahmi ghrita; Saraswatarishta (fermented preparation with brahmi, vacha, ashwagandha)
segments.condition: Epilepsy, psychosis, cognitive disorders, loss of consciousness

segments.title_inferred: Unmada Chikitsa — psychiatric medicine
segments.original_text: राजमाषादिं गणं जीवनीयम्। मनस्कान्तिकरं ब्राह्मी।
segments.translation: The rajamasha group promotes life. Brahmi brings mental clarity and lustre to the mind. The Charaka psychiatric chapter documents a sophisticated understanding of mental illness — distinguishing endogenous (nija) from exogenous (agantuja) causes, roughly corresponding to endogenous psychiatric disorders vs. infectious/toxic conditions. Plants for psychiatric conditions: brahmi, shankhapushpi, jatamansi (Nardostachys). Jatamansi contains valerianol — related to the active compound in valerian, establishing a cross-tradition connection for the same neurological indication.
segments.preparation: Multi-herb preparations with brahmi and shankhapushpi in ghee base
segments.condition: Psychosis, mania, depression, anxiety, cognitive disorders

segments.title_inferred: Vatarakta Chikitsa — gout treatment
segments.original_text: गुडूची वातरक्ते श्रेष्ठा। निम्बं च शोधनम्।
segments.translation: Guduchi is foremost in gout. Neem is purifying [for gout]. The Charaka gout chapter documents guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) as the primary treatment — an observation now validated by in vitro studies showing guduchi inhibits xanthine oxidase, the same enzyme target as the pharmaceutical drug allopurinol. This is one of the most precise mechanistic convergences in the archive between traditional Ayurvedic prescription and modern pharmacological target identification.
segments.preparation: Guduchi sattva (guduchi starch extract); Guduchi decoction
segments.condition: Gout (vatarakta), joint inflammation, uric acid conditions

segments.title_inferred: Dvisaptiya — 37 plants with the most extensive therapeutic applications
segments.original_text: बृहत्पञ्चमूलं मेद्यम् बल्यं वृष्यं रसायनम्। श्वासकासहरं चैव...
segments.translation: The Brihat Panchamula (five great roots — bilva, shyonak, gambhari, patala, agnimanth) are nourishing, strength-giving, aphrodisiac, rasayana, and destroy asthma and cough. This chapter documents the most important Ayurvedic plant groups: Dashamula (ten roots), Panchatikta (five bitters), and the various Ganas (groups) that form the basis of classical compound preparations. The Dashamula combination (ten roots including bilva, shyonak, and five others) is documented for respiratory, inflammatory, and neurological conditions — one of the most prescribed compound preparations in Ayurveda.

segments.title_inferred: Kalpana — pharmaceutical preparation methods
segments.original_text: स्वरसः कल्कश्च श्रितं हिमः फाण्टश्च। पञ्चविधः कषायः।
segments.translation: Fresh juice (svarasa), paste (kalka), decoction (kwatha/shrita), cold infusion (hima), hot infusion (phanta) — these are the five types of kashayas (medicinal preparations). Charaka's pharmaceutical chapter defines the five primary methods of plant medicine preparation that form the basis of all Ayurvedic pharmacy. This classification is more systematic than any European pharmaceutical tradition before the 18th century — distinguishing preparations by extraction method, temperature, and concentration.

segments.title_inferred: Vamana Dravya — emetic plants for panchakarma
segments.original_text: मदनफलं वमनोत्तमम्। इक्ष्वाकु च यष्टीमधुकं च।
segments.translation: Madana fruit (Randia dumetorum) is the foremost emetic. Ikshvaku (bottle gourd) and licorice root (yashtimadhu) also [serve as emetics]. The Vamana chapter documents the emetic plants used in panchakarma — the Ayurvedic therapeutic purification system. Licorice root (yashtimadhu / Glycyrrhiza glabra) appears here as an emetic adjuvant — the same plant documented in Persian, Greek/Roman, and Egyptian traditions for respiratory and digestive conditions. This represents convergence for licorice across five independent traditions.

segments.title_inferred: Virechana Dravya — purgative plants for panchakarma
segments.original_text: त्रिवृत् विरेचनानामुत्तमा। दन्ती दन्तीमूलञ्च।
segments.translation: Trivrit (Operculina turpethum) is the foremost of purgatives. Danti (Baliospermum montanum) and the root of danti also [serve as purgatives]. The purgative chapter documents 29 plants for the virechana stage of panchakarma. Trivrit is used for the same conditions as senna and cascara in other traditions — a convergence for laxative/purgative plants across all major medical traditions.

segments.title_inferred: Triphala — the three fruits, Ayurveda's most important compound
segments.original_text: आमलकी विभीतकं हरीतकी च — त्रयो मध्यस्था। त्रिफला रसायनी।
segments.translation: Amalaki (Phyllanthus emblica), vibhitaka (Terminalia bellirica), and haritaki (Terminalia chebula) — these three are moderate in nature. Triphala is a rasayana. Triphala — the combination of three fruits — is the single most prescribed compound in Ayurvedic medicine. Charaka documents it for: constipation, eye conditions, liver conditions, skin diseases, diabetes, obesity, and as a general rasayana. All three fruits are individually significant — haritaki is Ayurveda's single most important medicinal plant (documented in 49 chapters of Charaka).

segments.title_inferred: Shatavari — Ayurvedic women's medicine
segments.original_text: शतावरी बल्या वृष्या रसायनी स्तन्यजननी।
segments.translation: Shatavari gives strength, is aphrodisiac, is a rasayana, and promotes milk production. Asparagus racemosus (shatavari — literally 'who has a hundred husbands') is Ayurveda's primary women's medicine — documented by Charaka for: lactation support, menstrual regulation, fertility, menopausal symptoms, and as a general women's tonic. Multiple modern RCTs have confirmed lactation-promoting activity (galactagogue effect).
segments.preparation: Root powder in warm milk; shatavari ghrita; shatavari kalpa
segments.condition: Lactation, menstrual disorders, infertility, menopause, general women's health

segments.title_inferred: Nimba (Neem) — the universal purifier
segments.original_text: निम्बः सर्वज्वरघ्नः। कुष्ठघ्नः। कृमिघ्नः।
segments.translation: Neem destroys all fevers. It destroys skin diseases. It destroys worms/parasites. Neem (Azadirachta indica) is documented throughout Charaka for an extraordinary range of conditions — fever, skin diseases, parasites, diabetes, wound healing, and as a general antimicrobial. The three-fold claim (anti-fever, anti-skin disease, anti-parasitic) is directly validated by modern research: nimbidin (anti-inflammatory), azadirachtin (antiparasitic — the most studied natural pesticide), and gedunin (antimalarial).
segments.preparation: Neem leaf decoction; neem oil topically; neem bark decoction
segments.condition: Fever (all types), skin diseases, intestinal parasites, diabetes, wound infection

segments.title_inferred: Guduchi — the immunity plant
segments.original_text: गुडूची सर्वज्वरघ्नी। रसायनी। मेध्या।
segments.translation: Guduchi destroys all fevers. It is a rasayana. It enhances intelligence. Tinospora cordifolia (guduchi/giloy) is documented in Charaka for an exceptional range of conditions. Modern research has confirmed: (1) immunomodulatory activity — stimulates macrophage activity; (2) xanthine oxidase inhibition for gout; (3) anti-malarial activity; (4) hepatoprotective effects. During COVID-19, guduchi received significant attention in India as a potential immune support supplement.
segments.preparation: Guduchi sattva (starch extract); guduchi decoction; guduchi ghrita
segments.condition: Fever, gout, liver conditions, immunity, diabetes, general health

segments.title_inferred: Pippali (Long Pepper) — Ayurveda's most prescribed spice-medicine
segments.original_text: पिप्पली कासश्वासहरी। दीपनी। रसायनी।
segments.translation: Long pepper destroys cough and asthma. It kindles digestive fire. It is a rasayana. Pippali (Piper longum) is arguably the most frequently mentioned plant in the Charaka Samhita — appearing in Trikatu (with ginger and black pepper), in respiratory formulas, digestive preparations, and rasayana compounds. Piperine in long pepper is the same bioavailability-enhancing compound as in black pepper but at higher concentration. Piperlongumine — a compound unique to long pepper — shows documented anticancer activity.
segments.preparation: Trikatu (long pepper + ginger + black pepper); pippali rasayana in honey and ghee
segments.condition: Asthma, chronic cough, poor digestion, obesity, respiratory infections

segments.title_inferred: Matrashitiya: On the Proper Quantity of Food and Its Therapeutic Effects
segments.original_text: Matrashitiya — On the quantity of food and its effects. Chapter 5 of Sutrasthana documents the fundamental Ayurvedic principle of appropriate quantity: the proper measure of food is that which, without oppressing the vital breath, is digested in due time and does not harm the body. Specific plant-based foods and their optimal quantities: shali rice (best grain, light to digest), mung beans (best legume), rainwater (best water), ghee (best fat — Ayurveda's primary medicinal vehicle), honey (best sweet — medicinal at low doses). The chapter establishes the concept of pathya (dietary regimen) and apathya (contraindicated foods) as a therapeutic system parallel to pharmacological treatment. The most important principle: even the best medicine is harmful if taken in incorrect quantity at incorrect time. Plants mentioned: Shigru (Moringa oleifera — documented as nutritive vegetable), Vastuka (Chenopodium — best vegetable), Patola (pointed gourd — fever vegetable).
segments.translation: Chapter Five of the Sutrasthana, entitled Matrashitiya ('On the Measure of Food'), sets forth the foundational Ayurvedic doctrine of appropriate quantity [matra] as it applies to food and medicine alike. The proper measure of food is defined as that quantity which, without suppressing or obstructing the vital breath [prana], undergoes complete digestion within the expected time and produces no harm to the body or its constituent elements [dhatus]. Among grains, shali rice is held to be the finest, being light in quality and easy to digest. Among legumes, mung bean [mudga] is considered best. Among waters, rainwater [divyambhas, 'celestial water'] is regarded as purest and most wholesome. Among fats, ghee [ghrita] is pre-eminent and serves as the principal vehicle [anupana] for medicinal preparations throughout Ayurvedic therapeutics. Among sweet substances, honey [madhu] is foremost, though it is explicitly noted to possess medicinal properties only when taken in small and appropriate doses; in excess it becomes harmful. The chapter further elaborates the twin concepts of pathya [indicated or wholesome dietary regimen] and apathya [contraindicated or unwholesome foods], establishing these as a systematic therapeutic framework that operates in parallel with, and is inseparable from, pharmacological treatment. The overarching and most critical principle affirmed here is that even the finest medicine or the most wholesome food becomes injurious when consumed in an incorrect quantity or at an inappropriate time. Among the plant-based foods specifically discussed: Shigru [Moringa oleifera, drumstick tree] is recorded as a nutritive vegetable of therapeutic value; Vastuka [Chenopodium album, white goosefoot] is identified as the best among leafy vegetables; and Patola [pointed gourd, Trichosanthes dioica] is noted for its utility in febrile conditions.

segments.title_inferred: Sneha Adhyaya: Chapter on Oleation Therapy and Medicated Ghee Preparations
segments.original_text: Sneha Adhyaya — Chapter on oleation therapy and medicinal ghee preparations. The four primary oleation substances in Ayurveda: (1) Ghrita (clarified butter) — best for vata disorders, intellectual conditions, complexion; prepared with plant decoctions as medicated ghee; (2) Taila (sesame oil) — best for skin and strength; (3) Vasa (muscle fat) — for certain conditions; (4) Majja (bone marrow) — for extreme vata conditions. Major medicated ghee preparations: Brahmi ghrita (brahmi/Bacopa + ghee — primary cognitive medicine), Ashwagandha ghrita, Triphala ghrita (three fruits + ghee — eye and general conditions), Pancha tikta ghrita (five bitter plants + ghee — skin diseases). The ghee medium is not merely a vehicle — it enhances bioavailability of fat-soluble compounds and penetrates the blood-brain barrier. Modern pharmacology validates ghee as a lipid carrier for lipophilic plant compounds including curcumin and the withanolides of ashwagandha.
segments.translation: Chapter on Oleation [Sneha Adhyaya]: This chapter concerns oleation therapy [snehana] and the preparation of medicinal clarified butter [ghrita]. Four primary oleating substances [sneha dravyas] are enumerated in Ayurvedic practice: (1) Ghrita [clarified butter / ghee] — held to be the most efficacious for disorders of the vata humor, for conditions affecting the intellect, and for improvement of complexion; it is prepared as medicated ghee by processing it with plant decoctions [kashaya] and other herbal materials; (2) Taila [sesame oil, Sesamum indicum] — considered best for the maintenance of skin integrity and bodily strength; (3) Vasa [rendered muscle fat / intramuscular fat] — indicated for certain specified conditions; (4) Majja [bone marrow] — prescribed for severe or extreme manifestations of vata imbalance. Principal named medicated ghee [ghrita] formulations described herein include: Brahmi Ghrita, combining brahmi [Bacopa monnieri] with ghee, serving as the primary classical medicine for cognitive function and memory; Ashwagandha Ghrita, combining ashwagandha [Withania somnifera] with ghee, indicated for debility and rejuvenation; Triphala Ghrita, combining the three fruits [Terminalia chebula, Terminalia bellirica, and Phyllanthus emblica] with ghee, indicated for disorders of the eye and for general systemic conditions; and Pancha Tikta Ghrita, combining five bitter [tikta] plants with ghee, indicated principally for diseases of the skin [kushtha]. It is further noted that the ghee medium functions not merely as a passive vehicle [anupana] but actively enhances the bioavailability of fat-soluble constituents and facilitates their penetration of the blood-brain barrier. Contemporary pharmacological research is cited in support of ghee as a lipid-based delivery system for lipophilic plant compounds, including curcumin [from Curcuma longa] and the withanolides characteristic of ashwagandha [Withania somnifera].

segments.title_inferred: The Six Tastes (Shad Rasa) and Their Therapeutic Implications — Atreyabhadrakapiya
segments.original_text: Atreyabhadrakapiya — The fundamental pharmacological principles of Charaka. The six tastes (Shad Rasa) and their therapeutic implications: Madhura (sweet — nourishing, building, calming); Amla (sour — digestive, carminative); Lavana (salty — liquefying, laxative); Katu (pungent — stimulating, fat-burning); Tikta (bitter — detoxifying, anti-parasitic, anti-infective); Kashaya (astringent — healing, drying, hemostatic). The taste → action relationship is the foundational Ayurvedic pharmacological principle — 400+ years before Dioscorides' hot/cold/dry/moist classification system. Specific bitter-tasting plants for infection and parasites: Neem (nimba), Guduchi (tinospora), Chirayata (Swertia), Patola, Turmeric (haridra). The bitter taste classification explains why these plants were empirically identified as antimicrobial across multiple traditions: bitter compounds are often pharmacologically active as antimicrobials, antipyretics, and anti-parasitic agents.
segments.translation: This section, attributed to the Atreyabhadrakapiya chapter, sets forth the foundational pharmacological principles of the Charaka system. The six tastes [Shad Rasa] and their therapeutic properties are enumerated as follows: Madhura [sweet] — nourishing, tissue-building, and calming in action; Amla [sour] — promotes digestion and relieves flatulence [carminative]; Lavana [salty] — liquefies and loosens, acting as a laxative; Katu [pungent] — stimulating and conducive to the reduction of fat; Tikta [bitter] — detoxifying, anti-parasitic, and anti-infective; Kashaya [astringent] — promotes healing, dries excess moisture, and arrests bleeding [hemostatic]. The principle that taste directly governs pharmacological action constitutes the foundational doctrine of Ayurvedic materia medica, antedating by more than four centuries the Galenic hot/cold/dry/moist classification system attributed to Dioscorides. Among the bitter-tasting [Tikta] plants specifically indicated for infectious conditions and parasitic infestation, the following are enumerated: Nimba [Neem], Guduchi [Tinospora], Chirayata [Swertia], Patola, and Haridra [Turmeric]. The classification of these substances under the bitter taste category provides a systematic explanation for their empirically established efficacy as antimicrobial, antipyretic, and anti-parasitic agents across multiple medical traditions — an observation consistent with the known pharmacological activity of bitter secondary metabolites such as alkaloids, iridoids, and terpenoids.

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Thirst and Urinary Disorders (Prameha / Madhumeha) — Antidiabetic Materia Medica
segments.original_text: Trishna Chikitsa — Treatment of thirst (diabetes context). Charaka's documentation of diseases characterized by excessive thirst includes the earliest clinical description of what we now call diabetes mellitus: Prameha — 20 types of urinary disease, of which Madhumeha (honey-urine disease) corresponds to Type 2 diabetes. Documented symptoms: excessive urination that is sweet (tested by observation of insects attracted to urine), excessive thirst, weight gain in some forms, weight loss in others. Primary treatments: Bitter melon (karela/Momordica) — the most specific traditional diabetes medicine. Jamun/Black plum (Eugenia jambolana) — seeds documented for blood sugar reduction. Gurmar (Gymnema sylvestre) — literally 'sugar destroyer.' Fenugreek (methi/Trigonella) — now validated with multiple RCTs. These four plants are now among the most extensively studied anti-diabetic plants in the world, validating Charaka's specific prescriptions for Madhumeha across 2,000 years.
segments.translation: Chapter on the Treatment of Thirst [Trishna Chikitsa], in the context of urinary and metabolic disorders. Charaka's systematic account of diseases marked by excessive thirst contains what may be recognized as the earliest clinical description of the condition now designated diabetes mellitus. The text enumerates twenty varieties of urinary disorder under the heading Prameha [disorders of excessive or abnormal urination], of which Madhumeha — literally 'honey-urine disease,' so named because the urine is sweet — corresponds most closely to what modern medicine classifies as Type 2 diabetes mellitus. Clinical signs recorded include: profuse urination of a sweet character (verified diagnostically by the observation that ants and other insects are drawn to the patient's urine); unremitting thirst [trishna]; increased body weight in certain forms of the disorder; and progressive wasting in others, reflecting the ancient recognition that the disease presents in distinct constitutional patterns. The principal therapeutic agents prescribed are as follows: Bitter melon [karela; Momordica charantia] — designated the most specific [i.e., most directly indicated] traditional remedy for this class of disorder. Seeds of Jamun or Black Plum [Eugenia jambolana, syn. Syzygium cumini] — documented for their action in reducing the sweetness of urine, interpreted in modern terms as blood glucose reduction. Gurmar [Gymnema sylvestre] — the name itself signifying 'destroyer of sugar,' prescribed for its capacity to suppress the perception and absorption of sweetness. Fenugreek [methi; Trigonella foenum-graecum] — prescribed in seed form, a use now corroborated by multiple randomized controlled trials demonstrating glycaemic benefit. The clinical and pharmacological validation of these four botanical agents across the intervening two millennia constitutes a remarkable confirmation of Charaka's specific therapeutic prescriptions for Madhumeha.

segments.title_inferred: Shvayathu Chikitsa: Treatment of Inflammatory Swellings
segments.original_text: Shvayathu Chikitsa — Treatment of inflammatory swellings. This chapter documents Charaka's anti-inflammatory pharmacopoeia: Primary: Shunthi (dried ginger) + Haridra (turmeric) + Neem (nimba) — the basic three-herb anti-inflammatory combination. More complex: Dashemani (ten plant anti-inflammatory formula). Specific applications: Boswellia (shallaki) for joint inflammation — now one of the most validated plant anti-inflammatories with Cochrane evidence. Guggul (Commiphora mukul) for joint conditions — the resin studied for both anti-inflammatory and lipid-lowering properties. Eranda (castor, Ricinus communis) root decoction for inflammatory arthritis. The principle of targeting vata-causing inflammation differs from Greek/Roman humoral anti-inflammatory theory but produces similar empirical plant selections — both traditions independently identifying ginger and Boswellia species for inflammatory conditions.
segments.translation: Chapter on the Treatment of Shvayathu [inflammatory swellings]. This chapter sets out Charaka's anti-inflammatory pharmacopoeia as follows. The primary combination consists of Shunthi [dried ginger], Haridra [turmeric], and Nimba [neem], forming the foundational three-herb anti-inflammatory preparation. A more complex formulation is the Dashemani [a compound of ten plant substances with anti-inflammatory properties]. For specific conditions: Shallaki [Boswellia] is prescribed for inflammation of the joints, a use now among the most robustly validated in botanical medicine, with supporting evidence in systematic reviews. Guggul [the resin of Commiphora mukul] is employed for joint conditions and has been investigated in modern research for both anti-inflammatory and lipid-lowering effects. A decoction of Eranda [castor, Ricinus communis] root is prescribed for inflammatory arthritis [vata-predominant joint swelling]. The theoretical framework underlying these prescriptions — namely the pacification of vata as the primary driver of inflammation — differs in structure from the Greek and Roman humoral doctrine, yet the empirical selections arrived at by both traditions show notable convergence, most strikingly in the independent identification of ginger and Boswellia species as effective agents in inflammatory conditions.

segments.title_inferred: Pandu Chikitsa: Treatment of Pallor and Anaemia
segments.original_text: Pandu Chikitsa — Treatment of pallor and anemia. Charaka's anaemia treatment documents: the primary therapeutic food is iron-rich; specific plants with documented iron content: Drumstick tree (Moringa — shigru, highest plant source of iron), Pomegranate (dadima — 'healer of pallor'), Amla/Indian Gooseberry (amalaki — Vitamin C enhancing iron absorption). Primary medicine: Mandura (iron oxide, processed) combined with Triphala to enhance iron absorption — an early understanding that Vitamin C-containing plants enhance mineral absorption. Modern validation: Triphala's high Vitamin C content (particularly Amalaki) does indeed enhance non-haem iron absorption significantly. This Charaka prescription anticipates modern nutritional iron therapy: iron compound + Vitamin C enhancer. The convergence between Charaka's pallor treatment and modern iron-deficiency anaemia management is direct.
segments.translation: The chapter on Pandu Chikitsa concerns the treatment of the condition known as pandu, characterised by pallor, weakness, and loss of colour in the skin — corresponding broadly to anaemia in modern clinical terms. Charaka prescribes a regimen centred upon iron-rich therapeutic foods and medicines. Among the principal plants indicated: (1) Shigru, the drumstick tree [Moringa oleifera], recognised as bearing exceptional iron content among plant sources; (2) Dadima, the pomegranate [Punica granatum], traditionally designated a 'healer of pallor' (pandu-nashana); (3) Amalaki, the Indian gooseberry [Phyllanthus emblica / Emblica officinalis], valued for its role in promoting the uptake of iron. The primary medicinal preparation is Mandura [processed iron oxide / ferric oxide] administered in combination with Triphala [the compound preparation of amalaki, bibhitaki, and haritaki]. The underlying therapeutic rationale — explicit or implicit in the text — is that the sour, vitamin-rich constituents of Triphala, especially amalaki, potentiate the absorption of the iron compound. Modern nutritional science confirms that Triphala's high ascorbic acid content, concentrated particularly in amalaki, significantly enhances non-haem iron absorption. This prescription thus represents an early, empirically grounded anticipation of the contemporary clinical practice of co-administering an iron compound with a vitamin C-containing agent. The correspondence between Charaka's pandu treatment protocol and modern iron-deficiency anaemia management is substantive and direct.

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Diarrheal Diseases (Atisara Chikitsa)
segments.original_text: Atisara Chikitsa — Treatment of diarrheal diseases. Charaka documents the most pharmacologically sophisticated diarrhea treatment in any pre-modern tradition: Distinguishes 8 types of atisara by cause and character. Treatment principles: Astringent medicines first; specific plants: Lodhra (Symplocos racemosa) — documented anti-diarrheal, astringent bark. Kutaja (Holarrhena pubescens) — 'Indrayava' seeds specifically documented for amoebic dysentery. Bilva (Aegle marmelos / Bael) — unripe fruit for diarrhea, ripe fruit as laxative (same plant, different stage — parallel to Dioscorides' lentil observation). Ativisha (Aconitum heterophyllum, processed, low-dose) for severe diarrhea with fever. The Kutaja (Holarrhena) documentation for amoebic dysentery is remarkable: conessine, the primary alkaloid, is active specifically against Entamoeba histolytica. This is the most specific traditional match between a documented condition and the active mechanism in the entire Charaka corpus.
segments.translation: Chapter on the Treatment of Atisara [diarrheal flux]: Charaka sets forth the most pharmacologically refined account of diarrheal treatment found in any pre-modern medical tradition. He distinguishes eight varieties of atisara according to their respective causes and characteristic presentations. The foundational therapeutic principle calls for the administration of astringent [kashaya] medicines in the first instance. Among the specific botanical agents prescribed: Lodhra [Symplocos racemosa] — the bark, astringent in quality [rasa], is documented as an anti-diarrheal remedy. Kutaja [Holarrhena pubescens] — the seeds, designated 'Indrayava,' are specifically indicated for the form of dysentery associated with bloody, mucoid flux [raktatisara], corresponding to amoebic dysentery. Bilva [Aegle marmelos, Bael] — the unripe fruit is employed to arrest diarrhea, while the ripe fruit acts as a laxative [virechana]; this constitutes a documented instance of the same plant yielding opposing therapeutic actions at different stages of maturation, analogous to the observation made by Dioscorides concerning the lentil. Ativisha [Aconitum heterophyllum, specially processed and administered in low dosage] is indicated for severe diarrhea accompanied by fever [jvara-sahita atisara]. Editorial note: The documentation of Kutaja [Holarrhena pubescens] for amoebic dysentery is of exceptional pharmacological significance: conessine, the principal steroidal alkaloid of this plant, has been demonstrated to act specifically against Entamoeba histolytica, the causative organism of amoebic dysentery. This represents the most precisely corroborated correspondence between a classically documented clinical indication and the verified biochemical mechanism of action to be found anywhere in the Charaka corpus.

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Grahani (Malabsorption / IBS-Spectrum Disorders)
segments.original_text: Grahanidosha Chikitsa — Treatment of IBS and malabsorption syndrome. Charaka's documentation of Grahani (malabsorption/IBS-type condition) covers what modern medicine recognises as a spectrum including inflammatory bowel disease, IBS, and malabsorption syndromes. Primary treatment: Chitrakadi vati (Plumbago zeylanica + 9 other herbs) — the primary digestive stimulant formula. Shankha bhasma (conch shell ash, prepared) for acid conditions. Hingwashtak churna (asafoetida + 7 spices) — carminative, antispasmodic. Specific plants: Kutaja (Holarrhena) for infectious component. Bilva (bael) for normalizing bowel motility. Musta (Cyperus rotundus) — the primary digestive sedative. Pippali (long pepper) for stimulating digestive enzymes. Charaka's understanding that Grahani requires digestive fire restoration (not just symptom suppression) anticipates modern functional gastroenterology's emphasis on microbiome and enzyme function.
segments.translation: Treatment of Grahani-dosha [disorder of the seat of digestive fire, Grahani]. Charaka's systematic account of Grahani encompasses a clinical spectrum that modern medicine would recognise as including inflammatory bowel disease, irritable bowel syndrome, and malabsorption syndromes. The condition is understood in the classical framework as a failure of the digestive fire [agni] seated in the small intestine, resulting in disordered assimilation and irregular bowel function. The primary therapeutic formula prescribed is Chitrakadi vati, a compound tablet preparation based upon Chitraka [Plumbago zeylanica] combined with nine adjunct herbs, functioning as the principal stimulant of digestive fire [agni-deepana]. Shankha bhasma — a calcined preparation of conch shell [purified and processed ash] — is indicated specifically for acid-related presentations of the condition. Hingwashtak churna, a powdered compound of asafoetida [Ferula assa-foetida] with seven aromatic spices, is employed for its carminative and antispasmodic actions, relieving intestinal distension and colic. Among individual plant medicines, Kutaja [Holarrhena antidysenterica] is directed at the infectious or dysenteric component of Grahani. Bilva [bael fruit, Aegle marmelos] is prescribed for the normalisation of bowel motility, acting in both diarrhoea and constipation depending on preparation. Musta [Cyperus rotundus, nut-grass] serves as the principal digestive sedative and anti-inflammatory agent for the gut. Pippali [long pepper, Piper longum] is employed to stimulate secretion of digestive enzymes and to enhance bioavailability [yogavahi function] of co-administered medicines. The governing therapeutic principle articulated by Charaka is that Grahani demands restoration of the digestive fire at its source rather than mere suppression of presenting symptoms — an approach that prefigures the emphasis in contemporary functional gastroenterology upon gut microbiome reconstitution and enzyme sufficiency as primary therapeutic targets.

segments.title_inferred: Treatment of Hiccups and Asthma (Hikka-Shvasa Chikitsa): Respiratory Pharmacopoeia
segments.original_text: Hikka Shvasa Chikitsa — Treatment of hiccups and asthma. Charaka's respiratory pharmacopoeia: Primary plants for Shvasa (asthma): Kantakari (Solanum xanthocarpum) — the first-line Ayurvedic bronchodilator. Vasa (Adhatoda vasica/Malabar nut) — documented expectorant and bronchodilator. Pippali (long pepper) — primary respiratory stimulant. Shunthi (dried ginger) — anti-inflammatory. Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum/Holy basil) — antimicrobial, bronchodilating. The Adhatoda vasica (Malabar nut/Vasa) documentation is pharmacologically significant: vasicine, the primary alkaloid, is the source of bromhexine — a widely prescribed mucolytic/expectorant drug.
