The flowers are yellow, entirely without scent, and on account of their shape the Spaniards call them 'little birds' [pajaritos]; they are small like those of the broom [retama], having two petals larger than the rest that resemble the wings of birds, and beneath these, near the petiole, a small beak-like projection [piquillo] that corresponds to the beak of a bird. The name vilcu that this herb bears is taken from the Aymara language, which is the common tongue of the provinces of the Collao,

segments.title_inferred: On the Misuca and Panti Flowers (Chapters LII–LIII)
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LII
De la misuca

La misuca es una flor amarilla de tanto ruedo como un real de a cuatro y de la hechura de la flor de la manzanilla. La mata se levanta un codo de la tierra, echa muchos ramillos delgados, redondos, lisos y verdes; la hoja muy menuda y hendida, semejante a la de la artemisa.

Otra diferencia se halla de misuca muy parecida a ésta, salvo que los vastaguillos son algún tanto rojos y las hojas como las de la verbena, muy aserradas por los lados. A la primera especie de misuca llaman hembra los indios, y macho, a la segunda; y con ambas suelen teñir de amarillo. No dan de sí estas flores olor alguno. En la lengua aimará se dicen flor y planta misuca.

CAPITULO LIII
Del panti

El panti es una flor colorada de la hechura de la misuca; tiene de siete a ocho hojas en torno con el botón de en medio amarillo. Es tan grande esta flor como un real de a ocho; no tiene olor alguno. La mata crece un codo en alto, hace los tallos redondos, delgados y lisos; la hoja, menuda y hendida, como la de la artemisa; tiene la raíz gruesa, redonda o prolongada y tierna como criadilla de tierra. Llámase así esta flor en la lengua aimará.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LII
On the Misuca

The misuca is a yellow flower as wide in circumference as a four-real coin [roughly 3–4 cm across], and shaped like the flower of chamomile. The plant rises one cubit [approx. 45 cm] from the ground and puts out many thin, round, smooth, green little branches; the leaf is very small and cleft, resembling that of artemisia [mugwort].

Another variety of misuca is found, very similar to the first, except that the little stems are somewhat reddish and the leaves are like those of verbena, very finely serrated along the sides. The Indians call the first species the female, and the second the male; and with both they customarily dye [cloth or fiber] yellow. These flowers give off no scent whatsoever. In the Aymara language, both the flower and the plant are called misuca.

CHAPTER LIII
On the Panti

The panti is a red flower shaped like the misuca; it has seven to eight petals arranged around a yellow central button [disk]. This flower is as large as an eight-real coin [roughly 4–5 cm across]; it has no scent. The plant grows one cubit in height, producing round, thin, and smooth stems; the leaf is small and cleft, like that of artemisia; it has a thick root, round or elongated and tender like a truffle [criadilla de tierra, lit. 'earth testicle']. This flower is so called in the Aymara language.

segments.title_inferred: On the Ullaulla flower of the Peruvian highlands
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LIV
De la ullaulla

La Ullaulla es una florecita llamada así en la lengua aimará, de color entre blanco y morado, del tamaño y talle de la del saúco, algún tanto mayor; compónese de cinco hojitas en rueda, las cuales tienen las puntas botas; nacen a manojitos de ocho a diez florecillas cada uno. Tienen un olor grato al olfato y vivo, y son de las de mayor fragancia que producen las sierras del Perú, a donde nacen en gran abundancia y hermosean mucho los campos. La mata que produce estas flores es muy pequeña, extiende sus ramos sobre la tierra sin levantarse della, los cuales son largos un palmo y casi tan delgados como hilos, con unas venillas rojas. Las hojas son casi tan menudas como las de la zanahoria. Donde mayor copia destas florecitas he visto yo es en el contorno de la villa de Oruro.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LIV
On the Ullaulla

The Ullaulla is a small flower called by this name in the Aymara language, of a color between white and purple, of the size and form of the elderflower [saúco], though somewhat larger; it is composed of five small petals arranged in a wheel [i.e., radially], whose tips are blunt [botas]. They grow in small clusters of eight to ten florets each. They have a fragrance that is pleasing and lively to the sense of smell, and they are among the most fragrant flowers produced by the mountain ranges [sierras] of Peru, where they grow in great abundance and greatly beautify the fields. The shrub that produces these flowers is very small; it spreads its branches over the ground without rising from it, the branches being a hand's span [palmo] in length and almost as slender as threads, with small red veins. The leaves are almost as fine and small as those of the carrot. The place where I have seen the greatest abundance of these little flowers is in the environs of the town of Oruro.

segments.title_inferred: The Qixeaquea plant and closing remarks on American wildflowers
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LV
De la qixeaquea

La qixeaquea se llama así en la lengua aimará. Es una yerba pequeña; echa los ramillos largos un palmo, delgados, verdes y vellosos; sus hojas son como las del poleo, más puntiagudas, las cuales y los ramillos son pegajosos; produce unas florecicas blancas del tamaño y forma que las de la patacauri.
Dejo otras infinitas flores silvestres que nacen en diversas partes desta tierra, que fuera proceder en infinito quererlas contar todas, pues sólo las que en tiempo de garúas visten las lomas y cerros del contorno desta ciudad de Lima, y las que en la plaza de México venden las indias en curiosos ramilletes, no tienen número ni cuento. Basta haber hecho mención de las más conocidas, para que se entienda que no carecía esta América de la hermosura y adorno de las flores.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LV
On the qixeaquea

The qixeaquea is so called in the Aymara language. It is a small herb; it puts forth slender branches about a hand's span [palmo] in length, thin, green, and hairy; its leaves resemble those of pennyroyal [poleo], though more pointed, and both the leaves and the branches are sticky; it produces small white flowers of the same size and form as those of the patacauri.
I leave aside the countless other wildflowers that grow in various parts of this land, for it would be an endless task to attempt to enumerate them all — for those alone that during the season of the garúas [coastal drizzle-fogs] clothe the hills and slopes surrounding this city of Lima, and those that the Indian women sell in the plaza of Mexico City in carefully arranged bouquets, are beyond all number and reckoning. It is enough to have mentioned the best known of them, so that it may be understood that this America was by no means lacking in the beauty and adornment of flowers.

segments.title_inferred: On Tobacco: Trade, Regional Varieties, and Indigenous Names
segments.original_text:
os herbolarios.

Es tanta la cantidad de tabaco que se gasta en las Indias y se lleva a España, que hay provincias que todo el trato y granjerías de sus habitantes es cultivarlo y beneficiarlo; y tienen más precio los de unas partes que los de otras. En la Nueva España es famoso el de Papantla, y en este reino del Perú el de Jaén de Pacamoros. El instrumento en que los indios de la Española tomaban el tabaco en humo se decía tabaco, el cual nombre dieron los Españoles a esta yerba y con él se ha quedado hasta hoy. En la lengua general del Perú se llama sayri, y en la mexicana, picietl.
segments.translation:
...the herbalists [herbolarios].

So great is the quantity of tobacco [tabaco] consumed in the Indies and carried to Spain that there are provinces whose entire trade and livelihood [granjerías] of their inhabitants consists in cultivating and curing [beneficiarlo] it; and tobacco from some regions commands a higher price than that from others. In New Spain the tobacco of Papantla is the most renowned, and in this kingdom of Peru that of Jaén de Pacamoros. The instrument with which the Indians of Hispaniola inhaled tobacco smoke was called tabaco, which name the Spaniards gave to this herb and by which it has been known ever since. In the general language [lengua general] of Peru it is called sayri, and in the Mexican tongue, picietl.

segments.title_inferred: On the Itapallo and Chichicaste: Two Stinging-Nettle-Like Plants and Their Medicinal Uses
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LVII

Del itapallo y chichicaste

El itapallo es una yerba que en sus efectos parece especie de ortiga, aunque realmente se distingue della en esto: levántase de la tierra un codo; sus tallos son verdes, redondos, tiernos y no más gruesos que un delgado junco; produce desde el pie muchas hojas, las cuales son de un verde oscuro, largas un jeme, y por donde más anchas, tienen tres dedos; vanse desde junto al pezón estrechando hasta rematar en delgadas puntas; son muy hendidas por los lados en muchas puntas. Echa una flor esta yerba de color encarnado fino, muy vistosa, aunque de ningún olor; compónese de cinco hojitas tan juntas unas con otras, que parecen estar continuadas y no contiguas; tiene cada hojita, por la parte de afuera, un lomillo en medio tan relevado, que parece pliegue, y lo que le corresponde por la parte de dentro está hundido en forma de ángulo; con que viene a tener esta flor hechura de un botoncito redondo de cinco picos, tan grande como una cáscara de nuez puesta en figura redonda. Por las junturas de las hojas desta flor nacen, por la parte de dentro, otras cinco hojitas blancas y lisas y casi transparentes, tan retorcidas, que vienen a tener figura redonda y parece cada una una perfecta y reluciente perla. Los tallos, hojas y flores desta yerba están armados de muchas espinillas blancas muy delgadas y dos veces más largas que las de las borrajas, que punzan como las de las ortigas, aunque no escuecen tanto.

Es el itapallo de temperamento caliente y seco; usan los indios de su cocimiento con salmuera para resolver las hinchazones de los gotosos así de las piernas como de las manos. Hecho cocimiento con vino y aplicada la yerba caliente como sale del cocimiento sobre la región de la vejiga, vale contra la detención de orina. Usan las indias del cocimiento desta yerba en ayunas desde un día después de haber parido hasta nueve o diez, para efecto de quedar del todo limpias de la sangre que pudiera rezagarse; para lo cual comen también la yerba cocida en sus potajes. Demás desto, bebido de ordinario su cocimiento con miel de abejas, mundifica las llagas del pulmón, limpia las materias del pecho y estómago, es gran abstergente de las llagas de los riñones, desopila el bazo, adelgaza los humores viscosos, ensancha las vías y provoca la orina. Finalmente, cocida esta yerba con sus flores, raíces de hinojo, de perejil, de apio, pepitas de melón y agrio de limas, y tomando del cocimiento en ayunas con azúcar y miel un buen jarro, limpia poderosamente las arenas y piedras de riñones y vejiga. Llámase esta yerba, en la lengua aimará, itapallo.

Otra casta de ortigas se halla en la Nueva España llamada chichicaste; crece un estado de alto; tiene la hoja como de moral, pero más ancha; están las hojas y cogollos cubiertos de unas espinillas que punzan con más dolor que ortigas y levantan como sarpullido en la parte que pican.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LVII

On the Itapallo and the Chichicaste

The itapallo is an herb that in its effects resembles a species of nettle [ortiga], though it is truly distinct from it in the following respects: it rises from the ground about one cubit [codo] in height; its stems are green, round, tender, and no thicker than a slender reed; from the base it produces many leaves, which are of a dark green color, a hand-span [jeme] in length, and at their widest about three fingers broad; from near the stalk they narrow progressively until they end in slender points; they are deeply cut along the sides into many points. This herb puts forth a flower of a fine crimson color, very handsome, though entirely without scent; it is composed of five small petals so closely joined to one another that they appear to be continuous rather than merely contiguous; each little petal has, on its outer face, a raised ridge down the center so prominent that it looks like a fold, while the corresponding inner surface is hollowed in the form of an angle; whereby this flower comes to have the shape of a small round button with five points, as large as a walnut shell arranged in a round form. At the junctions of the petals of this flower there grow, on the inner side, five other small leaves that are white, smooth, and nearly transparent, so twisted that they take on a rounded shape, and each one resembles a perfect and lustrous pearl. The stems, leaves, and flowers of this herb are armed with many very fine white spines twice as long as those of borage [borrajas], which sting like those of nettles, though they do not burn as much.

The itapallo is of a hot and dry temperament [following Galenic humoral classification]. The Indians use a decoction of it with brine [salmuera] to resolve the swellings of gout sufferers, both in the legs and in the hands. Made into a decoction with wine and the herb applied hot, as it comes from the decoction, over the region of the bladder, it is efficacious against retention of urine. Indian women use the decoction of this herb, taken on an empty stomach, from one day after giving birth through the ninth or tenth day, for the purpose of being entirely cleansed of any blood that might remain; to the same end they also eat the herb boiled in their stews [potajes]. Beyond this, the decoction drunk regularly with honey from bees cleanses the ulcers [llagas] of the lungs, purges matter from the chest and stomach, is a great detergent [abstergente] of ulcers of the kidneys, unblocks [desopila] the spleen, thins viscous humors, opens the passages, and promotes urination. Finally, this herb boiled together with its flowers, roots of fennel, parsley, and celery, melon seeds, and lime juice, and drinking a good jarful of the decoction on an empty stomach with sugar and honey, powerfully cleanses gravel and stones from the kidneys and bladder. This herb is called, in the Aymara tongue, itapallo.

Another kind of nettle is found in New Spain called chichicaste; it grows to the height of a man [un estado]; its leaf resembles that of the mulberry, but broader; the leaves and shoots are covered with small spines that sting with more pain than nettles and raise something like a rash [sarpullido] on the part they prick.

segments.title_inferred: The Herb Called Pencácuc (the Shameplant)
segments.original_text:
CAPITULO LVIII
De la yerba llamada pencácuc

Este nombre es participio de un verbo de la lengua del Perú que significa avergonzarse, y lo dan los indios a cierta yerba por el efecto que hace en tocándole con la mano, que es encogerse por un rato, el cual pasado, vuelve a ponerse como estaba de antes, y suena lo mismo que si dijésemos La que se avergüenza, y los españoles por la misma razón la llaman la vergonzosa. Tiene la hoja pequeña, semejante a la del arrayán, y la raíz como de nabo. En esta planta se hallan macho y hembra y nacen siempre juntas entrambas; el macho tiene la hoja como de lirio y la raíz redonda; no se encoge cuando la tocan, pero tiene otra propiedad extraña, y es que comida su raíz enciende poderosamente en lujuria, y el remedio para apagar este fuego está en comer de la raíz de la hembra, que al punto lo remite; propiedad rara y muy conocida de los indios, particularmente de los de la provincia de Chachapoyas, diócesis de Trujillo, donde nace mucha desta yerba.
segments.translation:
CHAPTER LVIII
On the Herb Called Pencácuc

This name is a participle derived from a verb in the language of Peru [Quechua] meaning 'to feel shame,' and the Indians give it to a certain herb on account of the effect produced when one touches it with the hand, which is that it [the plant] draws itself in for a time, after which it returns to its former state. The name thus conveys the same meaning as if we were to say 'She who is ashamed,' and the Spaniards, for the same reason, call it 'la vergonzosa' [the shameful/bashful one]. It has small leaves resembling those of the myrtle [arrayán], and a root like that of a turnip. In this plant both male and female forms are found, and the two always grow together. The male has leaves like those of a lily and a round root; it does not draw in when touched, but it possesses another strange property, namely that eating its root powerfully inflames one with lust [lujuria], and the remedy for quenching this fire lies in eating the root of the female plant, which promptly extinguishes it. This is a rare and very well known property among the Indians, particularly those of the province of Chachapoyas, in the diocese of Trujillo, where much of this herb grows.

segments.title_inferred: Thorowax Root (Radix Bupleuri)
segments.original_text: Radix Bupleuri — Bupleurum falcatum L. (Apiaceae/Umbelliferae). Dried root of Bupleurum falcatum L. and related species including B. chinense DC. Major chemical constituents: triterpenoid saponins (saikosaponins a, b2, c, d; collectively 'saikosides', 0.3–2.3% dry weight), flavonoids (rutin, narcissin, isorhamnetin, quercetin), polyacetylenes, phytosterols (alpha-spinasterol, stigmasterol), essential oils (bupleurumol), polysaccharides (bupleurans 2IIb, 2IIc), and pectin-type polysaccharides. Saikosaponin a and d are considered the principal bioactive markers for pharmacopoeial standardisation. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 (1999), pp. 67–82.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Radix Bupleuri consists of the dried roots of Bupleurum falcatum L. and, in some pharmacopoeias, B. chinense DC., harvested in spring or autumn and dried at low temperature. The root is official in the pharmacopoeias of China, Japan, and the Republic of Korea, among others.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Bupleurum falcatum is a perennial herb of the family Apiaceae (Umbelliferae), typically 40–100 cm in height. Leaves are alternate, lanceolate to narrowly elliptic, with prominent parallel veins giving a characteristic sickle-like ('falcate') curvature — the origin of both the species epithet and the common name 'thorowax' (suggesting stems that appear to pierce the leaves). Flowers are small and yellow, arranged in compound umbels. The root is cylindrical, branching, brownish-grey externally, pale yellow internally, with a characteristic bitter taste.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native across a broad arc from Europe through Central Asia, China, Japan, and the Korean Peninsula. Widely cultivated commercially in China (Gansu, Liaoning, Hebei provinces) and Japan. The related species B. chinense is the primary commercial source in Chinese traditional medicine.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The principal pharmacologically active compounds are the triterpenoid saponins known as saikosaponins (designated a, b1, b2, c, and d), present at 0.3–2.3% of dry root weight. Saikosaponins a and d are regarded as the primary bioactive and quality-control markers. Additional constituents include: flavonoids (rutin, narcissin, quercetin, isorhamnetin); polyacetylenic compounds; phytosterols (alpha-spinasterol, stigmasterol); essential oils including bupleurumol; immunomodulatory polysaccharides (bupleurans 2IIb and 2IIc); and pectin-type polysaccharides. Chinese Pharmacopoeia standards require a minimum of 0.30% combined saikosaponin a and d content.

MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence: Radix Bupleuri is most extensively studied in the context of liver protection [hepatoprotection] and immune modulation. Clinical investigations have focused on: (1) chronic hepatitis — multi-centre trials in Japan and China using standardised saikosaponin-containing formulations (notably the traditional compound formula Sho-saiko-to / Minor Bupleurum Decoction, TJ-9) demonstrated significant reduction of serum transaminase levels (ALT/AST) in patients with chronic active hepatitis B and C; (2) anti-inflammatory applications, where clinical data support reduction of fever and inflammatory markers; (3) adjunct oncology use, where polysaccharide fractions have been studied for immunostimulatory effects in cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy. A landmark Japanese multi-centre study (n=260 patients with cirrhosis secondary to hepatitis C) found that Sho-saiko-to significantly reduced the incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma over a 5-year follow-up compared to control, though this finding pertains to the compound formula rather than isolated Radix Bupleuri.

MEDICINAL USES — Traditional: In East Asian traditional medicine (Traditional Chinese Medicine [TCM], Kampo), Radix Bupleuri ('Chai Hu') is categorised as a herb that 'releases the exterior' [jie biao] and 'raises yang qi' [sheng yang]. Principal traditional indications include: alternating fever and chills [shaoyang syndrome]; liver qi stagnation presenting as flank pain, irritability, and menstrual irregularities; prolapse of organs attributed to sinking of central qi; and febrile diseases. In European folk medicine, the plant has historically been used as an antipyretic and digestive tonic.

PHARMACOLOGY: Saikosaponins exert hepatoprotective effects by: (a) stabilising hepatocyte membranes and reducing lipid peroxidation; (b) inhibiting cytochrome P450-mediated hepatotoxin activation; (c) promoting hepatic regeneration by stimulating hepatocyte DNA synthesis. Anti-inflammatory mechanisms include suppression of prostaglandin E2 synthesis, inhibition of complement activation, and downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6). Saikosaponin d demonstrates significant corticosterone-potentiating activity, partially explaining antipyretic and anti-inflammatory effects via hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis modulation. Immunomodulatory polysaccharides (bupleurans) activate macrophages and natural killer (NK) cells and enhance interferon production in vitro. Antiviral activity against hepatitis B virus (HBV) surface antigen expression has been demonstrated in cell culture models.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials:
1. Hirayama et al. (Japan, 1989): Randomised controlled trial of Sho-saiko-to (containing Radix Bupleuri as principal herb) versus placebo in 222 patients with chronic hepatitis B. The treatment group showed statistically significant reductions in serum ALT (mean reduction 48% vs 12% placebo, p<0.01) and improved histological scores on liver biopsy at 12 months.
2. Oka et al. (Japan, 1995): Prospective multi-centre study (n=260) in cirrhotic hepatitis C patients. Long-term Sho-saiko-to administration associated with significantly lower incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) over 5 years (relative risk reduction approximately 40%) compared to matched controls. Note: Causality for the individual herb versus the compound formula cannot be definitively assigned.
3. Yamashiki et al. (Japan, 1997): Open-label study demonstrating that Sho-saiko-to normalised abnormal lymphocyte subsets and enhanced NK cell activity in chronic hepatitis C patients, corroborating the immunomodulatory mechanism of bupleuran polysaccharides.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include: pneumonitis and pleuropneumonia (rare but serious — particularly associated with Sho-saiko-to co-administered with interferon-alpha in hepatitis C treatment, representing a potentially life-threatening drug-herb interaction); mild gastrointestinal disturbance (nausea, diarrhoea); sedation at high doses. Saikosaponins are haemolytic in vitro; oral bioavailability is low due to first-pass hydrolysis, reducing systemic haemolytic risk. Elevated liver enzymes have been reported paradoxically in rare cases of hypersensitivity.

DRUG INTERACTIONS: Contraindicated with interferon-alpha therapy (risk of interstitial pneumonitis). May potentiate the effects of corticosteroids via HPA axis modulation. Possible interaction with anticoagulant agents warrants monitoring.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Concurrent use with interferon therapy. Hypersensitivity to Apiaceae family plants. Not recommended during pregnancy (emmenagogue properties in traditional use; insufficient safety data). Caution in patients with pre-existing lung disease.

DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Doses: Crude dried root: 3–9 g per day as decoction (煎劑), divided doses; consistent with Chinese Pharmacopoeia and Japanese Pharmacopoeia standards. Powdered root: 1–3 g per day. Dry extract (standardised to saikosaponins): equivalent doses per manufacturer's standardisation. Duration of use: traditionally up to 12 weeks for acute indications; chronic liver disease protocols extend to months under medical supervision. Paediatric dosing not established in WHO monograph.

segments.title_inferred: Gotu Kola
segments.original_text: Herba Centellae — Centella asiatica (L.) Urban (Apiaceae/Umbelliferae). Dried or fresh aerial parts of Centella asiatica. Key chemical constituents: triterpenoid saponins (asiaticoside, madecassoside, brahmoside, brahminoside; collectively 1–8% dry weight); triterpenoid aglycones (asiatic acid, madecassic acid, brahmic acid); flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, rutin); volatile oils (β-caryophyllene, trans-β-farnesene, germacrene D); polyacetylenes; phytosterols (sitosterol, stigmasterol); chlorogenic acid; isothankuniside; thankuniside. Total triterpenic fraction (TECA / TTF) standardized extracts commonly assayed at 40% asiaticoside, 29–30% asiatic acid, 29–30% madecassic acid, 1–2% madecassoside.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Herba Centellae consists of the dried or fresh whole aerial parts (leaves, stems, petioles, and occasionally roots) of Centella asiatica (L.) Urban, a slender, creeping herbaceous perennial of the family Apiaceae (Umbelliferae). The drug is used either as a crude herb, standardized dry extract, or refined triterpenic fraction.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Centella asiatica is a prostrate, stoloniferous perennial herb. Leaves are long-petiolate, reniform to orbicular, 2–5 cm in diameter, with crenate margins, and arise in clusters of 2–6 at each node along slender, rooting stolons. Flowers are small, inconspicuous, pinkish to red-purple, borne in umbellate clusters of 3–4 on short axillary peduncles. Fruit is a schizocarp of two mericarps, compressed laterally, with prominent longitudinal ribs. The plant typically grows low to the ground, preferring moist, shady environments.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to tropical and subtropical Asia, including India, Sri Lanka, China, Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Pacific Islands. Widely naturalized throughout Africa, Madagascar, southern and eastern Europe, Australia, and Central/South America. Commercially cultivated principally in India, Madagascar, and Sri Lanka for pharmaceutical-grade extract production.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The principal bioactive constituents are pentacyclic triterpenoid saponins, collectively comprising 1–8% of dry-weight herb. The four primary saponins are: asiaticoside (the predominant glycoside), madecassoside, brahmoside, and brahminoside. Their corresponding aglycones — asiatic acid, madecassic acid (= brahmic acid), and betulinic acid — are released on hydrolysis and are considered the primary pharmacologically active moieties. Commercial standardized extracts (TECA, Titrated Extract of Centella asiatica; also marketed as TTFCA, Total Triterpenic Fraction of Centella asiatica) typically contain approximately 40% asiaticoside, 29–30% asiatic acid, 29–30% madecassic acid, and 1–2% madecassoside. Additional constituents include flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, rutin), a volatile oil fraction containing β-caryophyllene, trans-β-farnesene, and germacrene D, polyacetylenic compounds, phytosterols (β-sitosterol, stigmasterol), and phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid, isochlorogenic acid). Minor glycosides isothankuniside and thankuniside have also been isolated.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: The primary evidence-based indications for Herba Centellae and its standardized extracts are: (1) Treatment and prevention of chronic venous insufficiency [CVI] and associated microangiopathy, including the amelioration of lower-limb edema, capillary permeability, and venous hypertension; (2) Promotion of wound healing and reduction of hypertrophic scarring and keloid formation; (3) Adjunctive treatment of minor burns, surgical wounds, and anal fissures. Evidence is derived from multiple randomized controlled trials [RCTs] using TTFCA (60–120 mg/day orally) in CVI populations, with clinically significant improvements in edema, heaviness, pain, and objective microcirculatory parameters.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Extensively documented in Ayurvedic medicine (as 'Mandukaparni' and 'Brahmi' — though true Brahmi is also attributed to Bacopa monnieri), traditional Chinese medicine ('Ji Xue Cao'), and Unani medicine. Traditional indications include: wound healing, leprosy, psoriasis, syphilis, lupus, and other dermatological disorders; mental fatigue and enhancement of cognitive function and memory; fever and upper respiratory infections; urinary tract disorders; and as a general nervine tonic and adaptogen. The plant is included in the pharmacopoeias of China, India, France, and several other nations.

PHARMACOLOGY: The dominant pharmacological mechanisms established in vitro and in vivo are:
1. COLLAGEN SYNTHESIS STIMULATION: Asiaticoside and asiatic acid stimulate type I collagen synthesis in human dermal fibroblasts and in wound-bed tissue, promoting tensile strength and organized extracellular matrix [ECM] deposition. This is the principal mechanism underlying wound-healing and antifibrotic (scar-remodeling) activity.
2. ANTI-INFLAMMATORY ACTIVITY: Madecassoside inhibits NF-κB activation and reduces TNF-α, IL-1β, and prostaglandin E2 production in macrophage and fibroblast cultures. Asiatic acid inhibits 5-lipoxygenase in vitro.
3. ANTIOXIDANT EFFECTS: Triterpenoid aglycones scavenge superoxide and hydroxyl radicals; flavonoid fraction contributes significant free-radical quenching activity.
4. VENOTROPIC / CAPILLARY-STABILIZING EFFECTS: TTFCA reduces capillary filtration rate and improves capillary fragility in patients with CVI, likely via direct effects on pericyte and endothelial cell function and inhibition of matrix metalloproteinases [MMPs] involved in basement membrane degradation.
5. ANXIOLYTIC / ADAPTOGENIC EFFECTS (animal models): Aqueous and ethanolic extracts demonstrate anxiolytic-like activity in rodent models (elevated plus-maze, open-field test), possibly mediated through modulation of the GABAergic system.
6. NEUROPROTECTIVE EFFECTS (in vitro/in vivo): Asiaticoside attenuates glutamate-induced neurotoxicity in cortical neurons; in vivo studies in rodents suggest enhancement of neurite outgrowth and cognitive performance in spatial learning tasks.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE (Key Trials):
TRIAL 1 — Chronic Venous Insufficiency (Pointel et al., Angiology, 1987): Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (n=94) evaluating oral TTFCA 120 mg/day vs. placebo for 8 weeks in patients with CVI. Primary outcome: lower-limb venous distensibility and capillary filtration rate assessed by plethysmography. Results: TTFCA produced statistically significant reductions in capillary filtration rate (p<0.05), leg edema volume, and symptom scores (heaviness, pain, cramps) compared to placebo. No significant adverse events reported.
TRIAL 2 — Microangiopathy in Diabetic/Hypertensive Patients (Cesarone et al., Angiology, 1994): Controlled trial (n=50) using TTFCA 60 mg twice daily for 6 months in patients with microangiopathy associated with diabetes or arterial hypertension. Outcome: laser Doppler flowmetry and transcutaneous pO2 measurements showed significant improvement in microcirculatory parameters versus control (p<0.01). The authors concluded TTFCA produced a measurable protective effect on capillary integrity under hyperglycaemic and hypertensive stress conditions.
TRIAL 3 — Wound Healing and Hypertrophic Scarring (Widgerow et al., Aesthetic Plastic Surgery, 2000): Prospective controlled study evaluating a standardized Centella extract topical formulation vs. control in patients with post-surgical and post-burn hypertrophic scars. Results demonstrated significantly faster scar maturation, reduced scar height, and improved pliability in the Centella-treated group. Mechanistic correlate: biopsy specimens showed increased organized collagen deposition and reduced myofibroblast density.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include: contact dermatitis and allergic skin reactions (most commonly associated with topical use; sensitization to asiaticoside reported); mild gastrointestinal discomfort (nausea, abdominal bloating) with oral preparations at higher doses; rarely, elevated serum transaminases suggesting hepatotoxicity with prolonged high-dose oral use (monitor liver function in long-term use). Photosensitization has been reported anecdotally. No significant pharmacokinetic drug–drug interactions formally established; theoretical caution with concurrent sedative or anxiolytic drugs (GABAergic interactions in animal models) and with anticoagulant/antiplatelet agents (limited human data).

CONTRAINDICATIONS: Known hypersensitivity to Centella asiatica or to any member of the Apiaceae family. Caution in patients with pre-existing hepatic disease or those taking hepatotoxic medications (given rare hepatotoxicity signal). Safety in pregnancy and lactation has not been established in controlled studies; use is not recommended in pregnant or breastfeeding women. Not recommended for use in children under 12 years without medical supervision.

DOSAGE (WHO-Recommended Adult Doses):
— Crude herb (oral infusion/decoction): 0.33–0.68 g dried herb per cup, three times daily (total approximately 1–2 g dried herb per day).
— Standardized dry extract (TTFCA, 40% total triterpenes): 60 mg twice daily (120 mg/day), orally.
— Fluid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 0.33–0.68 mL three times daily.
— Tincture (1:5 in 40% ethanol): 1–2 mL three times daily.
— Topical preparations: Standardized cream or gel (1% total triterpenic fraction) applied to affected skin area once to twice daily. Treatment duration for CVI: clinical trials typically used 4–12 weeks; longer use requires medical oversight.

segments.title_inferred: Cinnamon (Inner Bark)
segments.original_text: Cortex Cinnamomi — Cinnamomum verum J. Presl (syn. C. zeylanicum Blume; Fam. Lauraceae). Dried inner bark of the shoots of coppiced trees. Key chemical constituents: volatile oil (1.2–2.0% in bark; principal component trans-cinnamaldehyde, 55–90%), condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins, cinnamtannin B1), diterpenes (cinnzeylanol, cinnzeylanine), polyphenolic polymers (Type-A procyanidins), eugenol (<10% of oil), coumarin (trace in C. verum; higher in C. cassia), mucilage, and calcium oxalate.
segments.translation:
Cinnamon bark is the dried inner bark obtained from young shoots of Cinnamomum verum (true/Ceylon cinnamon), a member of the laurel family. The bark is distinguished from the related but distinct Cassia cinnamon (C. aromaticum/C. cassia) by its thin, multi-layered, tightly rolled quills and markedly lower coumarin content — a clinically important safety distinction.

**DEFINITION:** Cortex Cinnamomi designates the processed inner bark (cortex) stripped from coppiced branches, dried, and rolled into characteristic 'quills.' The bark must contain not less than 1.2% v/w volatile oil (European Pharmacopoeia standard).

**BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION:** An evergreen tree reaching 10–15 metres in the wild (kept shorter under cultivation by coppicing). Leaves are opposite, ovate-lanceolate, coriaceous, with three prominent veins. Flowers are small, yellowish-white, borne in panicles. Bark is thin, smooth, tan-brown; inner bark has the characteristic sweet-spicy aroma. Cultivated trees are coppiced every 2–3 years to harvest new shoots.

**GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION:** Native to Sri Lanka (Ceylon) and southern India. Now commercially cultivated in Sri Lanka, India, Madagascar, the Seychelles, and parts of Central America. Sri Lanka remains the world's primary source of pharmacopoeial-grade Cortex Cinnamomi.

**CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:** The pharmacological activity is attributed primarily to: (1) trans-cinnamaldehyde (55–90% of the volatile oil) — the dominant bioactive aldehyde responsible for antimicrobial, antifungal, and spasmolytic effects; (2) eugenol (<10% of oil) — contributes local anaesthetic and anti-inflammatory activity; (3) Type-A polyphenolic procyanidins — the fraction most closely associated with insulin-sensitising and antidiabetic effects in experimental models; (4) condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins including cinnamtannin B1) — astringent and antidiarrhoeal properties; (5) diterpenes (cinnzeylanol, cinnzeylanine) — limited data, possible antifungal contribution; (6) trace coumarin — unlike C. cassia, C. verum contains only negligible coumarin, substantially reducing hepatotoxicity risk with habitual use.

**MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE:** Supported by clinical and pharmacological data: (a) Dyspepsia and gastrointestinal spasm: carminative and antispasmodic action on GI smooth muscle, supported by both traditional pharmacopoeial listing and in vitro spasmolytic studies; (b) Loss of appetite [appetence disorders]: aromatics including cinnamon are listed in multiple pharmacopoeias as appetite stimulants; (c) Adjunctive use in type 2 diabetes / blood glucose modulation: the most clinically studied contemporary indication — see Clinical Evidence section.

**MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL:** Listed in the pharmacopoeias of multiple countries as a carminative, stomachic [digestive tonic], antispasmodic, antimicrobial, and flavouring agent. Traditional systems (Ayurveda, Unani, Chinese medicine) employ cinnamon for dyspepsia, nausea, flatulence, diarrhoea, and as a warming circulatory stimulant. The German Commission E approved Cortex Cinnamomi for loss of appetite and dyspeptic complaints including mild gastrointestinal spasms, bloating, and flatulence.

**PHARMACOLOGY:** (1) Antispasmodic: cinnamaldehyde and eugenol inhibit smooth muscle contraction in isolated gut preparations, likely via calcium channel antagonism and inhibition of prostaglandin biosynthesis. (2) Antimicrobial/antifungal: cinnamaldehyde demonstrates broad-spectrum activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (including E. coli, Staphylococcus aureus) and fungi (Candida albicans) in vitro, via disruption of cell membrane integrity. (3) Insulin-sensitising: water-soluble polyphenolic polymers (Type-A procyanidins) activate insulin receptor tyrosine kinase, inhibit protein tyrosine phosphatase (PTP1B), and increase glucose transporter (GLUT-4) translocation in adipocyte cell models — resulting in enhanced glucose uptake. (4) Antioxidant: bark extracts exhibit significant radical-scavenging activity (DPPH assay), attributed to the polyphenolic fraction. (5) Anti-inflammatory: inhibition of COX-1 and COX-2 pathways documented in vitro.

**CLINICAL EVIDENCE:** (1) Khan et al. (2003, Diabetes Care): In a randomised, placebo-controlled trial, 60 Pakistani patients with type 2 diabetes received 1 g, 3 g, or 6 g of C. cassia powder daily for 40 days. All three cinnamon doses significantly reduced fasting serum glucose (18–29%), triglycerides (23–30%), LDL cholesterol (7–27%), and total cholesterol (12–26%) vs. placebo. Note: This landmark trial used C. cassia, not C. verum; extrapolation to Cortex Cinnamomi (C. verum) requires caution, though the polyphenolic fractions are structurally analogous. (2) Mang et al. (2006, European Journal of Clinical Investigation): A double-blind RCT in 65 German patients with type 2 diabetes given aqueous cinnamon extract (C. cassia) 336 mg/day for 4 months demonstrated a significant reduction in fasting plasma glucose (10.3% reduction vs. 3.4% placebo; p=0.046) with no significant change in HbA1c. (3) Systematic reviews (Kirkham et al., 2009; Leach & Kumar, 2012): Meta-analyses of available RCTs concluded that cinnamon supplementation produces statistically significant reductions in fasting blood glucose and lipid parameters, but heterogeneity of trials (differing species, dosage, patient populations) limits definitive clinical recommendations. The WHO monograph acknowledges this evidence as promising but notes that high-quality, species-specific trials for C. verum remain limited.

**ADVERSE REACTIONS:** Generally regarded as safe at culinary doses. Adverse effects at medicinal doses may include: allergic contact dermatitis (sensitisation to cinnamaldehyde — a recognised allergen); mucous membrane irritation with undiluted essential oil; stomatitis [mouth inflammation] with prolonged oral mucosal contact. Drug interactions: theoretical potentiation of hypoglycaemic agents (insulin, oral antidiabetics) — blood glucose monitoring advised with concurrent use. Anticoagulant interaction risk is low for C. verum given negligible coumarin content (contrast: C. cassia at high doses may interact with warfarin).

**CONTRAINDICATIONS:** Hypersensitivity to cinnamon or Peruvian balsam [cross-reactivity via cinnamaldehyde]. Pregnancy (at doses substantially above culinary use) — uterine stimulant effects documented in animal models; culinary use is not contraindicated. Peptic ulcer disease — may exacerbate irritation. Liver disease — though primarily a concern with high-coumarin C. cassia, caution is advised.

**DOSAGE (WHO-recommended, adults):** (a) Crude bark/powder: 0.5–1.0 g, two to three times daily (Commission E); (b) Infusion [tea]: 0.5–1.0 g bark in 150 mL hot water, 2–3 times daily; (c) Tincture (1:5 in 70% ethanol): 2–4 mL, three times daily; (d) Essential oil (internal use): 0.05–0.2 mL daily — use only under professional supervision due to mucosal irritancy. Total daily dose of bark: typically 2–4 g/day for therapeutic indications.

segments.title_inferred: Goldthread (Rhizoma Coptidis)
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Coptidis — Coptis chinensis Franch. (Ranunculaceae). Major constituents: isoquinoline alkaloids, principally berberine (5–8% dry weight), with coptisine, palmatine, jatrorrhizine, epiberberine, columbamine, and magnoflorine as secondary alkaloids. Berberine content is the primary pharmacopoeial quality marker.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Rhizoma Coptidis consists of the dried rhizome of Coptis chinensis Franch. (family Ranunculaceae), collected in autumn, cleaned of fibrous roots and soil, and dried. The drug is official in the pharmacopoeias of China, Japan, and Korea, and is defined by a minimum berberine hydrochloride content (typically ≥5.5% in Chinese Pharmacopoeia standards). The characteristic bright-yellow colour of cut surfaces is attributable to berberine concentration in vascular tissue.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Coptis chinensis is a small perennial herb, 15–35 cm tall. Leaves are basal, long-petiolate, 3-foliolate with ovate to lanceolate leaflets that are deeply pinnately lobed, dark green above, paler below. Rhizomes are slender, clustered, laterally branched, irregularly knotted, 1–6 cm long and 2–5 mm in diameter, externally yellow-brown to dark brown, internally bright yellow. Flowers are small, white to yellowish-green, borne in cymose clusters. Fruit is a follicle. The genus name references the golden colour of the rhizome cross-section.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to central and southwestern China, primarily Sichuan, Yunnan, Guizhou, Hunan, and Hubei provinces. Cultivated extensively in Sichuan (regarded as the superior-quality producing region [dao di yao cai]). Also found in parts of Japan and Korea. Grows in moist, shaded mountain forests at elevations of 1000–2000 m. Commercial cultivation requires 5–6 years before harvest.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The dominant pharmacologically active constituent is berberine (an isoquinoline alkaloid), comprising approximately 5–8% of dried rhizome weight, with some analyses reporting up to 9.9% in high-grade material. Secondary alkaloids present in significant but smaller quantities include: coptisine (0.2–1.0%), palmatine (0.2–0.5%), jatrorrhizine, epiberberine, columbamine, and magnoflorine. Trace constituents include ferulic acid, worenine, and various lignans. The alkaloid profile provides the yellow colouration and constitutes the primary basis for pharmacological activity.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: (1) Antimicrobial / gastrointestinal infections: Berberine (as isolated constituent) has been evaluated in multiple randomised clinical trials for acute infectious diarrhoea and intestinal bacterial infections. A controlled trial (n=165) comparing berberine hydrochloride (400 mg/day orally) to tetracycline in patients with Vibrio cholerae-associated diarrhoea demonstrated significant reduction in stool volume and duration, though inferior to tetracycline monotherapy; combination use showed additive benefit. (2) Type 2 diabetes mellitus: A landmark randomised controlled trial (Zhang et al., 2008; n=116) compared berberine (500 mg three times daily for 3 months) to metformin in newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes patients. Berberine reduced HbA1c from 9.5% to 7.5% (p<0.0001), fasting blood glucose by 35%, and postprandial glucose by 44.7%, with comparable efficacy to metformin and additional beneficial effects on lipid profiles (triglyceride and LDL-cholesterol reduction). (3) Hyperlipidaemia: A randomised controlled trial (Kong et al., 2004; n=32) demonstrated berberine 500 mg twice daily for 3 months significantly reduced total cholesterol (29%), LDL-cholesterol (25%), and triglycerides (35%) versus placebo (p<0.05 for all measures).

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Rhizoma Coptidis has been used in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) for over 2000 years, documented in the Shennong Bencao Jing (Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica, circa 200 CE). Traditional indications include: clearing 'heat' and eliminating 'dampness' [qingre zaoshi]; treating dysentery, enteritis, and diarrhoea; reducing fever; treating oral ulcers and toothache (topical); managing gastritis, nausea, and vomiting; and treating skin infections and wounds topically. Used in classical formulations including Huang Lian Jie Du Tang (Coptis Decoction to Relieve Toxicity) for inflammatory and febrile conditions. Traditional dosage: 2–10 g dried rhizome per day as decoction.

PHARMACOLOGY: Berberine exerts its primary pharmacological actions through multiple well-characterised mechanisms: (1) Antimicrobial: Berberine intercalates with bacterial DNA, inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, and disrupts bacterial cell membrane integrity. Active against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus spp., Escherichia coli, Vibrio cholerae, Shigella spp., Salmonella spp., Helicobacter pylori, and multiple fungal species in vitro. MIC values for common pathogens range from 16–512 μg/mL. (2) Antidiabetic: Berberine activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) — a cellular energy sensor — enhancing glucose uptake in peripheral tissues, reducing hepatic glucose output, and improving insulin sensitivity independently of the insulin receptor pathway. Additionally upregulates hepatic low-density lipoprotein receptor expression via a post-transcriptional mechanism (mRNA stabilisation), explaining lipid-lowering effects. (3) Anti-inflammatory: Berberine inhibits NF-κB signalling, suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) expression in vitro and in animal models. (4) Antidiarrhoeal: Inhibits intestinal secretion by blocking chloride channels and reducing gut motility via smooth muscle relaxation in animal models. In vivo studies confirm significant antidiarrhoeal activity in rodent models of castor oil-induced and bacterial diarrhoea.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Most commonly reported adverse effects are gastrointestinal: nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, constipation (paradoxically, despite antidiarrhoeal use), occurring in approximately 5–10% of subjects in clinical trials. High doses of isolated berberine may cause hypotension, bradycardia, and have demonstrated QTc interval prolongation in in vitro cardiac models — clinical cardiac safety data at conventional doses are reassuring but monitoring is advisable in susceptible individuals. Theoretical risk of neonatal jaundice due to bilirubin displacement from albumin (berberine); this is a significant concern. Drug interactions: Berberine inhibits cytochrome P450 enzymes (notably CYP3A4, CYP2D6), potentially increasing plasma concentrations of co-administered drugs metabolised by these pathways (e.g., cyclosporine, certain statins, macrolide antibiotics). May potentiate hypoglycaemic effects of antidiabetic medications.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: (1) Pregnancy — contraindicated; berberine has demonstrated uterotonic activity and potential embryotoxicity in animal studies. (2) Neonates and infants — contraindicated; significant risk of haemolytic jaundice due to erythrocyte G6PD-related mechanisms and bilirubin displacement. (3) Patients with known hypersensitivity to Coptis species or berberine-containing plants. (4) Caution in patients with pre-existing cardiac arrhythmias or QTc prolongation. (5) Caution with concurrent use of CYP3A4-sensitive medications with narrow therapeutic indices.

DOSAGE — WHO-RECOMMENDED ADULT DOSES: Dried rhizome (decoction): 1.5–9 g per day, prepared as traditional water decoction. Powdered rhizome: 1.5–3 g per day in divided doses. Standardised berberine hydrochloride (isolated constituent, not whole rhizome): 200–500 mg three times daily (as used in clinical trials for diabetes and hyperlipidaemia). Topical preparations: Aqueous extract or decoction applied directly to affected areas for skin infections and oral ulcers. Treatment duration for acute conditions (diarrhoea): 3–7 days. For metabolic conditions (diabetes, dyslipidaemia): 8–24 weeks in clinical studies; long-term safety beyond 6 months not yet fully established by systematic review.

segments.title_inferred: Feverfew
segments.original_text: Herba Tanaceti Parthenii — Tanacetum parthenium (L.) Schultz Bip. (Asteraceae). Dried aerial parts of the flowering plant. Principal active constituents: sesquiterpene lactones (0.1–0.9% dry weight), predominantly parthenolide (the major germacranolide); flavonoids including apigenin, luteolin, quercetin, and their glycosides; volatile oils (camphor, borneol, chrysanthenyl acetate); polyacetylenes; and melatonin. Parthenolide content, expressed as a percentage of dry weight, is the primary quality marker used in pharmacopoeial standardization, with a minimum threshold of 0.2% recommended in several national standards.
segments.translation:
Feverfew (Tanacetum parthenium) is a perennial herb of the daisy family (Asteraceae) whose dried aerial parts — harvested during flowering — constitute the official medicinal drug 'Herba Tanaceti Parthenii.' The plant has a long history in European herbal medicine, principally as a remedy for fevers (hence the common name), headaches, arthritis, and menstrual irregularities. Modern pharmacological and clinical investigation has focused almost entirely on its use in the prophylaxis [prevention] of migraine headache.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Tanacetum parthenium is a strongly aromatic, branching perennial herb growing 25–60 cm in height. Leaves are alternate, yellowish-green, pinnately lobed with toothed margins, and covered in fine hairs. Flowerheads are daisy-like (capitula), typically 1–2 cm in diameter, with white ray florets and yellow disc florets, borne in dense corymbs [flat-topped clusters]. The achenes [seeds] are ribbed and lack a pappus. The plant has a characteristic bitter, pungent odour when crushed, attributable to its volatile oil fraction.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to southeastern Europe and western Asia (particularly the Balkan peninsula and Caucasus region), Tanacetum parthenium has been widely naturalised throughout Europe, North America, and temperate regions of South America and Australia. It is cultivated commercially in the United Kingdom, continental Europe, and North America for medicinal use, and is also a common garden ornamental.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The primary pharmacologically active constituents are the sesquiterpene lactones (0.1–0.9% of dry weight), of which parthenolide is the dominant compound and the internationally recognised quality marker. Parthenolide is a germacranolide-type sesquiterpene lactone bearing an alpha-methylene-gamma-lactone group responsible for much of its biological activity. Additional sesquiterpene lactones include canin, artecanin, secotanaparthenolide A and B, and reynosin. Flavonoids present include apigenin, luteolin, quercetin, and chrysoeriol and their glycosides. The volatile oil (0.02–0.07%) contains camphor as the principal component, with borneol, bornyl acetate, chrysanthenyl acetate, and trans-chrysanthenol. Melatonin has been identified in leaf tissue. Polyacetylenes and tanetin (a flavonoid) have also been reported. Commercial products vary considerably in parthenolide content; the minimum acceptable content for therapeutic preparations is generally set at 0.2% by dry weight.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: The predominant evidence-based indication is the prophylaxis of migraine. Multiple randomised controlled trials conducted from the 1980s onward support a statistically significant reduction in migraine attack frequency (though not necessarily severity or duration) when feverfew is taken continuously as a preventive agent. It is not indicated for the acute treatment of an established migraine attack. Evidence for anti-inflammatory and antiarthritic effects in humans remains preliminary.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: In traditional European herbal medicine (documented in pharmacopoeias from the 16th century onward), feverfew was used for: (1) reducing fevers; (2) relieving headaches and migraines; (3) treating arthritis and rheumatism; (4) promoting menstruation [emmenagogue action]; (5) relieving dizziness and tinnitus; (6) treating psoriasis; and (7) as a digestive tonic. Topical use as an insect repellent has also been recorded.

PHARMACOLOGY — MECHANISMS OF ACTION: Parthenolide and related sesquiterpene lactones exert their biological effects through multiple mechanisms. (1) Inhibition of platelet aggregation: Parthenolide inhibits platelet secretory activity, including the release of serotonin [5-hydroxytryptamine] from platelets — a process implicated in the pathophysiology of migraine. This is thought to occur via inhibition of phospholipase A2 and suppression of arachidonic acid release, thereby reducing thromboxane B2 and prostaglandin biosynthesis. (2) Anti-inflammatory activity: Feverfew extracts inhibit prostaglandin synthesis independently of cyclooxygenase [COX] inhibition (distinguishing it mechanistically from conventional NSAIDs [non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs]), and also inhibit the release of inflammatory mediators from polymorphonuclear leukocytes and macrophages. Parthenolide has been shown to directly alkylate and inhibit the NF-κB [nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells] signalling pathway, a central regulator of inflammation. (3) Smooth muscle effects: Feverfew extracts inhibit serotonin-induced smooth muscle contraction, relevant to vascular tone changes associated with migraine. (4) Inhibition of platelet phospholipase A2: Distinct from the above, this reduces generation of platelet-activating factor (PAF). In vivo studies in rodent models confirm anti-inflammatory and antinociceptive [pain-reducing] effects of feverfew extracts.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — KEY TRIALS:
(1) Johnson et al. (1985, London, UK): A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial in 17 patients with self-reported migraine who had been consuming fresh feverfew leaves daily. Patients withdrawn from feverfew to placebo experienced a significant increase in migraine frequency and severity, along with the re-emergence of associated symptoms (nausea, vomiting), confirming a prophylactic effect. Patients maintained on feverfew showed no deterioration. This study introduced the concept of a 'post-feverfew syndrome' upon cessation.
(2) Murphy et al. (1988, Nottingham, UK): A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled parallel-group trial (n=72) using one capsule per day of dried feverfew leaf (82 mg). Results showed a 24% reduction in the number of migraine attacks per month in the treatment group versus placebo (p<0.05), with associated reductions in nausea and vomiting, though no significant change in the severity of individual attacks that did occur. This remains one of the most cited positive trials.
(3) De Weerdt et al. (1996, Netherlands): A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial using a CO2 [carbon dioxide]-extracted feverfew preparation with a defined but low parthenolide content. This trial found no significant difference between treatment and placebo in migraine frequency. The negative result has been attributed to the low or altered parthenolide content in the CO2 extract, highlighting the critical importance of standardisation to parthenolide content for efficacy. A subsequent Cochrane systematic review (Pittler & Ernst) concluded that the evidence, while promising, remained insufficient for definitive recommendations due to methodological variability across trials.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: The most commonly reported adverse effect is oral ulceration [mouth ulcers], occurring in approximately 10–18% of users of fresh leaf preparations; this is less frequently reported with encapsulated dried leaf or extract products. Gastric irritation, nausea, abdominal pain, and flatulence are reported. Dermatitis (contact sensitisation) can occur in individuals handling fresh plant material, attributable to the alpha-methylene-gamma-lactone moiety of parthenolide — a recognised allergen in the Asteraceae/Compositae family. Upon abrupt cessation after prolonged use, a withdrawal syndrome ('post-feverfew syndrome') has been described, consisting of rebound headaches, anxiety, insomnia, muscle stiffness, and joint pain. Potential drug interactions: anticoagulants and antiplatelet drugs (additive effect on platelet inhibition, increased bleeding risk); theoretically, feverfew may potentiate warfarin and aspirin effects, though clinical data are limited.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: (1) Pregnancy: Feverfew is contraindicated in pregnancy due to traditional reports and plausible pharmacological basis for uterine stimulation [emmenagogue and abortifacient risk]; this is reinforced by the WHO Monograph. (2) Hypersensitivity to feverfew or to other members of the Asteraceae (Compositae) family (e.g., chamomile, ragweed, chrysanthemum, echinacea). (3) Children under 2 years of age. (4) Patients receiving anticoagulant therapy (relative contraindication requiring medical supervision).

DOSAGE — WHO RECOMMENDED (Adults): For migraine prophylaxis: dried leaf preparation standardised to a minimum of 0.2% parthenolide — 125 mg of dried leaf daily, taken orally with or after food, as a single dose or divided doses. Fresh leaf preparations: 2–3 fresh leaves (approximately 2.5 μmol parthenolide) daily, chewed with food (bread or similar to reduce mucosal irritation). Tincture (1:5 in 25% ethanol): 5 ml twice daily. Note: Feverfew must be taken continuously as a preventive (prophylactic) agent; it has no established role in acute migraine treatment. A minimum treatment period of 3 months is recommended to assess efficacy. Duration of use beyond 4 months has not been systematically evaluated in controlled trials.
segments.preparation: 1. Dried leaf capsules/tablets: 125 mg daily (standardised to ≥0.2% parthenolide by dry weight), taken orally with food, as a single or divided dose. 2. Fresh leaf: 2–3 leaves (approximately 2.5 μmol parthenolide equivalent) daily, chewed with food to reduce mucosal irritation. 3. Tincture (1:5 in 25% ethanol): 5 ml twice daily. All preparations are for continuous oral prophylactic use. Not indicated for acute migraine treatment. Minimum trial period of 3 months recommended before assessing clinical response.
segments.condition: Migraine prophylaxis (primary evidence-based indication); historically also used for fever, arthritis, rheumatism, dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation], and as a general anti-inflammatory agent (traditional/limited clinical evidence only).
segments.source_section: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 — Herba Tanaceti Parthenii

segments.title_inferred: Ginger
segments.original_text: Rhizoma Zingiberis — Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae). Dried or fresh rhizome. Major chemical constituents include: volatile oil (1–3%), comprising zingiberene (35%), ar-curcumene, β-sesquiphellandrene, bisabolene; oleoresin (4–7.5%), comprising gingerols (primary pungent principles, chiefly [6]-gingerol), shogaols (formed from gingerols on drying/heating), paradols, zingerone, gingerdiol, and diarylheptanoids; fixed oils, proteolytic enzymes (zingibain), and starch (up to 50%). Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Rhizoma Zingiberis consists of the fresh or dried rhizome (underground stem) of Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae), with or without cork. The rhizome is the pharmacopoeially recognized plant part used medicinally.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Zingiber officinale is a perennial herb reaching 60–120 cm in height. It produces an aromatic, branched, laterally compressed rhizome (the part used medicinally) that is pale yellow internally. The aerial pseudostem bears narrow lance-shaped [lanceolate] leaves 15–20 cm in length, arranged in two rows. Flowers are borne on separate stalks arising directly from the rhizome, in dense cone-like [spicate] inflorescences with overlapping green bracts tipped with yellow. The plant rarely sets seed in cultivation and propagates vegetatively by rhizome division.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to Southeast Asia (likely southern China or India; exact origin uncertain due to ancient cultivation). Now cultivated pantropically, with major commercial production in India, China, Jamaica, Nigeria, Australia, and Fiji. India is the world's largest producer. The plant requires a warm, humid climate and is grown at altitudes up to approximately 1,500 m.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The rhizome contains:
• Volatile (essential) oil (1–3% of dry weight): zingiberene (≈35%, the principal sesquiterpene), ar-curcumene, β-sesquiphellandrene, and bisabolene as dominant sesquiterpenes; monoterpenes including camphene, geranial, and linalool.
• Oleoresin / pungent principles (4–7.5%): Gingerols ([6]-gingerol predominant; also [8]- and [10]-gingerol) are the primary pungent constituents of fresh rhizome. On drying or heating, gingerols dehydrate to the more pungent shogaols ([6]-shogaol most abundant in dried ginger). Paradols, zingerone, gingerdiones, and diarylheptanoids are also present.
• Fixed (fatty) oils.
• Proteolytic enzyme: zingibain (a cysteine protease).
• Starch: the major component by weight (up to 50%).
• Resins, proteins, and minerals.

MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence Basis:
The following uses are supported by controlled clinical data:
1. Prevention and treatment of nausea and vomiting associated with motion sickness, postoperative nausea, and chemotherapy-induced nausea.
2. Symptomatic relief of nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (morning sickness) — multiple randomized trials support efficacy superior to placebo.
3. Dyspepsia [functional indigestion]: promotion of gastric emptying and relief of bloating, flatulence, and epigastric discomfort.

MEDICINAL USES — Traditional / Pharmacopoeial:
Traditional systems (Ayurveda, Traditional Chinese Medicine [TCM], Unani, and various European folk traditions) document ginger for: carminative and antispasmodic effects; stimulation of appetite and gastric secretion; treatment of colds, influenza, and mild febrile states (as a diaphoretic); anti-inflammatory and analgesic applications in rheumatic complaints; antimicrobial use; and as an adjuvant ('carrier herb') to enhance the bioavailability and absorption of other medicinal plants in compound preparations.

PHARMACOLOGY — Mechanisms of Action:
• Antiemetic: [6]-gingerol and [6]-shogaol antagonize 5-HT₃ (serotonin type 3) receptors in the gut, a key mechanism shared with ondansetron-class antiemetics. Additional interaction with substance P (NK₁) pathways has been proposed. Peripheral action on gastric motility complements central antiemetic effects.
• Prokinetic / carminative: Gingerols and shogaols enhance gastric antral contraction and accelerate gastric emptying by interaction with cholinergic and serotonergic receptors in the gastrointestinal tract; the volatile oil contributes carminative (gas-expelling) activity.
• Anti-inflammatory: Gingerols inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) and 5-lipoxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis, analogous in mechanism to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs [NSAIDs] but at higher concentrations. [6]-shogaol additionally suppresses nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) activation in vitro.
• Antioxidant: Gingerols, shogaols, and zingerone are potent free-radical scavengers demonstrated in multiple in vitro assays.
• Antiplatelet / cardioprotective: In vitro evidence shows inhibition of thromboxane synthetase and reduction of platelet aggregation; clinical significance at standard doses is uncertain.
• Analgesic: Animal (in vivo) studies demonstrate dose-dependent analgesic activity attributed to prostaglandin inhibition and serotonergic modulation.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials:
1. MOTION SICKNESS (Mowrey & Clayson, 1982; replicated in multiple subsequent trials): A double-blind RCT compared ginger rhizome powder (940 mg) against dimenhydrinate (100 mg) in subjects provoked by rotating chair. Ginger was statistically superior to placebo and comparable to the standard antiemetic in reducing vomiting and cold sweats. Effect attributed to peripheral gastrointestinal rather than central vestibular action.
2. POSTOPERATIVE NAUSEA AND VOMITING (PONV): A systematic review of multiple double-blind RCTs (including Bone et al. and Phillips et al.) found that ginger powder (0.5–1.0 g pre-operatively) significantly reduced nausea incidence and the need for rescue antiemetics compared to placebo in patients undergoing elective gynaecological surgery. Effect sizes were modest but consistent.
3. PREGNANCY-RELATED NAUSEA AND VOMITING: Fischer-Rasmussen et al. (double-blind crossover RCT, n=30) demonstrated that ginger extract (250 mg capsules × 4 daily = 1 g/day) produced significant relief of hyperemesis gravidarum [severe pregnancy vomiting] compared to placebo (p<0.001), with no adverse fetal outcomes noted at study doses. Findings corroborated by subsequent trials from Thailand and Australia.

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
• At recommended doses: generally well tolerated. Mild gastrointestinal effects (heartburn, eructation [belching], bloating, epigastric discomfort) are the most commonly reported, particularly with powdered dried rhizome on an empty stomach.
• Topical application of oleoresin may cause contact dermatitis in sensitized individuals.
• At very high doses (above 5 g/day): theoretical risk of mucosal irritation.
• Drug Interactions: Theoretical potentiation of anticoagulant therapy (warfarin) due to antiplatelet effects; clinical evidence for this interaction at standard medicinal doses is weak but caution is advised. May theoretically potentiate hypoglycaemic agents.

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
• Known hypersensitivity to Zingiber officinale or other Zingiberaceae.
• Gallstones [cholelithiasis] or bile duct obstruction: ginger stimulates bile secretion [cholagogue effect] and should be used with caution or avoided.
• Use in pregnancy beyond culinary amounts should only be undertaken under medical supervision, despite evidence of efficacy in morning sickness; first-trimester use at high doses requires medical oversight.
• Use in children under 2 years not established.

DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Doses:
• Crude dried rhizome powder (oral): 0.5–1.0 g, up to three times daily (total: 1–3 g/day). For motion sickness prevention: 1–2 g as a single dose 30 minutes before travel.
• Fresh rhizome: 0.5–1.0 g equivalent per dose.
• Fluid extract (1:1 in 45% ethanol): 0.7–2.0 mL per dose.
• Tincture (1:5 in 90% ethanol): 1.7–5.0 mL per dose.
• Infusion/decoction: 1–2 teaspoons (approx. 2–4 g) of coarsely powdered rhizome in 150 mL hot water, steeped 5–10 minutes.
Maximum recommended dose: 6 g dried rhizome/day (WHO); doses in excess of this are not clinically justified and may increase risk of adverse gastrointestinal effects.

segments.title_inferred: Marshmallow Root (Radix Althaeae)
segments.original_text: Radix Althaeae — Althaea officinalis L. (Malvaceae). Dried root of Althaea officinalis, collected in autumn from plants at least 2 years old, with outer bark removed. Major constituents: mucilaginous polysaccharides (mucilage 25–35%, principally glucuronoxylans, glucans, arabinogalactans, and acidic rhamnogalacturonans), pectin (11%), flavonoids (hypolaetin-8-glucoside, isoquercitrin, kaempferol), phenolic acids (caffeic, chlorogenic, p-coumaric, ferulic, p-hydroxybenzoic, salicylic, syringic, and vanillic acids), scopoletin (coumarin), sucrose (~10%), and starch (~35%). Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
segments.translation:
DEFINITION: Radix Althaeae consists of the dried, peeled (bark-removed) roots of Althaea officinalis L. (family Malvaceae), harvested in autumn from plants not less than two years old. The root must contain not less than 25% water-swelling mucilage by pharmacopoeial standard.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Althaea officinalis is a perennial herb reaching 1.5–2 m in height. Stems are erect, branched, and densely covered with stellate (star-shaped) hairs. Leaves are alternate, broadly ovate to cordate, shallowly 3–5-lobed, velvety-soft on both surfaces due to dense stellate tomentum [fine woolly covering]. Flowers are pale pink to white, axillary, with a characteristic 9-lobed epicalyx [outer whorl of sepal-like bracts]. The fruit is a schizocarp [a dry fruit splitting into segments] consisting of 15–25 mericarps. The taproot is fleshy, white to yellowish-white internally, with a tough, fibrous texture after drying.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to central and eastern Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. Naturally grows in salt marshes, riverbanks, and damp meadows. Commercially cultivated in France, Germany, Hungary, Bulgaria, Poland, Russia, and the United States. The root is sourced primarily from cultivation rather than wild collection.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:
— Mucilaginous polysaccharides: 25–35% (the primary active fraction); composed of glucuronoxylans, arabinogalactans, glucans, and acidic rhamnogalacturonans. These form a highly viscous colloidal gel upon hydration.
— Starch: approximately 35%.
— Pectin: approximately 11%.
— Sucrose: approximately 10%.
— Flavonoids: hypolaetin-8-glucoside [a C-glucoside flavone], isoquercitrin, kaempferol.
— Phenolic acids: caffeic, chlorogenic, p-coumaric, ferulic, p-hydroxybenzoic, salicylic, syringic, and vanillic acids.
— Scopoletin: a coumarin derivative with mild antispasmodic properties.
— Asparagine and other free amino acids.

MEDICINAL USES — Clinical / Evidence-Based:
The principal evidence-supported indication is symptomatic relief of irritation of oral and pharyngeal mucous membranes and associated dry cough [tussis sicca]. A secondary indication is mild symptomatic relief of gastrointestinal irritation, including gastritis and mild peptic discomfort. These uses are recognized by the German Commission E and by the European Scientific Cooperative on Phytotherapy (ESCOP).

MEDICINAL USES — Traditional / Pharmacopoeial:
Listed in numerous national pharmacopoeias (British, German, French, Austrian, and others). Traditional applications include: (1) soothing inflammation of mucous membranes of the mouth, throat, and bronchi; (2) relief of irritable and dry coughs; (3) symptomatic treatment of mild gastric and intestinal inflammation; (4) topical application to minor skin inflammation, wounds, and burns (as a poultice or decoction); (5) urinary tract irritation and mild cystitis. Documented in European traditional medicine from antiquity; Dioscorides and Galen both described the demulcent [soothing, film-forming] properties of mallow-family roots.

PHARMACOLOGY — Key Mechanisms of Action:
(a) DEMULCENT / MUCOADHESIVE ACTION: Upon contact with water, the high-molecular-weight polysaccharides (particularly the arabinogalactans and glucuronoxylans) hydrate and form a dense, viscous gel layer that physically coats and protects irritated mucous membranes of the oropharynx, esophagus, stomach, and intestines. This mechanical barrier reduces direct contact between irritants (stomach acid, pathogens, allergens) and the epithelium.
(b) ANTI-INFLAMMATORY ACTIVITY: In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of complement [a cascade of immune proteins involved in inflammation] activation pathways by the root polysaccharide fraction. Specifically, the mucilage fraction has been shown to inhibit the classical complement pathway at the C3 convertase step, reducing inflammatory amplification. Hypolaetin-8-glucoside has demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in carrageenan-induced paw edema models in rats (in vivo), with activity comparable at high doses to mild non-steroidal reference compounds.
(c) ANTITUSSIVE EFFECT: Proposed as a reflex mechanism — the coating of pharyngeal mucosa reduces afferent [sensory, toward the brain] nerve stimulation that triggers the cough reflex. No direct central antitussive action has been demonstrated.
(d) ANTIMICROBIAL ACTIVITY: An aqueous root extract has shown in vitro inhibitory activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Proteus mirabilis, among other organisms, though the clinical relevance of this finding remains unestablished.
(e) HYPOGLYCAEMIC ACTIVITY: Animal studies (in vivo, rodent) demonstrate significant blood glucose-lowering effects following oral administration of the root extract, attributed in part to the pectin and polysaccharide fractions slowing glucose absorption. Clinical significance in humans has not been formally established.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials:
(1) PHARYNGEAL IRRITATION / COUGH (Randomized Controlled Trial, Germany): A syrup preparation standardized to marshmallow root extract was evaluated in patients with irritative dry cough of non-infectious etiology (n = 822, open-label cohort with physician assessment). After 2 weeks of treatment, 89.9% of patients rated treatment efficacy as 'good' or 'very good.' Throat irritation scores and cough frequency scores both showed statistically significant reductions from baseline. Adverse events were rare and minor (primarily mild gastrointestinal complaints in <1% of participants). [Note: open-label design limits conclusions; placebo-controlled data are limited.]
(2) GASTRIC MUCOSA PROTECTION (In vitro / mechanistic study): Phosphate-buffered extracts of Radix Althaeae demonstrated dose-dependent inhibition of gastric mucosal cell damage induced by ethanol in vitro, with the polysaccharide-enriched fraction performing best. Protective effect was attributed to gel-forming adherence rather than pharmacodynamic inhibition of acid secretion.
(3) COMPLEMENT INHIBITION (In vitro): Purified polysaccharide fractions from Radix Althaeae (acidic arabinogalactan fraction) significantly inhibited zymosan-induced complement activation in human serum at concentrations achievable in vivo after oral administration. The IC50 [half-maximal inhibitory concentration] was in the range of 0.5–1.0 mg/mL. This supports a plausible mechanistic basis for the anti-inflammatory claim.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally very well tolerated. Reported adverse effects are rare and include: mild gastrointestinal discomfort (flatulence, mild nausea); hypersensitivity reactions (very rare; Malvaceae allergy should be considered). No serious adverse events have been documented in the published literature at recommended doses. Drug interactions: theoretically, the mucilage layer formed in the GI tract may delay or reduce absorption of concurrently administered oral medications; it is advised that marshmallow root preparations be taken separately (at least 30–60 minutes apart) from other oral drugs.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: No absolute contraindications established at recommended doses. Use with caution in patients with diabetes mellitus (possible hypoglycaemic effect). Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and pediatric populations below age 3 has not been formally established; traditional use suggests reasonable safety, but WHO monograph recommends caution pending formal data.

DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Doses:
— Cold maceration (preferred preparation, to preserve mucilage integrity): 2–8 g of cut or powdered root macerated in 150 mL cold water for 30–90 minutes with frequent stirring, then strained; taken 3 times daily.
— Dry root powder: 6 g/day in divided doses.
— Syrup (prepared from cold aqueous extract): 10 mL (equivalent to approximately 0.5 g dried root per dose), 3–5 times daily.
— Tincture (1:5, ethanol 25%): 5–15 mL, 3 times daily. [Note: alcohol-based preparations are less effective for mucilage delivery than aqueous cold preparations.]
— Topical poultice: 5 g powdered root mixed with warm water to form a paste; applied to affected skin areas as needed.
